reading.title: The Origins of Tea
reading.passage:
Tea is one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world, second only to water. According to a popular Chinese legend, tea was discovered by accident around 2737 BCE, when leaves from a wild bush blew into a pot of boiling water that the emperor Shen Nong was preparing. Whether or not this story is true, historical records confirm that tea drinking was well established in China long before it reached other parts of the world.

For many centuries, the secret of how to grow and process tea remained within China. Tea was transported along trade routes to neighbouring regions, and it gradually became a valuable item of exchange. By the time European traders arrived in Asia in the sixteenth century, tea was already deeply woven into the culture of several Asian societies.

The Portuguese and the Dutch were among the first Europeans to ship tea back to their home countries, where it was initially regarded as an expensive luxury available only to the wealthy. In Britain, tea did not become popular until the seventeenth century, but once it did, demand grew rapidly. The high price encouraged smuggling, and large quantities of tea entered the country illegally to avoid the heavy taxes imposed by the government.

Eventually, the British began cultivating tea in India in order to reduce their dependence on Chinese supplies. The plantations established there transformed tea from a rare luxury into an everyday drink that ordinary families could afford. Today, tea is produced in dozens of countries, and each region has developed its own distinctive varieties and traditions of preparation.

questions.q: Tea is consumed more than any other drink except water.
Knowledge of how to grow and process tea stayed within China for many centuries.
The Portuguese were the people who first introduced tea to China.
Tea became popular in Britain as soon as it first arrived there.
Smuggling of tea happened because of the high taxes placed on it.
The British began growing tea in ________ in order to depend less on Chinese supplies.
According to the passage, the tea plantations in India led to tea becoming:

listening.title: Joining the City Library
listening.script:
WOMAN: Good morning, I'd like to join the city library. What do I need to do?
MAN: Of course. I'll just take a few details. Can I have your full name?
WOMAN: Yes, it's Karen Mitchell. That's M-I-T-C-H-E-L-L.
MAN: Thank you. And your date of birth?
WOMAN: The twelfth of March, 1995.
MAN: Lovely. What's your current address?
WOMAN: It's forty-seven Park Avenue.
MAN: Now, there are two types of membership. The standard card is free and lets you borrow up to six books for three weeks. The premium card costs fifteen pounds a year and allows you to borrow twelve items, including DVDs.
WOMAN: I'll take the premium one, please.
MAN: No problem. The library is open from nine to eight on weekdays, but it closes early at five on Saturdays. We're closed on Sundays.
WOMAN: Good to know. Thank you so much.

questions.q: Surname: ________
Date of birth: 12th of ________ 1995
Address: ________ Park Avenue
Which membership does the woman choose?
Premium membership costs £________ a year.
On Saturdays the library closes at ________ o'clock.

speaking.q: Let's talk about your hometown. Where is it, and what do you like most about living there? Speak for about one to two minutes.

writing.q: Some people believe that children should begin learning a foreign language at primary school, while others think it is better to start in secondary school. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Urban Green Spaces
reading.passage:
As cities around the world continue to expand, urban planners are paying increasing attention to the role of green spaces such as parks, gardens and tree-lined streets. These areas were once viewed mainly as places for recreation, but research has shown that they provide a far wider range of benefits to city dwellers.

One of the most significant advantages of urban greenery is its effect on temperature. During hot weather, densely built areas can become noticeably warmer than the surrounding countryside, a phenomenon known as the urban heat island effect. Trees and plants help to cool the air by providing shade and releasing moisture, which can lower local temperatures by several degrees.

Green spaces also contribute to cleaner air. Vegetation absorbs certain pollutants and traps tiny particles that would otherwise be breathed in by residents. In addition, parks encourage physical activity, as people are more likely to walk, run or cycle when attractive outdoor spaces are available nearby.

There are psychological benefits too. Studies have repeatedly found that spending time in natural surroundings reduces stress and improves concentration. Some hospitals have even reported that patients with a view of greenery from their windows tend to recover more quickly than those without.

Despite these benefits, creating and maintaining green spaces can be costly, and land in city centres is often extremely valuable. Planners must therefore balance the demand for housing and commercial development against the long-term advantages that parks and gardens bring to a community.

questions.q: In the past, green spaces were mainly considered places for recreation.
The urban heat island effect makes city centres cooler than the surrounding countryside.
Plants can reduce local temperatures by providing shade and releasing moisture.
All hospitals now include gardens for their patients.
Vegetation absorbs pollutants and traps tiny ________ in the air.
Research shows that time in nature reduces stress and improves ________.
According to the passage, planners must:
options: ban all new housing in cities
balance development against the benefits of green spaces
move all parks to the countryside
reduce the value of city-centre land

listening.title: Student Accommodation Enquiry
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I'm looking for accommodation near the university for next term.
OFFICER: Sure. Are you interested in a hall of residence or a private flat?
STUDENT: A hall of residence, I think. It's my first year.
OFFICER: Good choice. We have two main halls. Riverside Hall is the closest, just five minutes' walk from campus, and a single room there costs ninety pounds a week. Greenfield Hall is a bit further, about twenty minutes away, but it's cheaper at seventy-five pounds a week.
STUDENT: Does the price include meals?
OFFICER: At Riverside, breakfast and dinner are included. Greenfield is self-catered.
STUDENT: And is there internet?
OFFICER: Yes, both halls have free wireless internet in every room.
STUDENT: What about laundry?
OFFICER: There's a laundry room in each building. It costs two pounds per wash.
STUDENT: Great. I think I'll go for Riverside Hall.
OFFICER: Excellent. I'll send you the application form by email today.

questions.q: What type of accommodation does the student choose?
Riverside Hall is ________ minutes' walk from campus.
A single room at Greenfield Hall costs £________ a week.
Which meals are included at Riverside Hall?
Using the laundry costs £________ per wash.
The student finally chooses ________ Hall.

speaking.q: Describe a skill you would like to learn. You should say: what the skill is, why you want to learn it, and how you would learn it; and explain how this skill would be useful to you. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of workers who travelled to work by public transport in four cities in 2000 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (% using public transport):
- City A — 2000: 35%, 2020: 52%
- City B — 2000: 48%, 2020: 45%
- City C — 2000: 20%, 2020: 38%
- City D — 2000: 60%, 2020: 66%

reading.title: The Science of Sleep
reading.passage:
Sleep is something every human being needs, yet for a long time scientists understood very little about why it is so important. Early researchers assumed that sleep was simply a period of rest, during which the body did almost nothing. Modern studies, however, have revealed that the brain remains remarkably active while we sleep.

A typical night's sleep is divided into several cycles, each lasting around ninety minutes. Within each cycle, the brain moves through different stages, including a phase known as REM, or rapid eye movement, sleep. It is during REM sleep that most vivid dreaming occurs. Researchers believe this stage plays an essential part in processing emotions and storing memories from the previous day.

The amount of sleep a person needs changes with age. Newborn babies may sleep for sixteen hours a day, whereas most adults function best with around seven to eight hours. Regularly getting too little sleep has been linked to a range of problems, including poor concentration, weakened immunity and an increased risk of certain illnesses.

Despite this knowledge, many people in modern societies do not get enough rest. Bright screens, busy schedules and artificial lighting can all interfere with the body's natural sleep patterns. Experts recommend keeping a regular bedtime, avoiding electronic devices before sleep, and ensuring that the bedroom is dark and quiet. Such simple measures, they argue, can greatly improve both the quality and the quantity of sleep that people obtain.

questions.q: Early researchers believed the body was almost inactive during sleep.
Each sleep cycle lasts about ninety minutes.
Most vivid dreaming happens outside REM sleep.
REM sleep is thought to help with processing emotions and storing ________.
Most adults function best with around seven to ________ hours of sleep.
Everyone in modern societies gets enough sleep.
Which of the following is NOT recommended by experts?
options: using electronic devices just before sleep

listening.title: Lecture: How Volcanoes Form
listening.script:
Good afternoon, everyone. Today we're going to look at volcanoes and how they form. A volcano is essentially an opening in the Earth's surface through which molten rock, gases and ash can escape. Beneath the surface, this molten rock is called magma, but once it reaches the surface and flows out, we refer to it as lava.

Most volcanoes are found along the edges of the giant plates that make up the Earth's outer layer. There are roughly fifteen hundred active volcanoes in the world today, and a large number of these are located around the Pacific Ocean, in a region often called the Ring of Fire.

Volcanic eruptions can be extremely destructive, burying entire towns and affecting the climate for months. However, they also bring benefits. Volcanic soil is exceptionally fertile, which is why many farming communities choose to live nearby despite the risks.

questions.q: Molten rock beneath the Earth's surface is called ________.
When molten rock flows out onto the surface it is called ________.
There are roughly ________ active volcanoes in the world today.
Many Pacific volcanoes lie in a region called the Ring of ________.
Why do many farming communities live near volcanoes?
The lecturer says volcanic eruptions bring no benefits at all.

speaking.q: We've been talking about technology. Some people say modern technology has made everyday life too complicated. To what extent do you agree, and how do you think people can manage technology in a healthier way? Develop your answer with reasons and examples.

writing.q: In many countries, the number of people choosing to live alone is increasing. What do you think are the causes of this trend, and what effects does it have on society? Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Honeybees and Pollination
reading.passage:
Honeybees are among the most important insects on the planet, not because of the honey they produce, but because of the vital role they play in pollination. Pollination is the process by which pollen is transferred from one flower to another, allowing plants to produce seeds and fruit. As bees move from flower to flower collecting nectar, pollen sticks to their bodies and is carried along with them.

It is estimated that a significant proportion of the crops grown for human consumption depend, at least in part, on pollination by insects. Without bees and other pollinators, many fruits, vegetables and nuts would become far more difficult and expensive to produce. For this reason, the health of bee populations is closely linked to global food security.

In recent decades, however, beekeepers in many countries have reported alarming declines in the number of bees. Scientists have identified several possible causes, including the use of certain pesticides, the loss of wild flowers, the spread of diseases and the effects of a changing climate. It is likely that these factors act in combination rather than alone.

Efforts to protect bees are now under way in many regions. Some governments have restricted the use of harmful chemicals, while individuals are being encouraged to plant flowers that provide food for pollinators. Researchers stress that protecting bees is not simply a matter of preserving a single species, but of safeguarding the complex natural systems on which human life ultimately depends.

questions.q: Honeybees are important mainly because of the honey they make.
Pollination allows plants to produce seeds and ________.
Many crops grown for human food depend partly on insect pollination.
Scientists are certain that one single factor is responsible for the decline in bees.
Which of the following is mentioned as a possible cause of bee decline?
Individuals are encouraged to plant ________ that provide food for pollinators.
Some governments have restricted the use of harmful chemicals.

listening.title: Booking a Guided Tour
listening.script:
AGENT: Good morning, City Tours, how can I help you?
CUSTOMER: Hi, I'd like to book a guided walking tour of the old town for this weekend.
AGENT: Certainly. We run tours on both Saturday and Sunday. Which day would you prefer?
CUSTOMER: Saturday, please.
AGENT: We have two times on Saturday, one at ten in the morning and one at two in the afternoon.
CUSTOMER: The morning one sounds good.
AGENT: Great. The tour lasts about two hours and the meeting point is in front of the Town Hall. How many people will be coming?
CUSTOMER: There'll be four of us, two adults and two children.
AGENT: The price is twelve pounds for adults and six pounds for children, so that comes to thirty-six pounds in total.
CUSTOMER: That's fine. Is there anything we should bring?
AGENT: I'd recommend comfortable shoes and a bottle of water. And please arrive ten minutes early.
CUSTOMER: Will do. Thank you very much.

questions.q: Which day does the customer book the tour for?
The morning tour starts at ________ o'clock.
The tour lasts about ________ hours.
The meeting point is in front of the ________ Hall.
The total price for the whole group is £________.

reading.title: The History of Lighthouses
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, sailors have relied on lights along the coast to guide them safely to harbour and to warn them of dangerous rocks. The earliest lighthouses were little more than open fires built on hilltops, but over time they developed into the tall, purpose-built towers that we recognise today.

One of the most famous structures of the ancient world was the lighthouse of Alexandria, in Egypt, which was completed more than two thousand years ago. Standing at a remarkable height, it was considered an extraordinary feat of engineering and remained in use for many centuries before it was eventually destroyed by earthquakes.

For most of their history, lighthouses depended on fire or oil lamps for their light. The major challenge was making the light visible from a great distance. This problem was largely solved in the early nineteenth century with the invention of a special lens that could focus a beam of light far out to sea. This invention dramatically improved the effectiveness of lighthouses around the world.

Traditionally, each lighthouse was operated by a keeper, who lived on site and was responsible for maintaining the light through the night. The work could be lonely and demanding, particularly in remote locations. In the twentieth century, however, advances in technology made it possible to operate lighthouses automatically. As a result, the role of the lighthouse keeper has now almost entirely disappeared, although the structures themselves continue to play an important part in keeping sailors safe.

questions.q: The earliest lighthouses were tall, purpose-built towers.
The famous ancient lighthouse was located in ________, in Egypt.
The lighthouse of Alexandria was eventually destroyed by earthquakes.
A special ________ invented in the early nineteenth century could focus a beam of light far out to sea.
What was the main challenge for early lighthouses?
options: making the light visible from a great distance

questions.q: Lighthouse keepers remain essential to operating lighthouses today.
In the twentieth century, technology made it possible to operate lighthouses ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Techniques for Improving Memory
listening.script:
Hello everyone. In today's session, I want to share a few practical techniques for improving your memory. The first technique is called chunking. This simply means breaking a long piece of information, such as a phone number, into smaller groups so that it is easier to remember.

The second technique involves using visual images. Our brains are very good at remembering pictures, so if you connect a piece of information to a strong mental image, you are far more likely to recall it later.

Finally, there is the power of repetition, but with a twist. Rather than reviewing material many times in a single session, it is much more effective to spread your review over several days. This method, known as spaced repetition, helps move information into your long-term memory. If you apply these three techniques regularly, you should notice a real improvement within a few weeks.

questions.q: The first memory technique is called ________.
Chunking means breaking information into smaller ________.
Why does the speaker say visual images help memory?
options: because brains are good at remembering pictures
because images are easy to draw

questions.q: Spreading your review over several days is known as ________ repetition.
The speaker recommends reviewing all material in one single session.
With regular practice, improvement should appear within a few ________.

speaking.q: Describe a memorable journey you have taken. You should say: where you went, who you went with, and what you did during the journey; and explain why it was so memorable. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q: Some people think that governments should spend money on public services such as health and education rather than on the arts, such as music and theatre. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Return of the Urban Bee
reading.passage:
For many years, bees were thought of as creatures of the countryside, living among open fields and wild flowers. Recently, however, scientists have noticed that bees are doing surprisingly well in large cities. In some studies, urban bee populations are now healthier than those in nearby farmland. This finding has changed the way many people think about nature in cities.

One reason for this success is the variety of plants found in towns. Parks, gardens and balconies often contain many different flowers, which bloom at different times of the year. As a result, bees can find food for a longer period than they can in farmland, where a single crop may flower for only a few weeks. City flowers also come from many countries, giving bees an unusually wide diet.

Another factor is the use of chemicals. On large farms, powerful sprays are often used to kill insects that damage crops. These chemicals can harm bees as well. In cities, such sprays are used far less often, so bees face fewer dangers from poisoning. Warmer temperatures in built-up areas may also help bees stay active for more months of the year.

Not everyone is convinced that cities are perfect for bees. Traffic, pollution and the loss of green spaces remain serious problems. Some experts warn that planting the wrong flowers can attract bees without truly feeding them. Even so, many researchers now encourage city residents to grow flowering plants, arguing that small actions by ordinary people can make a real difference to these important insects.

questions.q: Scientists once believed that bees mainly lived in rural areas.
In every study, city bees were found to be healthier than farmland bees.
City bees produce more honey than farmland bees.
According to the passage, why can city bees find food for longer?
options: Different city plants bloom at different times
Bees in cities eat less food

questions.q: What does the passage say about chemical sprays?
options: They are used more in cities
They are used less often in cities
They have no effect on bees

questions.q: Warmer temperatures in built-up areas may help bees stay active for more ________ of the year.
Some experts warn that planting the wrong flowers can attract bees without truly ________ them.
Pollution and traffic are no longer considered problems for city bees.

listening.title: Joining the Community Garden
listening.script:
WOMAN: Good morning, you've reached the Riverside Community Garden. How can I help?

MAN: Hi, I'd like to sign up for a small plot to grow some vegetables.

WOMAN: Lovely. Can I take your name?

MAN: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

WOMAN: Thank you, Daniel. Our smallest plots cost fifteen pounds a year, and the larger ones are thirty.

MAN: I'll take a small one to start with.

WOMAN: No problem. The garden is open from eight in the morning until seven in the evening. We ask members not to use chemical sprays, as we try to protect the local insects.

MAN: That suits me. I want to grow things naturally anyway.

WOMAN: Wonderful. We also hold a meeting on the first Saturday of every month, where members share tips and free seeds.

MAN: That sounds useful. Where do I collect my key?

WOMAN: From the wooden shed near the main gate. Just bring some form of identification, such as a passport.

MAN: Perfect. I'll come by this weekend. Thank you very much.

questions.q: The man's surname is ________.
A small plot costs ________ pounds a year.
Which size of plot does the man choose?
The garden closes at ________ in the evening.
Why are members asked not to use chemical sprays?
Members collect their key from the wooden ________ near the main gate.

speaking.q: Let's talk about nature. Do you have any plants or a garden at home? How often do you spend time in parks or green spaces, and why?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of bee colonies (in thousands) recorded in one city's parks over four years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Location | 2018 | 2020 | 2022
City centre parks | 12 | 18 | 25
Suburban gardens | 30 | 34 | 33

Write at least 150 words.

A garden with a wide variety of flowers attracts more insects.
These plants bloom in early spring.
Farmers protect their crops from insects.
Some chemicals used on farms can harm bees.

Warmer weather keeps the bees active for longer.

Small actions by ordinary people can make a difference.
Researchers encourage people to plant flowers.

reading.title: Saving Water in Dry Cities
reading.passage:
Across the world, many cities are running short of fresh water. Growing populations, hotter summers and long periods without rain have placed great pressure on local supplies. In response, governments and ordinary citizens have begun to look for clever ways to use less water and to make every drop count.

One popular method is the collection of rainwater. Simple tanks placed under roof pipes can store water during wet months. This stored water is later used for washing cars, cleaning floors or watering gardens. Although rainwater is not always safe to drink, it is perfectly suitable for many everyday tasks, and it reduces the demand on public supplies.

Cities are also rethinking the plants they grow in public spaces. Traditional lawns need large amounts of water to stay green. By replacing them with native plants that are used to dry conditions, cities can keep their parks attractive while using far less water. Some towns now offer small payments to residents who remove thirsty lawns from their gardens.

Technology plays a part too. Modern sensors can detect leaks in underground pipes, which once wasted huge volumes of water before anyone noticed. Smart meters allow households to see exactly how much they use, often encouraging them to be more careful. Experts agree that no single solution will solve the problem. Instead, a combination of new habits, sensible planting and better technology offers the best hope for cities facing a drier future.

questions.q: Hotter summers are one reason cities are short of water.
Rainwater collected in tanks is always safe to drink.
Rainwater collection is the cheapest of all water-saving methods.
Why do some cities replace traditional lawns with native plants?
What can modern sensors do?
Some towns offer small ________ to residents who remove thirsty lawns.
Experts agree that no single ________ will solve the problem.
The writer believes only one method is needed to save water.

listening.title: A Visit to the Water Centre
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Greenfield Water Centre. My name's Sophie, and I'll be your guide today. Let me give you a quick overview before we start the tour.

The centre opened in 2009, and last year alone we welcomed more than twenty thousand visitors. Our main aim is to teach people how to save water at home and at work.

We'll begin in the exhibition hall, where you can see how rainwater is collected and stored. After that, we move outside to the demonstration garden, which uses only native plants. These plants need very little water, yet they stay green all year round.

For lunch, the cafe sells sandwiches and drinks, and there's a free water fountain near the entrance, so do bring your own bottle. Please remember that photography is allowed everywhere except inside the laboratory.

The gift shop closes at four o'clock, half an hour before the centre itself. If you'd like to book a school visit, please email our office rather than phoning, as our staff are often busy with tours. Now, if everyone's ready, let's make our way to the exhibition hall.

questions.q: The water centre opened in ________.
Last year the centre welcomed more than ________ thousand visitors.
What is special about the demonstration garden?
Where is photography NOT allowed?
The gift shop closes at ________ o'clock.
To book a school visit, you should ________ the office rather than phone.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you tried to save water, energy or another resource. You should say:
- what the resource was
- what you did to save it
- why you decided to do this
and explain how you felt about the experience.

writing.q:
Some people believe that individuals can do little to protect the environment, and that only governments and large companies can make a real difference. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The city's water supply is under pressure.
Growing populations place pressure on resources.
The collection of rainwater is becoming common.
Tanks store water during the wet months.
Rainwater is suitable for cleaning floors.
Storing water reduces demand on public supplies.
Native plants survive in dry conditions.
The parks stay attractive while using less water.
Sensors can detect leaks in the pipes.
A small leak can waste a lot of water.
Smart meters help each household save water.
A combination of methods works best.
Sensible planting saves water in cities.

reading.title: The Hidden Life of Soil
reading.passage:
When we look at a field or a garden, we usually notice the plants growing above the ground. Yet beneath our feet lies a busy world that is just as important. Healthy soil is full of life, containing millions of tiny living things in every handful. These organisms quietly do work that keeps the whole environment running.

Among the most important are the bacteria and fungi that break down dead leaves and animals. As they feed, they release nutrients that plants need in order to grow. Without this constant process, the ground would become poor and lifeless, and few crops could survive. Earthworms also help by mixing the soil and creating spaces that allow air and water to pass through.

Unfortunately, soil is often treated carelessly. Heavy farming, building work and the loss of forests can damage it badly. Once the upper layer is removed or worn away, it may take hundreds of years to form again. Some scientists warn that the world is losing fertile soil far faster than nature can replace it.

There is, however, reason for hope. Farmers in many regions are returning to gentler methods, such as planting different crops in turn and adding natural materials to the ground. Schools and community groups are also teaching young people to value the earth beneath them. By understanding the hidden life of soil, people may learn to protect a resource on which all of us depend.

questions.q: A single handful of healthy soil contains millions of living things.
Bacteria and fungi release nutrients as they break down dead matter.
Earthworms are the most numerous organisms in the soil.
What can happen if the upper layer of soil is worn away?
options: It may take hundreds of years to form again

questions.q: Which method are some farmers now returning to?
Earthworms create spaces that allow air and ________ to pass through the soil.
Some scientists warn the world is losing ________ soil faster than nature can replace it.
The writer feels there is no hope for protecting soil.

listening.title: Lecture: Why Soil Matters
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to talk about something we usually ignore, and that's the soil under our feet. Most people think of it as just dirt, but in reality it's one of the most living parts of the planet.

Let's start with a simple fact. A single teaspoon of healthy soil can contain billions of bacteria. These tiny organisms break down dead plants and release the nutrients that new plants depend on.

Now, why does this matter to us? Well, around ninety-five percent of the food we eat comes, directly or indirectly, from soil. So when soil is damaged, our food supply is threatened.

The main problem is speed. It takes nature roughly five hundred years to build just one centimetre of fertile topsoil. Yet through poor farming, we can lose that same layer in only a few decades.

The good news is that recovery is possible. By covering fields with plants over winter, farmers protect the surface from wind and rain. Adding compost also feeds the soil naturally. In your reading this week, please focus on chapter three, which covers these methods in detail. We'll discuss them next time.

questions.q: A single teaspoon of healthy soil can contain billions of ________.
About ________ percent of the food we eat comes from soil.
How long does nature take to build one centimetre of topsoil?
Farmers protect the soil surface by covering fields with plants over ________.
What does the lecturer say also feeds the soil naturally?
Students should focus on chapter ________ in their reading.

speaking.q: Let's discuss farming and food production. Why do you think many people today know very little about where their food comes from? Do you believe schools should teach children more about agriculture and the natural world?

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of farmland affected by soil damage in three regions in 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Region | 2010 | 2020
Northern plains | 18% | 27%
Eastern hills | 25% | 24%
Southern coast | 12% | 21%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The soil is full of tiny organisms.
Plants need nutrients from the soil to grow.
Fungi break down dead leaves in the forest.
This constant process keeps the soil alive.
Soil is often treated carelessly.
Fertile soil produces healthy crops.
Nature cannot replace soil quickly.
Farmers use gentler methods now.
Young people are learning to value the earth.
All of us depend on healthy soil.
Bacteria release nutrients into the soil.
Soil damage threatens our food supply.
Recovery of damaged soil is possible.

reading.title: Bringing Back the Wetlands
reading.passage:
Wetlands are areas of land that are covered by shallow water for all or part of the year. They include marshes, swamps and the muddy edges of rivers and lakes. For a long time, people saw wetlands as useless or even dangerous, and many were drained to create farmland or space for building. Today, however, attitudes are changing quickly.

Scientists now understand that wetlands provide great benefits. They act like giant sponges, soaking up heavy rain and slowly releasing it, which helps to prevent floods in nearby towns. They also clean water naturally, as plants and mud trap harmful substances before the water flows onwards. In addition, wetlands are home to a remarkable number of birds, fish and insects.

Because of these benefits, several countries have begun ambitious projects to bring back lost wetlands. Old drainage channels are blocked, allowing water to return to dry fields. Native plants are then introduced, and within a few years wildlife often appears again in large numbers. Such projects can be expensive, but supporters argue that the long-term savings on flood damage make them worthwhile.

There are challenges, of course. Some farmers worry about losing valuable land, and not every restored wetland succeeds. The right balance between human needs and nature is never easy to find. Even so, the growing interest in wetlands shows that people are beginning to value landscapes they once ignored, recognising that protecting them may benefit everyone in the years ahead.

questions.q: In the past, many wetlands were drained to make farmland or building space.
Wetlands can help prevent floods in nearby towns.
Restoring wetlands is cheaper than building flood walls.
How do wetlands clean water?
options: Plants and mud trap harmful substances

questions.q: What is one way countries bring back lost wetlands?
Wetlands act like giant ________, soaking up heavy rain.

listening.title: Planning a Wetland Walk
listening.script:
MAN: Hi Ruth, I'm trying to organise the school trip to the Marshlands Nature Reserve. Can I check a few details with you?

WOMAN: Of course. I went there last month, so I can help.

MAN: Great. First, how long does the main walk take?

WOMAN: The full loop is about two hours, but there's a shorter path that takes forty minutes if the children get tired.

MAN: Good to know. And is there somewhere to eat?

WOMAN: Yes, there's a picnic area beside the visitor centre. There's no cafe, though, so everyone should bring their own lunch.

MAN: Right. What about the best things to see?

WOMAN: Definitely the bird hide near the lake. In spring you can watch dozens of nesting birds without disturbing them.

MAN: The children will love that. Do we need to book?

WOMAN: For groups of more than ten, yes. Just call the reserve office a week in advance. Oh, and wear boots, because the paths can get very muddy after rain.

MAN: Thanks, Ruth. That's really helpful.

The shorter path takes ________ minutes.
Visitors should bring their own ________, as there is no cafe.
What does Ruth recommend seeing?
options: The bird hide near the lake

writing.q:
In many countries, natural areas such as wetlands and forests are being replaced by farms and buildings. Some people think this is a necessary price for development, while others believe such areas should always be protected. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Problem with Light at Night
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the night sky was filled with thousands of stars. Today, in many cities, people are lucky to see more than a handful. The cause is light pollution, the bright glow produced by streetlamps, advertising signs and buildings that stay lit long after dark. While artificial light has brought clear advantages, scientists are increasingly concerned about its hidden costs.

Wildlife is among the first to suffer. Many animals rely on natural darkness to hunt, travel or rest. Newly hatched sea turtles, for example, normally move towards the moonlit sea, but bright lights on the shore can lead them in the wrong direction. Birds that migrate at night may become confused by city lights and collide with tall buildings.

Human health may also be affected. Our bodies use the natural change between day and night to control sleep. Too much light in the evening can disturb this rhythm, leaving people tired and restless. Some researchers believe that long-term exposure to bright nights could be linked to other health problems, though more study is needed.

The encouraging news is that light pollution is one of the easier problems to fix. Unlike many forms of pollution, it disappears the moment the light is switched off. Simple steps, such as pointing lamps downwards, using softer bulbs and turning off signs at night, can make a real difference. A few towns have even created special zones where the night sky is protected, allowing residents once more to enjoy the stars above them.

questions.q: People in many cities today can see fewer stars than in the past.
Bright shore lights can lead baby sea turtles in the wrong direction.
Light pollution is the most serious form of pollution facing cities.
Why is light pollution described as easier to fix than other pollution?
options: It disappears when the light is switched off

questions.q: What may happen to migrating birds because of city lights?
options: They become confused and hit buildings

questions.q: Too much light in the evening can disturb the body's natural ________.
A few towns have created special ________ where the night sky is protected.
Scientists are certain that bright nights cause serious long-term illness.

listening.title: Radio Talk: A Darker Town
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back to Science Hour. Tonight we're looking at light pollution, and how one small town decided to do something about it. With me is researcher Tom Hale.

TOM: Thanks for having me. The town I studied is called Brookvale, and three years ago its residents began to worry that they could no longer see the stars.

PRESENTER: So what did they do?

TOM: First, the council replaced the old streetlamps with softer ones that point downwards. This alone reduced the glow over the town by about sixty percent.

PRESENTER: That's a big change. Anything else?

TOM: Yes. Shops agreed to switch off their bright signs by eleven at night. And the local school started a project where children measured the brightness of the sky each week.

PRESENTER: And did it work?

TOM: Remarkably well. Within a year, residents reported seeing far more stars. Even the local wildlife seemed to benefit, with more bats and owls appearing. The whole project cost very little, which is why other towns are now interested.

PRESENTER: A bright idea, you might say. Thanks, Tom.

questions.q: The town that was studied is called ________.
Why did residents begin to worry three years ago?
options: They could no longer see the stars

questions.q: New streetlamps reduced the glow over the town by about ________ percent.
Shops agreed to switch off their bright signs by ________ at night.
What did children at the local school do?
options: Measured the brightness of the sky

questions.q: After the changes, more bats and ________ appeared in the town.

speaking.q:
Describe a night-time scene or place that you find beautiful or memorable. You should say:
- where it is
- when you saw or experienced it
- what you could see or hear there
and explain why it was special to you.

Location | 2000 | 2020
City centre | 50 | 20

Countryside | 800 | 700

reading.title: The Rise of Online Maps
reading.passage:
Not long ago, most travellers relied on paper maps folded into glove compartments or printed directions copied from a screen before leaving home. Today, digital maps on smartphones have largely replaced these older tools. With a single tap, a user can see their exact position, search for a nearby shop, and receive spoken instructions while driving. This convenience has changed the way ordinary people move through cities and unfamiliar places.

Digital maps work by combining several technologies. Satellites send signals that allow a phone to calculate its location, while large databases store information about roads, buildings, and businesses. When a user asks for directions, software compares many possible routes and selects the one that is fastest at that moment. Because traffic data is updated constantly, the suggested route may change even during a single journey.

These systems offer clear benefits, but they also raise concerns. Some experts worry that people are losing the ability to find their way without help, a skill that earlier generations developed naturally. Others point out that map companies collect detailed records of where users travel. Although such data can improve services, it may also be sold or shared in ways that users do not fully understand.

Despite these worries, digital maps continue to grow in popularity. Many now include reviews, photographs, and opening hours, turning a simple map into a guide for daily life. For better or worse, the printed road atlas has become a rare object, kept more for sentiment than for practical use.

questions.q: In the past, some travellers wrote down directions before starting a journey.
Digital maps calculate location using signals from satellites.
The route suggested by a map app never changes once a journey has begun.
Map companies are legally required to delete user travel data after one year.
According to the passage, why might a suggested route change during a trip?
options: Because the satellite signal is lost
Because traffic data is updated constantly
Because the user requests a new map
Because the phone battery is low

questions.q: What concern do some experts express about digital maps?
options: They are too expensive for most people
People may lose the skill of finding their own way
They work only in large cities

questions.q: Modern map apps often include reviews, photographs and ________ hours.
The printed road atlas is now kept mainly for ________ rather than practical use.

listening.title: Joining the City Library
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, welcome to the Riverside City Library. How can I help you?

VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to sign up for a library card, please.

STAFF: Of course. Can I take your full name?

VISITOR: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

STAFF: Lovely. And your home address?

VISITOR: It's 14 Maple Street, near the train station.

STAFF: Thank you. The standard card lets you borrow up to eight books at a time, for three weeks each. There's also a digital membership for e-books.

VISITOR: How much does the digital one cost?

STAFF: It's twelve pounds a year, but the basic card is completely free.

VISITOR: I'll take the basic card for now. Do I need anything to prove my address?

STAFF: Just a recent letter or bill with your name on it. You can bring it next time. Your card will be ready on Thursday.

reading.title: Working From Home
reading.passage:
A decade ago, the idea of doing one's job from the kitchen table was unusual for most workers. The spread of fast internet and video-calling software has made remote work possible for millions of people. Instead of travelling to an office each morning, many employees now open their laptops at home and begin the day with a digital meeting.

The advantages are easy to see. Workers save the time and money once spent on commuting, and they gain greater control over their daily schedules. Some say they feel more productive without the noise and interruptions of a busy office. Companies, too, can reduce their costs by renting smaller buildings and hiring staff who live far away.

However, remote work is not perfect. Without face-to-face contact, some employees feel isolated and find it harder to build friendships with colleagues. New workers may struggle to learn from experienced staff when everyone is in a different place. There is also the risk that the line between work and home life becomes blurred, so that people answer messages late into the evening.

Many organisations have responded by adopting a mixed approach. Under this model, employees come to the office for part of the week and work remotely for the rest. Supporters argue that this balance keeps the benefits of both styles while reducing their drawbacks. Whether such arrangements will last is uncertain, but it seems clear that the traditional five-day office week is no longer the only option.

questions.q: Fast internet and video software helped make remote work possible.
Companies can lower costs by renting smaller buildings.
All employees feel more productive when working from home.
The government has introduced a law requiring companies to offer remote work.
What problem does the passage link to a lack of face-to-face contact?
What is the 'mixed approach' described in the passage?
options: Splitting time between office and home

questions.q: Remote workers save the time and money once spent on ________.
The line between work and home life can become ________.

listening.title: Booking a Co-working Desk
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Hello, City Hub co-working space, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a desk for next week.

RECEPTIONIST: Sure. Which days were you thinking of?

CALLER: Just Monday and Tuesday, if possible.

RECEPTIONIST: No problem. A single desk costs twenty dollars per day, or you can buy a five-day pass for eighty dollars.

CALLER: I'll just take the two days for now.

RECEPTIONIST: Great. Can I get your name?

CALLER: It's Megan Clarke, C-L-A-R-K-E.

RECEPTIONIST: Thanks, Megan. The space opens at seven in the morning and closes at nine in the evening. There's free coffee, and the meeting rooms can be booked separately.

CALLER: Perfect. Is there parking?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, but it fills quickly, so I'd recommend arriving before eight. I'll email you a confirmation today.

reading.title: Smart Speakers at Home
reading.passage:
In recent years, small voice-controlled devices known as smart speakers have appeared in millions of homes. By speaking a short command, a user can play music, ask about the weather, set a timer, or switch on the lights. The appeal of these devices lies in their simplicity: there is no screen to touch and no keyboard to type on, only the sound of a human voice.

Behind this simple experience is a complicated process. When a person speaks, the device records the words and sends them to powerful computers over the internet. These computers turn the sound into text, work out what the user wants, and send back a response. All of this happens in roughly a second, which makes the conversation feel natural.

Smart speakers are especially helpful for certain groups. Older people who find small buttons difficult may prefer giving spoken instructions, and those with poor eyesight can use the device without reading anything. Families also enjoy the entertainment and reminders the speakers provide throughout the day.

Yet the technology has its critics. Because the device is always listening for its command word, some users worry that private conversations could be recorded by mistake. Companies insist that recording begins only after the command word is heard, but reports of errors have made many people cautious. As a result, some buyers now look closely at the privacy settings before placing a speaker in their living room.

questions.q: Smart speakers can be controlled without touching a screen.
The device processes all commands entirely on its own without the internet.
People with poor eyesight may find smart speakers useful.
Smart speakers are now sold in more countries than smartphones.
How long does the whole voice process usually take?
Why do some users feel cautious about smart speakers?
options: Private conversations might be recorded by mistake

questions.q: The device records the words and sends them to powerful ________ over the internet.
Companies say recording begins only after the ________ word is heard.

listening.title: A Talk on Household Gadgets
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good evening, everyone, and thanks for coming to tonight's talk on smart home gadgets. I'll keep it short. Let's start with the most popular item: the smart speaker. In our recent survey, around sixty percent of households said they owned at least one. The second most common device was the smart light bulb, found in about forty percent of homes.

Now, why do people buy these things? The top reason given was convenience, mentioned by nearly three-quarters of respondents. The second reason was saving energy, especially with lights and heating. Interestingly, only a small group said they bought gadgets for security.

There are downsides too. The most common complaint was that devices sometimes failed to understand commands. The second was concern about privacy. My advice is simple: start with one device, learn how it works, and check the settings carefully. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions now.

speaking.q: We've been talking about smart home devices. Now let's consider some wider questions. Do you think technology in the home makes people lazier, or does it free them to do more useful things? How might homes change in the next twenty years?

Device | 2015 | 2018 | 2021

reading.title: The Spread of Online Learning
reading.passage:
Education has long taken place inside classrooms, where a teacher stands before a group of students. In the past twenty years, however, the internet has opened a second path. Online courses now allow learners to study almost any subject from home, often at a time that suits them. A student in a small town can take a class taught by a teacher on the other side of the world.

The reasons for this growth are not hard to find. Online courses are usually cheaper than traditional ones, since there is no need for a physical building or printed materials. They are also flexible, which appeals to people who have jobs or families. Many platforms record their lessons, so a learner who misses a session can simply watch it later.

Nevertheless, learning online demands a great deal of self-discipline. Without a fixed timetable or a teacher in the room, some students fall behind and never complete their courses. Studies suggest that the number of learners who finish online courses is far lower than the number who begin them. Younger students, in particular, may find it hard to stay motivated.

To address these problems, course designers have begun adding live discussions, quizzes, and online groups where learners can support one another. Such features aim to recreate the sense of belonging that a real classroom provides. While online study will probably never replace schools entirely, it has clearly earned a permanent place in modern education.

questions.q: Online courses allow students to study subjects from home.
Online courses are usually more expensive than traditional ones.
Many online platforms record lessons for later viewing.
Most universities now require students to take at least one online course.
What does the passage say online learning demands from students?
Why have course designers added live discussions and online groups?
options: To recreate a sense of belonging
To reduce the number of teachers

questions.q: Online courses are described as flexible, which appeals to people who have ________ or families.
Without a fixed timetable, some students fall ________ and never finish.

listening.title: Choosing an Online Course
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Hello, I'm one of the course advisors here. You mentioned you're interested in our online programmes?

STUDENT: Yes, I want to improve my computer skills, but I work during the day.

ADVISOR: That's fine. Most of our courses are recorded, so you can watch the lessons in the evening. Our beginner course runs for six weeks and costs fifty pounds.

STUDENT: And is there a certificate at the end?

ADVISOR: Yes, if you complete all the quizzes and the final project. The pass mark is seventy percent.

STUDENT: That sounds good. How much time should I set aside each week?

ADVISOR: We recommend about four hours, including the live discussion on Wednesday evenings. It's optional, but students who join it tend to do better.

STUDENT: Great. How do I sign up?

ADVISOR: Just fill in the form online, and the course starts on the fifth of March.

reading.title: Social Media and Friendship
reading.passage:
For most of human history, friendships were limited by distance. People grew close to those they saw in person, and keeping in touch with someone far away meant writing letters that took weeks to arrive. Social media has transformed this picture. Today a person can share news, photographs, and short messages with hundreds of contacts in an instant, no matter where those contacts live.

Supporters of social media argue that it strengthens human connections. Old school friends who once lost touch can find each other again, and family members spread across different countries can follow one another's daily lives. For people who are shy or live in isolated places, online platforms can offer a welcome way to meet others with similar interests.

Critics, however, warn that these connections are often shallow. Scrolling through endless updates may give a feeling of contact without the depth of a real conversation. Some studies suggest that heavy use of social media is linked to loneliness, as users compare their ordinary lives with the polished images that others choose to share. The pressure to appear happy can leave people feeling worse, not better.

The truth probably lies somewhere in between. Social media is a tool, and its effect depends largely on how it is used. Those who use it to arrange real meetings and stay close to genuine friends may benefit greatly. Those who simply watch the lives of strangers may find that the screen offers connection in name only.

questions.q: In the past, staying in touch with distant people could take weeks.
Social media can help shy people meet others with similar interests.
All studies agree that social media reduces loneliness.
The average user spends more than three hours a day on social media.
According to critics, why might social media connections feel shallow?
options: They give contact without deep conversation

questions.q: What does the passage suggest about the overall effect of social media?
options: It depends on how it is used
It has no effect at all

questions.q: Some studies link heavy use of social media to ________.
Users often compare their lives with the ________ images that others share.

listening.title: A Survey on Phone Use
listening.script:
INTERVIEWER: Thanks for taking part in our short survey on phone habits. First, how many hours a day would you say you spend on your phone?

STUDENT: Honestly, probably about five hours. It's a lot.

INTERVIEWER: And what do you use it for most?

STUDENT: Mostly messaging friends, then watching videos. Social media is third, I'd say.

INTERVIEWER: Do you ever try to cut down?

STUDENT: Yes. Last month I turned off most notifications, and that helped. I also charge my phone in the kitchen, not the bedroom, so I'm not tempted to check it at night.

INTERVIEWER: Has that changed how you sleep?

STUDENT: Definitely. I fall asleep faster now, maybe twenty minutes earlier.

INTERVIEWER: Great. Last question: would you recommend a phone-free hour to others?

STUDENT: Absolutely. Even one hour a day makes a real difference.

What does the student use the phone for most?
To cut down, the student turned off most ________.
The student now charges the phone in the ________ instead of the bedroom.
The student falls asleep about ________ minutes earlier now.
What does the student recommend to others?

speaking.q:
Describe a time when technology helped you stay in touch with someone. You should say:
- who you were in touch with
- what technology you used
- how often you communicated
and explain how this technology made you feel.

Activity | 2019 | 2023
Messaging | 6 | 9
Watching videos | 4 | 11
Social media | 5 | 8
Reading news | 3 | 2

reading.title: Why We Forget
reading.passage:
Forgetting is often seen as a sign that something has gone wrong with the mind, but psychologists now believe it is a normal and even useful part of how memory works. The human brain takes in far more information each day than it could ever store, and forgetting allows it to clear away details that are no longer needed.

One early theory held that memories simply fade over time if they are not used. This idea, known as decay, can explain why a phone number repeated only once is quickly lost. However, decay alone does not account for everything. Many people can recall events from childhood in great detail, while forgetting things that happened only last week.

A second explanation is interference. According to this view, new information can get in the way of older memories, and older memories can make it harder to learn new ones. A student who studies two similar subjects on the same evening may find that the two sets of facts become mixed up. The more alike the material, the stronger this effect tends to be.

Forgetting is not always a failure of storage. Sometimes the memory is still present but cannot be reached, rather like a book that is in the library but has been shelved in the wrong place. A reminder, a smell, or a familiar place can suddenly bring back a memory that seemed to have disappeared. For this reason, researchers distinguish between losing a memory altogether and simply being unable to find it.

questions.q: Psychologists today regard forgetting as entirely harmful.
The theory that memories fade if they are not used is called ________.
Decay alone can explain every case of forgetting.
According to the interference theory, when is forgetting strongest?
options: when the material studied is very different
when the material studied is very similar
when the material is studied in the morning
when the material is read aloud

questions.q: Most people forget childhood events more easily than recent ones.
A memory that cannot be reached is compared to a ________ shelved in the wrong place.
Smells can sometimes help bring back a memory that seemed lost.
The brain can store almost all of the information it receives each day.

listening.title: Joining the University Memory Study
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, you've come about the memory study, haven't you? Let me take a few details. Can I start with your full name?

STUDENT: Yes, it's Daniel Hughes. That's H-U-G-H-E-S.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. And which course are you on?

STUDENT: I'm a second-year psychology student.

RECEPTIONIST: Perfect. The study runs for three weeks, and we'd need you to come in twice a week. Each session lasts about forty minutes. Does that suit you?

STUDENT: That's fine. What does the session actually involve?

RECEPTIONIST: You'll be shown lists of words on a screen and asked to recall them later. There are no tests of intelligence, so please don't worry. We also ask you to fill in a short sleep diary each night.

STUDENT: A sleep diary? Why's that?

RECEPTIONIST: We're looking at how rest affects memory. Oh, and there's a small reward — you'll receive a voucher worth twenty pounds at the end. Sessions are held in Room 4 of the Bennett Building.

questions.q: The student's surname is spelled ________.
What is the student studying?
The study lasts for ________ weeks.
Each session lasts about ________ minutes.
Participants must complete a short ________ diary each night.

options: a cash payment of fifty pounds

speaking.q: Let's talk about memory. Are you good at remembering things, such as names or dates? What do you usually do to help yourself remember important information? Do you prefer to write things down or keep them in your head?

Technique | 2010 | 2020

reading.title: The Power of First Impressions
reading.passage:
When we meet someone for the first time, we form an opinion of them remarkably quickly. Studies suggest that people make judgements about a stranger's character within seconds, long before any meaningful conversation has taken place. These rapid judgements are based on small clues such as facial expression, posture, and tone of voice.

This ability probably had a clear purpose in our distant past. Our ancestors often had to decide in an instant whether a stranger was a friend or a threat, and a quick judgement, even if not always correct, could mean the difference between safety and danger. The habit of judging quickly has stayed with us, even though modern life rarely demands such speed.

The trouble with first impressions is that they are sticky. Once we have decided that someone is, say, unfriendly, we tend to notice behaviour that fits this view and ignore evidence that contradicts it. Psychologists call this the confirmation effect. A person judged harshly at a first meeting may struggle to change that impression, however differently they later behave.

This does not mean first impressions are worthless. They can be surprisingly accurate, especially about qualities such as confidence or warmth. The wise approach, researchers suggest, is to treat a first impression as a starting point rather than a final verdict, remaining open to the possibility that it is mistaken.

questions.q: People usually need a long conversation before judging a stranger's character.
Rapid judgements are based on small clues such as facial expression, posture, and ________ of voice.
The ability to judge quickly was probably useful to our ancestors.
What does the writer mean by saying first impressions are 'sticky'?
options: they are hard to change once formed

questions.q: The tendency to notice only behaviour that fits our view is called the ________ effect.
First impressions are always inaccurate, according to the writer.
People tend to remember their very first impression for the rest of their lives.
What does the writer recommend doing with a first impression?
options: treating it as a final verdict
treating it as a starting point

listening.title: Booking a Room at the Community Centre
listening.script:

CALLER: The fourteenth of July, in the afternoon if possible.

STAFF: Let me check. Yes, two o'clock until four is free. The hire fee is twenty-five dollars an hour.

CALLER: That's fine. Does the room have a projector?

STAFF: It does, and there's free wifi as well. I should mention there's no parking on site, but there's a public car park just across the road.

CALLER: Good to know. How do I confirm the booking?

STAFF: I'll email you a form. Just complete it and pay a deposit of ten dollars to secure the room.

reading.title: The Need to Belong
reading.passage:
Human beings are deeply social creatures. From birth, we seek the company of others, and throughout life our sense of who we are is shaped by the groups to which we belong. Psychologists argue that this need to belong is not a mere preference but a basic drive, as fundamental in its way as hunger or thirst.

The evidence for this is striking. People who feel connected to others tend to be healthier and to live longer than those who are isolated. Loneliness, by contrast, has been linked to a range of problems, from poor sleep to a weakened immune system. The body, it seems, treats social rejection almost as a kind of physical pain.

Because belonging matters so much, people will go to great lengths to gain and keep it. We adjust our opinions to fit in, copy the behaviour of those around us, and feel real distress when we are excluded. In one well-known type of experiment, volunteers who were simply left out of a computer game reported feeling hurt, even though the other players were strangers and the game meant nothing.

This powerful pull towards the group has a darker side. The same need that binds communities together can also lead people to follow the crowd against their better judgement, or to treat outsiders with suspicion. Understanding the need to belong, then, helps explain both the warmth of human cooperation and some of its more troubling failures.

questions.q: Psychologists describe the need to belong as merely a preference.
People who feel connected to others tend to live longer.
Loneliness has been linked to poor sleep and a weakened ________ system.
How does the body appear to treat social rejection?
options: as almost a kind of physical pain

questions.q: Volunteers left out of a computer game felt hurt despite the game's meaninglessness.
The same need that binds communities can lead people to follow the ________.
Children feel the need to belong more strongly than adults.
What is the 'darker side' of the need to belong?
options: it can cause people to distrust outsiders

listening.title: Library Induction Tour
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Welcome, everyone, to your library induction. I'll keep this brief and then you can explore on your own. The library is open from eight in the morning until ten at night on weekdays, and shorter hours at the weekend.

Let's start with the ground floor. This is where you'll find the help desk and the short-loan collection — those are books in high demand, which you can borrow for just three days. Please don't try to take them for longer, as the fines are steep.

Up on the first floor we have the quiet study area. As the name suggests, no talking is allowed there, so it's perfect if you need to concentrate before an exam. If you'd rather work in a group, head to the second floor, where there are bookable discussion rooms.

Finally, a word about borrowing. With your student card you may take out up to ten items at a time. Books are loaned for two weeks and can be renewed online, unless another reader has requested them.

reading.title: The Science of Motivation
reading.passage:
What makes a person keep working at a difficult task when an easier path is available? The study of motivation tries to answer this question, and one of its most useful ideas is the difference between two kinds of reward.

The first kind comes from outside and is known as extrinsic motivation. A worker who labours only for a salary, or a child who reads only to earn a sticker, is driven by external rewards. The second kind comes from within and is called intrinsic motivation. Here the activity itself is the reward: the musician practises because playing brings joy, the scientist experiments out of curiosity.

For many years it was assumed that more rewards always produce more effort. Research has complicated this picture. In a series of experiments, people who were paid to do something they already enjoyed often lost interest once the payment stopped. The outside reward, it seemed, had quietly replaced the inner one. This surprising result became known as the overjustification effect.

None of this means that external rewards are useless. They can be very effective for dull but necessary tasks that few people would do for pleasure. The lesson, rather, is that motivation is more delicate than it appears. A reward offered carelessly may achieve the opposite of what was intended, weakening the very enthusiasm it was meant to encourage.

questions.q: Motivation that comes from outside is known as ________ motivation.
A musician who plays because it brings joy shows intrinsic motivation.
It was once assumed that more rewards always produce more effort.
What happened to people who were paid for an activity they already enjoyed?
options: they often lost interest once payment stopped

questions.q: This surprising result is called the ________ effect.
The writer claims external rewards are completely useless.
Intrinsic motivation is more common in children than in adults.
What is the main lesson the writer draws about motivation?
options: rewards should always be as large as possible
motivation is more delicate than it appears
money is the only true motivator

listening.title: Talk: Setting Up a Workplace Reward Scheme
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Thanks for coming. Today I'll outline the new staff reward scheme that begins next month. The aim is simple: to recognize good work without spending a fortune.

The scheme has three levels. At the first level, any manager can give a public thank-you in our weekly meeting. It costs nothing, but our surveys show staff value it highly. At the second level, employees who go beyond what's expected can earn an extra day of paid leave. And at the top level, one person each quarter will be named Colleague of the Quarter and receive a one-hundred-dollar gift card.

Now, a word of caution. Research warns that rewards can backfire if people feel they're being controlled rather than appreciated. So we'll keep the focus on genuine recognition, not pressure. Nominations open on the first of the month, and you can submit them through the staff portal. If you have questions, email the HR team, and please don't forget to read the full guidelines before nominating anyone.

options: to recognize good work without spending a fortune

questions.q: The scheme has ________ levels.

questions.q: The top-level winner receives a gift card worth ________ dollars.

reading.title: Why We Take Risks
reading.passage:
Some people climb mountains, gamble their savings, or quit secure jobs to start businesses, while others avoid even the smallest uncertainty. Psychologists have long been curious about why individuals differ so widely in their willingness to take risks.

Part of the answer lies in how we weigh gains against losses. Studies have shown that, for most people, the pain of losing something is felt more strongly than the pleasure of gaining the same thing. This tendency, called loss aversion, helps explain why many of us are cautious by default: the fear of loss looms larger than the hope of reward.

Yet people are not equally cautious in every situation. The way a choice is described, or framed, can change behaviour dramatically. When an option is presented in terms of what might be saved, people tend to play safe; when the very same option is described in terms of what might be lost, they are more willing to gamble. The facts have not changed, only the words.

Age and experience also play a part. Younger people, on the whole, take more risks, partly because the parts of the brain that judge consequences are still developing. As we grow older, we generally become more cautious, though there are always exceptions. Risk-taking, in short, is shaped not by a single cause but by a mixture of biology, situation, and the language in which choices are dressed.

questions.q: All people show roughly the same willingness to take risks.
The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equal gains is called loss ________.
Loss aversion helps explain why many people are cautious by default.
According to the passage, what can change risk behaviour even when the facts stay the same?
options: the way the choice is framed

questions.q: When an option is described in terms of possible losses, people are more willing to gamble.
Younger people take more risks partly because the brain regions that judge ________ are still developing.
Women take fewer risks than men, according to the passage.
What is the writer's overall conclusion about risk-taking?
options: it is shaped by a mixture of factors

listening.title: Lecture: How Crowds Influence Our Choices
listening.script:
LECTURER: In today's session we'll look at how the presence of other people shapes the choices we make. It's a field that sits at the heart of social psychology.

Let's begin with a classic finding. When people are unsure what to do, they tend to copy those around them. In one experiment, participants stood on a busy street and stared up at the sky. With just one person looking up, few passers-by joined in. But once five people were looking, a large crowd quickly gathered. The lesson is that uncertainty makes us look to others for guidance.

This copying can be helpful — it spreads useful behaviour, such as queuing politely or recycling. But it can also lead us astray. If everyone around us ignores a problem, we may assume it isn't serious, a pattern researchers call the bystander effect.

So what can we do? Awareness helps. Simply knowing that we are influenced by the crowd makes us a little more likely to pause and think for ourselves. Next week we'll examine how advertisers make use of these very tendencies.

questions.q: What field does the lecture belong to?
In the street experiment, a large crowd gathered once ________ people were looking up.
According to the lecturer, what makes us look to others for guidance?
Assuming a problem isn't serious because others ignore it is called the ________ effect.
The lecturer says copying others is always harmful.
Next week's session will focus on how ________ use these tendencies.

Describe a time when you took a risk to do something. You should say:

- what happened as a result
and explain how you felt about taking that risk afterwards.

Age group | Willing to take risks

reading.title: The Library of Alexandria
reading.passage:
More than two thousand years ago, the Egyptian city of Alexandria was home to one of the most famous libraries in the ancient world. It was founded in the third century BC, during the reign of the early Ptolemaic kings, who wished to gather the knowledge of every nation in a single place. The rulers believed that a great collection of books would bring fame to their city and attract clever scholars from many lands.

The library was part of a larger institution called the Museum, a centre for study where writers, doctors and mathematicians lived and worked. These scholars were paid by the state and given free meals, so that they could spend their days reading and arguing about ideas. Some historians claim that the library once held hundreds of thousands of scrolls, although the exact number is impossible to confirm today.

To build this enormous collection, officials used unusual methods. Ships arriving at the harbour were searched, and any books found on board were copied. Sometimes the copy was returned to the owner while the original stayed in Alexandria. Agents were also sent abroad to buy texts in Athens and other cities.

The library did not survive. Over several centuries it suffered fires, wars and a slow loss of support from later rulers. By the time the city changed hands repeatedly, much of the collection had already vanished. Although later writers blamed a single dramatic fire, modern researchers believe the decline was gradual rather than sudden. Today nothing of the original building remains, yet the library continues to symbolise the human desire to preserve and share knowledge.

questions.q: The library was founded by rulers who wanted to collect knowledge from many nations.
Scholars at the Museum had to pay for their own food.
The exact number of scrolls in the library is known with certainty.
The Ptolemaic kings personally selected which scholars could enter the Museum.
According to the passage, how did officials sometimes treat books found on ships?
options: They kept the original and returned a copy

questions.q: What do modern researchers believe about the library's decline?
options: It was caused by one sudden fire
It was planned by the rulers

questions.q: The library formed part of a larger institution called the ________.
Agents travelled to ________ and other cities to buy texts.

listening.script:

VISITOR: Brilliant. Do we need to pay now?
STAFF: No, you can pay on the day at the front desk.
