reading.title: The Silk Road
reading.passage:
The Silk Road was not a single road but a network of trade routes that connected China with the Mediterranean. For many centuries, merchants travelled along these paths carrying goods such as silk, spices, glass and precious stones. The journey was long and dangerous, crossing deserts and high mountains, so few traders made the entire trip themselves. Instead, goods were passed from one merchant to another, like a chain.

Silk was the most famous product to travel west, and it gave the route its modern name. For a long time, only China knew how to make silk, and the process was kept secret. People in distant lands were willing to pay very high prices for the soft cloth, which they regarded as a sign of wealth.

Yet the Silk Road carried far more than goods. Ideas, religions and inventions also moved along the routes. Paper-making, for example, slowly spread westward from China, changing the way people recorded information. Travellers exchanged stories and beliefs at the busy markets and quiet resting places that grew up along the way, and in this manner distant cultures slowly came to learn about one another.

The routes also helped spread diseases. When traders moved between cities, illness could travel with them, sometimes with terrible results. Historians believe that some major outbreaks followed the same paths as the caravans.

By the late medieval period, sea routes began to replace the overland journey, because ships could carry more goods at lower cost. The old roads slowly fell out of use, but their influence remained. The exchange of ideas they made possible shaped many cultures for centuries afterwards.

questions.q: The Silk Road consisted of one single road.
Most traders travelled the whole length of the route themselves.
China kept the method of making silk a secret for a long time.
The Chinese emperor personally controlled the price of silk in foreign lands.
What does the passage say spread westward from China?
Why did sea routes begin to replace the overland journey?
options: Ships could carry more goods at lower cost
The roads were closed by law

questions.q: Along with goods, the Silk Road also carried ideas, religions and ________.
The routes sometimes spread ________ between cities.

listening.title: A Talk About the Campus History Trail
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the campus history trail. My name's Daniel and I'll be showing you around today. The trail takes about forty-five minutes and covers six main stops. We'll begin here at the old clock tower, which was built in nineteen twelve. It's the oldest building on campus and was once the entire university. Our second stop is the original library, just behind me to the left. It now holds rare maps from the trade routes of Asia. Please don't touch the glass cases, as the maps are very fragile. After that, we'll visit the science block, which is much newer. The walk between these two buildings takes about five minutes. Toilets are available near the main café, which is our fourth stop, so we'll take a short break there. If anyone gets lost, my phone number is on the leaflet I handed out. Finally, please remember that photography is allowed outdoors but not inside the library. Any questions before we set off?

reading.title: The First Olympic Games
reading.passage:
The ancient Olympic Games began in Greece more than two and a half thousand years ago. They were held in a place called Olympia, in honour of the chief god, Zeus. According to tradition, the first recorded games took place in 776 BC, and afterwards they were repeated every four years. This four-year period became so important that the Greeks used it to measure time.

The early games were quite simple. At first the only event was a short running race, the length of the stadium. Over time, more events were added, including longer races, wrestling and chariot racing. Only free Greek men were allowed to compete, and they took part without clothing, which the Greeks did not consider shameful.

Winning at Olympia brought great honour. The prize itself was modest, usually a crown made from olive leaves, but a champion gained fame across the Greek world. Cities were proud of their winners and sometimes rewarded them with money or free meals for life. Poets wrote songs praising the most successful athletes.

The games were also a time of peace. During the festival, a special truce was declared so that travellers could reach Olympia safely, even if their cities were at war. This truce did not stop all fighting, but it allowed the games to continue without interruption for many centuries.

Eventually, the ancient games came to an end when the religious festivals connected to them were banned. For around fifteen hundred years there were no Olympic Games at all. Only in the late nineteenth century was the idea revived, leading to the modern competition we know today.

questions.q: The ancient games were held in honour of the god Zeus.
Women were allowed to compete in the early games.
The first games included wrestling and chariot racing.
The truce completely stopped all wars during the festival.
More people attended the games than any other Greek festival.
What prize did a winner usually receive at Olympia?
How did the Greeks use the four-year period of the games?
The first recorded games took place in ________ BC.

listening.title: Students Discuss a History Project

TUTOR: So, you two are working together on the project about ancient sport. How's it going?
MAYA: Quite well, I think. We've decided to focus on the ancient Olympic Games rather than Roman sport.
TOM: Yes, there's more material available, and Maya found some good sources in the library.
TUTOR: Good. Have you divided the work?
MAYA: We have. I'm covering the history and the rules, and Tom is looking at the role of the games in society.
TOM: I'm especially interested in how winners were treated when they returned home.
TUTOR: That's a strong angle. How long will the presentation be?
MAYA: We were told twenty minutes, with five minutes for questions.
TUTOR: Correct. And remember to include at least three images. When are you planning to finish?
TOM: We're aiming to have a first draft ready by Friday.
TUTOR: Excellent. One tip: don't just list facts. Try to explain why the games mattered to ordinary people.
MAYA: That's really helpful, thank you.

reading.title: The Invention of Paper Money
reading.passage:
For most of human history, people used coins made of metal to buy and sell goods. Coins were useful because they had a fixed value and were difficult to copy. However, they were also heavy, and carrying large amounts of money over long distances was both tiring and dangerous. The solution to this problem first appeared in China, where paper money was invented.

The earliest forms of paper money grew out of a simple idea. Merchants who did not want to travel with heavy coins left them with a trusted person and received a written note in return. This note proved that the coins existed and could be exchanged for them later. In effect, the paper itself became a kind of money.

Over time, the government began to issue official notes. These were easier to carry and helped trade to grow. Yet there was a serious risk. If too many notes were printed, each one became worth less, and prices rose sharply. This problem, which we now call inflation, troubled several governments that printed money too freely.

When European travellers visited China and saw paper money in use, many were amazed. The idea that a piece of paper could be worth as much as gold seemed strange to them. It took several centuries before paper money became common in Europe.

Today almost every country uses paper or plastic notes, and increasingly money exists only as numbers on a screen. The basic principle, however, remains the one discovered long ago: money works because people trust that it can be exchanged for something of real value.

questions.q: Coins were difficult to copy.
Paper money was first invented in Europe.
Printing too many notes could cause prices to rise.
European travellers immediately adopted paper money after seeing it.
The first Chinese paper notes were decorated with pictures of emperors.
What problem with coins does the passage mention?
According to the passage, why does money work?
options: Because it is made of valuable metal
Because the government forces people to use it
Because people trust it can be exchanged for real value
Because it is printed on special paper

questions.q: The problem caused by printing too many notes is now called ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Early Trade Systems
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we're looking at how early societies exchanged goods before money existed. The earliest system was simple barter, where one item was swapped directly for another. The obvious problem was matching needs. If I had grain but wanted a tool, I had to find someone with a tool who also wanted grain. Economists call this the double coincidence of wants, and it made trade slow and difficult. To solve it, communities began using certain objects as a standard of value. In some regions, this was cattle; in others, it was salt or shells. These items were widely accepted, easy to recognize, and reasonably durable. The next major step was metal coins, which appeared independently in several parts of the world. Coins had a stamped value, which meant people did not have to weigh them each time. By the way, your reading for next week is chapter four, not chapter three, so please make a note of that. We'll discuss paper money in the following session.

questions.q: What was the main problem with barter?
options: Matching the needs of two people
People did not trust each other

questions.q: The difficulty of matching needs is called the double coincidence of ________.
In some regions, ________ was used as a standard of value.
What advantage did coins have?
options: They had a stamped value, so no weighing was needed

questions.q: The reading for next week is chapter ________.
What topic will be discussed in the following session?

speaking.q: Let's talk about money. Do you prefer to pay with cash or with a card? Why? Do you think children should learn how to manage money at school?

writing.q: In many countries, the use of physical cash is decreasing as people pay with cards and phones. Do the advantages of a cashless society outweigh the disadvantages? Give reasons for your answer and include relevant examples. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Each coin had a fixed value.
Paper money was a clever solution.
He left his coins with a trusted friend.
The government began to issue official notes.
Inflation made everyday goods more expensive.
Travellers were amazed by the paper money.
The basic principle of money is trust.
Early people used barter to trade.
They swapped grain for tools.
Salt became a standard of value.
Coins were durable and lasted a long time.
Coins appeared independently in several places.
The objects were easy to recognize.
The note had the same value as gold.

reading.title: The Great Wall of China
reading.passage:
The Great Wall of China is one of the most famous structures ever built. Many people imagine it as a single, continuous wall stretching across the country, but in reality it is a series of walls and barriers built over many centuries by different rulers. Some sections were made of earth and wood, while later parts, the ones most tourists see today, were built of stone and brick.

The main purpose of the wall was defence. Northern groups on horseback often raided the settled lands to the south, and the wall was meant to slow them down rather than stop them completely. Towers were built along its length so that soldiers could watch for danger and send warning signals using smoke or fire. In this way, news of an attack could travel quickly over great distances.

Building the wall required enormous effort. Huge numbers of workers, including soldiers and ordinary people, were forced to labour in difficult conditions. Many died during construction, and stories about their hardship have been passed down for generations. The wall therefore came to represent both the power of the rulers and the suffering of the people.

Over time, the wall lost its military importance. New weapons made high walls less useful, and the borders of the country changed. Parts of the wall were neglected and slowly fell into ruin. In some areas, local people removed stones to use in their own buildings.

Today the Great Wall is protected as a treasured part of world heritage. Millions of visitors walk along its restored sections each year. Although it no longer defends anything, it remains a powerful symbol of human determination and skill.

questions.q: The Great Wall is a single continuous wall built at one time.
The wall was intended to slow down attackers rather than stop them completely.
Soldiers used smoke or fire to send warning signals.
The wall was the most expensive structure in the country's history.
Local people sometimes took stones from the wall for their own buildings.
Why did the wall lose its military importance?
options: New weapons made high walls less useful
It was too short to be useful

questions.q: What does the wall represent today, according to the passage?
Watchtowers were built so that soldiers could watch for ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient Defensive Walls
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to talk about why ancient societies built defensive walls, and what these structures tell us about their builders. We tend to assume walls were purely military, but that's only part of the story. Yes, the first function was protection from raiders and armies. A high wall forced attackers to slow down, gather in predictable places, and become easier targets. But walls also had a second, less obvious purpose: control. By limiting the number of gates, rulers could tax goods entering a city and monitor who came and went. A third function was psychological. A massive wall sent a clear message about the strength and wealth of those who built it, discouraging enemies before any fighting began. Now, an interesting point. Many famous walls were never actually attacked in a major battle. Their value lay in deterrence rather than combat. For your seminar on Tuesday, please read the article on the cost of wall-building, and think about whether the expense was really worth it.

questions.q: What does the lecturer say is the first function of walls?
By limiting the number of ________, rulers could tax goods entering a city.
What was the third, psychological function of walls?
options: To send a message about strength and wealth

questions.q: The lecturer says many walls' value lay in ________ rather than combat.
The seminar will take place on ________.
What should students think about for the seminar?
options: Whether the expense of wall-building was worth it

speaking.q:
Describe a famous historical building or monument you have seen or would like to see. You should say:
- what it is and where it is
- what it looks like
- why it is famous
and explain how you would feel about visiting it.

Site | 2017 | 2019 | 2021
Great Wall | 9.0 | 10.5 | 4.2
Ancient Castle | 3.5 | 4.0 | 2.1
Old City Walls | 1.2 | 1.8 | 1.0

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The wall is a huge stone structure.
It is not a single continuous wall.
The wall acted as a barrier against raiders.
The wall was built for defence.

They sent a warning signal with smoke.

reading.title: Why We Need Sleep
reading.passage:
Most adults spend about a third of their lives asleep, yet for a long time scientists were not sure why sleep was so important. Today, researchers agree that sleep is far from a simple period of rest. During the night, the brain and body carry out a range of tasks that cannot easily be done while we are awake.

One of the clearest benefits of sleep is its effect on memory. While we sleep, the brain appears to sort through the events of the day, strengthening the connections that matter and weakening those that do not. This is one reason why students who sleep well after studying tend to remember more than those who stay up late. A tired brain finds it much harder to store new information.

Sleep also plays a role in physical health. During deep sleep, the body releases hormones that help repair tissue and support growth. The immune system, which defends the body against illness, is also more active at night. People who regularly sleep too little are more likely to catch common infections such as colds.

Despite this, many people treat sleep as something that can be cut back whenever life becomes busy. Doctors warn that this is a mistake. Going without enough sleep for several nights affects concentration, mood and judgement, and the effects build up over time. Getting enough rest, they argue, is not a luxury but a basic requirement for a healthy life.

questions.q: Scientists have always clearly understood why sleep is important.
Sleep is simply a period of rest in which little happens.
According to the passage, why do students who sleep well remember more?
options: the brain strengthens useful connections during sleep
they feel more confident in exams
they read their notes again at night

questions.q: During deep sleep, the body releases ________ that help repair tissue.
The immune system is more active at night.
People who sleep too little are more likely to catch common infections.
Most cases of severe heart disease are caused directly by poor sleep.
Doctors say getting enough rest is not a luxury but a basic ________ for health.

listening.title: Booking a Health Check Appointment
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Greenfield Health Centre. How can I help?

CALLER: Hello, I'd like to book a general health check, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. Can I take your full name?

CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And your date of birth?

CALLER: The fourteenth of March, 1990.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. We have a slot next Tuesday at nine thirty in the morning, or Thursday at two in the afternoon.

CALLER: Thursday afternoon suits me better.

RECEPTIONIST: Right, Thursday at two. The check takes about forty minutes. Please bring a list of any medicines you take. Oh, and do try not to eat for two hours beforehand, as we'll be doing a blood test.

CALLER: No problem. How much does the check cost?

RECEPTIONIST: For new patients it's twenty-five pounds. You can pay by card on the day.

CALLER: Great. And where exactly is the centre?

RECEPTIONIST: We're at 47 Mill Road, just opposite the library.

CALLER: Perfect. Thank you very much.

reading.title: The Return of the Bicycle
reading.passage:
In many large cities, the bicycle is enjoying a remarkable return to popularity. For much of the twentieth century, the car was seen as the symbol of modern life, and roads were designed mainly with drivers in mind. In recent years, however, growing concern about traffic, pollution and public health has encouraged city leaders to look again at cycling.

The health benefits of regular cycling are well documented. Riding a bicycle to work or school provides gentle daily exercise that strengthens the heart and helps people maintain a healthy weight. Unlike going to a gym, this exercise fits naturally into the daily routine and costs almost nothing once a bicycle has been bought.

Cities also gain when more people cycle. Bicycles take up far less space than cars, both on the road and when parked. They produce no exhaust fumes, so the air becomes cleaner. Quiet streets full of cyclists are often more pleasant places than busy roads packed with vehicles.

Encouraging cycling is not always easy, however. Many people are reluctant to ride when they feel that the roads are dangerous. For this reason, the most successful cities have built networks of protected cycle lanes that separate riders from heavy traffic. Some have also introduced bicycle-sharing schemes, allowing people to hire a bicycle for a short journey and return it nearby. Where such measures are in place, the number of cyclists has often risen sharply.

questions.q: For most of the twentieth century, roads were designed mainly for drivers.
Why has cycling become more popular in cities recently?
options: bicycles have become cheaper to buy
concern about traffic, pollution and health has grown
cars are no longer allowed in cities

questions.q: Cycling to work fits naturally into the daily routine.
Bicycles produce no exhaust ________, so the air becomes cleaner.
Most people say they enjoy cycling in heavy traffic.
Successful cities have built networks of protected cycle ________.
Bicycle-sharing schemes are more expensive to run than building roads.
What happens where cycle lanes and sharing schemes exist?
options: the number of cyclists often rises sharply

listening.title: Joining a Community Cycling Club
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, and thanks for coming along to find out about the Riverside Cycling Club. Let me give you a quick overview.

We meet twice a week, on Wednesday evenings and Sunday mornings. The Sunday ride is the main one and it usually starts at eight o'clock from the car park near the bridge. We ride for about two hours, then stop for coffee.

Now, the club is open to all ages, but if you're under sixteen you'll need a parent with you. Membership costs fifty dollars a year, which covers insurance and a club shirt. You don't need an expensive bike to join — any bike in good working order is fine — but a helmet is compulsory. We won't let anyone ride without one.

For your first ride, please bring plenty of water and a light snack. We follow quiet roads and cycle paths wherever possible, and an experienced leader stays at the back so nobody gets left behind. If you'd like to sign up today, just see Maria at the front desk and she'll take your details.

questions.q: The main Sunday ride starts at ________ o'clock.
Members under ________ must be accompanied by a parent.
Membership costs ________ dollars a year.
What item is compulsory for riders?
options: a club shirt worn at all times

questions.q: For the first ride, members should bring water and a light ________.
An experienced leader stays at the back so nobody gets left behind.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you traveled somewhere without using a car. You should say:
- where you went and why
- how you traveled
- who you were with
and explain how you felt about the experience.

writing.q: Some people think that cities should ban cars from their centres to improve public health and reduce pollution. Others believe this would cause too many problems. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The bicycle is enjoying a remarkable return.
The car was a symbol of modern life.
Concern about pollution has grown.
The benefits are well documented.
Cycling helps maintain a healthy weight.
Bicycles produce no exhaust fumes.

Lanes separate riders from traffic.

reading.title: Eating with the Seasons
reading.passage:
For most of human history, people ate whatever food was available at a particular time of year. In spring and summer there were fresh fruits and vegetables; in winter there was far less choice. Today, thanks to modern transport and storage, many shoppers can buy almost any food at any time. Some experts, however, argue that there are good reasons to return to eating with the seasons.

One argument concerns taste and nutrition. Fruits and vegetables grown locally and picked when ripe often taste better than those transported long distances. They may also contain more vitamins, since food can lose some of its goodness during long periods of storage and travel.

There are environmental arguments too. Food that is grown out of season frequently has to be flown in from distant countries or raised in heated greenhouses. Both of these methods use large amounts of energy. By choosing seasonal produce, shoppers can reduce the energy used to bring food to their plates.

Seasonal eating is not without its difficulties. In countries with long, cold winters, the choice of local produce can be very limited for several months. Critics also point out that not everyone has the time or money to shop carefully. Even so, supporters believe that eating more seasonal food, even some of the time, is a small change that can benefit both health and the planet.

questions.q: In the past, people ate whatever food was available at the time of year.
Modern transport and ________ allow shoppers to buy most foods at any time.
Why might seasonal fruit and vegetables contain more vitamins?
options: food can lose goodness during long storage and travel

questions.q: Out-of-season food is often flown in or grown in heated greenhouses.
Heated greenhouses use very little energy.
Seasonal food is always cheaper than imported food.
In countries with cold winters, the choice of local ________ can be limited.
What do supporters of seasonal eating believe?
options: everyone must eat only seasonal food
even partial seasonal eating can benefit health and the planet

listening.title: A Talk on the Farmers' Market
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good afternoon. I'd like to tell you about our new farmers' market, which opens next month here in the town square.

The market will run every Saturday from eight in the morning until one o'clock. We've signed up more than thirty local producers, so there'll be plenty to choose from. You'll find fresh vegetables, of course, but also bread, cheese, honey and even fresh fish brought in from the coast.

One of our main aims is to support local farmers. When you buy directly from the grower, more of your money goes to the person who actually produced the food. It also means the food travels a very short distance, so it's fresher and better for the environment.

We're keen to make the market welcoming to families. There'll be a small play area for children, and each week a different farmer will give a short cooking demonstration using their own produce. Entry is completely free. We do ask, though, that you bring your own bags, as we're trying to cut down on plastic. We hope to see you there.

questions.q: The market runs every Saturday until ________ o'clock.
More than ________ local producers have signed up.
What is one main aim of the market?
options: to raise money for the council

questions.q: Each week a farmer gives a short cooking ________.
Entry to the market is free.
Visitors are asked to bring their own ________ to reduce plastic.

speaking.q: Let's discuss food and society. Do you think people today pay enough attention to where their food comes from? Why do some people prefer convenience over healthy eating? Should governments encourage people to eat local, seasonal food?

The table below shows the percentage of people in a town who bought four types of food from local markets in 2010 and 2020.

Food type | 2010 | 2020
Vegetables | 35% | 58%
Fruit | 30% | 49%
Bread | 22% | 31%
Cheese | 12% | 25%

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: People ate whatever food was available.
Modern storage keeps food longer.
The argument concerns taste and nutrition.

reading.title: The Power of Clean Water
reading.passage:
Few advances in public health have saved as many lives as the supply of clean drinking water. For most of human history, water was a common source of disease. People who drank from rivers or wells often had no way of knowing whether the water was safe, and outbreaks of illness such as cholera could spread rapidly through a community.

The link between dirty water and disease was not always understood. In the nineteenth century, a doctor in London studied an outbreak of cholera and traced it to a single public water pump. By mapping where victims lived, he showed that those who used the pump were far more likely to fall ill. His careful work helped convince others that contaminated water, rather than bad air, was the cause.

Once this connection was accepted, cities began to invest heavily in clean water systems. Engineers built reservoirs, laid pipes and developed methods of filtering and treating water before it reached homes. These projects were expensive, but the results were dramatic. Deaths from water-borne diseases fell sharply in the cities that adopted them.

In many parts of the world, safe water is now taken for granted. Yet millions of people still lack reliable access to it, and water-borne diseases remain a serious problem in some regions. Health organisations argue that providing clean water is one of the most effective ways of improving public health, often doing more good than expensive medical treatments.

questions.q: For most of history, water was a common source of disease.
How did the London doctor support his idea about cholera?
options: by testing water in a laboratory
by mapping where victims lived and linking them to one pump
by interviewing every patient in the city

questions.q: Many people once believed that bad air caused cholera.
Engineers built reservoirs and laid ________ to deliver clean water.
Clean water projects were cheap to build.
Deaths from water-borne diseases fell in cities that built these systems.
The London doctor later received an award from the government.
Health organisations say clean water can do more good than expensive medical ________.

listening.title: University Lecture: Water and Public Health
listening.script:
LECTURER: Okay, let's get started. Today we're looking at why clean water matters so much for public health.

Let me give you one striking figure first. Worldwide, unsafe water and poor sanitation are linked to around two million deaths every year, many of them young children. That's a number worth keeping in mind throughout this course.

Now, the diseases involved are usually spread in a similar way. A person who is sick passes the germs into the water supply, and others then drink that same water and become ill in turn. Breaking this cycle is the key goal of public health work.

There are three main approaches. The first is treatment, that is, cleaning the water before people drink it, often by filtering it and adding small amounts of chlorine. The second is improving sanitation, so that waste is kept well away from drinking water. The third, which is sometimes forgotten, is education, teaching people simple habits such as washing their hands.

Research shows that when all three are combined, the fall in disease is far greater than any single measure on its own. Next week, we'll look at how one country put these ideas into practice.

questions.q: Unsafe water and poor sanitation are linked to around ________ million deaths a year.
What is the key goal of public health work mentioned in the lecture?
options: breaking the cycle by which disease spreads through water

questions.q: Treatment often involves filtering water and adding small amounts of ________.
Improving ________ keeps waste away from drinking water.
Which approach does the lecturer say is sometimes forgotten?
Combining all three approaches reduces disease more than any single measure.

speaking.q: Let's talk about water. How much water do you drink each day? Do you usually drink tap water or bottled water? Is clean water easy to get where you live?

writing.q: Some people believe that governments should spend more money on providing clean water and good sanitation than on building hospitals and buying medical equipment. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Few advances have saved so many lives.
The supply of clean water saves lives.
An outbreak of cholera spread quickly.
He traced the outbreak to one pump.
Contaminated water caused the disease.
Cities began to invest in water systems.
Engineers built reservoirs.
Methods of filtering water were developed.
The results were dramatic.
Millions lack reliable access to water.
Poor sanitation spreads disease.
Germs pass into the water supply.
No single measure works as well alone.
Safe water is now taken for granted.

reading.title: Vaccines and How They Work
reading.passage:
Vaccines are among the most successful tools in modern medicine. They work by preparing the body to fight a particular disease before that disease is ever encountered. Thanks to widespread vaccination, illnesses that once killed or disabled millions of people have become rare in many countries.

The basic idea behind a vaccine is surprisingly simple. The immune system, which defends the body against illness, learns from experience. When it meets a harmful germ for the first time, it gradually builds defences against it, but this can take time, during which a person may become seriously ill. A vaccine introduces a harmless version or part of the germ, allowing the immune system to learn without the danger of real disease.

If the body later meets the real germ, it recognises the threat and responds far more quickly. In many cases the person never becomes ill at all. Some vaccines protect for life, while others need to be repeated after several years.

Vaccines also protect communities, not just individuals. When most people in a population are vaccinated, a disease finds it hard to spread, because there are too few people for it to pass between. This effect helps to protect those who cannot be vaccinated, such as very young babies. For this reason, public health experts stress that high vaccination rates benefit everyone, even those who are already protected.

questions.q: Vaccines prepare the body to fight a disease before it is encountered.
What does a vaccine introduce into the body?
options: a strong dose of the full disease
a harmless version or part of the germ
a chemical that kills all germs

questions.q: Building defences against a new germ can take time.
If the body later meets the real germ, it recognises the ________ and responds quickly.
All vaccines protect a person for their whole life.
When most people are vaccinated, a disease finds it hard to ________.
Why do high vaccination rates benefit everyone?
options: they help protect those who cannot be vaccinated
they remove the need for hospitals

questions.q: The first vaccine was developed to fight cholera.

listening.title: Pharmacy Vaccination Information
listening.script:
PHARMACIST: Hello there. I understand you'd like some information about getting the flu vaccine here at the pharmacy?

CUSTOMER: Yes, that's right. I've never had it before.

PHARMACIST: No problem at all. It's a quick and simple process. The vaccine itself costs eighteen pounds, though it's free if you're over sixty-five or have certain health conditions.

CUSTOMER: I'm forty-two and healthy, so I'll pay. How long does it take?

PHARMACIST: The injection takes just a few minutes, but we ask you to wait for about fifteen minutes afterwards, just to be on the safe side.

CUSTOMER: Are there any side effects?

PHARMACIST: Most people have none at all. A few notice a sore arm for a day, and very occasionally a mild fever. Nothing serious.

CUSTOMER: And when's the best time to have it?

PHARMACIST: Ideally in October, before the winter season begins. We're open for vaccinations Monday to Friday, no appointment needed. Just bring some form of identification.

CUSTOMER: Great, I'll come back next week.

questions.q: The flu vaccine costs ________ pounds for paying customers.
Who can get the vaccine free of charge?
options: people over 65 or with certain health conditions

questions.q: Customers are asked to wait for about ________ minutes afterwards.
What side effect does the pharmacist mention?
options: a sore arm for a day
a bad headache for a week

questions.q: The best time to have the vaccine is in ________.
Customers need to book an appointment in advance.

Describe a time when you or someone you know visited a doctor or health clinic. You should say:
- why the visit was needed
- where you went
- what happened during the visit
and explain how you felt afterwards.

The table below shows the percentage of children vaccinated against three diseases in a country in 2000 and 2020.

Disease | 2000 | 2020

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

Before the disease is ever encountered.

reading.title: Homework: Help or Hindrance?
reading.passage:
For more than a century, homework has been a routine part of school life in many countries. Teachers set written tasks to be completed at home, often to reinforce what has been taught in class. Yet the value of homework has long been debated. Some parents and educators see it as essential practice, while others argue that it places an unfair burden on young learners and reduces the time available for play, sport and family.

Research on the topic has produced mixed results. A large review carried out in 2015 examined dozens of studies and found that homework appeared to have only a small effect on the performance of primary-school pupils. For older students, however, the link between regular homework and better marks was clearer. The reviewers suggested that the type of task mattered more than the amount. Short, focused exercises seemed to help, whereas long and repetitive worksheets often led to boredom and frustration.

The amount of homework that students receive varies widely. In some education systems, ten-year-old children are given barely any work to take home, while in others they may spend an hour or more on it each evening. Critics of heavy homework loads point out that not all children have a quiet place to study or an adult who can help them. As a result, homework may actually widen the gap between pupils from different backgrounds rather than closing it.

Some schools have begun to experiment with alternatives. A few have abolished traditional homework altogether, asking pupils only to read for pleasure each evening. Early reports from these schools suggest that attitudes towards learning have improved, although it is still too soon to say whether examination results will follow the same trend.

questions.q: Homework has been a common feature of schooling in many nations for over a hundred years.
The 2015 review found a strong effect of homework on primary-school pupils.
The reviewers concluded that the quantity of homework was more important than its type.
Children who do homework score higher on national happiness surveys.

reading.title: Learning a Second Language as an Adult
reading.passage:
It is often claimed that children find it easier to learn a foreign language than adults do. Young children certainly pick up the sounds of a new language with remarkable ease, and they rarely feel embarrassed about making mistakes. For these reasons, many people believe that there is a 'critical period' in early life after which language learning becomes far more difficult.

However, the picture is more complicated than this popular belief suggests. Adults have several advantages of their own. They already understand how language works, so they can grasp grammar rules quickly and apply them in new situations. They can also use dictionaries, study notes and other tools that young children cannot. In tests of vocabulary and reading, motivated adult learners often make faster progress than children in the early months of study.

The main difficulty for adults is usually pronunciation. After a certain age, most learners keep a slight accent in the new language, no matter how hard they practise. Yet a foreign accent is rarely a serious problem, since the goal of most learners is simply to communicate clearly rather than to sound like a native speaker.

Motivation also plays a key role. An adult who studies a language for a clear purpose, such as a new job or travel, tends to improve quickly. By contrast, learners who feel forced to study often lose interest and give up. Teachers therefore stress that regular, enjoyable practice matters far more than the age at which a person begins. With patience and the right methods, adults can reach a high level in a language they once thought was beyond them.

questions.q: Young children are usually anxious about making mistakes in a new language.
Adults can use study tools that young children cannot.
Motivated adults sometimes learn vocabulary faster than children early on.
Adults who learn a language for work earn higher salaries than other learners.
What is described as the main difficulty for adult learners?
According to the passage, why is a foreign accent rarely a serious problem?
options: Most learners want to sound native
The aim is usually clear communication

questions.q: Many people believe there is a so-called ________ period in early life for language learning.
Teachers say that regular, ________ practice matters more than a learner's age.

STUDENT: What time does it start?
ADVISOR: The evening class begins at seven fifteen and finishes at nine.
STUDENT: That works for me. How long is the whole course?
ADVISOR: It lasts twelve weeks, and the fee is one hundred and eighty dollars. That includes the textbook.
STUDENT: Great. Is there a test at the end?
ADVISOR: There's a short speaking assessment, but it isn't compulsory. Most students just want a certificate of attendance.
STUDENT: Perfect. How do I pay?
ADVISOR: You can pay online or at our front desk. I'd recommend booking soon, as there are only six places left.
STUDENT: I'll book online tonight. Thanks for your help.

reading.title: The Rise of Online Learning
reading.passage:
Twenty years ago, the idea of attending a university lecture from your own bedroom would have seemed strange. Today, millions of people around the world study online, taking courses offered by colleges, private companies and free educational websites. The growth of online learning has been one of the most striking changes in education in recent decades.

There are clear reasons for its popularity. Online courses are often cheaper than traditional ones, and they allow students to study at their own pace. A person with a full-time job can watch recorded lectures in the evening, while a parent at home can fit study around family duties. For learners who live far from a college, the internet removes the need to travel, saving both time and money.

Despite these benefits, online learning is not suitable for everyone. Studying alone requires a great deal of self-discipline. Without the routine of a timetable and the presence of classmates, some students lose motivation and fail to complete their courses. In fact, the proportion of learners who drop out of online courses is generally higher than for traditional classes.

Technology can also be a barrier. A reliable internet connection and a suitable device are essential, yet many people, especially in poorer regions, do not have access to these. As a result, online learning can sometimes increase rather than reduce educational inequality. Most experts now agree that the best approach is a mixture of online and face-to-face teaching, often called blended learning, which combines the flexibility of the internet with the support of a classroom.

questions.q: Online courses generally cost less than traditional courses.
More students complete online courses than traditional ones.
Online learning always reduces educational inequality.
Universities earn more money from online courses than from on-campus courses.
What quality does the passage say online study especially requires?
What is 'blended learning' according to the passage?
options: A mix of online and face-to-face teaching

questions.q: A reliable internet connection and a suitable ________ are essential for online study.
Online courses allow students to study at their own ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Choosing an Online Course
listening.script: SPEAKER: Good afternoon, everyone, and thanks for coming to this short session on choosing an online course. Whether you're a beginner or returning to study, the same advice applies. First, think about your goal. Are you studying for a qualification, for your job, or simply out of interest? This will help you pick the right level. Second, check how long the course lasts. Our most popular program runs for ten weeks, with about four hours of study a week. Third, look closely at the cost. Some courses are free, but a certificate may carry an extra fee of forty dollars. Fourth, and perhaps most important, make sure you have a quiet space and a routine. Students who set fixed study times are far more likely to finish. Finally, don't be afraid to ask for help. Every course has a tutor who answers questions by email within two working days. If you'd like to enrol today, please collect a form from the desk at the back of the room. Registration closes on the fifteenth of March.

reading.title: Why Sleep Matters for Students
reading.passage:
Most teenagers and university students do not get enough sleep. Studies in several countries suggest that the average young person sleeps for around six and a half hours on school nights, far below the eight or nine hours that experts recommend. Late-night studying, part-time jobs and the constant glow of mobile phones all play a part in this shortfall.

The effects of poor sleep on learning are now well understood. During deep sleep, the brain reviews and stores the information gathered during the day. A student who stays awake all night before an exam may feel that they have done extra revision, but in reality their tired brain is less able to recall facts and solve problems. Memory, concentration and mood all suffer when sleep is cut short.

Researchers have also found that the body clock of teenagers naturally shifts later during adolescence. This means that many young people simply cannot fall asleep early, even when they go to bed at a sensible hour. When schools force them to start lessons at half past eight, these students are effectively learning at a time when their brains are still half asleep.

For this reason, a number of schools have experimented with later start times. In one well-known trial, a secondary school delayed the start of the day by an hour. Within a year, teachers reported that students were more alert, attendance had improved, and the number of pupils falling asleep in class had dropped sharply. Such results have encouraged other schools to consider similar changes, though many parents worry about how later finishes would affect after-school activities and family routines.

questions.q: Experts recommend that young people sleep more than six and a half hours.
Staying awake all night before an exam improves a student's memory.
The body clock of teenagers naturally shifts later during adolescence.
Schools with later start times spend more money on heating.
What happens in the brain during deep sleep, according to the passage?
options: It reviews and stores the day's information

questions.q: What did teachers report after the school delayed its start time?
options: Students were more alert and attendance improved

questions.q: Many parents worry about how later finishes would affect ________ activities.
Memory, concentration and ________ all suffer when sleep is cut short.

listening.title: A Student Health Workshop
listening.script: SPEAKER: Hello everyone, and welcome to today's workshop on healthy study habits. I'm Dr Allen from the Student Wellbeing Service. We'll be looking at how small changes can really improve how you feel and how well you learn. Let's start with sleep. Try to keep regular hours, even at weekends, and avoid screens for at least thirty minutes before bed. The light from phones tricks the brain into thinking it's still daytime. Next, take regular breaks while studying. We recommend the simple method of twenty-five minutes of focused work followed by a five-minute break. After four of these, take a longer rest of about twenty minutes. Don't forget to stay hydrated; aim for about two litres of water a day. And finally, exercise matters more than people think. Even a fifteen-minute walk can boost concentration. If you'd like personal advice, our drop-in clinic is open every Thursday afternoon in Room 12. You don't need an appointment, just come along. Thank you, and remember, looking after your body is part of looking after your studies.

reading.title: The Value of School Field Trips
reading.passage:
A field trip, in which a class leaves the school to visit a museum, factory or natural site, is one of the oldest teaching methods still in use. For many pupils, these outings are among the most memorable parts of their school years. Yet in recent times, rising costs and concerns about safety have led some schools to cut back on trips, raising the question of whether they are still worth the effort.

Supporters argue that field trips offer a kind of learning that cannot be reproduced in the classroom. Seeing a famous painting in person, touching a fossil or watching a process in a real workshop can bring a subject to life in a way that textbooks rarely manage. Such experiences often stay in the memory for years and can spark a lasting interest in a topic.

Field trips may also help pupils develop skills that are difficult to teach in lessons. Away from their desks, students must work together, follow instructions in an unfamiliar place and behave responsibly in public. Teachers frequently report that quieter pupils become more confident on a trip, and that relationships within a class improve afterwards.

There are, of course, real obstacles. Transport and entrance fees can be expensive, and not every family can afford to contribute. Organising a trip also takes a great deal of a teacher's time, and there is always some risk involved in taking children away from school. To address these issues, some schools now use virtual tours, which allow pupils to explore distant places on a screen. While these digital visits are cheaper and safer, most teachers agree that they cannot fully replace the excitement and richness of a real journey.

questions.q: Field trips are a relatively new teaching method.
Some schools have reduced trips because of cost and safety concerns.
Teachers report that quieter pupils often gain confidence on trips.
Field trips lead to better marks in final examinations.
Why do supporters value field trips, according to the passage?
options: They offer learning that cannot be reproduced in class

questions.q: What does the passage say about virtual tours?
options: They are more exciting than real trips
They are cheaper and safer but cannot fully replace real journeys
They are banned in most schools
They cost more than real trips

questions.q: Away from their desks, students must behave ________ in public.

We'll be leaving from the school car park, so please be there by eight forty-five. The coach won't wait for latecomers. The journey takes about an hour, so we should arrive around ten. Now, lunch. You'll need to bring a packed lunch, as the museum cafe is quite expensive. Please don't bring fizzy drinks; water or juice only. The entrance fee has already been paid by the school, but you might want to bring a few dollars for the gift shop. As for clothing, wear comfortable shoes because there's a lot of walking, and bring a light jacket in case it's cool inside. We'll split into groups of five, each with an adult helper. Finally, remember to hand in your signed permission form by Friday. Without it, I'm afraid you won't be able to come. Any questions, see me after class.

reading.title: The Story of the Poster
reading.passage:
The printed poster is so common today that we rarely stop to think about it. Yet for most of human history, large public images were rare and expensive. It was only in the nineteenth century, when new printing methods made colour cheap and fast, that the poster became a true mass medium. Suddenly, walls in busy cities were covered with bright advertisements for theatres, shops and trains.

One of the most important figures in this story was the French artist Jules Chéret. Working in Paris in the 1860s, he developed a technique that allowed several colours to be printed in quick succession. His posters, full of cheerful figures and bold lettering, decorated the streets so widely that some people began to call him "the father of the modern poster". Critics complained that the city was becoming an open-air gallery, but ordinary citizens enjoyed the colour the posters brought.

The poster was never purely decorative. Its main purpose was to persuade, and designers quickly learned that a single strong image worked better than a page of words. A traveller hurrying past needed to grasp the message in a moment. For this reason, posters tended to use simple shapes, limited text and one clear idea, principles that still guide graphic designers today.

During the twentieth century the poster took on new roles. Governments used it to spread information during times of war, while political movements relied on it to share their ideas. Although screens have since replaced many printed images, the poster has not disappeared. Musicians, film studios and charities continue to print them, proving that a well-designed image on a wall can still capture attention in a crowded world.

questions.q: Large public images were widely available before the nineteenth century.
Jules Chéret worked mainly in London.
Everyone in Paris welcomed the spread of posters across the city.
Chéret earned more money than other artists of his time.
According to the passage, why did posters favour simple images?
options: Passers-by had little time to read
The law limited the amount of text

questions.q: What does the passage say about posters in the twentieth century?
options: They were used only for advertising
They served political and informational purposes

questions.q: New printing methods made colour both cheap and ________ in the nineteenth century.
Today, musicians, film studios and ________ still print posters.

reading.title: Why Some Buildings Stay Cool
reading.passage:
Long before electric air conditioning existed, builders in hot countries found clever ways to keep indoor spaces cool. Many of these methods relied not on machines but on careful design, using the shape of a building and the materials it was made from to fight the heat. Today, as energy costs rise, architects are studying these traditional ideas once again.

One common method was the use of thick walls. Materials such as stone and clay are slow to heat up and slow to cool down. During the day, thick walls absorb the sun's heat without letting it pass quickly into the rooms inside. At night, when the air becomes cooler, the stored heat is released. In this way the building keeps a steady temperature, warmer than the outside at night and cooler during the day.

The position of windows also mattered a great deal. Builders often placed small windows on the sunny side of a house and larger ones where shade was likely. Some designs included narrow openings high in the wall, which allowed hot air to escape upwards while cooler air was drawn in from below. This gentle movement of air, created without any fan, could make a room feel noticeably fresher.

In certain regions, builders added towers that caught the wind. These tall structures guided breezes down into the living areas, cooling them even on still days. Such towers were a familiar sight in parts of the Middle East for centuries. Modern architects now combine these old principles with new materials, hoping to design comfortable buildings that use far less energy than those that depend entirely on machines.

questions.q: Builders in hot countries used design to reduce heat before air conditioning was invented.
Thick stone walls heat up very quickly during the day.
Wind towers were common in parts of the Middle East.
Modern wind towers are more expensive to build than traditional ones.
Why were large windows often placed on the shaded side of a house?
options: To avoid the direct heat of the sun

questions.q: How did high, narrow openings help cool a room?
options: They let hot air rise and escape
They stored heat for the night

questions.q: Thick walls release their stored heat at ________.

reading.title: The Rise of Public Sculpture
reading.passage:
Walk through almost any city centre and you will pass a piece of public sculpture, perhaps a statue of an old leader, a fountain, or a modern shape made of metal. These works are so familiar that many people barely glance at them. Yet public sculpture has a long history, and the reasons for putting art in shared spaces have changed greatly over time.

In ancient cities, sculpture in public places usually had a clear purpose. Statues honoured rulers, gods or victories in battle. They were not simply decorations but messages, reminding citizens of power, faith or duty. A large figure in a market square told everyone who passed by what their society valued, even if they could not read a single word.

By the nineteenth century, the purpose had begun to widen. Cities competed to appear cultured and proud, and a fine statue or monument was a way of showing this. Wealthy donors paid for sculptures of writers, scientists and local heroes. Such works were meant to educate and inspire, encouraging ordinary people to admire achievement and to feel connected to their city's past.

Modern public sculpture often works differently. Instead of celebrating a single famous person, many recent pieces are abstract, using shape, colour and material to create a feeling rather than to tell a story. Some city authorities argue that such art makes streets more lively and gives people something to talk about. Others complain that abstract works are confusing or a waste of money. Whatever the debate, public sculpture continues to shape how cities look and how people experience the spaces they share.

questions.q: Ancient public statues were created purely as decoration.
In the nineteenth century, cities used sculpture to appear cultured.
Modern abstract sculptures usually tell a clear story about a person.
Most modern sculptures are made of recycled materials.
What does the passage say a large statue in an ancient square communicated?
options: The wealth of a single donor

questions.q: Who often paid for sculptures in the nineteenth century?
Ancient statues honoured rulers, gods or ________ in battle.

listening.title: A Lecture on City Statues
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to talk about how cities choose public sculptures. The process is often longer than people imagine. First, a city committee decides on a theme, such as honouring a local writer or marking an important event. This stage alone can take up to six months.
Next, artists are invited to send in designs. Usually around twenty proposals arrive, and a panel selects three for closer study. The public is then invited to vote, and this is where opinions can become heated. People care deeply about what stands in their square.
Once a design is chosen, the real work begins. Making a large bronze sculpture can take a full year, and the cost often reaches two hundred thousand pounds. Funding usually comes from a mix of city money and private gifts. Finally, the finished work is revealed at a public ceremony, often with music and speeches. So a single statue can take more than two years from idea to unveiling.

reading.title: The Colours We Choose
reading.passage:
Colour is one of the first things we notice about a product, a room or a brand, yet few of us think carefully about why certain colours are chosen. Designers, however, spend a great deal of time on this question. They know that colour can affect how we feel, what we expect and even what we are willing to pay.

Research in design suggests that colours carry rough meanings that many people share. Blue is often linked with calm and trust, which is why banks and technology firms use it so frequently. Red can suggest energy or urgency, making it popular for sales and warnings. Green is widely connected with nature and health. These links are not fixed rules, but they are common enough for designers to rely on them.

Culture plays a part too. A colour seen as cheerful in one country may carry a different feeling in another. For this reason, a company selling products around the world cannot simply copy the same design everywhere. What works well in one market may seem strange, or even unpleasant, in another. Careful designers therefore study local tastes before deciding on a final colour scheme.

There is also the matter of practicality. Some colours fade quickly in sunlight, while others hide dirt or scratches better. A designer choosing the colour of a chair or a car must balance feeling with function. The most attractive shade is of little use if it looks worn after a single year. In the end, choosing colour is rarely about beauty alone; it is a careful decision shaped by psychology, culture and everyday use.

questions.q: Most people think carefully about why colours are chosen for products.
Blue is often associated with calm and trust.
The meanings of colours are the same in every culture.
Blue is the most commonly chosen colour for cars worldwide.
Why do banks often use the colour blue?
According to the passage, what must a global company consider about colour?
Red can suggest energy or ________.
A designer must balance feeling with ________.

listening.title: Planning a Product Design Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, for your group project, you'll redesign an everyday object. Let's go over the steps. First, choose your object by Friday and tell me what it is.
STUDENT: We were thinking of a water bottle.
TUTOR: Good choice. Step two is research. Spend about two weeks studying how people use it and what problems they have. Interview at least ten users.
STUDENT: Ten interviews, got it. What comes next?
TUTOR: Then you sketch ideas. I want to see at least five different designs before you choose one.
STUDENT: Should we make a model?
TUTOR: Yes. After choosing a design, build a simple model. You can use cardboard or clay, whatever is cheapest.
STUDENT: And the final part?
TUTOR: A short presentation, no longer than fifteen minutes, explaining your choices. The deadline for the whole project is the twentieth of March.
STUDENT: Thanks, that's really clear.
TUTOR: One last thing, send me your team list by email tonight.

reading.title: Designing for Everyone
reading.passage:
When designers create a product or a space, they often imagine a typical user: someone with good eyesight, steady hands and average height. Yet real people are far more varied. Some have poor vision, some use wheelchairs, and others may simply be carrying a heavy bag or holding a child. A growing movement in design argues that products should be made to suit as many people as possible, an idea known as universal design.

The principle is simple but powerful. Rather than creating a standard product and then adding special versions for people with particular needs, designers try to build in flexibility from the start. A door handle that can be pushed with an elbow, for example, helps a person whose arms are full just as much as it helps someone with weak hands. The benefit reaches everyone, not only a small group.

Supporters point out that designing for difference often improves products for all users. Wider doorways, clear signs and steps that are easy to climb make life smoother for the whole population. Features first intended for older people, such as larger buttons or simpler instructions, are frequently welcomed by younger users too. In this way, good universal design quietly removes small frustrations from daily life.

There are challenges, of course. Building in flexibility can raise costs, and some designers worry that products may look plain if they must please everyone. Yet many argue that the gains are worth it. A world designed only for an imaginary average person excludes a great many real ones. By thinking about variety from the beginning, designers can create spaces and objects that are genuinely open to all.

questions.q: Universal design aims to suit as many people as possible.
Universal design adds special versions only after the standard product is made.
Features made for older people are sometimes liked by younger users.
Universal design has been shown to reduce manufacturing time.
What is the main idea of the door handle example?
options: It is cheaper than other handles
It benefits many kinds of users

questions.q: What concern do some designers have about universal design?
options: It is illegal in many countries

questions.q: Designers often imagine a typical user with good eyesight and steady ________.
Designers try to build in ________ from the start.

listening.title: A Talk on Inclusive Museum Design
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome to today's talk on making museums easier for everyone to enjoy. Many visitors face barriers we rarely notice. Let me share a few changes our museum has made.
First, we lowered some display cases so that visitors in wheelchairs, and children, can see the objects clearly. The cases are now about ninety centimetres high.
Second, we added written descriptions next to every painting, printed in large letters. We also offer audio guides for those who prefer to listen, available in five languages.
Third, we improved the lighting. Older visitors told us that dim rooms made reading difficult, so we increased brightness in the main galleries.
Finally, we created a quiet room where people who feel overwhelmed can rest. It has soft seating and no loud sounds.
These changes were not expensive, costing under ten thousand pounds in total, yet visitor satisfaction rose sharply. We believe good design should welcome everyone, not just the average visitor.

and explain why you think its design is successful.

reading.title: The Rise of the Pop-Up Shop
reading.passage:
In recent years, a curious kind of store has appeared on busy shopping streets across many cities. Known as a "pop-up shop", it opens for only a short period, sometimes a single weekend and sometimes a few months, before closing again. These temporary stores are now a familiar feature of modern retail, and many well-known brands have used them to test new ideas.

The main appeal for a business is flexibility. Renting a permanent shop is expensive, and the owner must sign a contract that may last for years. A pop-up shop, by contrast, allows a company to rent a space for a short time and at a lower cost. If the location proves unpopular, the business simply moves on. This freedom makes pop-up shops especially attractive to small firms and online sellers who want to meet their customers face to face for the first time.

Large companies use pop-up shops for a different reason. For them, the goal is often publicity rather than profit. A striking temporary store can create excitement, attract journalists, and encourage shoppers to share photographs on social media. In this way, the shop becomes a form of advertising. Some brands deliberately open in unusual places, such as an old factory or a quiet side street, because the surprise itself draws a crowd.

Not everyone welcomes the trend. Critics argue that the constant opening and closing of stores can make a shopping district feel unstable. Yet for landlords with empty buildings, a short-term tenant is better than none, and the rent helps cover their costs while they search for a permanent occupant.

questions.q: A pop-up shop stays open for a fixed period of exactly one weekend.
Renting a permanent shop usually involves a long contract.
Large companies earn most of their yearly profit from pop-up shops.
According to the passage, why do small online sellers like pop-up shops?
options: To reduce the price of their products
To meet customers in person for the first time
To avoid paying any rent at all
To compete directly with large brands

questions.q: For large brands, the main purpose of a pop-up shop is usually to
Some brands open in unusual places because the ________ itself draws a crowd.
For landlords, a short-term tenant helps cover their ________ while they look for a permanent occupant.
Critics believe pop-up shops can make a shopping area feel unstable.

listening.title: Booking a Market Stall
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good morning, Riverside Weekend Market, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a stall to sell handmade candles.

OFFICER: Lovely. Our craft stalls cost fifteen pounds per day. Which day would you like?

CALLER: Saturday, please. And can I leave my van nearby?

OFFICER: Yes, traders park in the car park on Mill Lane. It's free if you show your stall pass.

CALLER: Great. What time should I arrive to set up?

OFFICER: Set-up starts at seven thirty in the morning, and the market opens to the public at nine.

CALLER: Perfect. Do I need to bring my own table?

OFFICER: We provide one table per stall, but you'll need your own chair. And please bring a fire blanket, since you're selling candles.

CALLER: Of course. How do I pay?

OFFICER: You can pay online once I send the form. Could I take your name?

CALLER: It's Diana Frost. That's F-R-O-S-T.

OFFICER: Thank you, Diana. I'll email the booking form today.

reading.title: Why Coffee Prices Change
reading.passage:
A cup of coffee may seem like a simple pleasure, but its price depends on a long chain of events that begins far from the shop. Most coffee is grown in warm countries near the equator, where small farmers tend the plants by hand. The journey from these farms to a customer's cup involves many steps, and a change at any stage can affect what people pay.

Weather is one of the biggest causes of price changes. Coffee plants are sensitive, and a sudden frost or a long dry season can damage an entire harvest. When this happens in a major producing country such as Brazil, the world supply falls, and the price rises quickly. Because buyers cannot easily find another source, they must accept the higher cost.

Transport also plays a part. Coffee beans travel thousands of kilometres by ship, and the cost of fuel and shipping can rise or fall sharply. In addition, many countries charge a tax on imported goods. When these taxes increase, the final price in the shop goes up, even though the farmers receive no extra money.

Finally, demand matters. As more people in growing economies develop a taste for coffee, the number of buyers increases. If supply cannot keep pace with this rising demand, sellers can charge more. Some experts believe that climate change will make good growing land scarcer in the future, which could push prices even higher. For the ordinary customer, all of these distant forces are hidden inside the simple act of ordering a drink.

questions.q: Most coffee is grown in cool countries far from the equator.
Bad weather in Brazil can reduce the world supply of coffee.
Coffee farmers earn more money when import taxes increase.
Brazil produces more coffee than any other single country.
Why do prices rise quickly when a major harvest is damaged?
options: Farmers refuse to sell their beans
Buyers cannot easily find another source

questions.q: According to the passage, what could make good growing land scarcer in future?
Coffee beans travel thousands of kilometres by ________.
If supply cannot keep pace with rising ________, sellers can charge more.

listening.title: A Talk on the Campus Café
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone. Before we finish the tour, let me tell you about the campus café, which most students use every day. It's located on the ground floor of the library building, just past the main entrance. The café opens at eight in the morning and closes at six in the evening on weekdays. On Saturdays it opens later, at ten. A regular coffee costs two dollars fifty, and if you bring your own cup, you get a fifty-cent discount. That's a popular choice, since it also helps reduce waste. The café sells sandwiches, salads, and a hot meal of the day, which changes each afternoon. If you have a food allergy, please tell the staff, and they'll check the ingredients for you. One more thing: during exam weeks, the café stays open until midnight so that students have somewhere warm to study. You can pay by card or by phone, but please note that cash is no longer accepted. Any questions can go to the help desk near the lifts.

reading.title: The Sharing Economy
reading.passage:
Not long ago, if you needed a tool, a car, or a place to stay on holiday, you usually had to buy or book it through a traditional company. Today, a different model has grown popular. Known as the "sharing economy", it allows ordinary people to rent out things they already own, often through a website or a phone app. A person with a spare room can offer it to travellers, and a driver with a free afternoon can give paid lifts to strangers.

Supporters of this model point to several benefits. First, it can save money for both sides. The owner earns extra income from something that would otherwise sit unused, while the customer often pays less than the price charged by a large firm. Second, it makes better use of resources. A car that is parked for most of the day, or a flat left empty during a trip, suddenly serves a useful purpose.

However, the sharing economy has also raised concerns. Because the people offering services are not always trained professionals, the quality can vary, and customers may have little protection if something goes wrong. Traditional businesses complain that these new arrangements avoid the rules and taxes that they themselves must follow. In some cities, the sudden growth of holiday rentals has reduced the number of homes available to local residents, pushing rents higher.

Governments are now trying to respond. Several have introduced new rules that limit how many nights a room can be rented, or that require owners to register and pay tax. Striking the right balance is difficult. Too many rules may discourage a useful service, while too few may leave customers and communities unprotected.

questions.q: In the sharing economy, people rent out items they already own.
Customers in the sharing economy always pay more than they would to a large firm.
The people offering services are always trained professionals.
The sharing economy is more popular in Europe than in Asia.
What problem in some cities is linked to holiday rentals?
According to the passage, why do traditional businesses complain?
options: The new services are too expensive
The new arrangements avoid rules and taxes
The apps are difficult to use

questions.q: The owner earns extra ________ from something that would otherwise sit unused.
Some governments now require owners to register and pay ________.

listening.title: Discussing a Car-Sharing Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Ravi, you chose car-sharing for your business project. Why that topic?

RAVI: Well, I noticed that most cars in my street are parked all day. It seemed wasteful. A sharing service could let neighbours use the same vehicle.

TUTOR: Interesting. Did you find any data?

RAVI: Yes. One study said that a single shared car can replace about eleven private cars. That surprised me.

TUTOR: It surprises most people. What about the costs?

RAVI: Members pay a monthly fee of twenty pounds, plus a charge for each hour they drive. The company covers insurance and fuel.

TUTOR: And the main challenge?

RAVI: Trust, I think. People worry the car will be returned dirty or late. So the app uses a rating system to encourage good behaviour.

TUTOR: Good. For next week, could you compare two cities where this works well?

RAVI: Sure. I was thinking of Bristol and Amsterdam.

TUTOR: Perfect choices. Send me your outline by Thursday.
