reading.title: Loyalty Cards and Customer Data
reading.passage:
Walk into almost any large supermarket and you will be offered a loyalty card. By scanning it at the till, shoppers collect points that can later be exchanged for discounts or free goods. At first glance, the deal seems simple: the customer saves a little money, and the shop encourages people to return. Yet behind this friendly arrangement lies a powerful business tool.

Every time a card is used, the store records exactly what was bought, when, and at which branch. Over months and years, this builds a detailed picture of each shopper's habits. A company can learn that a particular customer buys baby food, suggesting a young family, or that another regularly purchases healthy products. Armed with this knowledge, the shop can send personalised offers that are far more likely to succeed than general advertising.

The information is valuable in other ways too. By studying millions of purchases, a supermarket can decide which products to stock, when to lower prices, and how to arrange the shelves. It can even predict seasonal changes in demand and order supplies in advance. For the business, the modest cost of the discounts is repaid many times over.

Some shoppers, however, feel uneasy about how much is known about them. They worry that their data might be shared with other companies or used in ways they did not expect. In response, lawmakers in many countries now require firms to explain clearly what information they collect and to ask permission before sharing it. Whether customers read these explanations is another matter, but the rules at least give them a choice.

questions.q: Loyalty cards allow shoppers to collect points for discounts or free goods.
Supermarkets record what each card holder buys, including the time and branch.
Personalised offers are described as less successful than general advertising.
Most shoppers carefully read the explanations about data collection.
According to the passage, why is customer data useful to a supermarket?
options: It replaces the need for staff
It helps decide stock, prices, and shelf arrangement
It lowers the cost of loyalty cards

questions.q: What worries some shoppers about loyalty cards?
options: Their data might be shared or misused
The cards are easy to lose

questions.q: Over time, card use builds a detailed picture of each shopper's ________.
Lawmakers now require firms to ask ________ before sharing data.

listening.title: A Lecture on Customer Loyalty
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we'll look at why companies invest so heavily in customer loyalty. Research consistently shows that keeping an existing customer is far cheaper than finding a new one. In fact, one widely cited figure suggests it can cost five times as much to attract a new buyer. So loyalty is not just about being friendly; it's about saving money.

There are three main tools companies use. The first is the reward program, where points build toward a benefit. The second is personalised communication, such as an email on your birthday with a special offer. The third, and perhaps the most powerful, is excellent service, because a single bad experience can drive a customer away permanently.

Now, a word of caution. Loyalty programs can fail when they are too complicated. If customers cannot understand how to earn rewards, they simply give up. The best programs are clear and quick. For your assignment, choose one company and analyze which of these three tools it relies on most. The deadline is the fifteenth of March, and please keep it under two thousand words.

questions.q: According to the lecturer, why do companies value loyalty?
options: New customers are easy to find
Keeping a customer is cheaper than finding one

questions.q: Attracting a new buyer can cost about ________ times as much.
Which tool does the lecturer call perhaps the most powerful?
Loyalty programs can fail when they are too ________.
The assignment deadline is the ________ of March.
The assignment must be kept under ________ words.

speaking.q: Let's talk about saving money. Do you use any discount cards or apps when you shop? Do you think it is important to save money, or do you prefer to spend it? Why?

writing.q:
Many companies now collect large amounts of information about their customers' shopping habits. Some people think this is helpful, while others believe it is an invasion of privacy. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Return of the Local Market
reading.passage:
For much of the last century, large supermarkets seemed certain to replace the small local market. Their wide aisles, low prices, and huge range of goods drew shoppers away from the open stalls of town centres. In many places, the traditional market shrank or disappeared altogether. Yet in recent years, a surprising change has taken place: the local market is coming back.

Several factors lie behind this revival. One is a growing interest in fresh, locally produced food. Many shoppers now prefer fruit and vegetables grown nearby, partly for the taste and partly because shorter journeys mean less harm to the environment. Markets, where farmers often sell their own produce, are ideally suited to meet this demand. A second factor is the desire for a personal experience. At a market, customers can talk to the seller, ask about the goods, and feel part of a community, something a large store rarely offers.

Local councils have noticed these benefits and many now actively support markets. A lively market can bring visitors into a town centre, and those visitors often spend money in nearby shops and cafés afterwards. In this way, the market acts as a kind of magnet that helps the wider economy of the area.

Still, running a successful market is not easy. Stallholders must compete with the convenience of supermarkets, which open long hours and offer everything under one roof. Bad weather can keep customers away, and rising rents for stalls can discourage new traders. To survive, many markets now hold special events, such as food festivals or evening openings, to attract a steady flow of shoppers throughout the year.

questions.q: During the last century, supermarkets drew shoppers away from local markets.
Some shoppers prefer local produce partly because shorter journeys harm the environment less.
Local councils generally oppose the return of markets.
Markets selling local food charge lower prices than supermarkets.
Why does the passage compare a lively market to a magnet?
options: It brings visitors who spend in nearby shops

questions.q: What is one challenge faced by stallholders?
options: Competition with the convenience of supermarkets
An absence of visitors in summer

questions.q: At a market, customers can talk to the seller and feel part of a ________.
To survive, many markets now hold special ________ such as food festivals.

listening.title: Planning a Food Festival
listening.script:
MAYA: Right, Tom, we need to finalise the food festival for the local market. When should we hold it?

TOM: I think the last Saturday of September works best. The weather is usually still warm.

MAYA: Agreed. How many stalls are we planning?

TOM: We've had thirty applications, but the square only fits about twenty-five. So we'll have to turn a few away.

MAYA: That's a shame, but fair enough. What about entertainment?

TOM: I've booked a local band to play from noon until three. And there'll be a cooking demonstration on the small stage.

MAYA: Lovely. Now, the budget. The council gave us four thousand pounds. Most of that goes on tents and security.

TOM: Right. And we agreed entry will be free, so we won't earn ticket money.

MAYA: Exactly. We make our money from the stall fees instead. Oh, and could you arrange extra bins? Last year there wasn't enough room for the rubbish.

TOM: I'll order ten more. I'll email the supplier this afternoon.

reading.title: Why the Moon Has Phases
reading.passage:
Every month the Moon appears to change shape in the night sky. It can look like a thin curve, a half circle, or a bright full disc. These different shapes are called the phases of the Moon. Many people once believed that the Earth's shadow caused them, but this is not correct. The phases happen because of the way sunlight falls on the Moon as it travels around the Earth.

The Moon does not produce its own light. Instead, it reflects light from the Sun, just as a mirror reflects light in a room. At any moment, one half of the Moon is lit by the Sun and the other half is in darkness. As the Moon orbits the Earth, we see different amounts of the lit side. When the lit side faces away from us, we see almost nothing, and this is called a new moon. When the lit side faces us fully, we see a bright full moon.

A complete cycle of phases takes about twenty-nine and a half days. This period is slightly longer than the time the Moon needs to circle the Earth once, because the Earth itself is moving around the Sun during the same period. Ancient farmers used these regular changes to measure time and to decide when to plant crops, and many early calendars were built around the lunar cycle.

Today, scientists study the Moon for other reasons. Understanding its movement helps them predict tides, which rise and fall as the Moon's gravity pulls on the oceans. Although the Moon seems calm and unchanging, it plays a quiet but important role in life on Earth.

questions.q: The phases of the Moon are caused by the shadow of the Earth.
The Moon makes its own light.
A new moon occurs when the Sun-lit side of the Moon faces away from Earth.
The lunar cycle is exactly the same length as the time the Moon takes to circle the Earth.
Most countries today still use a calendar based only on the Moon.
Why is a full cycle of phases longer than one orbit of the Moon?
options: The Moon slows down at full moon
The Earth is moving around the Sun at the same time
Clouds hide the Moon for several days

questions.q: Scientists use the Moon's movement to predict ________, which rise and fall with its gravity.
A complete cycle of phases takes about ________ and a half days.

reading.title: The Race to Reach the Moon
reading.passage:
In the middle of the twentieth century, two powerful countries, the United States and the Soviet Union, competed to explore space. This competition became known as the Space Race. Each side wanted to show that its science and technology were the best in the world. For about twenty years, news of rockets and astronauts filled newspapers everywhere.

The Soviet Union took an early lead. In 1957 it launched Sputnik, the first artificial satellite to circle the Earth. A few years later, a Soviet pilot named Yuri Gagarin became the first human to travel into space. These achievements surprised many people in the United States and pushed its government to spend more money on its own space programme.

The Americans set themselves a clear goal: to land a person on the Moon before the end of the 1960s. Thousands of engineers and scientists worked for years to design a rocket powerful enough for the journey. In 1969 the mission finally succeeded, and two astronauts walked on the surface of the Moon while millions of people watched on television.

After the Moon landings, the urgency of the race faded. The two countries gradually began to work together rather than against each other. Today, astronauts from many nations live and work side by side on a space station that orbits the Earth. The fierce competition of the past has slowly turned into cooperation, though the desire to explore remains as strong as ever.

questions.q: The Space Race lasted for roughly twenty years.
The United States launched the first artificial satellite.
Yuri Gagarin was the first person to walk on the Moon.
Sputnik was destroyed shortly after its launch.
Why did the United States increase spending on its space programme?
options: It had run out of rockets
Soviet successes surprised and worried it

questions.q: What does the passage say happened after the Moon landings?
options: The two countries began to cooperate

questions.q: The Americans aimed to land a person on the Moon before the end of the ________.
Today astronauts from many nations live on a ________ that orbits the Earth.

reading.title: What Are Comets Made Of?
reading.passage:
Comets are among the most beautiful objects in the night sky. From time to time, one appears with a long, glowing tail that can stretch across the heavens for many nights. For most of human history, people did not understand what comets were, and some believed they were warnings of disaster. Today, scientists know that comets are simply lumps of ice, dust, and rock.

Most comets spend their lives far from the Sun, in the cold outer regions of the solar system. There they remain frozen and dark, too faint to see. Occasionally, the gravity of a passing star or planet pushes a comet onto a path that carries it towards the Sun. As the comet draws closer, the Sun's heat warms its surface and the ice begins to turn into gas.

This gas, together with tiny grains of dust, streams away from the comet and forms the bright tail. Interestingly, the tail always points away from the Sun, no matter which direction the comet is travelling. This happens because a steady flow of particles from the Sun pushes the gas and dust outward. The closer the comet comes to the Sun, the longer and brighter its tail grows.

After rounding the Sun, the comet travels back towards the cold outer reaches of the solar system. Many comets follow the same path again and again, returning after fixed periods of time. By studying these icy visitors, scientists can learn about the materials that were present when the planets first formed billions of years ago.

questions.q: In the past, some people thought comets were signs of disaster.
Comets are made mainly of metal.
A comet's tail always points away from the Sun.
Comets are the largest objects in the solar system.
Some comets return to the inner solar system at regular intervals.
What causes a comet's tail to form?
options: The Sun's heat turning ice into gas

questions.q: Why do scientists study comets?
options: To learn about early materials of the solar system
To measure the size of the Sun

questions.q: Comets are lumps of ice, dust, and ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Meteor Showers
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning, everyone. Today I want to talk briefly about meteor showers. Now, a meteor is simply a small piece of rock or dust that burns up as it enters our atmosphere. Most of these pieces are no larger than a grain of sand. When many of them appear together, we call it a meteor shower. These showers happen because the Earth passes through the trail of dust left behind by a comet. Because the Earth crosses the same trails each year, the major showers occur at predictable times. One of the most famous showers can be seen every August, when sky-watchers may count up to sixty meteors in a single hour. To enjoy a shower, you don't need a telescope at all. The best advice is to find a dark place, away from city lights, and let your eyes adjust for about twenty minutes. Then simply lie back and watch the sky. Patience is the key, as the meteors arrive in bursts rather than in a steady stream.

questions.q: What is a meteor, according to the lecturer?
options: A small piece of rock or dust burning up

reading.title: Living and Working on a Space Station
reading.passage:
High above the Earth, a large laboratory circles the planet roughly sixteen times every day. This is the International Space Station, often shortened to the ISS. It is home to a small crew of astronauts who live and work there for several months at a time. Life on the station is very different from life on the ground, mainly because almost everything floats.

Inside the station, there is no clear up or down. Astronauts must hold on to handrails to move from one part of the station to another, and they sleep inside attached bags so that they do not drift away while resting. Even simple tasks, such as eating or washing, require careful planning. Water does not pour but forms floating balls, so astronauts use sealed containers and special towels instead.

Much of the crew's time is spent on scientific experiments. Because the station is free from the pull of normal weight, researchers can study how plants grow, how liquids behave, and how the human body changes in space. These experiments may one day help people travel further, perhaps even to other planets. The astronauts also exercise for about two hours each day, because muscles and bones grow weaker without regular use.

Keeping the station running is hard work, and the crew must repair equipment and check supplies constantly. Yet many astronauts describe the experience as deeply rewarding. From the windows, they can watch the whole Earth turning slowly beneath them, a sight that very few human beings will ever see with their own eyes.

questions.q: The ISS circles the Earth about sixteen times a day.
Astronauts sleep in attached bags to avoid drifting away.
Water on the station pours normally into cups.
Astronauts exercise to stop their muscles and bones from weakening.
The crew is paid more than scientists who work on Earth.
Why can researchers study how liquids behave on the station?
options: It is free from normal weight

questions.q: What might the experiments help with in the future?
To move around, astronauts must hold on to ________.

listening.title: Interview with a Trainee Astronaut
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. I'm joined today by Maria, who is training to become an astronaut. Maria, how long has the training taken so far?
MARIA: Thanks for having me. I've been in training for about two years now.
HOST: Two years. And what part has been the most difficult?
MARIA: Honestly, the underwater practice. We train in a huge pool to copy the feeling of floating in space.
HOST: That sounds tough. How many hours a day do you spend studying?
MARIA: Usually around six hours of classroom study, plus physical training.
HOST: And do you have to learn another language?
MARIA: Yes, all of us learn Russian, because the crew comes from many countries.
HOST: Interesting. What do you look forward to the most?
MARIA: Seeing the Earth from above. Everyone says it changes how you think.
HOST: And when is your first flight planned?
MARIA: If everything goes well, I should fly in March next year.
HOST: Wonderful. We'll be cheering you on.

reading.title: Searching for Water on Mars
reading.passage:
For many years, scientists have wondered whether life could ever have existed on Mars. The planet is now cold and dry, with a thin atmosphere and a surface covered in red dust. Yet one of the most important questions in space science is whether liquid water once flowed across this barren world. Water is essential for all known forms of life, so finding signs of it would be a major discovery.

When robotic vehicles, known as rovers, were sent to explore the surface, they found surprising clues. In several places the rovers photographed dried-up channels that looked very much like the beds of ancient rivers. They also discovered certain minerals that, on Earth, usually form only in the presence of water. Together, this evidence strongly suggests that Mars was once a much wetter and warmer place than it is today.

Where this water went remains a puzzle. Some scientists believe that much of it escaped into space after the planet lost most of its atmosphere. Others argue that a large amount may still be locked away as ice beneath the surface or near the poles. Recent measurements have even hinted at salty water that could exist underground, although this idea is still debated.

The search continues with ever more advanced machines. Future missions may carry equipment able to drill deep into the ground, where any surviving water or traces of past life would most likely be found. If even the simplest living thing were discovered, it would change forever how we understand our place in the universe.

questions.q: Mars currently has a thick atmosphere.
Rovers photographed channels that resembled ancient riverbeds.
Scientists are certain about where all of Mars's water went.
The idea of salty underground water on Mars is still debated.
Humans will land on Mars within the next ten years.
What did the minerals found by rovers suggest?
options: The surface is made of metal

questions.q: What might future missions do to search for water or past life?
options: Build a base near the poles

questions.q: Robotic vehicles sent to explore Mars are known as ________.

listening.title: Talk on a Future Mars Mission
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thank you all for coming this evening. Tonight I'll outline our proposed mission to Mars, which we hope to launch in the year 2032. The journey itself is long; with current rockets it takes around seven months to reach the planet. Once there, the crew of four will stay for a full year before the planets line up correctly for the return trip. Now, one of our biggest challenges is supplies. We simply cannot carry enough water for such a long mission, so the spacecraft will carry a recycling system that cleans and reuses almost every drop. Food is another concern. Alongside packaged meals, the crew will grow fresh vegetables in a small greenhouse, which also helps to lift their mood. Radiation is perhaps the most serious danger, and the living quarters will be shielded with a thick protective layer. If all goes to plan, this mission will mark the first time human beings have set foot on another planet.

reading.title: How Honeybees Communicate
reading.passage:
Honeybees live in large groups called colonies, and the survival of a colony depends on the ability of its members to work together. One of the most remarkable features of honeybee life is the way in which bees share information about food. When a worker bee finds a good source of nectar, it must somehow tell the other bees where to go.

The Austrian scientist Karl von Frisch studied this problem in the early twentieth century. He noticed that a returning bee often performs a kind of movement on the surface of the honeycomb, which he called a dance. If the food is very close to the hive, the bee moves in small circles. This is known as the round dance, and it tells the other bees that food is nearby, although it does not give the exact direction.

When the food is further away, the bee performs a different movement called the waggle dance. During this dance, the bee walks in a straight line while shaking its body from side to side, then circles back to repeat the pattern. The direction of the straight run shows the direction of the food in relation to the sun, and the length of the run indicates roughly how far away it is.

Other bees crowd around the dancer and follow its movements closely. They also appear to detect smells carried on the dancer's body, which may help them recognise the type of flower they are looking for. In this way, a single bee can guide many others to a useful supply of food, saving the colony a great deal of time and energy.

questions.q: The survival of a honeybee colony depends on cooperation between its members.
Karl von Frisch was the first scientist to keep honeybees for honey production.
The round dance tells other bees the exact direction of the food.
During the waggle dance, the length of the straight run indicates
options: how far away the food is

questions.q: The direction of the waggle dance's straight run is given in relation to
When food is very close to the hive, the bee moves in small ________.

The dancing behaviour helps the colony save time and energy.

reading.title: The Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
Beavers are large rodents that live near rivers and lakes. They are well known for building dams across streams using branches, mud and stones. These dams slow the flow of water and create ponds, which the beavers use for shelter and protection from predators. For hundreds of years, beavers were hunted heavily for their fur, and in many parts of Europe they disappeared completely.

In recent decades, scientists have begun to understand how useful beavers can be for the natural world. The ponds they create provide homes for fish, frogs, insects and water birds. The slow-moving water also helps to trap mud and other materials, which improves the quality of the water further downstream.

Because of these benefits, several countries have started programmes to bring beavers back to rivers where they once lived. In Britain, small groups of beavers have been released into protected areas, and their progress is carefully monitored by researchers. Early results suggest that the animals settle quickly and begin building dams within a few months.

Not everyone welcomes the return of the beaver, however. Some farmers worry that the ponds may flood their fields or damage crops. To reduce these problems, experts sometimes fit pipes through the dams to control the level of the water. With careful management, supporters argue, beavers and people can share the same landscape without serious conflict.

questions.q: Beaver dams cause water to flow more quickly.

According to the passage, the ponds created by beavers
How do experts reduce the risk of flooding caused by beavers?
options: by fitting pipes through the dams

questions.q: Beavers build dams using branches, mud and ________.

listening.title: Reporting a Lost Item
listening.script:
STAFF: Good afternoon, this is the park information desk. How can I help?

VISITOR: Hi. I think I left my backpack somewhere in the park this morning, and I'm hoping someone handed it in.

STAFF: I can check for you. Can you describe it?

VISITOR: It's a dark green backpack with a small water bottle in the side pocket.

STAFF: Right. And where do you think you left it?

VISITOR: Near the lake, by the picnic tables. I was there at about eleven o'clock.

STAFF: Let me see. Yes, a green backpack was handed in around half past eleven. It's here at the desk now.

VISITOR: Oh, that's a relief. Thank you so much.

STAFF: No problem. I'll just need your name and a phone number for our records.

VISITOR: Sure. It's Daniel Foster, and my number is oh-four-double-two, three-one-seven, nine-eight-six.

STAFF: Got it. You can collect it whenever you're ready.

- what it was doing
and explain how you felt when you saw it.

writing.q:
Some people believe that wild animals should not be kept in zoos, while others think that zoos play an important role in protecting them. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Beavers are the second-largest rodent in the world.
The beaver built a dam across the narrow stream.
The pond protects the beaver from predators.
The animals use the pond as shelter.
Clean water flows downstream from the pond.
There are many benefits to having beavers in rivers.
Small groups of beavers were released into the wild.
Researchers carefully monitor the animals' progress.
The beavers settle quickly in their new home.
Careful planning can avoid conflict between farmers and beavers.
With careful management, problems can be reduced.
Beavers can change the shape of the whole landscape.
Slow water helps to trap mud and other materials.

reading.title: Why Leaves Change Colour
reading.passage:
In many parts of the world, the leaves of certain trees change colour in autumn before falling to the ground. For most of the year, leaves are green because they contain a substance called chlorophyll. Chlorophyll allows the tree to use sunlight to make food, a process known as photosynthesis. The green colour of chlorophyll is so strong that it hides the other colours present in the leaf.

As autumn approaches, the days become shorter and the temperature falls. These changes act as a signal to the tree. Slowly, the tree stops producing chlorophyll, and the green colour begins to fade. When this happens, other colours that were always present in the leaf become visible. Yellow and orange shades, for example, come from substances that help the leaf capture light.

The red and purple colours seen in some trees are produced in a different way. These colours are not present all summer; instead, they are made in the autumn from sugars trapped inside the leaf. Bright, sunny autumn days followed by cool nights tend to produce the strongest red colours.

Eventually, a thin layer of cells forms where the leaf joins the branch. This layer slowly cuts off the supply of water to the leaf, and the leaf falls. Although the tree loses its leaves, this is not a sign of damage. By dropping its leaves, the tree reduces water loss and is able to survive the cold winter months until spring returns.

questions.q: Chlorophyll gives leaves their green colour for most of the year.
Yellow and orange colours only appear in the leaf in autumn.
Red leaves are more attractive to insects than yellow leaves.
What signals the tree that autumn is coming?
The strongest red colours are produced by
The process by which a tree uses sunlight to make food is called ________.
Red and purple colours are made from ________ trapped inside the leaf.
Dropping its leaves helps the tree survive the winter.

listening.title: A Talk on Garden Birds
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to this short talk on attracting birds to your garden. Many people think you need a large garden, but in fact even a small balcony can help. The most important thing is to provide food and water all year round.

In winter, birds need food that is high in fat, such as seeds and nuts. You can hang feeders from a tree or a hook, but make sure they are out of reach of cats. A height of about two metres works well.

Water is just as important as food, especially in summer, when natural sources may dry up. A shallow dish of clean water will allow birds to drink and bathe. Remember to change the water every two days to keep it fresh.

Finally, try to plant native shrubs. These provide both shelter and natural food, and they attract insects, which many birds depend on. If you follow these simple steps, you may soon see a wider variety of birds visiting your garden.

questions.q: According to the speaker, what is the most important thing for attracting birds?
options: providing food and water all year

questions.q: In winter, birds need food that is high in ________.
Feeders should be hung at a height of about ________ metres.
The water should be changed every ________ days.
Why does the speaker recommend planting native shrubs?
options: they provide shelter and attract insects

questions.q: A shallow ________ of clean water lets birds drink and bathe.

speaking.q: Let's discuss the natural environment. Why do you think people feel calmer when they spend time in nature? Do you think modern lifestyles are taking people further away from the natural world?

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of land covered by forest in three countries in 2000 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Country | 2000 | 2020
Country A | 45% | 52%
Country B | 30% | 24%
Country C | 18% | 21%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Chlorophyll makes leaves appear green.
Leaves contain a substance called chlorophyll.
The green colour begins to fade in autumn.
The cold weather acts as a signal to the tree.
Other colours become visible as the green fades.
Some substances help the leaf capture light.

reading.title: The Coral Reef in Danger
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are among the most colourful and crowded habitats in the ocean. Although they cover only a tiny part of the sea floor, they are home to a huge number of fish and other sea creatures. A coral reef is built by millions of tiny animals called polyps, which produce hard outer cases. Over many years, these cases join together to form the large structures we call reefs.

Corals depend on a special relationship with tiny plants that live inside their bodies. These plants use sunlight to make food, and they share some of this food with the coral. In return, the coral gives the plants a safe place to live. The plants also give the coral its bright colours.

This relationship is delicate, however. If the water around the reef becomes too warm, the coral becomes stressed and forces the plants out. Without the plants, the coral loses its colour and turns white, a process known as bleaching. If the warm conditions continue for too long, the coral may die.

Scientists are worried because the world's oceans are slowly becoming warmer. In some areas, large sections of reef have already been damaged. Researchers are now testing several solutions, including growing young corals in protected tanks and moving them to damaged reefs. Some types of coral appear to cope better with heat than others, and these may prove especially valuable in the years ahead.

questions.q: Coral reefs cover a large part of the ocean floor.
The tiny plants inside corals provide them with food and colour.
Bleached coral recovers its colour within a few days.
What causes coral bleaching?
What are coral reefs built by?
The plants inside the coral use ________ to make food.
Researchers are growing young corals in protected ________.
Some types of coral are better able to cope with heat than others.

listening.title: Volunteering at the Aquarium
listening.script:
COORDINATOR: Thanks for coming in. So you'd like to volunteer here at the city aquarium?

VOLUNTEER: Yes, I love marine life and I'd like to help out at weekends.

COORDINATOR: Great. Most of our volunteers work in the visitor area, helping people find the different tanks and answering questions. Would that suit you?

VOLUNTEER: That sounds perfect. Do I need any experience?

COORDINATOR: No experience is needed. We give all new volunteers a half-day training session. The next one is on the fourteenth of March.

VOLUNTEER: I can make that. How many hours would I do each week?

COORDINATOR: We ask for a minimum of four hours a week. Most people do one shift on Saturday or Sunday.

VOLUNTEER: That works for me. Is there a uniform?

COORDINATOR: Yes, we'll give you a blue shirt to wear. You just need to bring comfortable shoes. Now, can you fill in this form with your contact details?

VOLUNTEER: Of course.

reading.title: The Long Journey of the Monarch Butterfly
reading.passage:
Each year, millions of monarch butterflies make one of the longest journeys in the insect world. These bright orange and black insects travel thousands of kilometres from the cooler regions of North America to warmer areas in the south, where they spend the winter. What makes this journey so surprising is that no single butterfly completes the whole trip in both directions.

The butterflies that fly south in autumn are not the same ones that flew north earlier in the year. In fact, the round trip takes several generations to complete. A butterfly that hatches in late summer is different from those born in spring; it lives much longer and is able to travel great distances before stopping to rest for the winter.

Scientists are still trying to understand exactly how the butterflies find their way. The insects appear to use the position of the sun as a kind of compass, and they may also be sensitive to the magnetic field of the Earth. Somehow, without ever having made the journey before, each new generation knows roughly which direction to take.

Unfortunately, monarch numbers have fallen sharply in recent years. One major reason is the loss of a particular plant called milkweed, which is the only plant that young monarchs can eat. As farmland has expanded, much of this plant has disappeared. Conservation groups are now encouraging people to grow milkweed in gardens and along roadsides, in the hope that the butterflies' remarkable journey will continue for generations to come.

questions.q: A single monarch butterfly completes the entire round trip.
Butterflies that hatch in late summer live longer than those born in spring.
Monarch butterflies travel faster than any other insect.
How do scientists think the butterflies find their direction?
options: by using the sun and possibly the Earth's magnetic field

questions.q: Why have monarch numbers fallen?
The only plant that young monarchs can eat is called ________.
The complete round trip takes several ________ to finish.
Conservation groups want people to grow milkweed in gardens.

listening.title: A Lecture on Animal Migration
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today I want to introduce the topic of animal migration, which is the regular movement of animals from one place to another. Animals migrate for several reasons, but the two most common are to find food and to reach a safe place to raise their young.

Many birds, for example, travel south in autumn when food in the north becomes scarce. Some, like the Arctic tern, travel astonishing distances. This small bird flies from the far north to the far south and back each year, a round trip of around seventy thousand kilometres.

Migration is not limited to birds, of course. Certain fish swim from the sea up into rivers in order to lay their eggs. Large land animals also move across plains in search of fresh grass after the rains.

Scientists track these journeys using small tags fixed to the animals. The information they collect helps us understand how animals navigate and why some routes are changing. In your reading this week, please focus on chapter four, which deals with navigation.

questions.q: What are the two most common reasons for migration?
options: finding food and a safe place to raise young

Why do certain fish swim up into rivers?

Describe an interesting fact about nature that you have learnt. You should say:
- what the fact is
- where or how you learnt it
- why you found it interesting
and explain whether you have shared this fact with other people.

Year | Number (millions)

reading.title: How Babies Learn to Talk
reading.passage:
Long before they speak their first word, babies are busy learning the sounds of language. Researchers now believe that this process begins even before birth. In the final weeks of pregnancy, a baby's hearing is developed enough to pick up the rhythm and melody of the mother's voice. After birth, newborns appear to prefer the language they heard most often in the womb.

During the first months of life, babies make a wide range of sounds. At around six months, they begin to babble, repeating simple syllables such as "ba-ba" or "ma-ma". Although these sounds do not yet carry meaning, they are an important step. Babbling allows infants to practise controlling their lips, tongue and breath, which they will later need for real speech.

Parents play a key role in this development. Many adults naturally speak to babies in a special way, using a higher pitch, simpler words and exaggerated tones. This style of speech, sometimes called "baby talk", helps infants notice the boundaries between words. Studies suggest that children whose parents talk to them often tend to develop larger vocabularies later.

Most children say their first recognisable word at around twelve months. By the age of two, a typical child can produce several hundred words and begins to join them into short phrases. Progress varies a great deal from child to child, and a slower start does not necessarily signal a problem. What matters most, experts say, is a rich environment full of conversation and warm responses.

questions.q: A baby's hearing is fully developed several months before birth.
Newborns tend to prefer the language they heard before they were born.
Babbling sounds such as "ba-ba" already carry clear meaning.
According to the passage, why is babbling important?
options: It shows the baby is unwell
It helps babies practise controlling the mouth and breath
It replaces the need for real words

questions.q: What does "baby talk" help infants to do?
options: Notice where words begin and end

questions.q: Most children say their first recognisable word at around ________ months.
By the age of two, a typical child can produce several ________ words.
A child who starts talking late always has a serious problem.

listening.title: Joining the Community Language Club
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, you've reached the Riverside Community Language Club. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to join the Spanish conversation group, please.
RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Could I take your name first?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Harper. That's H-A-R-P-E-R.
RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And which level would you say you are?
CALLER: I'd say beginner. I've only studied for about three months.
RECEPTIONIST: No problem. The beginner group meets on Thursdays at seven o'clock in the evening.
CALLER: Thursdays suit me well. How much does it cost?
RECEPTIONIST: It's twelve pounds per session, or you can pay sixty pounds for ten sessions.
CALLER: I'll take the ten-session option, thank you. Where exactly do you meet?
RECEPTIONIST: We're in Room 4 on the second floor of the library building.
CALLER: Perfect. Should I bring anything?
RECEPTIONIST: Just a notebook and a pen. We provide all the other materials.
CALLER: Great. I'll see you on Thursday.

reading.title: The Rise of Emoji
reading.passage:
Few forms of communication have spread as quickly as the emoji. These small digital images, ranging from smiling faces to animals and food, first appeared in Japan in the late 1990s. The word itself comes from two Japanese words meaning "picture" and "character". Today, billions of emoji are sent every day across the world.

At first, emoji were limited to a single mobile network in Japan. They became popular because they allowed people to add feeling to short text messages. A simple sentence can seem cold or even rude, but a smiling face at the end softens the tone. In this way, emoji do some of the work that facial expressions and gestures do in face-to-face conversation.

As smartphones spread around the globe, demand for a shared set of emoji grew. A non-profit organisation now decides which new symbols are added each year. Anyone can suggest an idea, but the process is careful. New emoji must be widely useful and should not favour one particular group or brand. Each year only a small number are approved.

Not everyone welcomes the trend. Some critics worry that relying on pictures may weaken people's writing skills. Others argue that emoji can be misunderstood, since the same symbol may carry different meanings in different cultures. Supporters reply that emoji are simply a new tool, not a replacement for words. Whatever the truth, these tiny images have clearly become part of how millions of people express themselves.

questions.q: Emoji first appeared in Japan in the late 1990s.
The word "emoji" is related to English words for emotion.
More emoji are sent in Japan than in any other country.
Why did emoji first become popular?
options: They added feeling to short messages
They were required by phone makers

questions.q: What is true of the process for approving new emoji?
options: It is careful and approves only a few

questions.q: Emoji do some of the work that facial expressions and ________ do in conversation.
Critics worry that relying on pictures may weaken people's ________ skills.
Supporters believe emoji should completely replace words.

listening.title: A Lecture on Texting Habits
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to talk briefly about how texting has changed the way young people write. First, let's look at speed. On average, a teenager sends around fifty text messages a day, far more than a phone call. Because they type so quickly, they tend to shorten words and drop punctuation. Now, some people see this as a decline in standards. But research at our university tells a more interesting story. We studied two hundred students over one year. We found that those who texted a great deal actually scored slightly higher on spelling tests. Why? Because playing with language, shortening it and changing it, makes people more aware of how words work. Of course, students still need to learn formal writing for essays and reports. The key skill, we believe, is knowing which style fits which situation. A text to a friend and a letter to an employer are simply not the same. So rather than banning informal language, schools should teach students to switch between styles with confidence.

questions.q: On average, a teenager sends around ________ text messages a day.
What did the university research find?
options: Heavy texters scored slightly higher on spelling

questions.q: The study followed ________ students over one year.
According to the lecturer, what is the key skill?
options: Knowing which style fits which situation

questions.q: Students still need to learn ________ writing for essays and reports.
What does the lecturer suggest schools should do?
options: Teach students to switch between styles

speaking.q:
Describe a message you received that made you very happy. You should say:
- who sent it to you
- when you received it
- what the message said
and explain why it made you so happy.

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Languages That Are Disappearing
reading.passage:
There are roughly seven thousand languages spoken in the world today, but many of them are in danger. Experts estimate that a language dies somewhere on Earth every few weeks. When the last fluent speaker passes away, a unique way of describing the world is lost forever.

Languages usually become endangered when younger people stop using them. This often happens because a larger, more powerful language offers better chances of work or education. Parents may decide to raise their children in this dominant language, believing it will help them succeed. Within a generation or two, the older language may survive only among the elderly.

The loss is more than sentimental. Each language carries detailed knowledge about local plants, animals and traditions. Some communities have words for medicines or weather patterns that have no exact translation. When a language disappears, this practical wisdom can vanish with it.

Fortunately, many people are working to reverse the trend. Communities are recording their elders, creating dictionaries and teaching children in schools. Modern technology has made this easier and cheaper than ever. Apps and online videos now allow learners to study rare languages from anywhere. While not every language can be saved, these efforts show that decline is not always inevitable. With enough commitment, some endangered languages are even beginning to grow again.

questions.q: About seven thousand languages are spoken in the world today.
A language dies somewhere in the world every few weeks.
Most endangered languages are spoken in Africa.
Why do languages often become endangered?
options: Older speakers refuse to teach them
They are too difficult to write

questions.q: According to the passage, why is language loss more than sentimental?
options: Practical knowledge can be lost with it

questions.q: Some communities have words for medicines or ________ patterns with no exact translation.
Communities are recording their elders and creating ________.

listening.title: Booking a Place on a Language Field Trip
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I'm interested in the field trip to record local dialects. Is there still space?
ORGANISER: Yes, there are a few places left. The trip runs from the ninth to the twelfth of July.
STUDENT: Great. Where exactly are we going?
ORGANISER: We'll be staying in a small town called Brookford, about two hours north of the city.
STUDENT: How will we travel there?
ORGANISER: By minibus. It leaves from the main campus gate at eight in the morning.
STUDENT: And what's the cost?
ORGANISER: The total fee is ninety-five dollars, which covers accommodation and meals.
STUDENT: Does that include the recording equipment?
ORGANISER: Yes, we provide microphones and recorders. You just bring warm clothes and good walking shoes.
STUDENT: Sounds good. Who should I speak to in the town?
ORGANISER: Our local contact is Mrs Patel. She'll introduce you to the speakers we interview.
STUDENT: Perfect. How do I confirm my place?
ORGANISER: Just send me an email by Friday with your student number.

reading.title: Why We Use Slang
reading.passage:
Every generation seems to invent its own words. Slang, the informal language used among friends, can puzzle older listeners and delight younger ones. Far from being lazy or careless, slang follows clear patterns and serves real purposes in social life.

One of the main reasons people use slang is to show that they belong to a group. When teenagers share words that adults do not understand, they create a sense of identity and closeness. The same is true of many professions. Doctors, gamers and musicians all have their own special terms, which mark them as members of a particular community.

Slang also changes very quickly. A word that sounds fresh and exciting one year may seem old-fashioned the next. This rapid change is part of the appeal. Using the very latest expression signals that a speaker is current and connected. Once a slang word becomes too popular, especially among older people, younger speakers often abandon it and invent something new.

Some teachers and parents worry that slang harms proper speech. However, linguists point out that using slang does not stop people from speaking formally when needed. Most speakers move easily between casual and serious styles, choosing the right one for each situation. Rather than damaging language, slang shows just how creative and flexible human communication can be.

questions.q: Slang follows clear patterns rather than being careless.
Only teenagers use slang.
Doctors use more slang than gamers do.
According to the passage, one main reason for using slang is to
options: show that you belong to a group

questions.q: What often happens when a slang word becomes too popular?
Using the very latest expression signals that a speaker is current and ________.
Linguists say slang shows how creative and ________ human communication can be.
Using slang prevents people from ever speaking formally.

listening.title: A Radio Talk on New Words
listening.script: PRESENTER: Welcome back. Today we're discussing how new words enter the dictionary. You might think dictionary editors simply invent words, but in fact the opposite is true. Editors are like detectives. They read newspapers, websites and social media, watching for words that appear again and again. A word must be used widely and for long enough before it is added. Usually editors wait at least five years to be sure a word is not just a passing fad. Last year, our national dictionary added around three hundred new entries. Many came from technology, but some came from cooking and sport. Interestingly, very old words sometimes return with brand-new meanings. The word "cloud", for example, once meant only the sky, but now also means online storage. So the dictionary is never finished. It changes constantly, reflecting the way real people actually speak and write. After the break, we'll hear from an editor about the strangest word she added this year.

questions.q: How does the presenter describe dictionary editors?
Editors usually wait at least ________ years before adding a word.
Last year the dictionary added around ________ new entries.
Where did many of the new words come from?
The word "cloud" now also means online ________.
What does the presenter say about the dictionary?

speaking.q: Let's talk about everyday conversation. Do you enjoy chatting with strangers? In what situations do you usually meet new people?

writing.q:
Some people think that schools should not allow students to use slang or informal language at all. Others believe informal language has its place even in education. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Teenagers often invent their own slang.
Slang is an informal way of speaking.
New slang can puzzle older listeners.
Shared words create a sense of identity.
Many professions have their own special terms.
Musicians use special terms among themselves.
The rapid change is part of slang's appeal.
Young speakers abandon words that become too common.
Linguists study how slang works.
People switch between casual and serious styles.
Human communication is highly flexible.
Editors check that a word is not just a fad.
The dictionary added three hundred new entries.
The dictionary reflects how people really speak.

reading.title: The Language of Bees
reading.passage:
Humans are not the only creatures that communicate in sophisticated ways. Honeybees, despite their tiny brains, share detailed information with one another using movement. This remarkable system was first studied closely in the twentieth century and continues to amaze scientists today.

When a worker bee finds a good source of food, it returns to the hive and performs what researchers call a "waggle dance". By moving in a figure-of-eight pattern and shaking its body, the bee tells the others where the food can be found. The angle of the dance indicates the direction relative to the sun, while the length of the waggling shows how far away the food is.

What makes this so impressive is its accuracy. Other bees can follow the instructions and fly directly to a flower patch several kilometres from the hive. The dance even adjusts for the movement of the sun across the sky during the day. In this sense, the humble bee performs a kind of calculation that would challenge many people.

Scientists value this research for more than its charm. Understanding how bees share information may help protect them at a time when many populations are falling. It also reminds us that complex communication is not unique to humans. From the songs of whales to the dances of bees, nature is full of messages, if only we learn how to read them.

questions.q: Honeybees communicate using movement despite having small brains.
The waggle dance was first studied in the nineteenth century.
Bees can communicate about water sources as well as food.
What does the angle of the waggle dance indicate?
options: The size of the food source
The direction relative to the sun

questions.q: What shows how far away the food is?
Bees can fly directly to a flower patch several ________ from the hive.

listening.title: A Student Talk on Animal Signals
listening.script: STUDENT: Hello everyone. For my project I looked at how animals send signals to each other. I'll give you three quick examples. First, vervet monkeys. They make different alarm calls for different dangers. One call means a snake, another means an eagle. When the others hear the eagle call, they look up and run for cover. Second, fireflies. They flash light in special patterns to attract a mate. Each species has its own pattern, a bit like a secret code. If you get the pattern wrong, no partner will respond. Third, and my favourite, elephants. They produce very low sounds that humans cannot hear. These deep calls can travel for several kilometres across the land, allowing distant herds to stay in contact. What I found most interesting is that all these signals are learned or shaped by the environment, not simply fixed at birth. So next time you walk through a forest, remember it is full of conversations we usually miss.

questions.q: What do vervet monkeys do with alarm calls?
options: Use one call for all dangers
Use different calls for different dangers

questions.q: When monkeys hear the ________ call, they look up and run for cover.
How do fireflies attract a mate?
Elephant calls can travel several ________ across the land.
What did the student find most interesting?
options: The signals are fixed at birth
The signals are learned or shaped by environment

questions.q: The speaker says a forest is full of ________ we usually miss.

and explain why you find this animal so interesting.

reading.title: The Return of Heritage Wheat
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, farmers in Europe and North America grew a small number of high-yielding wheat varieties. These modern types were bred to produce large quantities of grain and to stand up well to machine harvesting. However, in recent years a growing number of growers have begun to plant older, so-called heritage varieties that were common before the 1950s. Although these older wheats produce smaller harvests, supporters argue that they offer several advantages.

One reason for the renewed interest is flavour. Many bakers claim that bread made from heritage wheat has a richer, nuttier taste than bread made from modern flour. Restaurants and small bakeries are willing to pay higher prices for such grain, which makes it worthwhile for farmers to grow it despite the lower yields. A second reason concerns the environment. Heritage varieties often grow tall, and their long stems shade out weeds, so farmers can use fewer chemicals to control unwanted plants.

There are also possible benefits for health, although the evidence here is less certain. Some researchers suggest that older varieties may be easier for certain people to digest, but large studies have not yet confirmed this. What is clearer is that growing a wider range of crops protects against disease. When millions of fields contain the same plant, a single new pest can spread rapidly. A more varied mixture of crops acts as a kind of insurance for the food supply.

Despite these advantages, heritage wheat is unlikely to replace modern varieties on a large scale. The world still needs enormous quantities of cheap grain, and only high-yielding crops can meet that demand. Instead, most experts expect heritage wheat to remain a small but valuable part of farming, supplying speciality markets rather than feeding the world.

questions.q: Modern wheat varieties were developed partly to suit mechanical harvesting.
Heritage wheat varieties produce larger harvests than modern ones.
Restaurants pay less for heritage grain than for modern flour.
Scientists have proven that heritage wheat is healthier for everyone.
According to the passage, the tall stems of heritage wheat help farmers by
options: reducing the need for weed-control chemicals

questions.q: Why does growing a variety of crops protect the food supply?
options: It improves the flavour of bread.
A single pest cannot destroy everything at once.
It removes the need for modern wheat.

questions.q: Many bakers say bread from heritage wheat has a richer, ________ taste.

listening.title: Joining a Community Vegetable Box Scheme
listening.script:
ASSISTANT: Good morning, Greenfield Farm box scheme, how can I help?

CUSTOMER: Hi, I'd like to sign up for your weekly vegetable boxes.

ASSISTANT: Lovely. We have three sizes: the small box is six pounds, the medium is nine pounds, and the large is twelve pounds.

CUSTOMER: I think the medium one will be enough for two people.

ASSISTANT: Perfect choice. Boxes are delivered every Thursday. Could I take your name?

CUSTOMER: Yes, it's Helen Carter. That's C-A-R-T-E-R.

ASSISTANT: Thank you. And your delivery address?

CUSTOMER: Forty-two Maple Road.

ASSISTANT: Got it. One more thing, is there anything you don't eat? Some people ask us to leave out certain vegetables.

CUSTOMER: I'd rather not have any mushrooms, please.

ASSISTANT: No problem, I'll make a note of that. Your first box will arrive next Thursday, and payment is taken monthly by card.

CUSTOMER: Great, thank you so much.

reading.title: Vertical Farming in the City
reading.passage:
As cities continue to grow, feeding their populations becomes more difficult. Traditional farms lie far from urban centres, so fresh food must travel long distances before it reaches shops. One possible answer is vertical farming, in which crops are grown indoors on stacked shelves, often inside old warehouses or specially built towers.

Vertical farms have several attractions. Because the plants grow in carefully controlled rooms, farmers do not depend on the weather, and they can produce food all year round. The crops are also grown close to the people who eat them, which cuts the cost and pollution of transport. In addition, vertical farms use far less water than ordinary fields, because the water is recycled rather than lost into the ground.

There are drawbacks, however. The biggest is energy. Plants need light to grow, and indoor crops rely on electric lamps for many hours each day. Running these lamps is expensive, and unless the electricity comes from clean sources, the environmental benefit is reduced. For this reason, most vertical farms today grow only leafy greens and herbs, which are light, sell quickly, and do not need much energy. Crops such as wheat or potatoes are still far too costly to grow this way.

Supporters believe that vertical farming will become cheaper as technology improves, especially as the price of efficient lighting falls. Critics reply that the method will always suit only a narrow range of high-value crops. Whatever the outcome, vertical farms are already changing how some city dwellers think about where their salad comes from, and a few supermarkets now grow herbs in glass cabinets beside the shelves where they are sold.

questions.q: Vertical farms allow crops to be grown throughout the year.
Vertical farming uses more water than traditional field farming.
Most vertical farms currently grow wheat and potatoes.
Vertical farms create more jobs than traditional farms.
What is described as the main disadvantage of vertical farming?
options: the cost of energy for lighting

questions.q: Why do most vertical farms grow leafy greens and herbs?
options: They are heavy and store well.
They are light and need little energy.

questions.q: In vertical farms, crops are grown indoors on ________ shelves.

listening.title: A Tour of a City Vertical Farm
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to Skyrow Farms. Today I'll show you how we grow vegetables right here in the middle of the city. Let's start with some facts. This building has eight floors, and every floor is packed with growing shelves. We harvest a new crop of lettuce every twenty-five days, much faster than a normal farm.

VISITOR: That's amazing. What do you grow here?

GUIDE: Mostly leafy greens, like lettuce, spinach, and kale. We also grow basil and other herbs. We don't grow large crops such as potatoes because they would use too much electricity.

VISITOR: And how much water do you use?

GUIDE: That's our proudest point. We use ninety per cent less water than a traditional farm, because we recycle it. The water that the plants don't drink flows back into the system.

VISITOR: Where do you sell the vegetables?

GUIDE: Everything is sold within fifty kilometres of this building, usually on the same day it's picked. That keeps it really fresh. Now, please follow me to the second floor.

reading.title: Saving Seeds for the Future
reading.passage:
Deep inside a mountain on a cold island near the North Pole lies one of the most unusual buildings in the world. It is a seed vault, a giant store designed to protect samples of crop seeds from disaster. Governments and research centres from many countries send copies of their seeds to be kept there, safe from war, flood, and accident. The aim is to make sure that even if a crop disappears in one place, it can be grown again from the stored seeds.

The vault works rather like a bank. Each country still owns the seeds it sends, and only that country can ask for them back. The seeds are sealed in special foil packets and kept at a very low temperature, which allows many of them to survive for decades or even centuries. The cold rock around the vault helps keep the temperature steady, so the seeds remain safe even if the cooling machines fail for a time.

Why is such a place needed? Across the world, the number of crop varieties grown by farmers has fallen sharply over the past hundred years. As farmers switch to a few high-yielding types, many older varieties are lost forever. Each lost variety may have carried useful qualities, such as the ability to resist a disease or survive a drought. By storing seeds, scientists hope to keep these qualities available for the future, when conditions may change.

The vault has already proved its worth. After fighting damaged a seed store in the Middle East, researchers were able to withdraw copies of their lost seeds from the vault and start again. As the climate becomes less predictable, such reserves of crop diversity may become more valuable than ever.

questions.q: Countries that send seeds to the vault lose ownership of them.
The cold rock helps keep the temperature inside the vault steady.
The number of crop varieties grown by farmers has decreased over the last century.
The seed vault is the largest building of its kind in the world.
In what way does the vault work like a bank?
options: It charges money for storing seeds.
Each country can only withdraw its own seeds.
It lends seeds to any farmer.
It sells seeds to research centres.

questions.q: Why might older crop varieties be valuable?
options: They grow faster than modern types.
They may resist disease or survive drought.

questions.q: The seeds are sealed in special ________ packets.
After fighting damaged a seed store in the ________, researchers withdrew copies from the vault.

listening.title: A Lecture on Seed Banks
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we're looking at seed banks and why they matter. A seed bank is simply a place where seeds are stored for the long term. The world's largest holds more than one million different samples. Now, the key to storing seeds is temperature. Most seed banks keep their seeds at minus eighteen degrees, which slows down the natural ageing process.

At that temperature, seeds of many crops stay alive for a very long time. Wheat seeds, for example, can survive for roughly two hundred years. Of course, not all seeds last equally well. Some, like coffee, are much harder to store and die quite quickly.

Why does all this matter? The main reason is diversity. Imagine a new disease attacks the most common type of rice. If we have stored many other types, we can search for one that resists the disease. Without that variety, we'd have nothing to fall back on. So, before next week, please read chapter four, which covers how seeds are tested for quality. We'll discuss it in the seminar.

reading.title: The Honeybee and the Harvest
reading.passage:
Many of the foods we enjoy depend on a tiny worker: the honeybee. As bees move from flower to flower collecting nectar, they carry pollen with them. This pollen allows plants to produce fruit and seeds, a process known as pollination. Crops such as apples, almonds, and many berries rely heavily on bees, and without them these harvests would shrink dramatically.

In recent decades, beekeepers in several countries have reported worrying losses. During some winters, large numbers of bees have died or simply disappeared from their hives. Scientists have not found a single cause. Instead, they believe several problems act together. These include certain chemicals used on crops, the loss of wild flowers that provide food, and tiny mites that attack the bees and spread disease.

The consequences reach far beyond the beekeepers themselves. In regions where almond trees are grown on a huge scale, farmers must hire beekeepers to bring thousands of hives during the flowering season. If bee numbers fall, the cost of this service rises, and so does the price of the crop. Some farmers have even experimented with pollinating flowers by hand, but this is slow and extremely expensive compared with the free work that bees provide.

There is, however, reason for hope. Many farmers are now leaving strips of wild flowers along the edges of their fields to give bees more food. Others are reducing their use of harmful chemicals or applying them only when the bees are not active. Researchers are also breeding bees that can better resist the mites. While the problem is far from solved, these efforts show that the relationship between farmers and bees can be repaired with care and attention.

questions.q: Bees carry pollen between flowers as they collect nectar.
Scientists have found one single cause for the loss of bees.
Pollinating flowers by hand is cheaper than using bees.
Almond farmers earn more money than berry farmers.
Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a possible cause of bee losses?
What happens to the price of crops if bee numbers fall?
options: It is decided by the government.

questions.q: The process by which pollen allows plants to produce fruit is called ________.

listening.title: Volunteering with a Beekeeping Group
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Thanks for coming along to find out about our beekeeping group. Let me explain how it works. We meet every Saturday morning at the community garden on Bridge Lane. Sessions start at nine and finish around midday.

VOLUNTEER: Do I need to bring anything special?

ORGANISER: We provide the protective suits and gloves, so you don't need those. But please wear closed shoes, not sandals, for safety. Oh, and bring a bottle of water.

VOLUNTEER: How much does it cost to join?

ORGANISER: There's a small yearly fee of fifteen pounds, which helps us buy equipment. The first session is free, so you can try it before deciding.

VOLUNTEER: Will I get to take any honey home?

ORGANISER: Yes, members share the honey we collect in the autumn. Last year each person took home about three jars. Just remember, bees are calmest in warm, dry weather, so we cancel if it rains heavily. We'll text you if a session is called off.

reading.title: Reducing Food Waste on the Farm
reading.passage:
When people think about food waste, they usually picture leftovers thrown away at home. Yet a surprising amount of food is lost long before it reaches the kitchen. On farms around the world, large quantities of perfectly good fruit and vegetables are never harvested or sold. Understanding why this happens is the first step towards solving the problem.

One major reason is appearance. Supermarkets often demand produce of a particular size, shape, and colour. A carrot that is bent or an apple that is slightly marked may be rejected, even though it tastes exactly the same as a perfect one. Faced with such rules, farmers sometimes leave whole fields unpicked because the crop will not meet the buyer's standards. The cost of harvesting would simply be wasted.

Weather and timing also play a part. If prices fall suddenly, it may cost more to pick and transport a crop than the farmer would earn by selling it. In that case, the food is left to rot in the field. A late frost or an early heatwave can damage produce so that buyers refuse it. These losses are difficult to predict and hard to prevent.

Fortunately, attitudes are beginning to change. Some shops now sell so-called "wonky" fruit and vegetables at lower prices, and customers have responded with enthusiasm. Charities collect unsold produce and pass it to people in need, while other companies turn imperfect crops into soups, sauces, and juices. New phone apps even connect farmers directly with shoppers who are happy to buy surplus food cheaply. None of these solutions alone will end farm waste, but together they suggest that much of what is currently thrown away could be put to good use.

questions.q: Most food waste happens after food reaches the home.
Misshapen produce usually tastes worse than perfect produce.
Falling prices can make harvesting a crop unprofitable.
Most supermarkets now refuse to sell perfect-looking produce.
Why do farmers sometimes leave whole fields unpicked?
options: The crop will not meet the buyer's appearance standards.

questions.q: What do charities do with unsold produce?
options: pass it to people in need

questions.q: Some shops now sell so-called "________" fruit and vegetables at lower prices.

listening.title: A Radio Report on a Food-Rescue App
listening.script:
PRESENTER: On today's programme, we look at a new app called FreshLink that's helping to cut food waste. I spoke to its founder, Maria Lopez.

MARIA: The idea is simple. Farmers and shops list food they can't sell at the normal price, often because it looks unusual or is close to its sell-by date. Customers then buy it through the app at a big discount, sometimes half the usual price.

PRESENTER: How successful has it been?

MARIA: Better than we expected. We launched two years ago, and we now have over forty thousand users. Together they've rescued around three hundred tonnes of food.

PRESENTER: That's impressive. Is the app free?

MARIA: Yes, it's free to download. We take a small fee from each sale instead. Our busiest time is Friday evening, when shops clear out stock before the weekend.

PRESENTER: And what's next?

MARIA: We want to add a delivery option by next spring, so people don't even have to leave home. You can find FreshLink in any app store today.

reading.title: The Return of the Night Train
reading.passage:
For decades, the overnight sleeper train seemed to belong to the past. As budget airlines spread across Europe in the 1990s, many travellers chose cheap flights over slow rail journeys, and several night services were quietly withdrawn. By the early 2000s, a trip that once meant boarding a comfortable carriage in the evening and waking in another country had become difficult to arrange in many regions.

In recent years, however, the night train has begun to return. A growing number of passengers now worry about the environmental cost of flying, and trains produce far less carbon dioxide per traveller than aircraft. Some governments have encouraged this shift by funding new routes and reducing taxes on rail tickets. An Austrian operator, for example, expanded its sleeper network to connect cities such as Vienna, Hamburg and Brussels, and reported that demand often exceeded the number of available beds.

Supporters argue that night trains save time as well as emissions. A passenger who sleeps during the journey arrives rested and avoids paying for a hotel night. Critics point out that the trains remain relatively expensive to run, partly because each carriage carries fewer people than a daytime service. Maintaining old rolling stock is also costly, and building modern sleeping cars requires large investment.

Whether the revival will last is still uncertain. Much depends on whether operators can keep prices reasonable while covering their costs. Even so, the simple appeal of falling asleep in one city and stepping onto the platform of another has clearly not disappeared.

questions.q: Several night train services were stopped during the period when budget airlines were expanding.
Trains release more carbon dioxide per passenger than planes.

According to the passage, one reason night trains are costly to operate is that

questions.q: A benefit of night trains mentioned in the passage is that passengers
options: can avoid the cost of a hotel night

questions.q: Some governments have supported the revival by funding new routes and reducing ________ on rail tickets.

listening.script:
AGENT: Good morning, Brightway Travel, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book the two-day coastal coach tour.
AGENT: Lovely. The next departure is on the fourteenth of July. Coaches leave from the central bus station at half past seven in the morning.
CALLER: All right. How much is it per person?
AGENT: It's eighty-five pounds, and that includes one night in a guesthouse and breakfast.
CALLER: Does it include lunch?
AGENT: No, lunch isn't included, so you'll need to bring some money for that. Could I take your name?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
AGENT: Thank you. And how many seats would you like?
CALLER: Three, please. Myself and two friends.
AGENT: Perfect. One more thing, the coach has limited luggage space, so each passenger may bring one bag only.
CALLER: Understood. Can we pay on the day?
AGENT: You can pay a deposit of twenty pounds now and the rest on the morning of departure.
CALLER: Great, I'll do that.
