reading.title: Cities That Grew from Ports
reading.passage:
Many of the world's largest cities began as small harbours where ships could shelter and trade. A natural bay or a wide river mouth offered calm water, and merchants gathered there to exchange goods. Over time, warehouses, markets and homes were built nearby, and a town slowly took shape around the waterfront.

The location of a port strongly influenced how a city developed. Settlements with deep water close to the shore could welcome larger vessels, which carried more cargo and brought greater wealth. Towns on rivers that reached far inland became gateways for trade with the interior, sending imported goods upstream and collecting local products to ship abroad. In this way geography helped decide which harbours grew into major centres and which remained quiet fishing villages.

As trade increased, port cities often attracted people from many different regions. Sailors, traders and labourers settled permanently, bringing their languages, foods and customs. This mixing of cultures gave many port cities a lively, varied character that distinguished them from inland towns. Some became famous for districts where dozens of nationalities lived side by side.

In the twentieth century, however, the shipping industry changed dramatically. Goods were increasingly packed into standard steel containers, which were loaded by huge cranes rather than by hand. These container ports needed vast open space and deep channels, so operations frequently moved away from crowded historic docks to new sites outside the city. Old waterfronts, left empty, were often redeveloped into apartments, museums and parks.

Today many former dock areas are popular places to live and visit, yet they still carry traces of their working past. A restored crane or a converted warehouse reminds residents that the city owes its existence to the sea.

questions.q: Many large cities started as small harbours used for shelter and trade.
Ports with deep water near the shore could receive larger ships.
All fishing villages eventually grew into major trading centres.
Container ships are faster than the cargo ships used in the nineteenth century.
According to the passage, container ports were often built outside the city because
options: they needed large open space and deep channels

questions.q: The passage says port cities often had a varied character because
options: people from many regions settled there

questions.q: Towns on rivers reaching far inland became ________ for trade with the interior.
In the twentieth century, goods were increasingly packed into standard steel ________.

listening.title: Library Tour for New Students
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the university library. I'll give you a quick tour and tell you the most important things you need to know. The library is open from eight in the morning until eleven at night on weekdays. On weekends it closes earlier, at six o'clock. On the ground floor you'll find the help desk, where you can ask staff anything, and the returns machine. The first floor is the quiet study zone, so please switch your phones to silent there. The second floor has group study rooms, which you can book online for up to two hours. To borrow books, you simply scan your student card at the self-service machine near the entrance. You can keep most books for three weeks, but popular textbooks can only be borrowed for one week. If you return an item late, there's a fine of fifty cents per day. Finally, the printing room is on the second floor, and you'll need to add money to your account online before you can print. Any questions, just ask at the desk.

reading.title: Why People Leave the Countryside
reading.passage:
Across much of the world, people are moving from rural areas into towns and cities. This movement, known as urbanisation, has been one of the most powerful trends of the past century. In 1900 only a small share of the global population lived in cities; today more than half do, and the proportion continues to climb.

The reasons behind this shift are often economic. Farming employs fewer people than it once did, partly because machines now perform tasks that once required many hands. Young people in particular may struggle to find work in their home villages, while cities seem to offer factories, offices and shops in need of labour. The promise of higher wages, even if it is not always fulfilled, draws millions towards urban centres.

Services also play a part. Cities usually provide better hospitals, more varied schools and a wider range of entertainment than remote villages. A family hoping to give its children a good education may decide that moving is worthwhile. For some, the attraction is simply the excitement and freedom that a large city appears to promise.

Yet rapid urbanisation creates serious challenges. When people arrive faster than housing can be built, crowded informal settlements may spring up on the edges of cities, sometimes lacking clean water or proper sanitation. Traffic, pollution and the high cost of living can make urban life harder than newcomers expected. Meanwhile, the villages they leave behind may be left with an ageing population and empty fields.

Some governments now try to slow the flow by investing in rural roads, clinics and internet access, hoping that people will stay if opportunities improve at home. Whether such measures can truly compete with the pull of the city remains an open question.

questions.q: More than half of the world's population now lives in cities.
Farming today employs more people than it did in the past.
The higher wages that cities seem to offer are always achieved by those who move.
Most people who move to cities later return to their villages.
According to the passage, one problem caused by rapid urbanisation is
options: crowded informal settlements without proper sanitation

questions.q: Some governments hope to slow migration to cities by
options: improving rural roads, clinics and internet

questions.q: The movement of people from rural areas into cities is known as ________.
Villages that people leave may be left with an ________ population.

listening.title: A Talk on a Community Project
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming this evening. I want to tell you about our new community garden project, which is starting next month. The garden will be located on the empty lot behind the train station, and it covers about four hundred square meters. Our main goal is to give local families a place to grow their own vegetables. We're dividing the space into thirty small plots, and each household can rent one for just ten dollars a year. We already have a waiting list, so please sign up soon. Volunteers will run free workshops every Saturday morning, teaching things like planting, composting and watering. We'll also build a small shed to store tools, so members won't need to bring their own. One important rule: only natural fertilizers are allowed, because we want to keep the soil and water clean. Finally, we're looking for donations of seeds and gardening gloves. If you can help, please email the address on the flyer. Thank you.

reading.title: Mapping the Ocean Floor
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the bottom of the ocean was almost a complete mystery. Sailors could measure the depth of shallow water by lowering a weighted rope, but in the open sea this method was slow and unreliable. As a result, the vast plains, mountains and trenches that lie beneath the waves remained largely unknown long after the surface of the Earth had been mapped.

The situation began to change with the invention of sonar in the early twentieth century. A sonar device sends a sound pulse downward and measures how long the echo takes to return. Because sound travels at a known speed through water, the time of the echo reveals the depth. By moving a ship slowly back and forth and recording thousands of measurements, scientists could gradually build a picture of the seabed below.

These surveys produced surprising discoveries. Researchers found an enormous chain of underwater mountains running through the middle of the Atlantic, as well as deep trenches plunging far below the surrounding plains. Such findings helped support the theory that the Earth's outer shell is broken into moving plates, a idea that transformed the study of geology.

Modern technology has made mapping faster and far more detailed. Satellites can detect tiny bumps on the sea surface caused by the gravity of features below, while robotic vehicles dive into the depths to gather close-up images. Even so, much of the seafloor has still not been mapped in high resolution, and scientists estimate that large areas remain better known from space than from direct measurement.

Understanding the ocean floor matters for many reasons. Accurate maps help ships avoid hidden hazards, guide the laying of undersea cables, and improve predictions of how tsunamis will travel. They also remind us how much of our own planet is still waiting to be explored.

questions.q: Measuring deep ocean depth with a weighted rope was a quick and reliable method.
Sonar works by measuring how long a sound echo takes to return.
A chain of underwater mountains was discovered in the middle of the Atlantic.
Robotic vehicles can now map the entire seafloor within a single year.
According to the passage, satellites help map the ocean floor by
options: detecting tiny bumps on the sea surface caused by gravity

questions.q: The discovery of underwater mountains and trenches helped support the theory that
options: the Earth's outer shell is broken into moving plates

questions.q: Sonar can calculate depth because sound travels at a known ________ through water.
Accurate seafloor maps help ships avoid hidden ________.

listening.title: Geography Field Trip Briefing
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, a few details about Friday's field trip to the coast. We'll meet outside the science building at eight fifteen sharp, and the bus leaves at half past eight, so please don't be late. The journey takes about ninety minutes. When we arrive, we'll walk along the cliff path to study coastal erosion, so you must wear strong walking boots, not trainers. The weather may be windy, so bring a waterproof jacket as well. For lunch, there's no shop nearby, so pack your own food and at least one litre of water. Each of you will need a notebook and a pencil to record measurements; pens don't work well in the rain. We'll be collecting small rock samples, and the university provides the sample bags, so you don't need to buy any. The trip should finish by four o'clock, and we'll be back at the campus around half past five. Finally, remember to sign the safety form before Thursday, or you won't be allowed to come.

reading.title: The Long Journey of the Arctic Tern
reading.passage:
Each year a small seabird called the Arctic tern completes one of the most remarkable journeys in the natural world. Weighing little more than a hundred grams, it breeds in the far north during the brief Arctic summer and then flies south to spend a second summer near Antarctica. By chasing the sun in this way, the tern experiences more daylight in a year than almost any other animal on Earth.

The distances involved are extraordinary. Although the straight-line gap between the two regions is large enough, the birds do not fly directly. Instead they follow winding routes that take advantage of favourable winds and rich feeding grounds, so the total distance flown can be far greater than a simple line on a map would suggest. Over a lifetime of perhaps thirty years, a single tern may travel a distance equal to several trips to the Moon and back.

Scientists once knew little about these routes, because the birds are too small to carry heavy tracking equipment. The development of tiny, lightweight devices changed this. Attached to a bird's leg, such a device records light levels, allowing researchers to estimate its position from the timing of sunrise and sunset. When the tern is recaptured, the stored data can be downloaded and its path reconstructed.

These studies have revealed surprising patterns. Some terns appear to pause for several weeks in the middle of the ocean, resting and feeding before continuing south. Others take quite different routes on the return journey, looping across entire oceans to ride seasonal winds.

Understanding such migrations is more than a curiosity. Because terns depend on healthy seas at both ends of the planet and along the way, changes in their numbers can serve as an early warning that ocean ecosystems are under stress.

questions.q: The Arctic tern breeds in the far north during the Arctic summer.
Arctic terns fly in a straight line between the Arctic and the Antarctic.
Light-recording devices help researchers estimate a tern's position.
Arctic terns lay more eggs than most other seabirds.
According to the passage, the Arctic tern experiences more daylight than almost any other animal because it
options: lives only at the North Pole
chases the sun between two summers

questions.q: Scientists found it hard to track terns at first because the birds
options: are too small to carry heavy equipment

questions.q: A tracking device on the bird's leg records ________ levels to work out its position.
Changes in tern numbers can act as an early ________ that ocean ecosystems are under stress.

listening.title: Lecture on Bird Migration Research
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I'll outline how our team studies bird migration along the coast. Each autumn, thousands of shorebirds pass through our region on their way south, and we try to record as many as we can. We set up observation points at three main sites: the river mouth, the salt marsh, and the sandy bay. Volunteers count the birds at dawn, when they are most active. Last season we recorded over twelve thousand individual birds in just six weeks. We also fit some birds with small coloured bands on their legs, which lets us identify them again without recapture. If you ever spot a banded bird, please report the colours to our website. The biggest threat we've found is the loss of feeding areas, because mudflats are being drained for development. Our main recommendation to the council is to protect at least two of the three sites permanently. Next week's session will focus on how we analyse the data, so please bring your laptops.

questions.q: When do volunteers count the birds?
The team set up observation points at ________ main sites.
Last season they recorded over ________ thousand individual birds.
The counting took place over a period of ________ weeks.
What does the lecturer say is the biggest threat to the birds?
The team recommends protecting at least ________ of the three sites permanently.

speaking.q:
Describe an animal that travels a long distance, such as a migrating bird or fish. You should say:
- what the animal is
- where and when it travels
- why it makes the journey
and explain why you find this animal interesting.

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of migrating birds (in thousands) counted at three coastal sites over three years.

Site | 2021 | 2022 | 2023
River Mouth | 8 | 7 | 5
Salt Marsh | 5 | 6 | 9
Sandy Bay | 4 | 4 | 3

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The tern completes a remarkable journey.
Terns breed in the far north.
The distances involved are extraordinary.
The birds follow winding routes.
They take advantage of favourable winds.
Lightweight devices can be carried by small birds.
Researchers reconstruct the bird's path from data.

reading.title: The Slow Decline of the Evening Newspaper
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the evening newspaper was a fixture of daily life in many cities. Printed in the late morning and sold during the afternoon rush, it carried the day's latest sports results, stock prices and breaking stories that the morning edition had missed. Commuters bought a copy at the station and read it on the train home. At its peak in the 1960s, some large cities supported two or even three competing evening titles.

The arrival of television news in the 1970s began to weaken this market. Viewers could now watch reports of the day's events from their living rooms, often with film footage that no printed page could match. Advertisers, who had long funded newspapers, started to move some of their budgets to the new medium. Even so, many evening papers survived by focusing on local matters that national broadcasters ignored, such as council decisions, school events and regional sport.

The real blow came with the internet. By the early 2000s, readers could check scores and headlines on their phones at any hour, free of charge. The very advantage of the evening paper, its freshness, had vanished overnight. Printing and distribution remained expensive, while online advertising paid far less than the print version once had. One by one, famous titles either closed or merged with their morning rivals.

A handful of evening papers continue today, though most are now given away rather than sold. Their editors argue that a printed product still reaches readers who do not use the internet, and that a physical paper handed out at a station has a captive audience. Whether such reasoning can keep them alive for another generation remains far from certain.

questions.q: Evening newspapers were typically printed early in the morning.
Some large cities once had more than one evening newspaper.
Television news included pictures that newspapers could not provide.
The journalists who lost their jobs were later employed by television stations.
According to the passage, what made the internet especially damaging to evening papers?
options: It was more entertaining than print
It removed the paper's advantage of freshness
It was supported by the government

questions.q: How are most surviving evening papers distributed today?
At its peak, evening papers carried the latest sports results, stock prices and ________ stories.
Editors claim a printed paper still reaches readers who do not use the ________.

listening.title: Joining the University Radio Station
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to sign up to volunteer at the campus radio station. Where do I start?

STAFF: Welcome! First I'll take your details. Can I have your full name?

STUDENT: It's Daniel Croft. That's C-R-O-F-T.

STAFF: Lovely. And which course are you on?

STUDENT: Media Studies, second year.

STAFF: Great. We run training sessions every Tuesday at six in the evening, in Studio 2. The first session covers basic equipment and runs for about ninety minutes.

STUDENT: Do I need to bring anything?

STAFF: Just a notebook. We provide the headphones. There's a membership fee of fifteen pounds for the whole year, which covers your access card.

STUDENT: That's fine. What kind of shows can volunteers do?

STAFF: Most start by helping with the news desk, then move on to presenting music or interview shows. The most popular slot is the breakfast show, but newcomers usually begin with the late evening slot.

STUDENT: Sounds good. How do I book my first training?

STAFF: Just email the team at radio@campus.ac.uk and mention Tuesday. We'll confirm within two days.

Training sessions take place every ________ at six in the evening.
The first session lasts about ________ minutes.
The membership fee is ________ pounds for the year.
What must the student bring to the first session?
Which slot do newcomers usually begin with?

speaking.q: Let's talk about news. How do you usually get your news each day? Do you prefer reading the news, watching it, or listening to it, and why? Has the way you follow the news changed in recent years?

writing.q:
The table below shows how people in a town accessed daily news in 2010 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

Source of news (% of people) | 2010 | 2023
Printed newspaper | 42 | 11
Television | 35 | 28
Websites and apps | 18 | 54
Radio | 5 | 7

vocab.example: The morning coffee was a fixture of his daily routine.
Commuters filled the train every morning.
The evening edition carried the latest scores.
The report included dramatic footage of the storm.
Television became a powerful medium for advertising.
Distribution costs made the paper expensive to produce.
The two newspapers decided to merge to cut costs.
The paper merged with its old rival.
Commuters at a station are a captive audience for free papers.
Their reasoning was that print still reaches older readers.
The paper's main advantage vanished almost overnight.
Television news began to weaken the market for print.

Advertisers moved their budgets to television.

reading.title: How Search Engines Rank Web Pages
reading.passage:
When you type a question into a search engine, the results appear in less than a second, yet behind that speed lies a complicated process. Long before you search, the engine sends out automated programs, often called crawlers, that travel from link to link across the web. Each page they find is copied and stored in a vast collection known as an index. Without this index built in advance, no search could be fast enough to be useful.

The harder task is deciding which pages to show first. A single common word may appear on millions of pages, so the engine must judge which of them best answers the user's needs. Early search engines simply counted how often a word appeared on a page, but this method was easily tricked. Website owners learned to repeat words dozens of times in hidden text, pushing low quality pages to the top.

To solve this, engineers developed systems that look at links between pages. The basic idea is that a page receiving many links from other respected pages is probably valuable, rather like an academic paper that is widely cited. A link, in this view, acts as a kind of vote of confidence. Pages with many such votes are treated as more trustworthy and tend to rank higher.

Modern ranking is far more complex still. Engines now consider hundreds of signals, including how quickly a page loads, whether it works well on a phone, and how long visitors stay on it. They also try to understand the meaning behind a query rather than matching exact words. A search for the best way to remove a stain, for instance, may return advice that never uses the word remove at all.

This constant refinement creates a quiet contest. As engines improve their methods, some website owners try new tricks to climb the rankings. The engines respond by adjusting their rules, and the cycle begins again. For ordinary users, the result is usually a steadily improving service, though one whose inner workings remain largely hidden from view.

questions.q: Crawlers explore the web only after a user types a question.
Early search engines could be tricked by repeating words on a page.
A link from a respected page can act like a vote of confidence.
Modern engines employ more staff than they did in the past.
Why is an index built before any search takes place?
options: To make searches fast enough to be useful
To reduce the number of crawlers

questions.q: What does the passage compare a well-linked page to?
Automated programs that travel from link to link are often called ________.
Engines now consider hundreds of ________ when ranking pages.

listening.title: Booking a Library Study Room
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Good afternoon, City Library. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a group study room for next week.

LIBRARIAN: Sure. Which day were you thinking of?

CALLER: Thursday, if possible, in the afternoon.

LIBRARIAN: We have Room B free from two o'clock. How many people will there be?

CALLER: Six of us. Is that all right?

LIBRARIAN: Room B holds up to eight, so that's fine. The maximum booking is three hours.

CALLER: Perfect, we'll take the full three hours.

LIBRARIAN: Great. There's a projector in the room, but if you need a laptop you must bring your own. Can I take your library card number?

CALLER: It's seven, two, four, six, one.

LIBRARIAN: Thank you. One more thing, please arrive within fifteen minutes of your start time, or the room is released to others.

CALLER: Understood. Do I need to confirm anything by email?

LIBRARIAN: No, the booking is confirmed now. You'll get a reminder text on the morning of the day.

questions.q: The caller wants to book a room on ________ afternoon.
Room B is free from ________ o'clock.
The maximum booking length is ________ hours.
The library card number ends in the digit ________.
What must the group bring themselves if needed?
What happens if the group arrives more than fifteen minutes late?
options: The room is given to others

speaking.q:
Describe a website or app that you find very useful. You should say:
- what the website or app is
- how often you use it
- what you use it for
and explain why you find it so useful.

writing.q: Some people believe that search engines and the internet have made students lazy because answers are so easy to find. Others argue that easy access to information actually helps students learn more. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Automated programs scan the web every day.
The search engine stores pages in a huge index.

Trustworthy pages tend to rank higher.
Well-linked pages are treated as more trustworthy.
A widely cited paper is usually influential.
Constant refinement improves the service over time.
Loading speed is one signal used in ranking.

Ranking creates a quiet contest between owners and engines.
Engines adjust their rules to stop tricks.

reading.title: The Rise of the Podcast
reading.passage:
Twenty years ago, few people had heard the word podcast. The term, a blend of the words broadcast and a popular music player, described a simple idea: audio programmes that listeners could download and play whenever they chose. Unlike radio, which demanded that you tune in at a fixed time, a podcast waited patiently on your device until you were ready.

The early years were modest. Producing a podcast required some technical knowledge, and finding new shows was awkward, since there was no easy central place to browse them. Most early podcasters were enthusiasts speaking to small, loyal audiences about narrow hobbies. Few expected to earn money, and most did not.

Several developments changed this. Smartphones put a capable audio player in almost every pocket, and dedicated apps made discovering and subscribing to shows straightforward. At the same time, the cost of decent recording equipment fell sharply, so a person could produce professional sounding audio from a spare room. The barrier to entry, once fairly high, had dropped to almost nothing.

The content also grew more ambitious. A few carefully produced series, particularly in true crime and investigative journalism, attracted millions of listeners and proved that podcasts could tell long, complex stories with real skill. Advertisers took notice, and a new business model emerged in which hosts read out advertisements personally, lending them a friendly, trusted tone that traditional radio spots often lacked.

Today the medium is crowded. Hundreds of thousands of shows compete for attention, and standing out is harder than ever. Critics warn that too many podcasts chase the same popular topics, while genuinely original voices struggle to be heard. Yet for listeners, the abundance is mostly a gift. Whatever your interest, from medieval history to amateur astronomy, there is almost certainly a show devoted to it, ready to play the moment you press a button.

questions.q: The word podcast combines parts of two existing words.
In the early years, it was easy to find new podcasts in one central place.
Most early podcasters earned a good income from their shows.
True crime podcasts are more popular than investigative journalism podcasts.
What made producing a podcast much easier over time?
options: Cheaper recording equipment and better apps

questions.q: Why are host-read advertisements considered effective?
options: They are louder than radio adverts

questions.q: The cost of decent recording equipment fell ________.
Critics warn that too many podcasts chase the same ________ topics.

listening.title: A Tutor Explains a Group Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: Okay everyone, let me explain the group podcast project for this term. You'll work in teams of four to produce one short episode.

The episode should be between eight and ten minutes long, no more. Each team chooses a topic related to local history. You'll submit a written plan first, then record the audio.

The plan is due on the fourteenth of October, and it must include your chosen topic, a rough script, and who is responsible for each part. The final recording is due three weeks later, on the fourth of November.

Now, marks. The plan is worth twenty percent, the recording is worth sixty percent, and the last twenty percent is for a short reflection you write individually afterward.

For equipment, the media lab has microphones you can borrow, but you must book them at least two days ahead. Editing software is already installed on the lab computers, so you don't need your own.

One tip: keep your script simple and conversational. Don't try to cram in too many facts. A clear, well-paced story always scores better than a rushed one. Any questions, email me before Friday.

questions.q: Each team has ________ members.
The episode should be between eight and ________ minutes long.
The written plan is due on the ________ of October.
The recording is worth ________ percent of the mark.
How far in advance must microphones be booked?
What advice does the tutor give about the script?
options: Include as many facts as possible
Make it longer than ten minutes

speaking.q: Let's discuss audio media more broadly. Why do you think podcasts have become so popular in recent years? Do you think they will eventually replace traditional radio? What are the advantages and disadvantages of getting information through audio rather than text?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average weekly time (in hours) that people in three age groups spent on different audio media in one country in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

reading.title: Fact-Checking in the Digital Age
reading.passage:
False information has always existed, but the internet has given it remarkable speed. A misleading claim can now travel around the world in minutes, shared by thousands of people before anyone pauses to question it. In response, a growing number of organisations have devoted themselves to fact-checking, the careful work of confirming whether public statements are actually true.

The process is more demanding than it might appear. A fact-checker cannot simply declare a claim false; they must trace it back to its origin, examine the evidence, and consult reliable sources such as official records, scientists or eyewitnesses. A single article may take days to prepare, and checkers are careful to explain not only their conclusion but also the reasoning behind it, so that readers can judge for themselves.

One difficulty is that false claims are often more striking than the truth. A dramatic but invented story tends to spread faster than a dull but accurate correction. By the time a careful check appears, the original claim may already have reached millions, and many of those readers will never see the correction at all. Fact-checkers describe this as a race they are usually losing.

Technology offers some help. Software can now scan vast numbers of posts and flag statements that resemble claims already shown to be false, allowing human checkers to focus their attention. Yet machines still struggle with context, sarcasm and images that have been subtly altered. For the foreseeable future, careful human judgement remains essential.

Perhaps the most important defence, however, lies with ordinary readers. Experts argue that a healthy scepticism, a habit of pausing before sharing and of asking where a claim comes from, does more to limit false information than any single organisation can. Teaching these habits, especially to young people growing up online, may prove the most lasting solution of all.

questions.q: False information existed before the internet was invented.
A fact-checker should declare a claim false without examining its origin.
Dramatic false stories tend to spread faster than accurate corrections.
Most fact-checking organisations are funded by governments.
Why do fact-checkers explain their reasoning, not just their conclusion?
options: So readers can judge for themselves

questions.q: According to the passage, what do machines still struggle with?
Fact-checking is the careful work of confirming whether public statements are actually ________.
Experts say a healthy ________ does more than any single organisation can.

listening.title: A Talk on Spotting Fake News
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good morning. Today I want to give you four simple steps for checking whether a news story online is reliable.

First, look at the source. Is it a website you've heard of, or one with a strange address you can't recognise? A trustworthy story usually names its publisher clearly.

Second, check the date. People often share old stories as if they were new, especially during a crisis. Always look for when the article was actually published.

Third, read beyond the headline. Headlines are written to grab attention, and sometimes they exaggerate or even contradict the article underneath. Read at least the first few paragraphs before deciding.

Fourth, and most important, see whether other reliable outlets report the same thing. If a dramatic claim appears on only one obscure site, be very cautious.

Now, a practical tip. If an image looks suspicious, you can search for it online to see where else it has appeared. Often you'll discover it's an old photo from a completely different event.

Remember, the goal isn't to distrust everything. It's simply to pause for thirty seconds before you share. That small habit makes an enormous difference.

questions.q: The speaker gives ________ simple steps for checking a story.
The first step is to look at the ________.
People often share old stories during a ________.
What does the speaker say about headlines?
options: They sometimes exaggerate or contradict the article

questions.q: What can you do if an image looks suspicious?
options: Search for it online to find where else it appeared

questions.q: The speaker advises pausing for ________ seconds before sharing.

speaking.q: Let's talk about information and trust. When you read news online, how do you decide whether to believe it? Have you ever shared something and later found out it was not true? Do you think people in your country trust the news?

Fact-checkers confirm whether a statement is true.
They trace the claim back to its origin.
It is important to find a claim's origin.
Checkers may consult an eyewitness.

reading.title: The Return of the Public Library
reading.passage:
A decade ago, many commentators predicted the slow death of the public library. In an age when almost any text could be found online and bought with a single tap, the idea of a building full of borrowed books seemed quaintly old-fashioned. Funding was cut in many towns, and some branches closed their doors for good. Yet the obituaries proved premature. In a number of countries, library visits have actually risen in recent years, and the reasons are revealing.

Part of the explanation is that libraries quietly redefined themselves. Rather than relying on books alone, they became community spaces offering far more than reading material. A modern branch may host job-search workshops, language classes for newcomers, after-school clubs, and free internet for those who lack it at home. For many residents, especially in poorer areas, the library is one of the few warm, safe public places where no one is expected to spend money.

The digital shift, far from killing libraries, gave them a new role. As more of daily life moved online, applying for jobs, contacting government offices, even booking medical appointments, those without computers or skills risked being left behind. Libraries stepped in, lending devices, teaching basic skills and offering patient help. In doing so they became a bridge across the so-called digital divide.

Books have not disappeared, of course. Borrowing remains popular, and many libraries now lend electronic books and audiobooks alongside printed ones, reaching readers who would never visit in person. The collection has simply grown to include new formats rather than abandoning the old.

None of this guarantees the library's future. Budgets remain tight, and each service a branch offers must compete for limited funds. But the past decade has taught an unexpected lesson: an institution once thought to depend entirely on paper has survived precisely because it learned to offer so much more.

questions.q: Ten years ago, some people expected public libraries to decline.
Library visits have fallen in every country in recent years.
Modern libraries offer services beyond lending books.
Libraries now employ more staff than they did ten years ago.
How did the digital shift affect libraries, according to the passage?
options: It gave them a new role helping people online

questions.q: What lesson does the final paragraph draw?
options: Libraries should focus only on books
Paper books are no longer needed
The library survived by offering much more than paper
Budgets are no longer a problem

questions.q: The library is often one of the few warm, safe public places where no one is expected to ________ money.

listening.title: A Library's New Services
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome to Riverside Library. Let me walk you through the new services we launched this year.

First, on the ground floor, we've opened a digital skills centre. It's open every weekday from ten until four, and the sessions are completely free. You can learn anything from sending an email to filling in online forms.

Upstairs, we now lend tablets and laptops. You can borrow a device for up to two weeks, just like a book. There's no charge, but you'll need a valid library card and proof of address.

We've also expanded the children's section, with a story time every Saturday morning at eleven. Parents tell us it's become the highlight of their week.

For job seekers, we run a workshop on Wednesday afternoons. Our adviser helps with writing applications and preparing for interviews. Booking is recommended because spaces are limited to twelve people.

Finally, our e-book collection has grown to over forty thousand titles, all free to borrow with the library app. If you've never used it, staff at the front desk will happily show you how.

That's the tour. Please do ask if you have any questions.

reading.title: The Idea of Trial by Jury
reading.passage:
The jury system is one of the oldest features of many legal traditions. In a trial by jury, a group of ordinary citizens, rather than a single judge, decides whether a person accused of a crime is guilty. The basic idea is that the fate of an individual should rest in the hands of members of the wider community, not in the hands of the state alone. Supporters argue that this protects citizens against unfair use of power by governments.

The modern jury usually consists of twelve people, although the exact number varies from country to country. These people are chosen more or less at random from the general population. They listen to the evidence presented by both sides, follow instructions given by the judge about the relevant law, and then discuss the case in private. In most serious criminal trials, they must reach a unanimous decision, which means that every juror has to agree.

Critics of the system point out several weaknesses. Ordinary jurors may not fully understand complicated evidence, especially in cases involving science or finance. There is also a risk that jurors are influenced by emotion, by clever lawyers, or by what they have read in the news. Long trials can place a heavy burden on people who have jobs and families, and some are reluctant to serve at all.

Despite these concerns, the jury remains popular in many places. Reformers have suggested changes rather than removing it altogether. Some propose smaller juries, others suggest giving jurors written summaries of the evidence, and a few recommend special panels of experts for very technical cases. Whatever the outcome of these debates, the underlying principle that justice should involve ordinary people continues to attract strong support.

questions.q: In a trial by jury, a single judge decides whether the accused is guilty.
Every country uses exactly twelve people on a jury.
Jurors discuss the case in private before reaching a decision.
Most jurors say they enjoy the experience of serving.
According to the passage, what is the main argument in favour of the jury system?
options: It is cheaper than using judges
It protects citizens against unfair use of state power

questions.q: Which weakness of juries is mentioned in the passage?
options: Jurors are always paid too much

questions.q: In most serious criminal trials, jurors must reach a ________ decision.

listening.title: Signing Up for Jury Duty Information
listening.script:
CLERK: Good morning, Court Information Office. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I received a letter asking me to attend jury service, but I have a few questions.
CLERK: Of course. Could I take your reference number first?
CALLER: Yes, it's J-four-seven-two-six.
CLERK: Thank you. I can see your record. Your service is booked to start on the fourteenth of March.
CALLER: Right. And how long does it usually last?
CLERK: Most people serve for about two weeks, though it can be shorter.
CALLER: I see. Will I be paid?
CLERK: You won't receive a salary, but you can claim travel costs and a daily allowance of up to sixty-five pounds.
CALLER: That's helpful. Where exactly should I go?
CLERK: Please report to the main entrance on King Street by nine o'clock. Bring your letter and some photo identification.
CALLER: And what if I'm unwell on the day?
CLERK: Then call this number before eight thirty so we can rearrange your date.
CALLER: Perfect. Thank you very much.
CLERK: You're welcome. Goodbye.

reading.title: Whistleblowers and the Public Good
reading.passage:
A whistleblower is a person who reveals information about wrongdoing within an organisation, usually their own employer. The wrongdoing might involve fraud, danger to public health, or the breaking of laws. Whistleblowers often believe that the public has a right to know, even when sharing the information may damage their company or end their own career.

History contains many famous examples. Some employees have exposed factories that polluted rivers, while others have revealed that companies hid the dangers of their products. In several cases, the information they provided led to changes in the law and saved lives. For this reason, many people regard whistleblowers as brave individuals who place the public good above their own interests.

However, the position of a whistleblower is rarely easy. Those who speak out may lose their jobs, face legal action, or be treated as traitors by former colleagues. Some find it difficult to obtain new work because employers fear they will report problems again. The personal cost can therefore be very high, even when the person was clearly acting honestly.

To address this problem, several countries have passed laws designed to protect whistleblowers. These laws may make it illegal to dismiss someone simply for reporting wrongdoing, and they sometimes offer financial rewards. Critics argue that such protection is still too weak and that many whistleblowers receive little real support. Supporters reply that the laws send an important message: that honesty in the workplace should be encouraged, not punished. The debate over how best to protect these individuals continues in many parts of the world.

questions.q: A whistleblower usually reveals wrongdoing within their own organisation.
All whistleblowers are rewarded with large sums of money.
Some whistleblowers find it hard to get new jobs afterwards.
The first whistleblower laws were passed in the United States.
Why do many people admire whistleblowers?
options: They put the public good above their own interests

questions.q: What do critics say about whistleblower laws?
Whistleblowers may be treated as ________ by former colleagues.
Some laws make it illegal to ________ someone simply for reporting wrongdoing.

listening.title: A Talk on Reporting Workplace Problems
listening.script: SPEAKER: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to this short session on how to report problems at work. My name's Diane, and I work in the ethics office. Today I'll explain the basic steps, and there's a leaflet to take home as well. First, if you notice something that worries you, write down what happened, including the date and time. Clear notes make a report much stronger. Second, you don't have to go straight to senior management. You can speak to your line manager, or, if you prefer, contact our confidential helpline on extension three-one-zero. The helpline is open from eight in the morning until six in the evening. Third, please remember that all reports are treated seriously and, where possible, kept anonymous. We never reveal your name without your permission. Finally, after you make a report, you should receive a written reply within ten working days. If you don't hear back, please call us again. Honesty protects everyone, so don't be afraid to raise a concern. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions.

reading.title: The Purpose of Punishment
reading.passage:
When a person breaks the law, society usually responds with some form of punishment. Yet experts have long disagreed about why we punish people at all. Understanding these different aims helps us judge whether a particular penalty is fair and useful.

One traditional aim is retribution. According to this view, an offender deserves to suffer because they have done something wrong, and the punishment should match the seriousness of the crime. A second aim is deterrence. Here the idea is that the fear of punishment will stop both the offender and other people from committing crimes in the future. A third aim is the protection of the public: by keeping a dangerous person in prison, society is kept safe for a period of time.

A fourth and more modern aim is rehabilitation. Supporters of this approach argue that punishment should help offenders change their behaviour so that they can return to ordinary life. Programmes may include education, training, or treatment for problems such as addiction. Those who favour rehabilitation point out that simply locking people up often fails, because many return to crime after release.

In practice, most legal systems try to balance all of these aims at once, which is not easy. A harsh sentence may satisfy the desire for retribution but do little to change the offender. A gentle one may help the individual but anger victims who feel that justice has not been done. Sentencing therefore involves difficult choices, and judges must weigh competing goals whenever they decide a case. As public attitudes shift, the balance between these aims continues to change over time.

questions.q: Experts fully agree on why we punish people.
Retribution means the punishment should match the seriousness of the crime.
Rehabilitation is described as a more modern aim.
Most prisoners in the writer's country reoffend within a year.
What is the aim of deterrence?
options: To make offenders suffer for its own sake
To stop people committing crimes through fear of punishment
To educate the public about law

options: Many offenders return to crime after release
It is illegal in most countries

listening.title: A Seminar on Prison Reform
listening.script: TUTOR: Right, let's begin today's seminar on prison reform. I want to share a few facts before we discuss them. First, in our study, the average cost of keeping one person in prison for a year was thirty-eight thousand pounds. That's a very large figure when you consider the numbers involved. Second, we found that roughly forty per cent of released prisoners committed another crime within two years. Programmes that offered job training, however, lowered that figure noticeably. Third, prisoners who took part in education classes were far more likely to find work after release. In fact, employment after release was the single strongest factor in preventing further crime. Now, some critics argue that reform programmes are a waste of money. Our results suggest the opposite: spending on training and education actually saves money in the long run by reducing reoffending. Of course, these programmes need careful design. For your essay, please focus on one type of programme and evaluate the evidence. The deadline is the ninth of May. Any questions before we move on?

reading.title: Copyright in the Digital Age
reading.passage:
Copyright is a legal right that allows the creators of books, music, films, and other works to control how their creations are used. The basic aim is to reward creativity: if writers and artists can earn money from their work, they have a reason to keep producing it. Without such protection, others could simply copy and sell a work without paying anything to the person who made it.

For most of history, copying was slow and expensive. Producing a printed book required a printing press, and making copies of a film needed special equipment. The arrival of digital technology changed this completely. Today, a song or a film can be copied perfectly in seconds and shared with millions of people across the world at almost no cost. This has created enormous problems for the law.

Many people now download music, films, and books without paying. Some do so without realising that it is illegal, while others believe that information should be free for everyone. Creators and companies, however, argue that they lose huge amounts of money every year because of this behaviour. They claim that, in the long run, unpaid copying could discourage people from making new works at all.

Governments and businesses have tried various responses. Some have introduced tougher laws and heavy fines, although these are hard to enforce when so many people are involved. Others have focused on offering cheap and convenient legal services, hoping that people will pay a small fee rather than break the law. Many experts believe that this second approach is more promising, because it changes behaviour without trying to police every household. The challenge of protecting creativity in a connected world is far from solved.

questions.q: Copyright is intended to reward creativity.
Copying was always quick and cheap throughout history.
Some people download files without knowing it is illegal.
The writer states exactly how much money the film industry loses each year.
What happened when digital technology arrived?
options: Copies could be made perfectly and shared cheaply

questions.q: Which approach do many experts find more promising?
options: Offering cheap and convenient legal services

questions.q: Tougher laws are hard to ________ when so many people are involved.

listening.title: Library Talk on Using Online Sources
listening.script: LIBRARIAN: Hi everyone, thanks for coming to this short session about using online sources fairly. I'm Marcus from the library, and I'll keep it brief. First, a quick reminder: just because something is online does not mean it's free to copy. Most images, articles, and songs are protected by copyright. So, what can you do? Number one, always check the license. Some works are released under a Creative Commons license, which tells you exactly how you may use them. Number two, when you use someone else's words or ideas, you must give credit. In your essays, that means a proper citation including the author and the year. Number three, if you need an image, try our free media library; it has over five thousand pictures you can use legally. Number four, if you're ever unsure, just ask a librarian before you publish anything. Oh, and one more thing: our workshop on referencing software runs next Thursday at two o'clock in room twelve. Sign up at the front desk. Any questions, come and find me afterwards.

questions.q: What is the librarian's main point about online material?
options: Being online does not mean it is free to copy

reading.title: The Ethics of Self-Driving Cars
reading.passage:
Self-driving cars, also known as autonomous vehicles, are no longer just an idea from science fiction. Several companies are now testing cars that can steer, brake, and change lanes with little or no help from a human driver. Supporters believe these vehicles could make roads much safer, since most accidents are caused by human error. Yet the technology also raises difficult ethical and legal questions that society has only begun to consider.

The most famous of these is sometimes called the trolley problem. Imagine that a self-driving car suddenly faces a situation in which a crash cannot be avoided. Should the car protect its passengers at all costs, even if this means harming pedestrians? Or should it try to reduce the total number of injuries, even if that puts its own passengers at risk? Whatever choice the car makes will have been decided in advance by the people who wrote its software. In other words, engineers must make moral choices long before any accident happens.

There is also the question of responsibility. If a self-driving car causes an accident, who should be blamed? Possible answers include the owner, the company that built the car, and the programmers who designed the software. Current laws were written with human drivers in mind and do not deal clearly with these cases. Until the law is updated, victims may find it hard to obtain compensation.

Public trust is another challenge. Surveys suggest that many people remain nervous about giving up control to a machine, even one that may be statistically safer than a human. Building this trust will require not only better technology but also clear rules and honest communication. The arrival of autonomous vehicles shows how new technology can force society to rethink old ideas about responsibility, safety, and even right and wrong.

questions.q: Most road accidents are caused by human error, according to supporters.
The trolley problem involves a situation where a crash cannot be avoided.
Current laws clearly explain who is responsible when a self-driving car crashes.
Self-driving cars are already more common than ordinary cars in most cities.
According to the passage, when are the car's moral choices actually decided?
options: By the passenger during the crash
In advance, by the people who write the software
By the police after the accident

questions.q: What does the passage say about public trust?
options: Many people remain nervous about giving up control

questions.q: Self-driving cars are also known as ________ vehicles.
Until the law is updated, victims may find it hard to obtain ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Technology and the Law
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning. Today I want to look at how the law struggles to keep up with new technology, using self-driving cars as our example. Let me give you three points to remember. First, the technology is moving fast. In a recent trial, autonomous vehicles drove more than two million kilometres on public roads with very few problems. That figure surprised many people. Second, the law is moving slowly. Most road traffic laws were written decades ago, long before anyone imagined a car without a driver. As a result, courts often have no clear rules to follow. Third, and perhaps most importantly, public opinion matters. In one survey, only about thirty per cent of people said they would feel comfortable as a passenger in a fully self-driving car. That number must rise before these vehicles become common. For next week, I'd like you to read chapter seven and prepare a short summary. We'll also have a guest speaker, an engineer, on Friday the twenty-second. Please bring your questions. Right, let's take a short break.

questions.q: In the trial, vehicles drove more than ________ million kilometres.
What does the lecturer say about most road traffic laws?
Only about ________ per cent of people would feel comfortable as a passenger.
For next week, students should read chapter ________.
The guest speaker, an engineer, will come on Friday the ________.
Why does the lecturer say public opinion matters?
options: Comfort levels must rise before the cars become common

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology and responsibility. When a machine makes a harmful mistake, who do you think should be responsible? Do you believe new technology generally makes life safer or more dangerous? How should governments decide which new technologies to allow?

writing.q:
The table below shows the results of a survey in which people were asked how comfortable they would feel as passengers in a self-driving car, by age group. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Age group | Comfortable (%) | Unsure (%) | Not comfortable (%)
18-29 | 45% | 25% | 30%
30-49 | 33% | 27% | 40%
50-69 | 21% | 24% | 55%
70+ | 12% | 20% | 68%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Autonomous vehicles need no driver.

reading.title: The Return of the Windmill
reading.passage:
For centuries, windmills were a familiar sight across the flat landscapes of northern Europe. Farmers used them to grind grain and to pump water from low-lying fields. By the early twentieth century, however, cheap coal and the spread of the electricity grid made these machines seem old-fashioned, and thousands of them fell into ruin.

In recent decades, the wind has been put back to work, though in a very different form. Modern wind turbines are tall steel towers fitted with three long blades, and a single large turbine can produce enough electricity for hundreds of homes. Unlike the old windmills, which served a single farm, today's turbines feed power into the national network.

Wind energy has clear advantages. It does not burn fuel, so it releases no carbon dioxide while it operates, and the wind itself is free. Once a turbine has been built, the cost of running it is fairly low. For these reasons, many governments have encouraged companies to build large wind farms both on land and out at sea.

There are, however, problems that cannot be ignored. The wind does not blow steadily, so the amount of electricity a turbine produces varies from hour to hour. When demand is high but the air is still, other sources of power must fill the gap. Some people also object to the appearance of turbines, arguing that rows of tall machines spoil the look of the countryside. Engineers are now working on better batteries that could store electricity generated on windy days for use when the air is calm.

questions.q: Old windmills were typically used to supply a single farm rather than a wide area.
Most of Europe's traditional windmills are still in working condition today.
Modern wind turbines cost more to run each year than coal power stations.
According to the passage, why did windmills decline in the twentieth century?
options: Coal and electricity made them seem outdated

questions.q: What is given as a disadvantage of wind power?
options: Its output changes with the weather
It cannot be connected to the grid

questions.q: A single large turbine can supply electricity for ________ of homes.
Engineers are developing better ________ to store power for calm days.
Some people dislike the way wind turbines look in the landscape.

listening.title: Booking a Wind Farm Tour
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Greenhills Wind Farm Visitor Centre, how can I help?
VISITOR: Hello, I'd like to book a guided tour for my family, please.
STAFF: Of course. The standard tour lasts ninety minutes and costs twelve pounds per adult.
VISITOR: And for children?
STAFF: Children under sixteen are six pounds each. How many of you are coming?
VISITOR: Two adults and three children.
STAFF: Lovely. Which day would suit you?
VISITOR: Could we come on Saturday?
STAFF: Saturday tours start at half past ten in the morning. You'll need to arrive fifteen minutes early to collect your tickets.
VISITOR: That's fine. Is there anything we should bring?
STAFF: Yes, please wear sturdy walking boots, because part of the path is uneven. And do bring a waterproof jacket, as it can be windy at the top of the hill.
VISITOR: Of course. Can we see inside one of the turbines?
STAFF: You can stand at the base, but for safety reasons no one is allowed to climb the tower. Could I take a name for the booking?
VISITOR: Yes, it's Foster, F-O-S-T-E-R.
STAFF: Thank you, Mr Foster. You're all booked in.

reading.title: Power from the Sun
reading.passage:
Sunlight is by far the largest source of energy reaching the Earth. In a single hour, the planet receives more energy from the sun than the whole of humanity uses in a year. For most of history, people had no way to capture this energy directly, but the invention of the solar cell changed that.

A solar cell, or photovoltaic cell, is a thin piece of treated material that produces a small electric current when light falls on it. On its own, one cell generates very little power. When many cells are joined together into a panel, however, and many panels are placed on a roof or in a field, the amount of electricity produced becomes useful.

The appeal of solar power is easy to understand. The fuel, sunlight, costs nothing and will not run out for billions of years. Panels have no moving parts, so they rarely break down and need little maintenance. As factories have learned to make panels more cheaply, the price of solar electricity has fallen sharply, and in many sunny regions it is now among the cheapest forms of power available.

The technology is not perfect. Panels produce nothing at night and far less on cloudy days, so a steady supply still depends on batteries or on other power stations. Making the panels also requires energy and certain rare materials, and old panels must eventually be dealt with carefully. Even so, the spread of solar power has been remarkably rapid. In countries with strong sunshine, rows of panels now cover hillsides and rooftops that were bare only a decade ago, quietly turning light into electricity.

questions.q: The Earth receives more energy from the sun in an hour than people use in a year.
A single solar cell can power an average house.
Solar panels last longer than wind turbines.
Why do solar panels need little maintenance?
options: They are made of rare materials

questions.q: What has caused the price of solar electricity to fall?
A solar cell is also known as a ________ cell.
Panels produce nothing at ________ and less on cloudy days.
Old solar panels can simply be thrown away without concern.

listening.title: A College Solar Project Talk
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to this short talk about our college's new solar project. Last summer we installed solar panels on the roof of the library, and I'd like to share a few results with you. In total we fitted two hundred panels, which together cover an area about the size of a tennis court. Since they were switched on in March, the panels have supplied roughly forty per cent of the library's electricity. On bright days that figure can rise to seventy per cent, while on dull, rainy days it drops well below twenty. The savings have been better than we expected. In the first year alone, the college reduced its electricity bill by around eight thousand dollars. The panels themselves cost sixty thousand dollars to install, so we expect them to pay for themselves within about eight years. We're now considering a second set of panels on the sports hall, and any student interested in helping with the project should email the office before the end of the month. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions.

The panels cover an area about the size of a what?

In the first year the electricity bill fell by about ________ dollars.
The panels are expected to pay for themselves within about ________ years.

reading.title: Energy from the Earth's Heat
reading.passage:
Deep beneath our feet, the Earth is surprisingly hot. The temperature rises steadily the further down one goes, and at the planet's centre it is hotter than the surface of the sun. In some places, this heat comes close enough to the surface to be useful, and people have learned to turn it into a source of energy known as geothermal power.

The idea is not new. For thousands of years, people have bathed in natural hot springs, where water heated underground rises to the surface. In countries such as Iceland, where volcanic activity keeps the ground especially warm, hot water is piped directly into homes to provide heating. This is a simple and very efficient use of the Earth's heat.

Producing electricity from geothermal energy is more complicated. Engineers drill deep wells to reach hot rock, then bring up steam or hot water to drive turbines. Because the heat below ground is always present, a geothermal power station can run day and night, in any weather. This makes it more reliable than wind or solar power, which depend on conditions above ground.

Geothermal energy does have limits. It works best in regions with the right geology, so it cannot be used everywhere. Drilling deep wells is expensive, and in rare cases pumping water underground has been linked to small earthquakes. Despite these drawbacks, interest in the technology is growing. As drilling methods improve, engineers hope to reach hot rock in more places, allowing more countries to draw steady, clean power from the heat that lies beneath them.

questions.q: The centre of the Earth is hotter than the sun's surface.
Geothermal power can be used equally well in every country.
Iceland exports geothermal electricity to other countries.
Why is a geothermal power station considered reliable?
options: The underground heat is always available

questions.q: What is mentioned as a possible risk of geothermal energy?
In Iceland, hot water is piped directly into ________ for heating.
Engineers drill deep ________ to reach hot rock.
People have used natural hot springs for bathing for thousands of years.

listening.title: A Visit to a Geothermal Plant
listening.script: GUIDE: Right, everyone, gather round and I'll explain how this geothermal plant works. The plant has been running since nineteen ninety-eight, so it's been supplying power for over twenty years now. Beneath us, we've drilled wells that reach down nearly two kilometres into the hot rock. At that depth, the temperature is about two hundred degrees, hot enough to turn water into steam. That steam rushes up the well and spins the turbines you can hear behind me. The plant produces enough electricity for around forty thousand homes, and because the heat never stops, we run all year without a break. One thing visitors often ask about is the smell. You may have noticed a faint smell of eggs in the air; that comes from a natural gas that rises with the steam, and it's quite harmless in these small amounts. We also use the leftover warm water to heat a public swimming pool in the nearby town. Now, if you'll follow me, we'll take a look at the control room, but please keep behind the yellow line at all times.

questions.q: The plant has been running since ________.
The wells reach down nearly ________ kilometres.
What is the temperature at the bottom of the wells?
The plant produces electricity for around ________ homes.
What causes the smell of eggs?
options: A natural gas in the steam

questions.q: Visitors are told to keep behind the ________ line.

speaking.q: Let's discuss energy on a larger scale. Why do you think some countries are slow to develop new sources of clean energy? What could persuade governments to invest more in renewable power?

The table below shows where four countries got their electricity from in 2020 (percentage by source).

Source | Iceland | Norway | Italy | Poland
Geothermal | 30% | 2% | 6% | 0%
Hydropower | 70% | 88% | 16% | 1%
Fossil fuels | 0% | 5% | 60% | 80%
Other renewables | 0% | 5% | 18% | 19%

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Geothermal power uses heat from underground.
The temperature rises steadily as you go deeper.

reading.title: Cities That Save Energy
reading.passage:
More than half of the world's people now live in cities, and that share is still rising. Cities use a large part of the energy produced worldwide, mostly for lighting, heating, transport and the running of countless buildings. Because so much energy passes through them, even small improvements in how cities are designed can lead to large savings.

Buildings are a good place to start. Older buildings often leak heat through thin walls and single windows, forcing people to burn more fuel to stay warm. Newer designs use thick insulation, double glazing and clever shapes that make the most of natural light and warmth from the sun. A well-designed office can use a fraction of the energy of an old one while remaining just as comfortable.

Transport is another major factor. When homes, shops and workplaces are spread far apart, people must travel long distances, usually by car. Planners now argue for denser, more mixed neighbourhoods, where daily needs are within walking distance and good public transport links the rest. Such designs cut both fuel use and air pollution, and they tend to make streets livelier and safer.

None of this is simple. Rebuilding a city takes decades, and people do not always welcome change to familiar places. Improvements also cost money, and the benefits may take years to appear. Yet the pressure to act is growing. As energy becomes more expensive and concern about pollution deepens, more city leaders are treating energy-saving design not as a luxury but as a basic part of planning for the future.

questions.q: Most of the world's population now lives in cities.
Older buildings generally keep heat in better than newer ones.
Most new office buildings in the world now use double glazing.
What do planners suggest to reduce transport energy use?
options: Spreading homes and shops farther apart

questions.q: According to the passage, why is improving cities difficult?
Newer buildings use thick ________ to keep heat in.
Denser neighbourhoods cut fuel use and air ________.
Energy-saving design is increasingly seen as a basic part of planning.

listening.title: Radio Feature on a Green Neighbourhood
listening.script:
PRESENTER: This week on City Futures, we're visiting Maple Court, a neighborhood that was rebuilt with energy in mind. I'm joined by its designer, Dana Lewis. Dana, what makes Maple Court different?
DANA: Well, the first thing people notice is that almost everything is close by. A resident can reach the shops, the school and the clinic on foot in under ten minutes. That means far fewer car trips.
PRESENTER: And the buildings themselves?
DANA: Every home here has thick insulation and large south-facing windows, so they stay warm with very little heating. On average, residents use about half the energy of a typical household in this city.
PRESENTER: That's impressive. Was it expensive to build?
DANA: It cost around fifteen per cent more than an ordinary development. But families save so much on bills that most recover the difference within seven years.
PRESENTER: And how have people responded?
DANA: Very positively. We surveyed residents last spring, and nine out of ten said they would recommend living here to a friend.
PRESENTER: Wonderful. Dana Lewis, thank you for joining us.

questions.q: What do people first notice about Maple Court?
Residents can reach key places on foot in under ________ minutes.
Which feature helps the homes stay warm?
Residents use about ________ the energy of a typical household.
Building Maple Court cost around ________ per cent more than usual.
What did the resident survey find?
options: Nine out of ten would recommend it

speaking.q: Let's talk about getting around your area. How do you usually travel to work or school? Would you prefer to walk or cycle more if it were easier where you live?

writing.q: In many cities, the use of private cars is one of the main causes of pollution and high energy use. Some people think governments should make it more difficult and expensive to own and drive a car in city centres. Do you agree or disagree? Give reasons for your answer and include relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The share of people living in cities is rising.
Old buildings leak heat through thin walls.
Thick insulation keeps the heat inside.

A new office uses a fraction of the energy.

Such designs make streets livelier.
