reading.title: The Problem of Storing Power
reading.passage:
One of the greatest challenges facing clean energy is not how to produce it but how to store it. Wind and solar power are now cheap to generate, yet they are not produced on demand. The wind blows when it chooses, and the sun shines only by day. Electricity, however, must be supplied at the exact moment people need it. Bridging this gap is the job of energy storage.

The most familiar solution is the battery. Modern batteries can absorb electricity when supply is plentiful and release it later when demand rises. They respond in an instant, which helps keep the grid steady. Their weakness is that they remain costly and can hold only a limited amount of power before they must be recharged, so they are best suited to handling short gaps of a few hours.

For larger amounts of energy, engineers turn to other methods. One long-established technique is to use surplus electricity to pump water uphill into a reservoir. When power is needed, the water is allowed to flow back down through turbines. Other ideas under trial include spinning heavy wheels, compressing air in underground caverns, and even using spare electricity to produce hydrogen gas, which can be stored and burned later.

No single method is likely to solve the problem alone. Each has its own balance of cost, speed and capacity, and the best choice depends on local conditions. What seems clear is that as more power comes from the wind and the sun, the ability to store energy will become just as important as the ability to generate it. Without good storage, even the cleanest power may go to waste.

questions.q: Wind and solar power can be produced exactly when they are needed.
Batteries can respond quickly to changes in demand.
Hydrogen is currently the cheapest way to store large amounts of energy.
What is described as the main weakness of batteries?
options: They are costly and hold limited power

questions.q: How does the pumped-water method store energy?
options: By moving water uphill and later releasing it

questions.q: Batteries are best suited to handling short gaps of a few ________.
Spare electricity can be used to produce ________ gas for later use.
The writer believes one single storage method will solve the whole problem.

listening.title: Lecture: Keeping the Lights On
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to look at why storing electricity matters so much in a clean-energy system. Imagine a sunny, windy afternoon. At such times, our turbines and panels may produce far more power than the country actually needs. Without storage, that extra electricity is simply lost. Now picture a still, dark evening, when demand for heating and lighting is at its highest but the wind has dropped. Suddenly there is too little power. Storage is what allows us to move energy from the first moment to the second. The oldest large-scale method is pumped hydro, which still provides over ninety per cent of the world's stored electricity. Batteries are growing fast, but at present they cover only short periods, typically up to about four hours. For longer storage, lasting days or even weeks, researchers are pinning their hopes on hydrogen. The key point I want you to remember is this: a clean grid needs not one storage method but several, each doing a different job. We'll examine the costs of each in next week's session.

questions.q: What happens to extra electricity on a sunny, windy day without storage?
Pumped hydro provides over ________ per cent of the world's stored electricity.
Batteries typically cover periods of up to about ________ hours.
For storage lasting days or weeks, researchers are hopeful about what?
Demand for heating and lighting is highest on a still, ________ evening.
What is the lecturer's key point?

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you had to do without electricity for a while. You should say:
- when and where it happened
- why there was no electricity
- what you did during that time
and explain how the experience made you feel.

writing.q:
The table below shows the share of the world's stored electricity provided by four storage methods in 2015 and 2025.

Method | 2015 | 2025
Pumped hydro | 96% | 78%
Batteries | 2% | 18%
Compressed air | 1% | 2%
Hydrogen | 1% | 2%

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Solar power is not produced on demand.
Storage helps bridge the gap between supply and demand.
Batteries absorb electricity when supply is plentiful.
They store power when supply is plentiful.
Batteries respond in an instant.
Batteries must be recharged once empty.
Surplus electricity is used to pump water uphill.
Water is pumped into a high reservoir.
Some plans compress air in underground caverns.
Air can be stored in underground caverns.
Each method differs in cost, speed and capacity.
Without storage, clean power may go to waste.
Pumped hydro is a long-established technique.
Researchers pin their hopes on hydrogen.

reading.title: The Discovery of Pompeii
reading.passage:
In AD 79, the Roman town of Pompeii, near the Bay of Naples in southern Italy, was buried under several metres of volcanic ash when Mount Vesuvius erupted. For nearly seventeen centuries the town lay hidden and largely forgotten. Although a few earlier workers had stumbled upon ancient walls, the first organised excavations did not begin until 1748, when engineers digging on behalf of the King of Naples uncovered painted rooms and household objects.

What made Pompeii so remarkable to early archaeologists was not gold or treasure but the ordinary details of daily life. The ash had sealed the town quickly, preserving loaves of bread in ovens, jars of fruit, and the layout of shops and kitchens. Streets still showed the ruts cut by cart wheels, and walls carried painted advertisements and casual graffiti. For the first time, scholars could study how Romans actually lived rather than relying only on the writings of poets and historians.

One of the most moving discoveries came later, in the nineteenth century. Giuseppe Fiorelli, who directed the site from 1863, noticed that the bodies of victims had decayed inside the hardened ash, leaving hollow spaces. By pouring liquid plaster into these cavities and allowing it to set, he produced detailed casts of people in their final moments. These figures revealed clothing, facial expressions and even the positions in which individuals had tried to shield one another.

Modern archaeologists work far more cautiously than their predecessors. Early excavators often removed objects without recording where they had been found, and some wall paintings faded once exposed to air. Today, large areas of Pompeii remain deliberately unexcavated, partly because experts believe future methods will allow more information to be recovered. The town therefore continues to teach lessons not only about the Roman past but also about how archaeology itself has changed.

questions.q: Excavations of Pompeii were organised systematically from the moment the town was buried.
Early archaeologists valued Pompeii mainly because of the precious metals found there.
Fiorelli used plaster to create casts of the victims' bodies.
The casts produced by Fiorelli were displayed in museums across Europe.
What did the volcanic ash help to preserve?
options: Everyday objects and the town's layout

questions.q: Why do experts leave parts of Pompeii unexcavated?
options: The remaining areas contain no objects
They expect better techniques in the future
The ground is too dangerous to enter

questions.q: Organised excavations of Pompeii first began in the year ________.
On the streets, the wheels of carts had cut visible ________ into the stone.

listening.title: Booking a Museum Tour
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, City Archaeology Museum, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a guided tour for next week, please.
STAFF: Of course. Which day were you thinking of?
CALLER: Thursday, if that's possible.
STAFF: Thursday is fine. We have a morning tour at ten o'clock and an afternoon one at two.
CALLER: The afternoon one suits me better. How long does it last?
STAFF: It runs for ninety minutes, and it covers the new Roman gallery.
CALLER: Lovely. And how much is a ticket?
STAFF: Adult tickets are twelve pounds each. How many people will there be?
CALLER: Four adults, please.
STAFF: That's forty-eight pounds in total. Can I take a name for the booking?
CALLER: Yes, it's under Bennett, B-E-N-N-E-T-T.
STAFF: Thank you. And a contact number?
CALLER: It's oh-seven-seven-one, four double-five, three nine two.
STAFF: Perfect. Please arrive about ten minutes early and ask for the guide at the main desk.
CALLER: Will do. Thank you very much.

Which tour does the caller choose?

speaking.q: Let's talk about history. Did you enjoy studying history at school? Why or why not? Are there any historical places near where you live that you have visited?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of visitors (in thousands) to four archaeological sites in one country in 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Site | 2010 | 2020
Roman Baths | 120 | 180
Old Castle | 90 | 85
Cave Paintings | 40 | 95
Ancient Harbour | 60 | 110

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The volcano began to erupt without warning.
The excavation lasted for several years.
The dry climate helped to preserve the documents.
They found many ordinary household objects.
Ancient graffiti covered the walls of the town.

The plaster cast showed the victim's clothing.
He tried to shield the child from danger.

reading.title: Reading the Rosetta Stone
reading.passage:
For centuries, the writing system of ancient Egypt remained a mystery. The carved symbols known as hieroglyphs covered temples and tombs, yet no living person could read them. Scholars assumed each picture stood for a single idea, and many wasted years trying to interpret the symbols as a kind of secret picture-language. The breakthrough came not from clever guessing but from a single broken slab of stone.

The Rosetta Stone was found in 1799 by French soldiers near the Egyptian town of Rosetta. It carried the same official message written three times: in hieroglyphs, in a later Egyptian script called Demotic, and in ancient Greek. Because scholars could already read Greek, they realised that the stone offered a rare chance to compare a known language with an unknown one.

Progress was slow and competitive. An English scientist, Thomas Young, correctly suggested that some hieroglyphs recorded sounds rather than ideas, particularly in the names of rulers. However, it was the French scholar Jean-Francois Champollion who finally cracked the system in 1822. He showed that hieroglyphs were a mixture: some signs stood for sounds, while others represented whole words or ideas. His knowledge of Coptic, a descendant of the ancient Egyptian language, proved essential to this achievement.

The decipherment transformed our understanding of the ancient world. Texts that had been silent for almost two thousand years could suddenly be read, revealing details about religion, law, trade and daily life. Today the Rosetta Stone is one of the most visited objects in the British Museum, and its name has come to stand for any key that unlocks a difficult problem.

questions.q: Before the stone was studied, scholars could already read hieroglyphs easily.
The Rosetta Stone contained the same message in three forms of writing.
Thomas Young completed the full decipherment of hieroglyphs.
Champollion travelled to Egypt to study the stone in person.
Why was the Greek text on the stone so useful?
options: It was the longest of the three texts

questions.q: What did Champollion show about hieroglyphs?
options: They mixed sound signs and idea signs

questions.q: The Rosetta Stone was discovered in the year ________.
Champollion's knowledge of ________, a descendant of ancient Egyptian, helped him succeed.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient Scripts
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to introduce the idea of writing systems in the ancient world. When we talk about scripts, we usually divide them into two broad types. The first type uses signs that stand for whole words or ideas. We call these logographic systems. The second type uses signs that stand for sounds, and these are often easier for outsiders to learn. Now, many ancient scripts were actually a mixture of the two. Egyptian hieroglyphs are a good example, because some signs carried sounds while others carried meanings. This mixture is exactly why they were so hard to decipher. Another point worth remembering is that writing usually developed first for practical reasons, such as recording trade, taxes and harvests, rather than for literature or poetry. The earliest clay tablets from Mesopotamia, for instance, were simple lists of grain and animals. Only much later did people use writing to record stories and laws. For next week, please read chapter four and prepare two questions for our seminar.

questions.q: What does a logographic system use signs for?
Scripts that use signs for sounds are often easier for ________ to learn.
Egyptian hieroglyphs were a ________ of sound signs and meaning signs.
According to the lecturer, writing first developed mainly for what reason?
The earliest clay tablets came from ________.
Students must read chapter ________ for next week.

speaking.q:
Describe an interesting object from the past that you have seen. You should say:

reading.title: The Bog Bodies of Northern Europe
reading.passage:
Across the peat bogs of Denmark, Ireland and northern Germany, farmers cutting fuel have occasionally uncovered something unexpected: the remarkably preserved bodies of people who died thousands of years ago. These so-called bog bodies are among the most striking finds in European archaeology. Unlike skeletons, they often retain skin, hair and even the contents of the stomach, offering a vivid glimpse of life in the distant past.

The preservation is the result of unusual chemical conditions. Peat bogs are cold, acidic and contain very little oxygen. Bacteria that would normally break down a body cannot survive in such an environment. Instead, the acids in the bog tan the skin much as leather is tanned, turning it dark brown and leathery. Bones, however, are often dissolved by the same acids, so many bog bodies have soft tissue but fragile or missing skeletons.

One of the best-known examples is Tollund Man, found in Denmark in 1950. His face was so well preserved that the workers who found him at first believed he was a recent murder victim and called the police. Scientific study later showed that he had lived around 2,400 years ago. A rope was still around his neck, suggesting that he had been hanged, possibly as part of a ritual rather than as a punishment.

Archaeologists remain uncertain about why so many bog bodies show signs of violent death. Some researchers argue that these individuals were offerings to the gods, perhaps to ensure a good harvest. Others suggest they were criminals or prisoners. Because written records from the period are scarce, much of the interpretation depends on careful comparison between sites. What is clear is that the bogs have preserved not only bodies but also an enduring mystery.

questions.q: Bog bodies usually have well-preserved skeletons but little soft tissue.
The acidic conditions of bogs prevent normal bacteria from surviving.
The workers who found Tollund Man initially thought he was a modern victim.
Tollund Man was discovered close to a famous ancient temple.
What turns the skin of bog bodies dark and leathery?
Why is the interpretation of bog bodies difficult?
options: The bodies are too damaged to study
There are very few written records from the time
The sites are difficult to reach

questions.q: Tollund Man was found in Denmark in the year ________.
Some researchers believe bog bodies were ________ to the gods.

listening.title: Volunteering at a Dig
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Hello, are you here about the volunteer dig?
VOLUNTEER: Yes, I signed up online last week. My name's Sara Khan.
ORGANISER: Welcome, Sara. Let me just check the details. You're with us for how many days?
VOLUNTEER: Three days, from Monday to Wednesday.
ORGANISER: Great. Now, a few practical points. Please wear sturdy boots and bring a hat, because there's very little shade on site.
VOLUNTEER: Should I bring my own tools?
ORGANISER: No, we provide all the trowels and brushes. But do bring a refillable water bottle. The nearest shop is two miles away.
VOLUNTEER: Good to know. What time do we start?
ORGANISER: We begin at eight in the morning and finish around four. There's a lunch break at half past twelve.
VOLUNTEER: And where do I meet the team?
ORGANISER: Come to the blue tent by the car park. Ask for the supervisor, Mr Doyle.
VOLUNTEER: Blue tent, Mr Doyle. Thank you so much.

reading.title: The Terracotta Army
reading.passage:
In 1974, farmers digging a well in Shaanxi province in north-western China made an extraordinary discovery. Beneath the soil lay thousands of life-sized clay soldiers, arranged in battle formation. These figures, now famous as the Terracotta Army, had been buried for more than two thousand years to guard the tomb of Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China.

The scale of the project is astonishing. Archaeologists estimate that around eight thousand soldiers were created, along with horses, chariots and even acrobats and musicians. Each warrior appears to have an individual face, suggesting that the sculptors may have based them on real people. The figures were originally painted in bright colours, although most of this paint flaked away soon after the army was exposed to the dry air.

The purpose of the army was deeply tied to ancient beliefs about death. The emperor wished to rule in the afterlife just as he had ruled in life, and so he ordered an entire underground world to be built around his tomb. Ancient writers recorded that the burial complex contained rivers of mercury representing the great rivers of China, and modern soil tests have indeed found unusually high levels of this metal nearby.

Despite decades of study, much of the site remains unexplored. The central tomb of the emperor himself has never been opened, partly out of respect and partly because experts fear that current methods might damage whatever lies inside. As with so many great discoveries, the Terracotta Army has answered some questions while raising many more, and it continues to attract millions of visitors each year.

questions.q: The Terracotta Army was discovered by farmers digging a well.
Each clay soldier was given the same standardised face.
Soil tests near the tomb found high levels of mercury.
The emperor's tomb was opened soon after the army was discovered.
The Terracotta Army is now the most visited site in all of China.
Why did the emperor order the army to be built?
options: To rule in the afterlife as he had in life

questions.q: What happened to the original paint on the figures?
options: It flaked away after exposure to the air
It was washed off by rain

questions.q: Archaeologists estimate that about ________ soldiers were created.

listening.title: A Tour of the Exhibition
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to our special exhibition on ancient China. I'll give you a quick overview before you explore on your own. As you enter, the first room on your left contains the largest object in the show: a full-sized clay warrior on loan from a museum overseas. Please don't touch the glass case, as the figure is extremely valuable. In the second room, you'll find smaller objects, including bronze weapons and tools. Many of these are over two thousand years old, yet some of the blades are still surprisingly sharp. The third room is devoted to daily life, with pottery bowls, coins and jewellery. There's also a short film showing how the figures were originally painted. The film runs every twenty minutes and lasts about eight minutes. Finally, the gift shop is at the far end, past the cafe. Audio guides are available at the entrance for five dollars, and I'd really recommend one, as the labels are quite brief. Enjoy your visit.

reading.title: Mapping the Past from the Sky
reading.passage:
For most of its history, archaeology was carried out at ground level, with researchers walking across fields in search of clues. In the twentieth century, however, a new perspective changed the discipline: the view from above. Aerial photography, first developed during wartime, allowed archaeologists to spot patterns in the landscape that were invisible to anyone standing on the ground.

The technique works because buried structures affect the way crops grow. Where ancient walls lie just beneath the surface, plants have less soil and water, so they grow shorter and ripen earlier. Above buried ditches, by contrast, the soil is deeper and richer, so crops grow taller and greener. From the air, these differences appear as faint lines and shapes, known as crop marks, which trace the outlines of vanished buildings and roads.

In recent decades, technology has advanced far beyond simple photographs. A method called lidar uses laser pulses fired from an aircraft to measure the exact height of the ground thousands of times per second. By removing the trees and vegetation digitally, researchers can reveal hidden features beneath dense forest. Lidar surveys in Central America, for instance, have exposed vast networks of ancient settlements that had lain unnoticed under thick jungle for centuries.

These tools have transformed how archaeologists plan their work. Rather than digging blindly, they can study large areas quickly and decide exactly where excavation is most likely to be productive. This saves time and money, and it also reduces unnecessary damage to fragile sites. Yet experts caution that remote methods can only suggest what lies below. In the end, the spade must still confirm what the camera and the laser merely hint at.

questions.q: Aerial photography was originally developed for archaeological purposes.
Crops above buried walls tend to grow taller than surrounding plants.
Lidar can reveal features hidden beneath dense forest.
Lidar surveys in Central America were funded by international universities.
Why do crops above buried ditches grow taller?
options: The soil there is deeper and richer
The sun reaches them more easily

questions.q: What is the main advantage of remote survey methods?
options: They remove the need for any digging
They let researchers study large areas quickly
They guarantee that treasure will be found

questions.q: Faint lines in fields that show buried structures are known as ________ marks.
Lidar uses ________ pulses fired from an aircraft to measure ground height.

listening.title: A Talk on Archaeological Technology
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thank you for coming to today's talk on technology in modern archaeology. I want to focus on three tools that have changed our work. The first is ground-penetrating radar, which sends signals into the soil and detects what lies beneath without any digging at all. It is especially useful in places where excavation is not permitted, such as historic city centres. The second tool is the drone. Drones carry cameras high above a site and capture detailed images in just a few minutes, a task that once took days. They are also relatively cheap, which matters for projects with small budgets. The third tool is computer modelling. Once data has been collected, we can build a three-dimensional model of a site on screen, allowing us to test ideas before we ever pick up a spade. However, I must stress that technology does not replace traditional skills. A machine can tell us where to look, but it cannot interpret what we find. That still depends on human judgement and experience.

questions.q: How many tools does the speaker focus on?
Ground-penetrating radar sends signals into the ________.
Why are drones described as useful for small projects?
Computer modelling lets researchers build a ________-dimensional model of a site.
The speaker says technology cannot ________ what we find.
Interpreting finds still depends on human judgement and ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology and discovery. In what ways has modern technology changed how we learn about the past? Do you think relying on machines could cause people to lose traditional skills? Should governments invest more money in scientific research or in protecting historic sites?

writing.q:
The table below shows how much money (in millions of dollars) one country spent on three areas of archaeological work in 2015 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Area | 2015 | 2022
Excavation | 25 | 30
Technology and surveys | 8 | 22
Site protection | 15 | 18

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The view from above gave a new perspective.
Aerial photography revealed hidden walls.
The technique changed the discipline.
The patterns were invisible from the ground.
The crops ripen earlier above old walls.
Crops grow taller above a buried ditch.
Lidar removes the vegetation digitally.

reading.title: The History of the Metronome
reading.passage:
Musicians have always needed a way to keep a steady beat, but for many centuries they had to rely only on their own sense of timing. A piece played too fast or too slow could lose much of its intended character. The problem became more serious as written music grew more complicated, because composers wanted performers to play their works at a particular speed.

The first practical solution appeared in the early nineteenth century. A device called the metronome was developed, which produced a regular ticking sound. By adjusting a small weight on a swinging arm, a player could set the number of beats per minute. Although several people contributed to its design, the instrument is usually associated with Johann Maelzel, who began selling metronomes in 1816 and secured a patent for the design.

Composers were quick to see the value of the new device. Ludwig van Beethoven was among the first to add metronome markings to his scores, giving an exact speed for each movement. This allowed musicians who had never met the composer to perform a work much as he had imagined it. Some critics, however, argued that strict timing could make a performance sound mechanical and lifeless.

Even today, the debate continues. Many teachers insist that beginners should practise with a metronome to build a reliable sense of rhythm. Others believe that experienced performers should feel free to speed up or slow down slightly for emotional effect, a technique known as rubato. Modern electronic and digital metronomes are far more accurate than the early mechanical ones, yet the basic purpose has not changed: to help musicians keep time.

questions.q: Before the metronome was invented, musicians depended on their own sense of timing.
Maelzel obtained a patent for his metronome design.
Beethoven personally trained the musicians who first used his metronome markings.
Everyone agreed that strict metronome timing improved performances.
According to the passage, why did the need for a steady beat become more serious over time?
What does the metronome marking on a score tell a performer?
options: The exact speed of the music

questions.q: Maelzel began selling metronomes in the year ________.
Slightly speeding up or slowing down for emotional effect is known as ________.

listening.title: Joining the Community Choir
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good evening, Riverside Community Choir, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I saw a poster and I'd like to join. Can you tell me about the rehearsals?

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We meet every Tuesday evening at seven o'clock in the church hall on Mill Street.

CALLER: And how long does a rehearsal last?

RECEPTIONIST: Usually about two hours, so we finish around nine. There's a short break in the middle for tea.

CALLER: Do I need to audition?

RECEPTIONIST: No audition is required for new members. We welcome all abilities. The only thing we ask is that you attend regularly.

CALLER: That sounds great. Is there a fee?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, it's forty pounds for the whole term, which covers the music and the hire of the hall.

CALLER: Should I bring anything to the first rehearsal?

RECEPTIONIST: Just a pencil and a bottle of water. We provide all the song sheets. Oh, and please arrive ten minutes early so we can sign you in.

CALLER: Perfect. My name's Daniel Pearce. I'll see you on Tuesday.

reading.title: Why People Get Stage Fright
reading.passage:
Many performers, from beginners to famous professionals, suffer from a feeling of fear before they step onto a stage. This condition is commonly known as stage fright, and it can affect singers, actors, public speakers and musicians alike. The symptoms include a racing heart, shaking hands, a dry mouth and, in serious cases, the sudden inability to remember well-practised material.

Scientists explain stage fright as a natural response of the body to a situation that feels threatening. When we sense danger, the body releases a hormone called adrenaline, which prepares us either to fight or to run away. On stage, of course, neither reaction is helpful, so the energy has nowhere to go. This is why performers often feel restless and uncomfortable just before they begin.

Interestingly, a small amount of nervousness is not always a bad thing. Many experienced performers say that a little fear keeps them alert and helps them give a sharper, more exciting performance. The problem arises only when the fear becomes so strong that it interferes with the ability to perform at all.

There are several techniques that can reduce the worst effects. Slow, deep breathing helps to calm the body and slow the heartbeat. Some performers imagine the performance going well before it happens, a method sometimes called visualisation. Others find that thorough preparation is the best cure, since confidence usually grows when a person knows their material extremely well. Above all, experts agree that facing an audience repeatedly is the most effective way to become comfortable over time.

questions.q: Stage fright affects only inexperienced performers.
Adrenaline prepares the body to fight or to run away.
A small amount of nervousness can improve a performance.
Most professional musicians take medicine to control stage fright.
Why do performers often feel restless before a performance?
options: The adrenaline energy has nowhere to go

questions.q: According to the passage, what do experts consider the most effective long-term solution?
options: Performing in front of audiences repeatedly

questions.q: The hormone released when the body senses danger is called ________.
Imagining the performance going well in advance is called ________.

listening.title: Booking a Practice Room
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to book a practice room for my band, please.

STAFF: Sure. How many people are in the band?

STUDENT: There are five of us, and we've got a drum kit, so we need a bigger room.

STAFF: In that case you'll want Room C. It's the largest and it already has a drum kit installed.

STUDENT: That's perfect. When can we book it?

STAFF: It's free on Saturday afternoon. How long do you need?

STUDENT: About three hours, if possible. Say, from one o'clock?

STAFF: One until four, that's fine. The cost is fifteen dollars an hour, so forty-five dollars in total.

STUDENT: Do we pay now or on the day?

STAFF: You can pay on the day, but I'll need a deposit of ten dollars to hold the booking.

STUDENT: No problem. Is there anything we should know?

STAFF: Just remember to bring your own cables and microphones. And please leave the room tidy for the next group.

STUDENT: Will do. Thanks very much.

reading.title: The Rise of the Vinyl Record
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the vinyl record was the main way in which people listened to recorded music at home. A vinyl record is a flat disc with a long, spiral groove cut into its surface. As the disc turns on a record player, a needle travels along the groove and picks up tiny vibrations, which are then turned into sound through a loudspeaker.

Vinyl records first became popular in the late 1940s, when they replaced older, more fragile discs. They offered better sound quality and could hold more music on each side. The larger albums, which spun at thirty-three revolutions per minute, could contain around twenty minutes of music per side, enough for a full collection of songs. Smaller discs, known as singles, usually carried just one or two tracks.

During the 1980s, however, the compact disc, or CD, arrived and quickly took over the market. CDs were smaller, harder to scratch and easier to carry. Sales of vinyl fell sharply, and many people assumed the format would disappear completely. For a time, it seemed that records belonged only to the past.

Surprisingly, vinyl has made a strong comeback in recent years. Younger listeners, many of whom were not even born when records were common, have begun buying them again. Some say they prefer the warmer sound, while others enjoy the large cover artwork and the feeling of owning a physical object. Whatever the reason, sales of vinyl have risen steadily, proving that an old technology can find new life in a digital age.

questions.q: A needle picks up vibrations as it travels along the groove.
Vinyl records replaced older discs that were more fragile.
CDs were more easily scratched than vinyl records.
Vinyl records are now cheaper to produce than they were in the 1980s.
Larger vinyl albums could hold about how much music per side?
What reason do some younger listeners give for buying vinyl again?
Large vinyl albums spun at ________ revolutions per minute.
Smaller discs carrying one or two tracks were known as ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Caring for Records
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome, everyone. Today I'll give you a few simple tips on how to look after your vinyl records so they last for many years.

First, always hold a record by its edges. The oils on your fingertips can damage the surface and affect the sound, so never touch the grooves directly.

Second, keep your records clean. A soft brush, used gently before each play, will remove dust. Avoid ordinary cloths, as they can leave tiny scratches.

Third, storage matters a great deal. Records should be stored standing upright, never stacked flat on top of one another. Stacking puts pressure on the discs and can cause them to warp.

Temperature is also important. Keep your collection away from direct sunlight and heat. A cool room, ideally below twenty degrees, is best.

Finally, replace the needle on your record player regularly. A worn needle is the most common cause of damage. Most experts suggest changing it after about one thousand hours of play.

Follow these steps, and your records will sound wonderful for decades. Now, are there any questions?

speaking.q: Let's discuss music and technology. How has the way people listen to music changed over the past few decades? Do you think these changes have been mostly positive or negative? Why do some people still prefer older formats such as vinyl records?

reading.title: The Origins of the Orchestra
reading.passage:
The modern symphony orchestra, with its dozens of players seated in carefully arranged sections, did not appear overnight. It developed slowly over several hundred years, shaped by changes in musical taste, the invention of new instruments, and the growing wealth of audiences who paid to attend concerts.

In the early seventeenth century, groups of musicians were often quite small and varied greatly from place to place. A composer might write for whatever players happened to be available, and there was no fixed idea of how an orchestra should be organised. Over time, however, certain combinations of instruments proved especially pleasing, and these gradually became standard.

The string section, made up of violins, violas, cellos and double basses, formed the heart of the orchestra. To this core were added wind instruments such as flutes and oboes, and later brass instruments and a wide range of percussion. As the number of players grew, it became necessary for someone to keep them together, and the role of the conductor emerged. Standing at the front, the conductor sets the speed and shapes the overall sound, although he or she does not actually play an instrument.

By the nineteenth century, orchestras had grown very large, sometimes including a hundred musicians or more. Composers wrote increasingly ambitious works to take advantage of these forces, demanding greater volume and richer combinations of sound. Concert halls were built specially to seat such large groups and to allow the music to be heard clearly. The orchestra had become one of the most powerful means of musical expression, a position it still holds today.

questions.q: The modern orchestra developed gradually over several centuries.
In the early seventeenth century, orchestras everywhere had the same fixed arrangement.
The conductor plays an instrument while leading the orchestra.
Nineteenth-century orchestras were more expensive to run than earlier ones.
Which group of instruments formed the heart of the orchestra?
Why did the role of the conductor emerge?
options: The number of players grew and needed coordinating

questions.q: The conductor sets the speed and shapes the overall ________.
By the nineteenth century, some orchestras included ________ musicians or more.

listening.title: A Lecture on Orchestra Seating
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today I want to explain how a typical symphony orchestra is arranged on stage, and why it is set out the way it is.

The conductor stands at the very front, on a small raised platform called a podium. From there, every player can see the conductor clearly.

Directly in front of the conductor, you'll find the strings, spread out in a wide curve. The first violins sit on the conductor's left, and the cellos on the right. Placing the strings at the front makes sense because they are the quietest instruments and need to be heard.

Behind the strings come the woodwind instruments, such as flutes and clarinets, seated in the center. Behind them are the louder brass instruments, including trumpets and trombones.

Finally, right at the back, you'll find the percussion section, with the drums and other instruments that produce the most powerful sound. Putting the loudest players furthest away helps to balance the overall volume.

This basic layout has remained remarkably stable for over a century, because it simply works. Next week, we'll look at how recording changes these arrangements.

questions.q: The conductor stands on a raised platform called a ________.
Where are the strings placed?
The first violins sit on the conductor's ________.
Why are the strings placed at the front?
options: They are the quietest and need to be heard

questions.q: The ________ section sits right at the back of the stage.
What will the next lecture cover?

speaking.q: Let's talk about concerts and live events. Have you ever been to a concert or a live musical event? Would you like to attend more live performances in the future? Do you prefer listening to music live or recorded? Why?

writing.q: Some people think that governments should spend public money on supporting orchestras, theatres and other forms of traditional art. Others argue that this money would be better spent on more practical needs such as healthcare and schools. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The players are carefully arranged in sections.
Early groups varied greatly from place to place.
There was no fixed idea of organisation.
Certain combinations of instruments were pleasing.
These combinations became standard.
The strings formed the core of the orchestra.

The conductor shapes the overall sound.

The works demanded greater volume.

reading.title: Folk Songs and Cultural Memory
reading.passage:
Long before music could be written down or recorded, ordinary people passed songs from one generation to the next simply by singing them. These traditional pieces, usually known as folk songs, were not the work of famous composers. Instead, they grew out of the daily lives of farmers, sailors, soldiers and workers, and they often described events, feelings and beliefs that mattered deeply to a community.

Because folk songs were learned by ear rather than from a printed page, they tended to change over time. A singer might forget a line and replace it with a new one, or alter a tune to suit a different region. As a result, the same song could exist in many slightly different versions, each shaped by the place where it was sung. Few people thought of this as a problem; it was simply how the tradition worked.

In the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, a number of scholars became worried that these songs might be lost forever as people moved to cities and old ways of life disappeared. They travelled through the countryside, listening to elderly singers and writing down both the words and the melodies. Some of these collectors later published large books of folk songs, helping to preserve material that might otherwise have vanished.

Today, folk songs are valued not only as music but also as a kind of historical record. They tell us how ordinary people once lived, what they hoped for, and what they feared. Many modern musicians continue to perform and reshape these old songs, ensuring that the voices of the past are still heard.

questions.q: Folk songs were usually written by well-known composers.
Folk songs often changed because they were learned by ear.
Some scholars feared that folk songs might be lost as people moved to cities.
The collectors were paid by governments to gather the songs.
Why did the same folk song often exist in many versions?
options: Singers changed lines and tunes over time

questions.q: How are folk songs valued today, according to the passage?
options: As a kind of historical record

questions.q: Folk songs were learned by ________ rather than from a printed page.
Some collectors published large ________ of folk songs to preserve them.

listening.title: A Radio Feature on a Folk Festival
listening.script:
PRESENTER: This week our local radio feature looks ahead to the annual Greenfield Folk Festival, now in its twentieth year.

The festival runs over a single weekend, from Friday evening to Sunday night, and takes place in Greenfield Park, just outside the town centre. Organisers expect around eight thousand visitors this year, the largest crowd so far.

There will be three stages in total. The main stage hosts the best-known performers, while a smaller tent is reserved for new and local artists. The third area is dedicated entirely to workshops, where visitors can learn to play traditional instruments or join a singing class.

Tickets cost twenty-five pounds for the full weekend, though children under twelve enter free. Camping is available in a nearby field for an extra charge.

Food stalls will offer dishes from around the world, and there's a special market selling handmade instruments and crafts.

Organisers remind visitors to bring warm clothing, as evenings can be cold, and to use public transport where possible, since parking is very limited. For more details, visit the festival website.

Organisers expect around ________ visitors this year.
What is the smaller tent reserved for?
A full weekend ticket costs ________ pounds.
Why do organisers recommend using public transport?
options: There is no car park at all

questions.q: Children under ________ can enter free.

speaking.q: Let's discuss traditional music and culture. Why do you think it is important to preserve traditional songs from the past? Should young people be encouraged to learn about traditional music? How might a country's traditional music change as the world becomes more connected?

The table below shows the number of visitors (in thousands) to a folk music festival across four categories of activity in two different years.

Activity | 2018 | 2023
Main stage | 5.0 | 6.5
Workshops | 1.2 | 2.8
Food market | 3.0 | 4.0
Camping | 0.8 | 1.5

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

That was simply how the tradition worked.

Scholars feared the songs would be lost.
They wrote down the words and the melodies.
They helped preserve material that might have vanished.

reading.title: The Return of the Tram
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, trams were seen as relics of the past. Many European and North American cities removed their tram networks in the 1950s and 1960s, believing that buses and private cars represented the future of urban transport. The metal rails were torn up, the overhead wires were taken down, and the old vehicles were sold for scrap or left to rust in depots.

In recent decades, however, attitudes have changed dramatically. As cities have grown more crowded and concerns about air pollution have increased, planners have begun to view the tram as a clean and efficient solution. Modern trams, often called light rail, run on electricity and produce no exhaust fumes along their routes. They can carry far more passengers than buses, and because they follow fixed rails, drivers find them smooth and predictable to operate.

The city of Nordhaven offers a striking example. In 2009 it opened its first new tram line in over fifty years, connecting the central station to a growing residential district in the east. The project was expensive, costing an estimated 240 million euros, and some residents complained about years of disruptive construction. Yet within a decade the line was carrying more than 60,000 passengers a day, far exceeding the original forecasts.

Supporters argue that trams also encourage development. Shops, cafes and apartment blocks tend to appear along the routes, because investors trust that the rails are permanent in a way that bus stops are not. Critics, however, point out that building tram systems is slow and costly, and that the same money might fund a wider network of electric buses. The debate continues, but in city after city the tram is quietly making its comeback.

questions.q: Many cities removed their tram networks because they thought cars and buses were more modern.
Modern trams release exhaust fumes only at the start of their journeys.
The mayor of Nordhaven personally opened the new tram line in 2009.
What does the passage say about the Nordhaven tram line?
options: It carried more passengers than predicted

questions.q: Why do investors prefer building near tram routes?
Modern trams are also known as ________ rail.
The Nordhaven line cost an estimated ________ million euros.
Critics suggest the money could instead fund a wider network of electric ________.

listening.title: Joining the City Bike Scheme
listening.script:
ASSISTANT: Good morning, you've reached the City Bike Hire desk. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to sign up for the bike sharing scheme, please.
ASSISTANT: Of course. Can I take your full name?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
ASSISTANT: Thank you. And which membership would you like? We offer a monthly plan at 15 pounds, or an annual plan at 120 pounds.
CALLER: I think the annual one makes more sense for me.
ASSISTANT: Good choice. With the annual plan, the first thirty minutes of every ride are free. After that it's one pound per half hour.
CALLER: That sounds fair. Where's my nearest docking station?
ASSISTANT: If you give me your postcode I can check.
CALLER: It's GL5 2RT.
ASSISTANT: Right, your closest station is on Maple Street, just two minutes from there. You'll need to collect a key card from our office on Bridge Road. We're open until six o'clock on weekdays.
CALLER: Perfect. I'll come in this afternoon.

reading.title: Designing Streets for Walking
reading.passage:
For decades, the streets of many cities were designed mainly for cars. Wide roads, large car parks and fast traffic lanes made driving convenient, but they often made walking unpleasant or even dangerous. Pedestrians were squeezed onto narrow pavements, forced to wait at long traffic lights, and exposed to noise and fumes. In response, a growing number of urban designers now argue that streets should be planned around people on foot.

The idea is sometimes called the walkable city. Its supporters believe that when streets are pleasant to walk along, the whole community benefits. People are more likely to visit local shops, neighbours meet one another more often, and residents take more daily exercise. Studies in several countries suggest that streets with wide pavements, trees and benches attract more foot traffic, which in turn helps small businesses to thrive.

Creating a walkable city is not simply a matter of widening pavements. Designers also consider the distance between useful destinations. If a school, a shop and a park are all within a short walk of home, people have little reason to drive. This approach is often summarised by the phrase the fifteen-minute city, meaning that daily needs should be reachable within roughly fifteen minutes on foot or by bicycle.

Not everyone is convinced. Some critics argue that walkable design works best in dense central districts but is harder to apply in spread-out suburbs, where homes are far from shops and services. Others worry that improving certain neighbourhoods can push up rents, making them less affordable for existing residents. Even so, the principle that cities should serve pedestrians, not only drivers, is steadily gaining ground among planners worldwide.

questions.q: In the past, many city streets were designed mainly to make driving convenient.
The fifteen-minute city idea means daily needs should be reachable within a short walk or cycle.
More cyclists are injured in suburbs than in central districts.
Critics say walkable design is easiest to apply in spread-out suburbs.
According to the passage, what is one benefit of walkable streets?
What concern do some critics raise about improving neighbourhoods?
Streets with wide pavements, trees and ________ attract more foot traffic.
The principle that cities should serve ________ is gaining ground among planners.

listening.title: A Walking Tour Briefing
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome everyone to today's city walking tour. Before we set off, let me give you a few details. The tour lasts about two hours and we'll cover roughly four kilometres, so I hope you've worn comfortable shoes. We'll start here at the Town Hall and finish at the harbour. Our first stop is the old market square, which dates back to 1840. From there we'll walk down to the cathedral, where we'll pause for about twenty minutes so you can look inside. Photography is allowed, but please don't use flash. After the cathedral, we'll follow the river path to a small museum, and entry is included in your ticket. Finally, we'll reach the harbour, where there are plenty of cafes if you'd like a coffee afterwards. One last thing: please stay together as a group, and if you have any questions, just raise your hand. The weather looks fine, but I'd still recommend bringing a bottle of water. Right, shall we begin?

speaking.q:
Describe a place in your town or city that you enjoy walking in. You should say:
- where it is
- how often you go there
- what you usually do while you are there
and explain why you enjoy walking in this place.

reading.title: The Challenge of Rush Hour
reading.passage:
In almost every large city, transport networks face the same daily problem: rush hour. For a few hours each morning and evening, trains, buses and roads become crowded as millions of people travel to and from work at the same time. During the rest of the day, the same vehicles and tracks may be half empty. This uneven pattern of demand creates serious difficulties for transport planners.

The main problem is that systems must be built to handle the busiest moments, not the average ones. A railway line that is comfortable at midday may be dangerously overcrowded at eight in the morning. To cope with the peak, operators have to buy extra trains, hire more staff and maintain larger stations, even though much of this capacity sits unused for most of the day. The result is an expensive system that is difficult to run efficiently.

One solution that has attracted attention is spreading demand more evenly across the day. Some employers now allow staff to start and finish work at different times, a practice known as flexible working. If office workers arrive between seven and ten rather than all at nine, the morning crush can be reduced. A number of transport authorities have also experimented with cheaper fares for travel outside peak hours, hoping to tempt passengers away from the busiest trains.

The rise of remote work has added a new dimension to the debate. When many employees work from home for part of the week, fewer people commute, and some experts predicted that rush hour would disappear altogether. In practice, the pattern has simply shifted: trains are quieter on Mondays and Fridays but remain busy in the middle of the week. Managing these peaks, it seems, will remain a central task for years to come.

questions.q: During rush hour, transport networks become crowded as many people travel at once.
Transport systems must be built to handle average levels of demand.
Flexible working means letting staff begin work at different times.
Remote work has completely removed rush hour in most cities.
The author believes flexible working is more effective than off-peak fares.
Why is much transport capacity described as inefficient?
options: It is used only at night
It sits unused for most of the day
It is too small for the peak

questions.q: What happened to rush hour after remote work increased?
options: Its pattern shifted across the week

questions.q: Some authorities offer cheaper fares for travel outside ________ hours.

listening.title: Reporting a Lost Travel Card
listening.script:
STAFF: Customer services, how can I help you today?
PASSENGER: Hello, I think I've lost my travel card and I'd like to report it.
STAFF: I'm sorry to hear that. Do you have your card number?
PASSENGER: I'm afraid not, but I have the account details.
STAFF: That's fine. Can I take the name on the account?
PASSENGER: Yes, it's Rebecca Lowe, L-O-W-E.
STAFF: Thank you. And when did you last use the card?
PASSENGER: On Tuesday, I think, on the number 47 bus.
STAFF: Right, I can see the account. I'll block the old card now so nobody else can use it. A replacement will be posted to you, and it usually arrives within five working days.
PASSENGER: Is there a charge for that?
STAFF: There's a small fee of 6 pounds, which we'll add to your account. Any remaining balance will be transferred to the new card automatically.
PASSENGER: Great. And what should I do in the meantime?
STAFF: You can buy single tickets on the bus, or use the mobile app to travel. Is there anything else I can help with?
PASSENGER: No, that's everything. Thank you.

questions.q: The name on the account is Rebecca ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss work and travel in modern cities. Why do you think rush hour is such a difficult problem to solve? Some people now work from home several days a week. How do you think this trend will affect cities and their transport systems in the future?

The table below shows the average number of passengers (in thousands) on a city railway line at four times of day on weekdays and weekends.

Time | Weekday | Weekend
7-9 am | 85 | 20
12-2 pm | 30 | 35
5-7 pm | 80 | 25
9-11 pm | 15 | 40

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

reading.title: Cooling the Concrete City
reading.passage:
On a hot summer day, a city can feel several degrees warmer than the countryside that surrounds it. This phenomenon, known as the urban heat island, has been studied for many years. The cause lies in the very materials from which cities are built. Concrete, brick and dark asphalt absorb heat from the sun during the day and release it slowly at night, so urban areas struggle to cool down even after sunset.

The problem is made worse by the shortage of greenery in many city centres. Trees and plants provide shade and release moisture into the air, both of which help to lower temperatures. Where streets are lined with buildings and car parks instead, this natural cooling is lost. Traffic and air conditioning add still more heat, creating a cycle in which hot weather leads to greater energy use, which in turn produces yet more warmth.

Urban heat is not merely uncomfortable; it can be dangerous. During severe heatwaves, the elderly and the very young are particularly at risk, and hospitals often report a rise in admissions. As global temperatures climb, planners are searching for ways to make cities more bearable in summer.

Several promising solutions have emerged. Painting roofs white or a pale colour can reflect sunlight rather than absorb it, lowering indoor temperatures noticeably. Planting more street trees, creating parks and even installing gardens on rooftops can all help to cool the surrounding air. Some cities have begun to open public buildings as cooling centres during the hottest days, giving vulnerable residents a comfortable place to rest. None of these measures alone can solve the problem, but together they may make the concrete city a little kinder in a warming world.

questions.q: Cities can feel warmer than the countryside around them.
Concrete and asphalt release the heat they absorb very quickly after sunset.
Trees help to cool cities by providing shade and releasing moisture.
Heatwaves cause more deaths in cities than in rural areas.
According to the passage, why is white paint useful on roofs?
What are cooling centres?
options: Public buildings opened in hot weather

questions.q: The effect described in the passage is called the urban heat ________.
During heatwaves, the elderly and the very ________ are particularly at risk.

listening.title: A Talk on Green Roofs
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming to this short talk on green roofs. So, what exactly is a green roof? Put simply, it's a roof that's partly or completely covered with plants. They're becoming more common in cities, and for good reason. First, green roofs help to keep buildings cool. In summer, they can lower the temperature inside the top floor by as much as five degrees, which means less need for air conditioning. Second, they soak up rainwater. During heavy storms, a green roof can absorb a large share of the rain that falls on it, easing pressure on the city's drains. Third, they provide a home for insects and birds, bringing a little nature back into the city. Of course, there are some drawbacks. Green roofs are more expensive to build, and they need regular maintenance, especially in the first two years. The plants must be watered until their roots are established. Even so, many city councils now offer grants to encourage building owners to install them. If you're interested, leaflets are available at the back of the room.

questions.q: What is a green roof?
A green roof can lower the top-floor temperature by up to ________ degrees.
Green roofs soak up ________ during heavy storms.
What is mentioned as a drawback of green roofs?
Plants must be watered until their ________ are established.
What do many city councils offer to encourage green roofs?

speaking.q: Let's talk about weather and your city in summer. Does your city get very hot in summer? What do you usually do to keep cool when the weather is hot? Are there enough parks or green spaces where you live?

writing.q: Many cities are becoming uncomfortably hot in summer because of the materials used in buildings and roads. Some people think governments should require new buildings to include features such as trees, gardens and reflective roofs. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this view? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Rising sea levels are a worrying phenomenon.
Dark surfaces absorb more heat.
The road was paved with black asphalt.
The square was brightened by plenty of greenery.
Plants release moisture into the air.
The region suffered a severe drought.
The summer heatwave lasted ten days.
Older people are more vulnerable to heat.
Pale surfaces reflect sunlight.

The room felt noticeably cooler.

reading.title: The Promise and Problems of Self-Driving Cars
reading.passage:
Few ideas in modern transport have generated as much excitement as the self-driving car. For more than a decade, technology companies and traditional carmakers have invested enormous sums in vehicles that can steer, brake and navigate without a human driver. Supporters claim these machines could transform our cities, but the road to a driverless future has proved longer and bumpier than many predicted.

The potential benefits are certainly attractive. Most road accidents are caused by human error, such as tiredness, distraction or speeding. A car controlled by sensors and software, its champions argue, would not get tired or look at its phone, and might therefore make roads far safer. Self-driving vehicles could also give independence to people who cannot drive, including the elderly and those with certain disabilities. Some experts even suggest that fleets of shared driverless cars could reduce the total number of vehicles a city needs.

Yet serious obstacles remain. Teaching a computer to handle every situation on a busy street is extraordinarily difficult. Human drivers constantly make judgements about unpredictable events, from a child running into the road to a cyclist signalling unclearly. A self-driving system must cope with all of these, in rain, fog and darkness, without error. Even rare mistakes can have tragic consequences, and a single high-profile accident can shake public confidence in the whole technology.

There are also difficult questions that have nothing to do with engineering. Who is responsible if a driverless car causes a crash: the owner, the manufacturer or the software company? How should such cars behave when an accident is unavoidable? And will workers whose jobs depend on driving, such as taxi and lorry drivers, be left without employment? Until these questions are answered, the fully driverless city is likely to remain a vision of tomorrow rather than a reality of today.

questions.q: Companies have spent large amounts of money developing self-driving cars.
The passage states that most road accidents are caused by mechanical faults.
Self-driving cars could help people who are unable to drive.
Driverless cars are already legal in most countries.
Why is teaching a computer to drive described as very difficult?
What effect can a single high-profile accident have?
Which of these is described as a non-engineering question?
options: Who is responsible for a crash

questions.q: Most road accidents are caused by human ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Future City Transport
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to look at how transport in our cities might change over the next thirty years. Let's begin with a simple fact: by 2050, around two-thirds of the world's people are expected to live in cities. That puts huge pressure on transport systems. So what are the options? First, electrification. Most experts agree that buses and cars will increasingly run on electricity rather than petrol or diesel. This reduces local air pollution, though of course the electricity itself must come from clean sources. Second, sharing. Instead of owning a car that sits unused for most of the day, more people may use shared vehicles, booked through an app. Third, and more uncertain, is automation, that is, vehicles that drive themselves. Trials are already running in several cities, but progress has been slower than companies hoped. My own view is that the biggest changes will come not from new technology but from better planning, putting homes, jobs and shops closer together. Next week we'll examine a case study from one European city. Please read the chapter beforehand.

questions.q: By 2050, around ________ of the world's people are expected to live in cities.
What does the lecturer say about electrification?
options: The electricity must come from clean sources

questions.q: Shared vehicles can be booked through an ________.
What does the lecturer say about automation?
options: Progress has been slower than hoped

questions.q: The lecturer believes the biggest changes will come from better ________.
What are students asked to do before next week?

speaking.q: Let's discuss the future of transport. Some people believe self-driving cars will make our roads much safer, while others are worried about them. What is your view? More generally, do you think new technology is always the best way to solve a city's transport problems, or are there better approaches?

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of new cars sold in one country that were electric, hybrid or petrol/diesel in three different years.

Fuel type | 2015 | 2020 | 2025
Petrol/diesel | 88 | 65 | 40
Hybrid | 10 | 25 | 30
Electric | 2 | 10 | 30

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The plan generated a lot of debate.
They spent an enormous amount of money.
The car can navigate narrow streets on its own.
The internet has transformed how we work.
Phones are a common distraction while driving.
A car gives many people a sense of independence.
Cost is the biggest obstacle to the project.
City traffic can be highly unpredictable.
Small mistakes can have serious consequences.

reading.title: The Return of the Monsoon
reading.passage:
Across much of South and Southeast Asia, the word "monsoon" describes a seasonal shift in wind direction that brings heavy rain for several months each year. The system is driven by a simple difference in temperature. During summer, land heats up faster than the surrounding ocean. Warm air rises over the land, and moist air is pulled in from the sea to take its place. As this wet air rises and cools, it releases enormous amounts of rain.

For farmers, the timing of the monsoon is critical. In India, roughly half of all farmland depends entirely on monsoon rain rather than on irrigation from rivers or wells. If the rains arrive a few weeks late, planting is delayed and harvests can shrink. If they fail completely, the result can be a serious shortage of food. For this reason, weather agencies spend large sums trying to predict when the rains will begin and how heavy they will be.

Yet the monsoon has always been difficult to forecast. Small changes in ocean temperature thousands of kilometres away can strengthen or weaken it. One such influence is a periodic warming of the Pacific Ocean known as El Nino, which tends to make the monsoon weaker than usual. Scientists have noticed that in recent decades the rainfall has become more uneven: long dry spells are now often followed by short, violent downpours that cause flooding.

Managing this changing pattern is a major challenge. Engineers have built dams and reservoirs to store water from the wettest weeks for use later in the year. Some cities have begun collecting rainwater from rooftops. Even so, experts agree that better forecasting remains the single most valuable tool, because it allows millions of farmers to decide exactly when to plant.

questions.q: The monsoon is caused by land and sea heating at different speeds.
About half of India's farmland relies on monsoon rain rather than irrigation.
El Nino usually makes the monsoon stronger than normal.
Most farmers in India now use rooftop rainwater collection.
According to the passage, why is the timing of the monsoon so important?
options: It affects when tourists visit the region
Late rain can delay planting and reduce harvests

listening.title: Joining a Weather-Watch Group
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good morning, you've reached the Riverside Weather-Watch Group. How can I help?

CALLER: Of course.

OFFICER: Thank you. And which area of town do you live in?

CALLER: Near the old bridge, on Mill Lane.

OFFICER: Lovely, that's a useful spot. The membership fee is twelve pounds a year, which covers the equipment. Meetings are held on the first Tuesday of each month at seven o'clock, in the library.

OFFICER: Just a notebook. We'll show you how to read the gauge and how to log your figures on the website.

CALLER: Perfect. And how soon can I start?

reading.title: Reading the Clouds
reading.passage:
Long before satellites and computers, people learned to predict the weather by watching the sky. Clouds, in particular, offered useful clues. A sky full of thin, feathery clouds high above the ground often warned that rain was approaching within a day. Low, dark clouds that gathered quickly usually meant a storm was close. Sailors and farmers passed this knowledge from one generation to the next, often in the form of short rhymes that were easy to remember.

Modern meteorologists now classify clouds into three main groups according to their height. High clouds, made almost entirely of ice crystals, form above six kilometres. Middle clouds appear between two and six kilometres and are made of a mixture of ice and water droplets. Low clouds, which sit below two kilometres, are the ones most likely to bring steady rain or drizzle. Each group is then divided into further types based on shape.

The shape of a cloud reveals how the air around it is behaving. Flat, layered clouds form when air rises gently and spreads out. Tall, puffy clouds, by contrast, form when warm air rushes upward quickly. The largest of these can grow into thunderstorms, reaching heights of more than ten kilometres. Inside, powerful currents of air lift water droplets until they freeze, fall, and melt again, sometimes producing hail.

Despite all the modern technology available, observing clouds remains a skill worth learning. Pilots study them to avoid dangerous turbulence, and hikers in the mountains watch them closely for early signs of changing conditions. The old rhymes were not always reliable, but they captured a genuine truth: the sky is constantly sending signals to anyone who takes the time to look up.

questions.q: People predicted weather from clouds before satellites existed.
High clouds are made mostly of ice crystals.
Low clouds form above six kilometres.
Weather rhymes were first written down by sailors in Europe.
What does the shape of a cloud reveal?
options: How the surrounding air is behaving
The distance to the nearest storm

questions.q: How do the tallest clouds sometimes produce hail?
options: By absorbing heat from the ground
Through currents that freeze and drop droplets repeatedly
By mixing with high ice clouds
By spreading out into flat layers

questions.q: Meteorologists classify clouds into three groups according to their ________.
Pilots study clouds to avoid dangerous ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Storm Safety
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to today's short session on staying safe during summer storms. Storms here can build up very quickly, so a little preparation goes a long way.

First, always check the forecast before you head outdoors. If a warning is issued, postpone any long walks. Most accidents happen because people ignore the early signs, such as a sudden drop in temperature or distant thunder.

Second, if you do get caught outside, avoid tall trees and open fields. The safest place is inside a building or a car. Stay there for at least thirty minutes after the last thunder you hear.

Third, keep an emergency kit at home. It should include a torch, bottled water, and a battery radio, because power often fails during severe storms. Check the batteries twice a year.

Finally, make a plan with your family. Decide on one room where everyone gathers, ideally on the ground floor away from windows. Practising this just once can save valuable seconds. Are there any questions before we move on to the demonstration?

questions.q: You should check the ________ before going outdoors.
Where is the safest place if caught outside in a storm?
Stay indoors for at least ________ minutes after the last thunder.
An emergency kit should include a torch, bottled water and a battery ________.
Batteries should be checked ________ a year.
Where should the family gathering room ideally be?
options: On the ground floor away from windows

speaking.q:
Describe a time when the weather affected your plans. You should say:
- when and where it happened
- what the weather was like
- what you had planned to do
and explain how you felt about the situation.

reading.title: Cities That Trap Heat
reading.passage:
On a warm summer evening, a large city can feel noticeably hotter than the countryside around it. This difference, which scientists call the urban heat island effect, can reach several degrees and is strongest after dark. The cause lies in the materials from which cities are built. Concrete, brick and dark road surfaces absorb heat from the sun during the day and release it slowly through the night, keeping streets warm long after the surrounding fields have cooled.

The shape of a city makes the problem worse. Tall buildings stand close together, blocking the wind that would otherwise carry heat away. They also reduce the open sky that allows warmth to escape upward. At the same time, traffic, air conditioners and factories all add their own heat directly to the air. Trees and grass, which cool the air naturally as water evaporates from their leaves, are often scarce in busy districts.

The consequences are not only uncomfortable but also costly. During heatwaves, hospitals in large cities tend to admit more patients suffering from the effects of high temperatures, particularly the elderly. Demand for electricity rises sharply as residents switch on cooling systems, which in turn produces still more heat outdoors. In this way, a hot city can trap itself in a cycle that is difficult to break.

Fortunately, planners have a range of solutions. Painting roofs white or a pale colour reflects sunlight rather than absorbing it. Planting more trees along streets provides shade and cools the air. Some cities have created new parks and even installed gardens on rooftops. None of these measures is expensive on its own, yet together they can lower a city's temperature enough to make summer far more bearable for its residents.

questions.q: The urban heat island effect is strongest during the daytime.
Concrete and dark roads release heat slowly at night.
Tall buildings can block wind that would carry heat away.
Heatwaves in cities are becoming longer each year.
Why does demand for electricity rise during heatwaves?
How do trees and grass cool the air?
options: Through water evaporating from their leaves
By absorbing heat into the soil

questions.q: Painting roofs white or a pale colour ________ sunlight instead of absorbing it.
During heatwaves, the ________ are particularly likely to suffer from high temperatures.

listening.title: Planning a City Cooling Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, for your group project on cooling the city centre, where would you like to begin?

STUDENT: We thought we'd start with the main square. It has no shade at all right now.

TUTOR: Good choice. What's your first proposal?

STUDENT: Planting twenty new trees around the edges. They'd give shade by the afternoon and cool the air.

TUTOR: Sounds sensible. Have you looked at the cost?

STUDENT: Each tree, with planting, is about ninety dollars. So roughly eighteen hundred in total.

TUTOR: Reasonable. And the second idea?

STUDENT: Repainting the rooftops of the shops white. The council estimates that could lower the local temperature by two degrees.

TUTOR: Impressive. When do you plan to start?

STUDENT: In March, before summer arrives. We'll need permission from the city office first.

TUTOR: Right. And how will you measure success?

STUDENT: We'll record the temperature every day for a month and compare it with last year's figures.

TUTOR: Excellent. That's a clear and practical plan.
