reading.title: The Science of Snow
reading.passage:
Snow is far more complex than the simple white blanket it appears to be. Each snowflake begins high in the atmosphere when water vapour freezes around a tiny particle of dust. As the growing crystal falls, it passes through layers of air at different temperatures and levels of moisture, and these conditions shape its final form. This is why snowflakes display such an astonishing variety of patterns, although almost all share a six-sided structure.

The type of snow that reaches the ground depends largely on temperature. When the air is very cold and dry, flakes stay small and powdery, and they do not stick together well. Skiers prize this light snow because it is fast and soft. When the air is closer to freezing, the flakes are wetter and heavier, clumping together easily. This kind of snow is ideal for building, but it places great strain on roofs and power lines.

Snow has a surprising influence on temperature itself. Its bright surface reflects most of the sunlight that strikes it, sending the sun's energy back into the sky rather than allowing the ground to warm. A landscape covered in fresh snow therefore stays cold, which in turn helps the snow to last. Scientists studying the climate pay close attention to how much of the Earth's surface is covered by snow and ice at any given time.

For communities in cold regions, snow is both a gift and a burden. A deep winter snowpack acts as a natural store of water, slowly melting in spring to feed rivers and fill reservoirs. Yet the same snow can block roads, collapse buildings and cut off remote villages for days. Learning to predict heavy snowfall accurately therefore remains an important goal for forecasters everywhere.

questions.q: A snowflake forms around a tiny particle of dust.
Almost all snowflakes share a six-sided structure.
Very cold, dry conditions produce wet, heavy snow.
Snowflakes are larger now than they were a century ago.
Why does a snow-covered landscape stay cold?
options: The snow traps heat near the ground
Its bright surface reflects sunlight back into the sky
The air above it is always still

questions.q: How does a winter snowpack benefit communities?
options: It melts slowly to feed rivers and reservoirs
It prevents all flooding in spring

questions.q: Skiers prize light, ________ snow because it is fast and soft.
Wet, heavy snow places great ________ on roofs and power lines.

listening.title: A Lecture on Mountain Snowpack
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to look at how we measure the snow that builds up in mountains over winter, and why it matters so much for water supply.

The most basic tool is the snow stake, a marked pole pushed into the ground. Observers simply read the depth against the markings. It's cheap, but it tells us nothing about how much water the snow contains.

For that, we weigh a sample. A column of snow is collected in a tube and weighed, giving what we call the water equivalent. Two snowpacks of equal depth can hold very different amounts of water, so this figure is far more useful for planning.

Increasingly, though, we rely on automatic sensors. These devices sit on a flat pad and record the weight of the snow above them every hour, sending the data by radio. A single station can run for an entire season without a visit.

Why does all this matter? In many regions, more than half of the summer river flow comes from melting mountain snow. If we can estimate the snowpack accurately in March, we can predict the water supply for the months ahead.

questions.q: The most basic measuring tool is the snow ________.
What is the main weakness of the basic tool?
options: It cannot show how much water the snow contains
It breaks easily in cold weather
It needs two people to use

questions.q: Weighing a snow sample gives the water ________.
Automatic sensors record the weight of the snow every ________.
How do the automatic sensors send their data?

speaking.q: Let's talk about cold weather and winter. Does it snow where you live? What activities do people in your country enjoy during the colder months?

Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: When the Wind Does the Work
reading.passage:
Wind is simply air on the move, flowing from areas of high pressure towards areas of low pressure. The greater the difference in pressure between two places, the faster the wind blows. Because pressure differences are created mainly by uneven heating of the Earth's surface, wind is ultimately powered by the sun. This endless movement of air carries heat and moisture around the planet and shapes weather everywhere.

For thousands of years, humans have found ways to put the wind to work. Sailing ships once carried goods across entire oceans, and windmills ground grain and pumped water long before electricity existed. Today the most visible use of wind is the wind turbine, a tall tower topped by long blades that spin as the air passes. The spinning drives a generator, which converts the movement into electricity without burning any fuel.

The attraction of wind power is clear. It produces no smoke and uses a resource that will never run out. However, it also has limitations. The wind does not blow steadily, so output rises and falls throughout the day and from season to season. Engineers cannot simply switch a turbine on when demand is high. To manage this, electricity grids combine wind with other sources and increasingly store surplus power in large batteries for later use.

Where turbines are built matters greatly. The best sites are open coasts, hilltops and offshore waters, where the wind is strong and reliable. Yet such places are sometimes valued for their scenery or used by wildlife, and local residents may object to the appearance or the noise. Planners must therefore weigh the clear environmental benefits of wind power against these genuine concerns, a balance that is rarely simple to strike.

questions.q: Wind flows from areas of high pressure towards areas of low pressure.
Windmills were used to grind grain before electricity existed.
Wind turbines burn a small amount of fuel to start spinning.
Wind power is now cheaper than all other forms of electricity.
What is ultimately the source of the wind's power?
options: The rotation of the Earth alone

questions.q: Why can engineers not simply switch on a turbine when demand is high?
options: The blades are too heavy to start
The wind does not blow steadily
Turbines need fuel that may be unavailable
Grids are not connected to turbines

questions.q: Surplus electricity is increasingly stored in large ________ for later use.

listening.title: A Visit to a Wind Farm
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome to Ridgeway Wind Farm. Before we walk out to the turbines, let me give you a few facts about the site.

The farm opened in two thousand and nineteen and now has fourteen turbines spread across the hilltop. Each one stands about eighty metres tall to the centre of the blades.

VISITOR: That's enormous. How much electricity does the whole farm produce?

GUIDE: On a windy day, enough to power roughly nine thousand homes. Of course, output drops on calm days, which is why we're connected to the national grid.

VISITOR: Do the turbines need much maintenance?

GUIDE: They're inspected every six months. A small team climbs inside the tower to check the machinery. It's a tough job, especially in winter.

VISITOR: And what about the birds? People often worry about that.

GUIDE: It's a fair concern. We carried out a two-year study before building and chose this ridge partly because few birds use it. We still monitor them closely.

VISITOR: That's reassuring. Shall we head up?

GUIDE: Yes, please follow the marked path and keep your helmets on.

options: It was the cheapest land available
It was close to a city

reading.title: Why Athletes Need Recovery
reading.passage:
Many people assume that the harder an athlete trains, the better they will perform. In reality, modern sports science suggests that rest is just as important as effort. When the body is pushed hard during exercise, tiny tears appear in the muscle fibres. It is during periods of rest, rather than during the workout itself, that these fibres repair and grow stronger. Without enough recovery time, the body never has the chance to adapt, and performance may actually decline.

One of the most valuable forms of recovery is sleep. During deep sleep, the body releases growth hormone, which plays a key role in repairing tissue. Studies of professional athletes have found that those who sleep fewer than seven hours a night tend to suffer more injuries and recover more slowly. For this reason, some elite teams now employ sleep specialists alongside their coaches.

Nutrition also influences how quickly the body bounces back. After intense exercise, muscles need protein to rebuild and carbohydrates to restore their energy stores. Eating the right foods soon after training can shorten recovery time considerably. Drinking enough water matters too, because even mild dehydration can slow the repair process and reduce concentration.

Not all recovery means doing nothing. Light activity, often called active recovery, can help as well. A gentle jog or an easy swim increases blood flow to tired muscles, which carries away waste products and delivers nutrients. Many coaches now plan recovery days as carefully as they plan training sessions, treating rest not as a weakness but as an essential part of becoming a better athlete.

questions.q: Muscle fibres mainly grow stronger during the workout itself.
Athletes who sleep fewer than seven hours tend to get injured more often.
Sleep specialists earn more than coaches on elite teams.
Mild dehydration can reduce an athlete's concentration.
According to the passage, active recovery helps because it:
options: increases blood flow to tired muscles

questions.q: During deep sleep, the body releases ________ hormone, which helps repair tissue.
After training, muscles need protein to rebuild and ________ to restore energy stores.
What is the writer's main point about rest?
options: It is a sign of weakness
It is less important than effort
It is an essential part of training
It is only needed by beginners

listening.title: Booking a Gym Induction
listening.script:
STAFF: Good afternoon, Riverside Fitness Centre, how can I help?

STAFF: Certainly. Can I take your name?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Brooks. B-R-O-O-K-S.

CALLER: Great. When are they available?

CALLER: Thursday morning suits me better.

CALLER: No problem. Is there parking?
STAFF: Yes, it's free for the first two hours. After that it's three pounds.
CALLER: Perfect. See you on Thursday.

reading.title: The Rise of Wearable Fitness Trackers
reading.passage:
In the past decade, small electronic devices worn on the wrist have changed the way millions of people exercise. Known as fitness trackers, these gadgets count steps, measure heart rate and estimate how many calories a person burns each day. For many users, simply being able to see this information encourages them to move more and sit less.

The technology behind these devices is surprisingly clever. Tiny sensors detect the movement of the body, while others shine light onto the skin to measure the flow of blood. From these signals, the device calculates a wide range of figures, from the distance walked to the quality of a night's sleep. The data is then sent to a smartphone, where it is displayed in colourful charts.

Supporters argue that fitness trackers make people more aware of their habits. Setting a daily target, such as ten thousand steps, gives users a clear goal to aim for. Some studies have shown that people who wear trackers do indeed become slightly more active, at least in the first few months. Many users also enjoy comparing their results with friends, which adds a competitive element.

However, the devices are not without their critics. The figures they produce are estimates rather than precise measurements, and the accuracy can vary from one brand to another. There are also concerns about privacy, since personal health data is stored online. Perhaps the biggest weakness is that motivation often fades. Many people wear their tracker enthusiastically for a few weeks, then quietly leave it in a drawer. The device alone cannot create lasting change; that still depends on the person wearing it.

questions.q: Fitness trackers can estimate the number of calories a person burns.
All brands of fitness tracker measure data with the same accuracy.

A common daily target is ________ thousand steps.
Concerns about ________ arise because personal health data is stored online.
What does the final sentence suggest?
options: Change depends on the user, not the device

listening.title: A Tutorial on Sports Data
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, today I want to talk about how teams use data to improve performance. Years ago, coaches relied mostly on what they could see. Now, players wear small devices during matches that record their movement.
STUDENT: What kind of information do they collect?
TUTOR: Quite a lot. The most common figure is total distance covered in a game. A midfield player in football, for example, might run around eleven kilometres in ninety minutes.
STUDENT: That's a lot. What else?
TUTOR: They also track sprint speed and heart rate. This helps the coach see when a player is getting tired, so they can be substituted before they get injured.
STUDENT: So the data prevents injuries?
TUTOR: It can reduce them, yes. But remember, the numbers must be interpreted carefully. A high distance doesn't always mean good play. For your essay, due on Friday, focus on both the benefits and the limits of using data.

and explain whether you think it has really helped you become healthier.

reading.title: The Science of Hydration in Sport
reading.passage:
Water makes up more than half of the human body, so it is no surprise that staying properly hydrated is vital for anyone taking part in sport. When we exercise, the body warms up, and to cool itself it produces sweat. As this sweat evaporates from the skin, it carries heat away. The problem is that sweating also removes water and important salts known as electrolytes, and if these are not replaced, performance can suffer.

The effects of dehydration can appear sooner than many people expect. Losing as little as two per cent of body weight through sweat can cause noticeable tiredness and a drop in concentration. As the loss increases, an athlete may experience headaches, muscle cramps and a faster heartbeat. In hot conditions, severe dehydration can even become dangerous, which is why marathon organisers place water stations along the route.

For most everyday exercise, plain water is enough to keep the body topped up. However, during long or intense activity lasting more than an hour, sports drinks may be useful. These contain not only water but also sugar for energy and electrolytes to replace those lost in sweat. Critics point out, though, that many people drink them unnecessarily, adding extra sugar to their diet without any real benefit.

Interestingly, it is also possible to drink too much. In rare cases, athletes who consume enormous amounts of water dilute the salt in their blood to a dangerous level. The best advice, experts say, is simple: drink when you feel thirsty, and pay attention to the colour of your urine, which should be pale rather than dark.

listening.title: A Talk on Preparing for a Race
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to this session on preparing for your first ten-kilometre race. Let me share a few practical tips. First, training. You should build up your distance slowly over about eight weeks, never increasing your weekly mileage by more than ten per cent.
The night before the race, eat a meal rich in carbohydrates, such as pasta or rice, but don't try anything unfamiliar. On race morning, have a light breakfast around two hours before the start.
Hydration matters too. Drink water regularly in the days before, but don't overdrink on the morning itself. During the race there will be water stations at the three- and six-kilometre marks.
Finally, pacing. The most common mistake is starting too fast. Begin slightly slower than your target speed, and you'll have energy left for a strong finish. Remember to register online by Wednesday, and arrive at least thirty minutes early to collect your number.

reading.title: The Mental Side of Performance
reading.passage:
When we watch a top athlete win a tight contest, we often praise their physical strength or natural talent. Yet coaches increasingly agree that what happens in the mind can matter just as much as what happens in the body. At the highest level, where competitors are separated by tiny margins, the ability to stay calm under pressure can decide who wins and who loses.

One technique used by many athletes is visualisation. Before a competition, they close their eyes and imagine themselves performing perfectly, picturing every movement in detail. Although this may sound unscientific, research suggests that mentally rehearsing an action activates many of the same areas in the brain as actually performing it. In effect, the athlete practises without moving a muscle.

Managing nerves is another important skill. A certain amount of anxiety is normal and can even sharpen focus, but too much can cause muscles to tense and decisions to slow. Sports psychologists teach athletes to control their breathing and to repeat short, positive phrases to themselves. Such routines help the competitor feel in control, even in front of a huge crowd.

Confidence, however, must be built on solid foundations. Telling yourself you will win is of little use if you have not done the necessary training. The most effective athletes combine careful preparation with mental strategies, so that on the day of the event their bodies and minds work together. As one well-known coach put it, the body achieves what the mind believes, but only when the body has been properly prepared.

questions.q: Coaches now believe the mind can matter as much as the body.
Visualisation activates similar brain areas to actually performing an action.
A small amount of anxiety can help sharpen an athlete's focus.
Most athletes prefer visualisation to physical training.
According to the passage, confidence is only useful when it is combined with:
Sports psychologists teach athletes to control their ________ to manage nerves.
Mentally practising a movement is called ________.
The coach's final quotation suggests that mental belief works:

listening.title: Interview with a Sports Psychologist
listening.script:
HOST: Today I'm speaking with Dr Lena Hart, a sports psychologist. Lena, what does your job actually involve?
DR HART: Well, I help athletes train their minds, not just their bodies. A big part of my work is helping them deal with pressure during important events.
HOST: What's the most common problem you see?
DR HART: Fear of failure, definitely. Many athletes are so worried about losing that they hold back and don't perform freely.
HOST: How do you help with that?
DR HART: We work on focusing on the process rather than the result. Instead of thinking about the medal, the athlete concentrates on each small step.
HOST: Does that really work?
DR HART: It does. We also use goal-setting. I ask athletes to set goals that are challenging but realistic, and to write them down. Studies show written goals are far more likely to be achieved.
HOST: Fascinating. Where can people learn more?
DR HART: I run a free workshop every month, and details are on my website.

reading.title: Bringing Older Adults Back to Sport
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, organised sport was thought of as an activity for the young. Older people were often advised to take things easy, and many believed that vigorous exercise in later life was risky. In recent years, however, this attitude has changed dramatically. A growing body of research shows that regular physical activity offers enormous benefits to older adults, and communities around the world are working to bring them back into sport.

The advantages go well beyond physical fitness. Of course, staying active helps to keep muscles strong, supports the heart and reduces the risk of several diseases. But studies also point to clear mental benefits. Older adults who exercise regularly report better sleep, improved mood and a sharper memory. Team sports in particular help to combat loneliness, which can be a serious problem in later life.

Designing suitable activities, however, requires care. High-impact sports that place great strain on the joints may not be appropriate for everyone. Instead, many programmes focus on gentler options such as swimming, cycling and walking groups. A version of football played at walking pace, sometimes called walking football, has proved surprisingly popular, allowing people to enjoy the game without the risk of hard falls.

Despite these efforts, barriers remain. Some older people lack confidence, fearing that they are too unfit or too old to begin. Others face practical obstacles, such as the cost of facilities or a lack of transport. Successful schemes tend to be those that are welcoming and affordable, and that treat participants as capable individuals rather than as people to be looked after. When given the right opportunities, the organisers say, age need be no barrier to an active life.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, sport was mainly seen as an activity for young people.

Walking football is played at the same speed as normal football.

PRESENTER: Good evening, everyone. I'm here to tell you about our new community programme, Active Years, aimed at adults over sixty. Our goal is simple: to get more older residents moving, in a friendly and relaxed setting.
We offer four main activities. There are walking groups every Monday morning, gentle yoga on Wednesdays, swimming sessions on Friday afternoons, and a very popular walking football match on Saturdays.
Now, cost. Membership is just five dollars a month, which covers all the activities. If you'd rather try one session first, a single visit costs two dollars.
We meet at the Greenfield Community Hall on Maple Street. There's free parking, and the bus number fourteen stops right outside.
To join, you can sign up at the front desk or call us on the number on your leaflet. We do ask new members to fill in a short health form first. We look forward to seeing you there.

reading.title: The Four-Day Working Week
reading.passage:
In recent years, a growing number of companies have begun to experiment with the four-day working week. Under this arrangement, employees work four days instead of five but continue to receive the same pay. The idea is not simply to give workers more rest; supporters argue that a shorter week can actually make people more productive during the hours they do work.

The largest trial to date took place in the United Kingdom, where around sixty companies allowed their staff to work fewer hours for six months. The results were encouraging. Most of the firms reported that output remained stable or even improved slightly. Many employees said they felt less stressed and more able to balance their jobs with family life. At the end of the trial, the great majority of the companies chose to keep the new system.

Not every business found the change easy, however. Some managers complained that fitting the same amount of work into fewer days created pressure rather than removing it. In certain industries, such as healthcare and customer service, where staff must be available at all times, the model proved difficult to apply. Critics also warned that the benefits seen in short trials might not last over several years.

Despite these doubts, interest in the four-day week continues to spread. Governments in several countries are now funding their own studies, and some economists believe that, as technology takes over more routine tasks, working time may gradually fall in any case. Whether the four-day week becomes the norm or remains an exception, it has already changed the way many people think about the relationship between time and work.

questions.q: Workers on a four-day week usually receive less pay than before.
The UK trial involved companies from more than ten different countries.
What did most firms in the UK trial report about output?
options: it remained stable or improved slightly

questions.q: At the end of the trial, the great ________ of companies decided to keep the new system.

Some economists think working time may fall as technology takes over more ________ tasks.

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a desk for next month. I'm a freelancer and I need somewhere quiet to work.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We offer two main options. A flexible day pass costs fifteen pounds a day, or you can take a fixed desk for the whole month at two hundred and forty pounds.

CALLER: I think the monthly desk would suit me better. Does that include internet?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, high-speed internet and unlimited coffee are both included. The only extra charge is for the meeting rooms, which cost ten pounds an hour.

CALLER: Perfect. Can I start on the first of July?

RECEPTIONIST: Certainly. Could I take your name and email address so I can send the confirmation?

CALLER: It's Daniel Carter, and my email is dcarter at mail dot com.

reading.title: Automation and the Changing Factory
reading.passage:
The image many people hold of a modern factory is one of long rows of machines doing work that humans once did by hand. Automation, the use of machines to carry out tasks without constant human control, has indeed transformed manufacturing over the past century. Yet the effect on workers has been more complicated than simple replacement.

When factories first introduced large machines, many feared that human workers would no longer be needed. In some cases jobs did disappear, particularly those involving repetitive physical labour. However, new kinds of work also emerged. Machines had to be designed, built, programmed and repaired, and these tasks required skills that did not exist before. The total number of jobs did not always fall; instead, the nature of the work shifted.

Today, a further wave of change is taking place. Modern robots can learn from experience and adjust their behaviour, allowing them to handle tasks that were once thought too delicate or unpredictable for a machine. In some factories, robots and humans now work side by side on the same production line, with the machine taking on the heavy or dangerous parts of a job while the worker handles judgement and quality.

Economists disagree about where this trend will lead. Some warn that, as machines grow more capable, fewer workers will be needed and wages may suffer. Others argue that history shows technology tends to create as many jobs as it destroys, even if the new roles look very different. What seems clear is that the workers of the future will need to keep learning, since the skills valued today may not be the ones in demand tomorrow.

questions.q: Automation has affected workers in a simple and straightforward way.

According to the passage, what often happened to the nature of factory work?
options: it shifted to new kinds of tasks

listening.title: Talk: A Tour of the Robotics Lab
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the university robotics laboratory. I'll give you a quick tour before you try the equipment yourselves. To your left is our main workshop, where students build and test small robots. Please don't touch the machines while they're running, as the moving arms can be dangerous.

Straight ahead is the testing area. This is where we measure how accurately a robot can pick up and place objects. Last year our best team built a robot that could sort thirty items a minute, which is a record for the lab.

Now, a few practical points. The lab opens at nine and closes at six on weekdays. If you want to use it at the weekend, you'll need to book a slot online in advance. Storage lockers are available, but you must bring your own padlock.

Finally, safety glasses are compulsory in the workshop at all times. You can collect a pair from the box by the door. Right, are there any questions before we begin?

reading.title: The Rise of the Gig Economy
reading.passage:
Over the last decade, a new way of working has spread rapidly across many countries. In the so-called gig economy, people are paid for individual tasks or short assignments rather than holding a permanent job with a single employer. A driver might deliver meals for one company in the morning and parcels for another in the afternoon, choosing their own hours through a smartphone application.

For many workers, the appeal lies in flexibility. They can decide when and how much to work, fitting their jobs around studies, family or other commitments. Companies, too, benefit, since they can expand or reduce their workforce quickly according to demand, without the cost of employing large numbers of permanent staff. Supporters argue that this freedom suits a modern economy in which needs change from week to week.

Critics, however, point to a darker side. Gig workers are usually classed as self-employed, which means they often lack the protections that ordinary employees enjoy, such as sick pay, paid holidays and a guaranteed minimum wage. Income can be unstable, rising and falling with demand, and workers may feel pressure to accept poor conditions for fear of losing access to the platform that provides their work.

Governments are now struggling to decide how such workers should be treated under the law. In some places, courts have ruled that certain gig workers are in fact employees and are therefore entitled to greater protection. The companies involved have often resisted, arguing that stricter rules would raise costs and reduce the very flexibility that makes the model attractive. The outcome of these disputes is likely to shape the future of work for millions of people.

options: they can adjust their workforce quickly according to demand

reading.title: Lifelong Learning in a Changing Job Market
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, a typical career followed a simple pattern. A person trained for a particular trade or profession when young, then practised it for several decades before retiring. The skills learned in early life were expected to remain useful for an entire working lifetime. In today's economy, this assumption is increasingly questioned.

The reason is the speed at which industries now change. New technologies appear and spread within a few years, reshaping the tasks that workers are asked to perform. A skill that is highly valued today may become unnecessary surprisingly quickly, while entirely new kinds of expertise are suddenly in demand. As a result, many experts argue that learning can no longer be confined to the first part of life but must continue throughout it.

This idea, often called lifelong learning, takes many forms. Some workers return to formal education, taking evening courses or studying online while continuing to work. Others learn more informally, picking up new skills from colleagues or through short training programmes provided by their employers. Governments in several countries have begun to support these efforts, offering grants or tax benefits to those who retrain.

There are obstacles, however. Adults with busy jobs and family responsibilities may struggle to find the time or money to study. Older workers, in particular, sometimes feel discouraged, fearing that employers prefer younger staff. Supporters of lifelong learning insist that these barriers can be reduced through flexible, affordable courses, and that the alternative — a workforce whose skills slowly fall out of date — is far worse. In an age of rapid change, they argue, the ability to keep learning may itself be the most valuable skill of all.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, early skills were expected to last for a whole working life.
Why is the traditional career pattern now being questioned?
Experts argue that learning must now continue ________ life rather than only at the start.
Lifelong learning can only take place through formal education.

Online courses have been shown to be more effective than evening classes.

options: a workforce whose skills become out of date

listening.title: Lecture: Skills for the Future Workplace
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to talk about the skills that employers say they will need most in the coming decade. Surveys of large companies point to a clear shift. Technical knowledge still matters, of course, but increasingly firms are looking for what we call soft skills.

The first of these is communication. As teams become more spread out, often working across different countries, the ability to explain ideas clearly is essential. The second is adaptability. Because roles change so often, workers who can adjust quickly are highly valued.

The third skill, perhaps surprisingly, is creativity. Machines are good at routine work, but they are still poor at coming up with genuinely new ideas. So the tasks left to humans are often those that require imagination.

Now, an important point: these skills are difficult to teach in a traditional classroom. They are usually developed through practice and experience. That is why many universities are now redesigning their courses to include more group projects and real-world problems. I'll hand out a reading list at the end that explores this in more detail.

reading.title: Working From Anywhere: The Remote Revolution
reading.passage:
Not long ago, the idea that large numbers of people might do their jobs from home, or indeed from almost anywhere, seemed unlikely. Office work was tied to a particular building, and being physically present was widely seen as proof of effort. Within a remarkably short period, this view has been overturned, and remote working has become a normal feature of many industries.

The shift was driven partly by necessity and partly by technology. When circumstances forced offices to close, companies discovered that, with reliable internet and the right software, much office work could continue smoothly outside the workplace. Video calls replaced meetings, and documents could be shared instantly across great distances. Many employers were surprised to find that productivity did not collapse, as some had feared.

The benefits have been considerable. Workers save the time and expense of commuting, and some report a better balance between their jobs and personal lives. Companies can recruit talented people regardless of where they live, widening the pool of candidates far beyond a single city. A few firms have even closed their offices entirely, passing the savings on in other ways.

Yet remote work is not without drawbacks. Some employees miss the social contact of an office and find it harder to separate work from home life, leading to longer hours and, at times, a sense of isolation. Managers worry about maintaining a shared culture when staff rarely meet in person. For these reasons, many organisations have settled on a compromise known as hybrid working, in which employees split their time between home and the office. Whether this balance proves lasting remains to be seen, but the assumption that work must happen in one fixed place has gone, perhaps for good.

questions.q: In the past, being physically present at work was widely seen as proof of effort.
What surprised many employers about remote work?
options: no one wanted to do it
it was impossible with current technology

questions.q: Workers save the time and expense of ________ when they work remotely.
Remote work allows companies to recruit people regardless of where they live.
Some employees feel a sense of ________ when working away from the office.
Most workers say they would never want to return to an office at all.
Many organisations have adopted a compromise known as ________ working.
What does the writer suggest about the future of work?
options: work will return to one fixed place
the idea of one fixed workplace has probably gone for good
remote work will soon be banned

listening.title: Meeting: Planning a Hybrid Schedule
listening.script:
MANAGER: Thanks for joining, everyone. Today we need to agree on our new hybrid schedule for the team. As you know, head office wants us in the building at least two days a week.

EMPLOYEE: Could we choose which two days ourselves?

MANAGER: To some extent, yes, but we'd like everyone present on Wednesdays for the team meeting. The second day is up to you.

EMPLOYEE: That works for me. What about the people who live further away?

MANAGER: Good question. For staff who travel more than two hours each way, we'll allow one office day a week instead of two. They just need to let me know by email.

EMPLOYEE: And the desks? Will we each have our own?

MANAGER: No, we're moving to a booking system. You'll reserve a desk through the app the day before. There are forty desks for sixty staff, so please book early. Right, I'll send round the full details this afternoon, and we'll start the new system on Monday the third.

reading.title: The Hidden Power of Ocean Currents
reading.passage:
Beneath the surface of the world's oceans lies a vast system of moving water that shapes the climate of entire continents. These ocean currents act rather like rivers within the sea, carrying warm or cold water across thousands of kilometres. They are driven mainly by two forces: the wind blowing across the surface, and differences in the temperature and saltiness of the water itself. Where water is warm and less salty it tends to rise, while colder, saltier water sinks, creating a slow but powerful circulation.

One of the best known currents is the Gulf Stream, which transports warm water from the tropics towards north-western Europe. Without it, countries such as Britain and Norway would experience far harsher winters, since their latitude is similar to that of regions in Canada that lie under deep snow for months. The heat released by this current keeps coastal cities milder and supports the mild, wet weather many Europeans take for granted.

Currents also play a vital role in marine life. As deep water rises towards the surface in a process called upwelling, it brings nutrients that have settled on the ocean floor. These nutrients feed tiny plants known as plankton, which in turn support fish, seabirds and larger animals. Some of the richest fishing grounds on the planet are found exactly where upwelling occurs, such as off the coast of Peru.

Scientists are increasingly concerned that a warming planet could weaken some of these currents. Melting ice adds large amounts of fresh water to the northern seas, which may slow the sinking of cold, salty water that helps drive the whole system. If the circulation were to slow significantly, weather patterns across the globe could shift in ways that are difficult to predict. For this reason, researchers now monitor the great currents closely, treating them as an early warning sign of wider changes in the climate.

questions.q: Ocean currents are driven partly by differences in how salty the water is.
Without the Gulf Stream, winters in Britain would probably be colder.
Upwelling carries nutrients from the surface down to the ocean floor.
The Peruvian government has introduced laws to protect its fishing grounds.
According to the passage, melting ice may weaken ocean currents because it
options: raises the temperature of the tropics
adds fresh water that slows the sinking of cold, salty water
blocks the path of the Gulf Stream

questions.q: Scientists monitor the great currents mainly because they
options: can act as an early warning sign of climate change
hope to redirect them towards Europe
need to map the ocean floor

questions.q: Tiny plants called ________ feed on the nutrients brought up by upwelling.

listening.title: Booking a Coastal Boat Tour
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Harbour Wildlife Tours, how can I help you?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a place on one of your boat trips to see the seals.

STAFF: Lovely. The seal-watching tour runs every morning at half past nine and lasts about two hours.

CALLER: Great. How much is a ticket?

STAFF: It's eighteen pounds for an adult, and twelve for children under twelve.

CALLER: I'll take two adults and one child, please.

STAFF: That's forty-eight pounds altogether. Could I take a name?

CALLER: Yes, it's Sarah Doyle. That's D-O-Y-L-E.

STAFF: Thank you. And which day would you like?

CALLER: This Saturday, the fourteenth.

STAFF: Perfect. Please arrive at least fifteen minutes early, at the ticket office on Pier Three. Do bring a warm jacket, as it can be windy out on the water.

CALLER: Will do. Is there parking nearby?

STAFF: There's a car park right behind the harbour, but it fills up quickly, so public transport is best.

CALLER: Thanks very much.

reading.title: The Problem of Ocean Plastic
reading.passage:
Every year, millions of tonnes of plastic waste end up in the world's oceans. Some of it is dropped on beaches or thrown from boats, but the majority is carried out to sea by rivers that pass through crowded cities. Once in the water, plastic does not simply disappear. Instead, sunlight and waves slowly break it into ever smaller pieces, until much of it becomes tiny fragments known as microplastics, less than five millimetres across.

These small particles are now found almost everywhere, from the deepest ocean trenches to the ice of the polar regions. Because they are so small, microplastics are easily swallowed by fish and other sea creatures, which mistake them for food. The plastic can fill their stomachs without providing any nourishment, and harmful chemicals attached to the surface may pass up the food chain. Scientists have even detected microplastics in seafood sold for human consumption.

Larger pieces of plastic cause problems of their own. Discarded fishing nets, sometimes called ghost gear, continue to trap fish, turtles and seabirds long after they have been lost or abandoned. Floating waste also gathers in huge patches where currents meet, the most famous lying between California and Hawaii. Cleaning up such areas is extremely difficult, as the plastic is spread thinly over an enormous region.

Many experts argue that stopping plastic at its source is more effective than removing it from the sea. Some countries have banned single-use plastic bags, while companies are being encouraged to design packaging that can be reused or easily recycled. Individuals, too, can reduce the flow of waste by avoiding unnecessary plastic and disposing of rubbish responsibly. Although the scale of the problem is daunting, a combination of better design, stricter rules and changes in everyday habits offers real hope of cleaner oceans in the future.

questions.q: Most ocean plastic enters the sea through rivers rather than directly from beaches or boats.
Microplastics are defined as pieces larger than five millimetres.
Microplastics have been found in seafood intended for people.
The largest patch of floating plastic lies between Japan and Australia.
The first ban on single-use plastic bags was introduced in Europe.

options: They are too large to swallow

They block the fish's gills only

listening.title: A Talk on the Beach Clean-up Programme
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, and thanks for joining our community beach clean-up. Before we start, let me explain how the morning will run. We'll be working in three teams, and each team will cover a different section of the shore. Team One takes the northern end near the rocks, Team Two the central area by the café, and Team Three the southern dunes.

Each of you will be given a pair of thick gloves and a large bag. Please don't pick up anything sharp with your bare hands. If you find something heavy or dangerous, such as broken glass or a piece of metal, leave it and tell a team leader. We'll deal with those items separately.

We'll work until eleven o'clock, then meet back here at the main hut for a free drink and a short talk on recycling. Last month our volunteers collected over three hundred kilograms of rubbish in a single morning, so every bag really counts. Remember to drink plenty of water, and wear a hat, because there's very little shade. Right, let's get started.

reading.title: Life in the Deep Sea
reading.passage:
The deep ocean is one of the least explored places on Earth. Below about two hundred metres, sunlight can no longer reach the water, and the world becomes a region of permanent darkness, crushing pressure and near-freezing temperatures. For a long time, scientists assumed that almost nothing could survive in such conditions. Yet research using deep-diving submarines has revealed a surprising variety of life adapted to this hostile environment.

Many deep-sea creatures have developed remarkable features to cope with the lack of light. A large number produce their own glow through a chemical process called bioluminescence. Some use this light to attract prey, dangling a glowing lure in front of their mouths, while others flash signals to confuse predators or to find a mate. Because food is scarce, many fish have enormous mouths and stomachs that allow them to swallow prey larger than themselves, since they cannot afford to miss a meal.

In 1977, researchers made one of the most important discoveries in marine biology. Near volcanic openings on the ocean floor, known as hydrothermal vents, they found thriving communities of animals living entirely without sunlight. Instead of relying on plants, these ecosystems are supported by bacteria that obtain energy from the chemicals pouring out of the vents. Giant tube worms, crabs and clams gather around the warm water, forming oases of life in the surrounding desert of cold mud.

Studying the deep sea is difficult and expensive, requiring specially built equipment that can withstand immense pressure. Even so, scientists believe that countless species remain undiscovered. Some researchers also worry that mining companies, attracted by valuable metals on the sea floor, could damage these fragile habitats before they are properly understood. As exploration continues, the deep ocean is proving to be far richer and more mysterious than anyone once imagined.

Scientists always expected the deep sea to be full of life.
Some deep-sea fish can swallow prey larger than their own bodies.
The communities near hydrothermal vents depend on plants for food.
The discovery of hydrothermal vents won a major scientific prize.
According to the passage, bioluminescence is used by some creatures to
Why are some scientists concerned about deep-sea mining?
options: It could damage fragile habitats before they are understood
It makes the water warmer everywhere

listening.title: Student Tutorial on a Marine Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Maya, you've chosen the deep sea for your research project. Have you decided on a focus yet?

MAYA: Yes, I'd like to look at how creatures survive without light, especially bioluminescence.

TUTOR: That's a strong topic. How are you planning to gather your information?

MAYA: Mainly from journal articles, but I'd also like to interview a marine biologist if possible.

TUTOR: Good idea. I can give you the email of Dr Patel, who studies vent ecosystems. Just keep your questions short and clear.

MAYA: Thank you. How long should the final report be?

TUTOR: Around two thousand words, plus a list of references. And remember the deadline is the third of May, so don't leave it too late.

MAYA: Should I include diagrams?

TUTOR: Yes, one or two clear diagrams really help, but make sure you label the source. Also, try to read at least eight academic sources rather than relying on websites.

MAYA: Right. I'll start with the library database this afternoon.

TUTOR: Perfect. Send me a short plan by Friday and we'll review it together.

reading.title: The Vanishing Coral Reefs
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often described as the rainforests of the sea, and the comparison is well deserved. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they provide a home for around a quarter of all marine species. Fish, crabs, sea snails and countless other creatures depend on the shelter and food that reefs supply. For many coastal communities, healthy reefs also mean steady incomes from fishing and tourism, as well as natural protection against powerful storm waves.

A coral reef is built by tiny animals called polyps, which form hard skeletons of limestone over many years. Inside their tissues live microscopic algae that use sunlight to produce food, sharing it with the coral and giving the reef its brilliant colours. This partnership is delicate. When the surrounding water becomes too warm, even by just one or two degrees for several weeks, the coral expels the algae and turns ghostly white, a process known as bleaching. Without the algae, the coral begins to starve, and if the heat continues, large areas of reef may die.

Climate change is the greatest long-term threat, but reefs face many other pressures. Pollution from farms and cities feeds harmful seaweed that smothers the coral, while careless fishing methods and the dropping of anchors cause direct physical damage. In some regions, divers and boats bring valuable tourism, yet too many visitors can also harm the very reefs they come to admire.

Despite the gloomy picture, conservationists have not given up. Marine protected areas now restrict fishing in vulnerable zones, allowing fish populations to recover. Scientists are growing young corals in underwater nurseries and replanting them on damaged reefs, and some are even searching for heat-resistant varieties that might cope better with warmer seas. Saving the reefs will require global action on climate, but these local efforts show that recovery is still possible.

questions.q: Coral reefs are home to roughly a quarter of all marine species.
Bleaching happens when coral takes in extra algae.
Pollution from farms can encourage seaweed that smothers coral.
Coral reefs cover more than half of the ocean floor.
Tourism income from reefs is higher than fishing income in most countries.
According to the passage, the algae living inside coral
options: produce food using sunlight and share it with the coral

options: Growing young corals to replant them

listening.title: Radio Feature on Reef Tourism
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Today we're talking about how tourism can help, rather than harm, coral reefs. I'm joined by reef guide Tom Bryce. Tom, welcome.

TOM: Thanks for having me.

PRESENTER: So, can tourists really make a difference?

TOM: Absolutely. On our tours, every visitor pays a small reef fee of five dollars, and that money goes straight into local conservation projects.

PRESENTER: What kind of projects?

TOM: Mostly coral planting. Last year alone we replanted around two thousand young corals along one stretch of reef.

PRESENTER: Impressive. And how do you protect the reef from the divers themselves?

TOM: Good question. Before anyone enters the water, we run a short briefing. The main rule is simple: look, but never touch. We also ask people not to wear ordinary sunscreen, as some chemicals damage coral.

PRESENTER: What should they use instead?

TOM: A reef-safe product, which we sell at the centre. And we keep group sizes small, no more than six divers per guide.

PRESENTER: That sounds sensible. Where can listeners find out more?

TOM: Just visit our website, reefcare dot org.

reading.title: Solving the Problem of Fresh Water
reading.passage:
Although our planet is covered mostly by water, only a small fraction of it is fresh and suitable for drinking or farming. The vast majority is salty seawater, while much of the remaining fresh water is locked away in distant ice caps and glaciers. As populations grow and cities expand, many regions are finding that their supplies of clean water are no longer enough to meet demand. In response, engineers have turned to a process that removes salt from seawater, known as desalination.

The most common modern method forces seawater through extremely fine membranes that allow water molecules to pass while blocking the salt. This technique, called reverse osmosis, has become far more efficient over recent decades. Large desalination plants now supply millions of people in dry countries, particularly in the Middle East, where rivers are scarce but energy from the sun is plentiful. Some coastal cities elsewhere have built smaller plants to protect themselves against years of low rainfall.

Desalination is not a perfect solution, however. The process requires a great deal of energy, which can be expensive and, if it comes from burning fuel, harmful to the climate. The plants also produce a thick, salty liquid called brine, which must be disposed of carefully. If it is simply pumped back into the sea near the shore, it can harm the marine life living there. For these reasons, desalinated water remains costly compared with water drawn from rivers or underground.

Researchers are working hard to make the technology cleaner and cheaper. Some plants now run partly on solar or wind power, cutting both costs and pollution. Others are testing new membranes that need less pressure, and therefore less energy, to operate. While saving water through careful use will always matter, many experts believe that improved desalination will play a growing role in supplying the cities of the future.

questions.q: Only a small fraction of the planet's water is fresh and suitable for drinking.

options: It has scarce rivers but plentiful solar energy

options: By adding more salt to the water

listening.title: Lecture on Managing Water Supplies
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to look at how cities can manage their water more wisely, because building new supplies is only part of the answer. The other part is reducing waste.

Let's start with leaks. In some older cities, as much as a third of treated water is lost from cracked pipes before it ever reaches a home. Repairing this hidden network is unglamorous work, but it can save enormous amounts of water at relatively low cost.

Next, consider how we use water at home. The single biggest use is not drinking or cooking, but flushing toilets and watering gardens. Simple measures, such as fitting efficient taps and collecting rainwater, can cut household use by around twenty per cent.

Finally, pricing matters. When water is almost free, people tend to waste it. Several cities have found that charging a fair price, while protecting poorer families, encourages everyone to be more careful. In our next session, we'll examine a case study from a city that combined all three approaches and reduced its total water use by a quarter within five years.

reading.title: The Quiet Strength of Bamboo
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, builders across Asia have relied on bamboo as a cheap and abundant material. Recently, however, materials engineers have begun to study it far more seriously, treating it not as a humble plant but as a high-performance natural composite. Tests carried out in several university laboratories suggest that, weight for weight, some species of bamboo can resist pulling forces almost as well as mild steel, while remaining remarkably light.

The secret lies in its internal structure. A bamboo stem is made up of long fibres bound together in a softer surrounding material. These fibres are packed more densely near the outer wall, exactly where stress is greatest when the stem bends in the wind. Engineers describe this as a clever natural design, because the plant places its strongest material precisely where it is most needed and wastes very little. Modern factories struggle to copy this graded arrangement in artificial composites.

Despite these advantages, bamboo has clear limitations. It absorbs moisture easily, which can cause it to swell, crack or rot, and untreated stems are vulnerable to insects. To address this, researchers now soak the harvested stems in mild chemical solutions and dry them under carefully controlled heat. Some teams go further, slicing bamboo into thin strips and gluing them into dense engineered boards that behave more predictably than the natural stem.

Supporters argue that bamboo could play an important role in greener construction. Unlike steel or concrete, it grows back within a few years and absorbs carbon dioxide as it does so. Yet experts caution that bamboo is unlikely to replace conventional materials entirely. Instead, they expect it to be used alongside them, particularly in regions where it grows naturally and where building costs must be kept low.

questions.q: Bamboo has only recently been used as a building material in Asia.

options: That is where stress is greatest during bending
It helps the plant absorb water

options: It grows back quickly and absorbs carbon dioxide

reading.title: The Return of Recycled Glass
reading.passage:
Glass is one of the oldest manufactured materials, yet it remains surprisingly modern. Made mainly from sand, soda ash and limestone, it can be melted and reshaped again and again without losing quality. This property has made recycled glass increasingly attractive to engineers searching for ways to reduce waste and save energy in manufacturing.

When old bottles and jars are collected, they are crushed into small fragments known as cullet. Adding cullet to a furnace lowers the temperature needed to melt fresh raw materials, because the broken glass softens more readily than sand. Factories report that every additional portion of cullet trims their energy bills and cuts the carbon dioxide released during production. For this reason, many plants now aim to use as much recycled glass as the process allows.

There are, however, practical difficulties. Glass of different colours cannot always be mixed freely, since the final product may take on an unwanted tint. Bits of metal, ceramic or plastic that slip through the sorting process can also weaken the finished glass or damage equipment. As a result, the quality of recycled glass depends heavily on how carefully the waste is separated before it ever reaches the furnace.

Beyond containers, crushed glass is finding new uses. Some road builders mix it into the lower layers of pavements, where its sharp grains lock together and provide a firm base. Others blend fine glass powder into concrete, hoping to replace part of the cement and lower emissions. Early trials are promising, though engineers stress that long-term testing is still needed before such mixtures are widely adopted.

questions.q: Glass loses quality each time it is melted and reshaped.
Adding cullet reduces the temperature needed in the furnace.
Mixing glass colours can produce an unwanted tint.
Recycled glass is now banned from use in food containers.
What is 'cullet'?
Why can metal or ceramic in the waste be a problem?
options: They can weaken glass or damage equipment

questions.q: Crushed glass can be mixed into the lower layers of ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Recycling Centres
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to today's short talk on how our local recycling centre handles glass. When your bottles arrive, the first step is sorting. Workers and machines remove anything that isn't glass, such as plastic lids or paper labels. This stage matters because even small pieces of metal can spoil an entire batch.

Next, the glass is crushed into a material we call cullet. It's then sorted by colour, since clear, brown and green glass are usually kept apart. The cleaned cullet is stored in large containers before being sent to factories.

One fact that surprises many visitors is the energy saving. Using recycled glass can cut the energy needed for melting by about thirty per cent. That's a significant figure when you think about how much glass a city produces each week.

Finally, a quick note for your visit tomorrow. Please wear sturdy shoes, and don't touch the machinery. We'll meet at gate two at nine o'clock sharp. The tour lasts roughly one hour, and there's a small shop at the end selling recycled products.

options: It can spoil an entire batch

reading.title: Self-Healing Concrete
reading.passage:
Concrete is the most widely used building material on Earth, yet it has a stubborn weakness. As it ages and bears heavy loads, tiny cracks appear on its surface. At first these cracks seem harmless, but water and air can seep through them and slowly attack the steel bars hidden inside. Over time this corrosion weakens the structure, and repairs can be both costly and disruptive.

In the past two decades, researchers have searched for a material that can mend itself. One of the most striking solutions involves adding harmless bacteria to the concrete mixture. These bacteria lie dormant, sometimes for years, sealed inside tiny capsules along with a food source. When a crack forms and rainwater reaches them, the bacteria wake up, feed, and produce limestone, which gradually fills the gap.

Laboratory trials have shown that such concrete can close cracks several millimetres wide without any human help. This could greatly extend the life of bridges, tunnels and underground pipes, where ordinary inspection is difficult and expensive. Engineers are particularly hopeful about structures that are hard to reach once they are built.

There remain serious obstacles, however. Self-healing concrete is currently far more expensive than the standard kind, partly because preparing the bacteria and capsules is delicate work. Critics also point out that most tests have taken place in controlled laboratories rather than on real building sites, where conditions are messier and harder to predict. Even so, several pilot projects are now under way, and supporters believe that prices will fall as production grows. If they are right, the cracks that have troubled builders for centuries may one day repair themselves.

questions.q: Cracks in concrete can allow water and air to reach the steel inside.
The bacteria in self-healing concrete are active from the moment of mixing.
The bacteria produce limestone to fill cracks.
Self-healing concrete has already been used to build a major airport.
What wakes the dormant bacteria?
Why is self-healing concrete especially useful for tunnels and pipes?
options: Inspection there is difficult and expensive

questions.q: One major obstacle is that the material is currently more ________ than standard concrete.
Most tests have been carried out in controlled ________ rather than on real sites.

listening.title: Two Students Discuss a Project
listening.script:
MIA: So, for our materials project, I think we should focus on self-healing concrete. It's a great topic.

LEO: I agree, but it's huge. We need a clear angle. How about comparing cost and benefit?

MIA: Good idea. I read that it can be up to three times more expensive than normal concrete right now.

LEO: Three times? That's a lot. But repairs on bridges cost a fortune too, so maybe it pays off over time.

MIA: Exactly. We could make a simple chart showing repair savings over twenty years.

LEO: I like that. Who does what? I can handle the cost data if you write the introduction.

MIA: Deal. And we should interview Dr. Park. Her office hours are on Wednesday afternoon.

LEO: Perfect. Let's email her today. Oh, and the report is due on the fifteenth, so we shouldn't leave it late.

MIA: Agreed. Let's aim to finish a draft by the tenth.

LEO: Sounds good. I'll start the research tonight.

reading.title: Shape-Memory Alloys
reading.passage:
Imagine bending a metal paperclip out of shape and then watching it spring back to its original form when warmed by a cup of tea. This is not a trick but a real property of a remarkable family of materials known as shape-memory alloys. These metals can return to a previously set shape after being deformed, provided they are heated above a certain temperature. The effect was first noticed in the mid-twentieth century, but it has only become commercially important in recent decades.

The behaviour depends on the way the atoms inside the metal are arranged. At lower temperatures, the atoms sit in a soft, easily bent structure, so the metal can be twisted without much force. When heat is applied, the atoms snap into a stiffer, more orderly pattern, pulling the whole object back towards its memorised shape. Because the change is driven by temperature, engineers can design components that respond automatically to their surroundings.

The applications are surprisingly varied. In medicine, thin wires made from these alloys are used to open blocked blood vessels; once inside the warm body, the wire expands to hold the vessel open. In eyewear, flexible frames can be bent almost flat and still recover. Engineers have even fitted such alloys to aircraft and spacecraft, where parts must adjust shape during flight without bulky motors.

Yet these materials are not perfect. They are expensive to produce, and repeated cycles of bending and recovery can gradually tire the metal until it no longer returns fully to shape. Manufacturing them also demands very precise control of the mixture, since even small errors can ruin the memory effect. For these reasons, researchers continue to search for cheaper alloys that perform just as reliably.

questions.q: Shape-memory alloys can return to a set shape when heated above a certain temperature.
The shape-memory effect was discovered only a few years ago.
Medical wires made from these alloys can expand inside the warm body.
Shape-memory alloys are now the cheapest metals available to engineers.
These alloys are used in mobile phone batteries.
What causes the metal to return to its memorised shape?
What is one disadvantage mentioned in the passage?
options: They cannot be bent at all
Repeated cycles can tire the metal
They are too heavy to use

questions.q: Manufacturing these alloys demands very precise control of the ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Smart Metals
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today I want to introduce what are sometimes called smart metals, or more correctly, shape-memory alloys. The basic idea is simple: these metals remember a shape and return to it when heated. Let me give you three points to note.

First, the temperature that triggers the change can be adjusted during manufacturing. By altering the mixture slightly, we can make an alloy that responds at body temperature, or one that needs boiling water. This flexibility is hugely useful.

Second, consider the cost. At the moment, producing one kilogram can be several times more expensive than ordinary steel. That limits where engineers can afford to use it.

Third, durability. After about ten thousand cycles of bending and recovery, many alloys begin to weaken. So they suit applications that don't need constant repetition.

For your assignment, please write five hundred words on one real-world use of these alloys. The deadline is the twenty-second of March, and you should submit it through the online portal, not by email.

Producing one kilogram can be several times more expensive than ordinary ________.

reading.title: The Promise of Aerogels
reading.passage:
Among the strangest materials ever made by scientists are the substances known as aerogels. Often described as 'frozen smoke' because of their faint blue, ghostly appearance, aerogels are extraordinarily light, with as much as ninety-nine per cent of their volume made up of empty air. A block the size of a brick may weigh barely more than a feather, yet it can support many times its own weight without crushing.

Aerogels are created by taking a wet gel and carefully drying it so that the liquid inside is removed without collapsing the delicate solid framework. What remains is a sponge-like network of tiny threads with countless minute pockets of air. Because these pockets are so small, they make it very difficult for heat to pass through, which means aerogels are among the best insulators ever produced.

This property has attracted strong interest from engineers. Thin sheets of aerogel can keep buildings warm in winter while taking up far less space than ordinary insulation. Space agencies have used them to protect instruments from the extreme cold of distant planets, and they have even been employed to trap fine dust travelling through space at enormous speed. On Earth, manufacturers are exploring their use in lightweight jackets and in pipes that must carry very hot or very cold fluids.

Despite this promise, aerogels have not yet become everyday materials. The traditional drying process is slow and costly, and the finished product can be brittle, crumbling under a sharp knock. Researchers are now developing tougher, cheaper versions, sometimes by blending aerogels with flexible fibres. If these efforts succeed, a material once confined to laboratories and spacecraft may eventually find its way into ordinary homes.

questions.q: Aerogels are very heavy for their size.
Up to ninety-nine per cent of an aerogel's volume can be air.
Aerogels are among the best insulators ever produced.
The tiny air pockets make it hard for heat to pass through aerogels.
Aerogels are currently the cheapest insulation available to builders.
Aerogels have been used in underwater diving suits.
Why are aerogels sometimes called 'frozen smoke'?
options: Of their faint, ghostly blue appearance

listening.title: Phone Enquiry About Insulation
listening.script:
CUSTOMER: Hello, I'm calling to ask about your aerogel insulation panels for my house.

ADVISOR: Certainly. They're very popular at the moment. May I ask which room you want to insulate?

CUSTOMER: The attic, mainly. It gets freezing in winter.

ADVISOR: The attic is a great choice. Our standard panel is one metre by half a metre, and it's only ten millimetres thick.

CUSTOMER: That's thin. Does it really work?

ADVISOR: Surprisingly well. One thin panel insulates as well as a much thicker layer of ordinary foam. The price is forty dollars per panel.

CUSTOMER: And delivery?

ADVISOR: Delivery is free for orders over two hundred dollars. Otherwise it's fifteen dollars.

CUSTOMER: Good. How long does it take to arrive?

ADVISOR: Usually about five working days. And I'd recommend wearing gloves when handling the panels, as the edges can be a little fragile.

CUSTOMER: Thanks, that's really helpful. I'll place an order online.

reading.title: The Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
For centuries the European beaver was a familiar sight along the rivers of Britain. By the sixteenth century, however, it had been hunted to extinction across the island, valued for its thick fur and for a substance from its glands once used in medicine. For roughly four hundred years, no wild beavers lived in British waters at all.

In recent decades, attitudes have changed. Conservationists began to argue that beavers are a "keystone species", meaning that their activities shape the whole environment around them. By building dams from branches and mud, beavers slow the flow of water and create ponds and wetlands. These new habitats attract insects, fish, frogs and birds, increasing the variety of life in an area. The dams also trap sediment and can reduce the risk of flooding downstream, because water is released more gradually after heavy rain.

Not everyone welcomed their reintroduction. Some farmers worried that flooded fields would damage crops, and that beaver dams might block drainage channels. To address these concerns, several trial projects placed beavers in fenced enclosures, where their effects could be studied carefully before any wider release. Scientists monitored water quality, plant growth and animal numbers over a period of years.

The results were largely encouraging. In one Scottish trial, the number of water plants rose sharply, and local people reported seeing otters and dragonflies that had been rare before. Officials eventually granted the beaver legal protection, recognising it as a native species once more. Today, small populations live in several rivers, and supporters hope the animal will gradually spread. Critics still call for careful management, but few now deny that the beaver's engineering skills can benefit the wider landscape.

questions.q: Beavers were hunted in Britain partly because of a substance used in medicine.
Beavers disappeared from British rivers for about two hundred years.
The beaver fur trade was more profitable than any other fur trade in Europe.
Why are beavers described as a "keystone species"?
options: They are the largest animals in British rivers.
Their activities shape the environment around them.
They are protected by special laws.
They were the first species to be reintroduced.

questions.q: Why were beavers first placed in fenced enclosures?
options: To breed them in large numbers
To study their effects before wider release
To keep them away from otters

questions.q: Beaver dams trap ________ and can reduce the risk of flooding downstream.

listening.title: Joining a River Clean-Up
listening.script:
VOLUNTEER COORDINATOR: Hello, thanks for ringing the Riverside Trust. Are you calling about the weekend clean-up?

CALLER: Yes, I'd like to volunteer. What do I need to do?

COORDINATOR: Lovely. First I'll take a few details. Can I have your full name?

CALLER: It's Daniel Foster, F-O-S-T-E-R.

COORDINATOR: Thank you. And the event is this Saturday. We meet at nine o'clock at the old stone bridge on Mill Lane.

CALLER: Nine at the stone bridge. And how long does it last?

COORDINATOR: About three hours, so we usually finish around midday. We provide gloves and bags, but please bring your own boots, as the bank can be very muddy.

CALLER: Right, boots. Should I bring lunch?

COORDINATOR: No need. There's a small café nearby, and we offer each volunteer a free hot drink afterwards. Oh, and the fee to join is just two pounds, which goes towards new equipment.

CALLER: That's fine. Is there anything else?

COORDINATOR: Just wear something warm. The weather by the water can be cold, even in spring.

reading.title: Coral Reefs Under Pressure
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are among the most colourful and crowded environments on Earth. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they support roughly a quarter of all marine species. A reef is built by tiny animals called coral polyps, which produce a hard outer skeleton of calcium carbonate. Over thousands of years, the skeletons of countless polyps form the massive structures that divers admire today.

The bright colours of healthy coral come not from the polyps themselves but from microscopic algae that live inside their tissues. This relationship benefits both partners: the algae receive shelter, while the coral gains food produced by the algae through photosynthesis. When the water becomes too warm, however, the coral expels the algae. Without them, the coral turns white, a process known as bleaching. Bleached coral is not dead, but it is weakened and may die if normal conditions do not return quickly.

Rising sea temperatures are not the only threat. The ocean is becoming slightly more acidic as it absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Greater acidity makes it harder for polyps to build their skeletons, slowing the growth of reefs. Pollution from farms and cities adds further stress, as excess nutrients encourage the growth of algae that smother coral.

Despite these pressures, scientists have not given up hope. In several countries, researchers grow young corals in underwater nurseries and later attach them to damaged reefs. Some teams are searching for naturally heat-resistant corals that might cope better with warmer seas. Such projects cannot replace the world's vast reefs, which took millennia to form, but they may help certain areas recover. Most experts agree that the long-term survival of coral depends above all on reducing the emissions that warm and acidify the oceans.

questions.q: Coral reefs cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor.
The colour of healthy coral comes mainly from the polyps themselves.
Coral nurseries are more expensive to run than other conservation methods.
What happens during coral bleaching?
options: The coral expels the algae and turns white.

questions.q: Why does greater ocean acidity harm reefs?
options: It makes the water too cold.
It kills all the fish immediately.
It makes building skeletons harder for polyps.
It increases the amount of sunlight.

questions.q: Coral polyps produce a hard outer skeleton made of calcium ________.

TOUR AGENT: Good morning, Reef Adventures, how can I help?

CUSTOMER: Hi, I'd like to book a snorkelling tour on the reef. What do you offer?

AGENT: We have two trips daily. The morning trip leaves at eight, and the afternoon one at one o'clock. Most people prefer the morning, as the water is calmer.

CUSTOMER: I'll take the morning one, then. How much is it?

AGENT: It's eighty dollars per adult, and that includes all your equipment and a light lunch on the boat.

CUSTOMER: Great. Do I need to bring anything?

AGENT: Just a towel, some sunscreen, and a hat. We supply masks, fins and life jackets.

CUSTOMER: Perfect. And how long is the trip?

AGENT: You're out for about five hours in total, returning to the harbour around one. Oh, and a marine biologist comes along to explain what you'll see.

CUSTOMER: That sounds wonderful. Can I book for this Friday?

AGENT: Of course. Can I get your name and a contact number?

CUSTOMER: It's Maria Lopez, and my number is 0-4-double-one, six-three-two, nine-eight-five.
