reading.title: The Hidden World of Soil
reading.passage:
When we think of life on Earth, we usually picture animals and plants on the surface. Yet beneath our feet lies one of the richest and least understood environments of all: the soil. A single handful of healthy garden soil may contain more living organisms than there are people on the planet. These include bacteria, fungi, tiny worms and insects, all interacting in ways that scientists are only beginning to map.

Soil does far more than hold plants in place. It stores water, recycles nutrients and locks away large amounts of carbon. The decomposers within it break down dead leaves and other waste, releasing minerals that growing plants can use again. Fungi form fine threads that spread through the ground and connect the roots of different plants, allowing them to share water and even chemical signals. Some researchers describe this underground network as a kind of natural communication system.

Despite its importance, soil is under threat in many regions. Intensive farming, with heavy machinery and frequent ploughing, can damage its structure and reduce the number of organisms it contains. When soil loses its living matter, it becomes harder, drier and less fertile, and rain washes it away more easily. The loss of fertile topsoil is now a serious concern for agriculture worldwide.

Fortunately, soil can recover if treated with care. Farmers who plant cover crops between harvests, reduce ploughing and add organic material often see their soil improve within a few seasons. Such methods, sometimes grouped under the term "regenerative farming", aim to rebuild the living community below ground rather than simply feeding plants with chemicals. Supporters argue that healthier soil produces stronger crops and stores more carbon, offering benefits both for farmers and for the climate. Although the science is still developing, interest in protecting this hidden world is steadily growing.

questions.q: A handful of healthy soil may contain more organisms than the human population.
Soil only serves to hold plants in place.
Regenerative farming produces higher profits than traditional farming.
What do fungi threads in the soil allow plants to do?
What happens when soil loses its living matter?
options: It becomes harder, drier and less fertile.

questions.q: The ________ within soil break down dead leaves and release minerals.
Farmers can plant ________ crops between harvests to improve soil.
Heavy machinery and frequent ploughing can damage soil structure.

listening.title: A Lecture on Composting
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to talk about composting, which is simply the natural breaking down of organic waste into rich material for the garden. It's one of the easiest ways to reduce household rubbish.

Let's start with what you can add. Fruit and vegetable peelings are ideal, as are coffee grounds, eggshells and dead leaves. However, you should avoid adding meat or dairy products, because these attract rats and produce unpleasant smells.

A good compost heap needs a balance of two things: green material, which is wet and rich in nitrogen, and brown material, such as dry leaves and cardboard, which adds carbon. Aim for roughly equal amounts of each. Turn the heap with a fork about once a week to let in air, as the tiny organisms doing the work need oxygen.

With the right care, you'll have usable compost in about six months. It will look dark and crumbly, rather like chocolate cake, and smell pleasantly of the forest floor. Spread it on your garden beds, and your plants will thank you.

questions.q: What should NOT be added to a compost heap?
Green material is wet and rich in ________.
Brown material such as dry leaves and cardboard adds ________.

Fungi form a network linking plant roots.
Good compost looks dark and crumbly.

reading.title: Cities for Wildlife
reading.passage:
For a long time, cities were viewed as the opposite of nature, places where concrete and traffic left little room for wild creatures. Yet ecologists now recognise that towns and cities can support a surprising range of wildlife, and that careful planning can make them even richer. As natural habitats shrink in the countryside, urban areas are becoming an unexpected refuge for some species.

Gardens, parks and even railway embankments provide food and shelter for birds, insects and small mammals. Foxes, hedgehogs and a variety of butterflies have all adapted to life among buildings. Some species actually thrive in cities, where winters are slightly warmer and predators are fewer. The challenge for planners is to connect these scattered green spaces, so that animals can move between them rather than becoming trapped in isolated patches.

One popular solution is the "green corridor", a continuous strip of vegetation that links parks and gardens across a city. These corridors may follow rivers, canals or disused railway lines. By allowing wildlife to travel safely, they help maintain healthy populations and reduce the risk that a small, isolated group will die out. Cities have also begun installing green roofs, planting trees along streets and creating ponds, all of which add valuable habitat.

These measures bring benefits for people as well as animals. Trees and plants cool the air during hot weather, absorb rainwater that might otherwise cause flooding, and filter some pollution from the atmosphere. Studies suggest that access to green space improves residents' mood and health. For these reasons, a growing number of city governments now treat urban nature not as a luxury but as an essential part of planning. Designing cities that welcome wildlife, supporters argue, ultimately makes them better places for humans to live too.

questions.q: Ecologists now accept that cities can support a wide range of wildlife.
Cities are colder in winter than the surrounding countryside.
Green corridors are more expensive to create than green roofs.
What is the main purpose of a "green corridor"?
options: To link green spaces so animals can move between them

questions.q: Why do some species thrive in cities?
options: Winters are warmer and predators are fewer.
There are no plants to compete with.

questions.q: Trees and plants can absorb ________ that might otherwise cause flooding.
Some green corridors follow rivers, canals or disused ________ lines.
Access to green space is said to improve residents' mood and health.

listening.title: Planning a Community Garden
listening.script:
ORGANIZER: Thanks for coming to the meeting about our new community garden. Let me run through the plan, and then we'll take questions.

RESIDENT: Sounds good. Where exactly will the garden be?

ORGANIZER: It'll be on the empty lot behind the library on Park Street. The city has agreed to let us use it for free.

RESIDENT: That's great news. When do we start?

ORGANIZER: We're aiming to begin clearing the site next month, in early April. The first job is removing the old fencing and the rubbish.

RESIDENT: What will we actually grow?

ORGANIZER: Mainly vegetables and herbs, but we'll also plant a row of fruit trees along the back wall and some flowers to attract bees.

RESIDENT: How much will it cost to join?

ORGANIZER: There's a small annual fee of twenty dollars, which covers seeds and tools. Each member gets their own plot, about three meters wide.

RESIDENT: And how often do we need to help?

ORGANIZER: We ask everyone to volunteer for at least two hours a week. Many hands make light work!

reading.title: The Long Journey of Migrating Birds
reading.passage:
Every year, billions of birds undertake one of the most remarkable journeys in the natural world. As the seasons change, many species fly thousands of kilometres between their breeding grounds and the warmer regions where they spend the winter. The Arctic tern, for example, travels from pole to pole and back again, covering a distance that, over a lifetime, could equal several trips to the Moon. How birds manage such feats has fascinated scientists for generations.

Researchers now know that migrating birds rely on several methods to find their way. Some follow visible landmarks such as coastlines, mountain ranges and rivers. Others appear to use the position of the sun by day and the patterns of the stars by night. Perhaps most astonishing of all, many species can sense the Earth's magnetic field, which acts like an invisible compass guiding them across featureless oceans. Young birds of some species inherit the route, setting off correctly even on their first journey without any older bird to lead them.

Migration is dangerous as well as impressive. Birds must build up large reserves of fat to fuel their flight, and many die from exhaustion, storms or lack of food along the way. They depend on a chain of resting and feeding sites, often wetlands, where they can recover before continuing. If even one of these crucial stopover sites is drained or built upon, an entire population may suffer.

This is why conservationists place such importance on protecting these sites along migration routes, sometimes called flyways. Because birds cross many national borders, their survival often requires cooperation between countries. Climate change presents a further challenge, as warmer springs can cause plants and insects to appear earlier, before the birds arrive to feed on them. Understanding and protecting migration has therefore become an international effort, uniting scientists and governments across continents in a shared cause.

questions.q: The Arctic tern travels between the two poles during its migration.
Young birds of all species need an older bird to lead them on their first journey.
Storms cause more bird deaths during migration than lack of food.
How do many birds navigate across featureless oceans?
options: By sensing the Earth's magnetic field

questions.q: Why are stopover sites so important?
options: They are where birds build nests.
They let birds rest and feed before continuing.
They protect birds from the sun.
They are where birds learn their routes.

questions.q: Migrating birds build up reserves of ________ to fuel their flight.
Migration routes are sometimes called ________.
Protecting migrating birds often requires cooperation between different countries.

listening.title: A Talk on Bird Watching
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to our morning bird-watching walk. Before we set off, let me give you a few tips to make the most of it.

First, the best time to see birds is early in the morning, just after sunrise, when they're most active and searching for food. By the middle of the day, many birds become quiet and hard to spot.

Second, please keep your voices low and avoid sudden movements. Birds are easily frightened, and a loud noise can send a whole flock flying off before you've had a proper look.

Now, a word about equipment. A good pair of binoculars is essential. I'd also recommend a small notebook to record what you see, including the date, the place and the weather. Over time, these notes become a valuable record.

Today we're hoping to spot some visiting geese that arrive here every autumn from the north. There are usually about two hundred of them on the lake. If we're lucky, we may also see a kingfisher near the river. Right, let's head down to the water quietly.

questions.q: When is the best time to see birds?
Visitors should keep their ________ low to avoid frightening the birds.
A good pair of ________ is essential equipment.
What should be recorded in the notebook?
About ________ geese are usually on the lake.
The visiting geese arrive every ________ from the north.

speaking.q:
Describe an animal you find interesting. You should say:
- what animal it is

- what it looks like or what it does
and explain why you find this animal interesting.

Estimated number of birds recorded
Species   | 2012   | 2022
Geese     | 3,200  | 4,500
Ducks     | 5,000  | 3,800
Waders    | 1,500  | 2,600

reading.title: The Rise of Contactless Payment
reading.passage:
Only a decade ago, most shoppers in many countries reached for cash or signed a paper receipt at the till. Today, a quick tap of a card or a phone is often enough to complete a purchase. Contactless payment, which relies on a short-range wireless signal between the card and the terminal, has spread far faster than many banks expected. In some cities, a majority of small everyday transactions are now made without entering a single digit.

The technology itself is not new. The wireless chips used in contactless cards were first developed for public transport systems, where speed at the gate mattered more than anything else. Passengers grew used to touching a card against a reader, and retailers slowly realised that the same idea could work in a coffee shop or a supermarket. When smartphones added the ability to store card details securely, the habit became even harder to resist.

Supporters argue that contactless payment saves time and reduces the handling of dirty banknotes. During recent health scares, many businesses actively encouraged customers to avoid cash for hygiene reasons. Critics, however, worry that the ease of tapping makes people spend without thinking. Because no signature or code is needed for small amounts, a lost card could in theory be used by a stranger until it is reported and blocked.

Banks have responded by setting limits on how much can be spent in a single contactless transaction, and by asking for a code after a certain number of taps. Some analysts believe that cash will never disappear entirely, particularly among older shoppers and in regions with weak internet coverage. Yet the direction of travel seems clear. For a growing number of consumers, the simple act of reaching into a pocket for coins already feels strangely old-fashioned.

questions.q: Contactless payment has grown more quickly than many banks predicted.
The wireless chips in contactless cards were originally created for shopping.
Contactless payment terminals are more expensive than older card machines.
According to the passage, what made the contactless habit harder to resist?
What is one concern raised by critics of contactless payment?
options: It works only in large cities
It is too slow at busy tills
It cannot be used on public transport

questions.q: Banks have responded by setting ________ on how much can be spent in a single contactless transaction.
Some analysts think cash will survive among ________ shoppers and in areas with weak internet.
Many businesses asked customers to avoid cash during recent health scares.

listening.title: Opening a Mobile Banking Account
listening.script:
CLERK: Good morning, welcome to Riverside Bank. How can I help you today?

CUSTOMER: Hi, I'd like to open a mobile banking account, please.

CLERK: Of course. Can I take your full name first?

CUSTOMER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

CLERK: Thank you, Mr Foster. And your date of birth?

CUSTOMER: The twelfth of March, nineteen ninety-five.

CLERK: Lovely. Now, the basic account is free, but if you'd like contactless payments and travel insurance, the premium plan is four pounds a month.

CUSTOMER: I'll take the premium plan, thanks.

CLERK: Great choice. You'll get a card within five working days. Could I have a contact number?

CUSTOMER: It's oh-seven-seven-double-one, three-four-nine, two-oh-six.

CLERK: Perfect. And which branch would you like as your home branch?

CUSTOMER: The one on Market Street.

CLERK: Done. One last thing: please download the app and set a six-digit passcode before your card arrives.

CUSTOMER: Will do. Thank you very much.

Method | 2014 | 2019 | 2024

reading.title: Working From Home
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the idea of an office and the idea of work were almost the same thing. Employees travelled to a shared building each morning, sat at fixed desks, and travelled home again in the evening. In recent years, however, faster internet connections and cheap video software have made it possible for millions of people to do the same jobs from their kitchens or spare bedrooms.

The sudden shift was driven partly by necessity. When offices closed during a public health emergency, companies that had never trusted remote work were forced to try it overnight. To the surprise of many managers, productivity in some teams did not fall at all. Workers reported that they could concentrate better without the constant noise and interruptions of an open-plan office.

The benefits are not shared equally, however. People with quiet, spacious homes adapted easily, while those in small flats or with young children often struggled to find a corner to focus in. Some employees also missed the casual conversations that happen naturally in a shared space, the kind of chat that can spark new ideas or simply make a long day more pleasant.

Employers face difficult choices too. Renting a large office is expensive, so many firms now ask staff to come in only two or three days a week, a pattern often called hybrid working. This arrangement aims to keep the social and creative advantages of the office while saving money and giving workers more flexibility. Critics warn that hybrid teams can split into two groups, with those who attend in person receiving more attention and promotions than equally able colleagues who stay at home.

Whether the office ever returns to its former importance remains uncertain. What seems clear is that, for many kinds of work, the link between a particular building and a particular task has been permanently loosened.

questions.q: Throughout the twentieth century, work was closely tied to a shared building.
Every company welcomed remote work before the health emergency.
Remote workers earn higher salaries than office workers.
Why did some workers concentrate better at home?
options: They avoided office noise and interruptions

questions.q: What is 'hybrid working' described as in the passage?
options: Splitting time between home and the office

questions.q: People in small flats or with young children often struggled to find a corner to ________ in.

listening.title: Booking a Co-working Desk
listening.script:
STAFF: Hello, Hub Space, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a desk for next week.

STAFF: Sure. Are you after a hot desk or a fixed desk?

CALLER: A fixed desk, please, so I can leave my things.

STAFF: No worries. A fixed desk is thirty dollars a day, or one hundred and twenty for the full week.

CALLER: I'll take the week, thanks.

STAFF: Great. Which floor would you prefer? The second floor is quiet, and the third has a view of the park.

CALLER: The third floor sounds lovely.

STAFF: Done. Can I get a name for the booking?

CALLER: Yes, it's Amara Nkemdi. The last name is N-K-E-M-D-I.

STAFF: Thanks, Amara. The building opens at seven thirty in the morning and closes at nine at night. You'll need a key card, which you can collect from the front desk.

CALLER: Perfect. Is there free coffee?

STAFF: Of course! And meeting rooms are free for up to two hours a day.

CALLER: Brilliant, thank you.

reading.title: The Spread of Online Reviews
reading.passage:
Before the internet, a traveller choosing a hotel in an unfamiliar city had little to rely on beyond a glossy brochure or the advice of a friend. Today, the same traveller can read hundreds of opinions from strangers within seconds. Online reviews have become one of the most powerful forces in modern commerce, shaping decisions about everything from restaurants and gadgets to doctors and universities.

The appeal is easy to understand. A single review may be unreliable, but a large number of them, taken together, can give a surprisingly accurate picture of a product or service. Businesses that consistently disappoint their customers find it harder to hide, while small firms with excellent service can build a reputation that once would have taken decades. In this sense, reviews have shifted some power from companies to ordinary consumers.

Yet the system is far from perfect. Some companies pay for false praise or post negative comments about their rivals. Researchers who study review websites estimate that a noticeable fraction of all reviews may be fake, although the exact figure is hard to measure and varies widely between platforms. Even genuine reviews can mislead, because angry customers are more likely to write than satisfied ones, giving a darker impression than the average experience deserves.

Review platforms have introduced various defences. They use software to detect suspicious patterns, such as many five-star ratings appearing on the same day, and they sometimes require proof that a reviewer actually made a purchase. Despite these efforts, the contest between honest feedback and clever manipulation continues.

For consumers, the practical lesson is to read reviews thoughtfully rather than trusting a single score. Looking at the overall pattern, paying attention to detailed comments, and treating extreme opinions with caution can all help. Reviews remain a valuable tool, but only for those who use them with a healthy degree of scepticism.

questions.q: Before the internet, travellers had limited information when choosing a hotel.
A single review is generally a reliable guide to quality.
Most fake reviews are written by professional review companies.
According to the passage, why can genuine reviews still mislead?
options: Angry customers write more often than satisfied ones
They are written only by experts

questions.q: What is one defence used by review platforms?
Reviews have shifted some power from companies to ordinary ________.
Consumers are advised to treat ________ opinions with caution.
The exact proportion of fake reviews is difficult to measure.

listening.title: A Talk on Spotting Fake Reviews
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to share three simple ways to spot a fake online review. First, look at the language. Fake reviews often use very general praise, like "amazing product, best ever," without mentioning a single specific detail. Honest reviewers usually describe how they used the item.

Second, check the timing. If a business suddenly receives twenty glowing reviews in one day, that's a warning sign. Real reviews tend to arrive slowly, spread out over weeks or months. Our research found that genuine feedback usually builds at a steady rate.

Third, look at the reviewer's history. A trustworthy account has written about many different products over time. An account that has posted only one review, all five stars, deserves suspicion.

Now, a quick tip on numbers. Don't be impressed by a perfect score. A rating of four point two out of five, based on three hundred reviews, is often more reliable than a perfect five based on just eight. Volume matters as much as the score itself. Read the detailed comments in the middle, the three-star ones, because they are frequently the most balanced and honest of all.

questions.q: The talk gives ________ simple ways to spot a fake review.
What is the first sign of a fake review?
options: Very general praise with no detail

questions.q: A warning sign is receiving twenty glowing reviews in ________ day.
Which account deserves suspicion?
options: One reviewing many products over time
One with only one five-star review

questions.q: A rating of ________ out of five from 300 reviews may be more reliable than a perfect score.
The speaker says the ________-star comments are often the most balanced.

speaking.q: Let's discuss online opinions in society. Why do you think people trust the reviews of strangers? Do you think online reviews do more good than harm? How might companies be stopped from posting fake reviews about themselves or their rivals?

writing.q:
The table below shows how shoppers in one country found information about products before buying, in two different years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Source | 2010 | 2024
Advice from friends/family | 40% | 22%
Online reviews | 12% | 51%
In-store staff | 30% | 15%
Printed ads/brochures | 18% | 12%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Reviews are a powerful force in modern commerce.
Good service builds a reputation quickly online.

Researchers estimate many reviews are fake.
A fraction of reviews may be false.

reading.title: The Problem of Electronic Waste
reading.passage:
Every year, the world throws away tens of millions of tonnes of phones, laptops, televisions and other electronic devices. This growing mountain of discarded technology, known as electronic waste or e-waste, has become one of the fastest-rising forms of rubbish on the planet. As gadgets become cheaper and fashions change more quickly, many products are abandoned long before they stop working.

E-waste is a particular worry because of what it contains. Inside a typical smartphone are small amounts of valuable metals such as gold and copper, but also poisonous substances like lead and mercury. When old devices are dumped in landfill sites or burned carelessly, these harmful materials can leak into soil and water, threatening both human health and wildlife. In some regions, informal workers, sometimes including children, break devices apart by hand to recover metals, exposing themselves to serious danger.

Recycling offers a partial solution. Properly equipped facilities can recover most of the useful metals, reducing the need to mine fresh materials from the ground. Surprisingly, a tonne of old phones can contain more gold than a tonne of rock from a typical mine. Despite this, only a small share of the world's e-waste is currently recycled in safe and responsible ways.

Governments and manufacturers are slowly responding. Some countries now require shops to take back old devices when customers buy new ones, and a number of companies have promised to design products that are easier to repair and dismantle. Campaigners argue that the deeper problem is a culture that treats expensive technology as disposable. They call for longer-lasting designs, cheaper repairs, and clearer information about how to dispose of devices safely.

Until such changes become widespread, the e-waste mountain seems likely to keep growing. The challenge is not only technical but also social, demanding that consumers, companies and governments rethink the entire life cycle of the devices they depend on.

questions.q: Many electronic products are thrown away before they stop working.
A tonne of old phones contains less gold than a tonne of typical mine rock.
E-waste recycling creates more jobs than traditional mining.
Why is e-waste described as a particular worry?
options: It is too heavy to transport
It contains both valuable and poisonous materials
It cannot be recycled at all

questions.q: What do campaigners say is the deeper problem?
options: A culture that treats technology as disposable

questions.q: Some countries require shops to take back old devices when customers buy ________ ones.
Harmful materials can leak into soil and ________ when devices are dumped.
Only a small share of e-waste is currently recycled responsibly.

listening.title: A Lecture on Recycling Old Devices
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I'll outline how a recycling plant handles old electronics, in four stages.

In the first stage, collection, devices arrive from drop-off points and shops. Staff sort them into categories: phones, laptops, batteries, and large appliances. Batteries are separated immediately because they can catch fire.

The second stage is dismantling. Workers remove parts by hand and take out anything dangerous, such as the mercury in some older screens. This is slow but important for safety.

Third comes shredding. The remaining material is broken into tiny pieces, and powerful magnets pull out the steel. After that, other machines separate the copper and aluminium.

Finally, in the recovery stage, chemical processes extract the small amounts of precious metals, including gold. One striking figure: recycling a million phones can recover around thirty kilograms of gold, plus much larger amounts of copper and silver.

The whole process keeps poisonous materials out of the ground and saves energy compared with mining. Remember, though, that the greenest device is the one you keep using. Repairing a phone almost always beats recycling it.

questions.q: The lecturer describes the process in ________ stages.
Why are batteries separated immediately?
In the shredding stage, powerful ________ pull out the steel.
Recycling a million phones can recover around ________ kilograms of gold.
What does the lecturer say is the greenest option?
options: Keeping and using a device for longer

questions.q: The second stage of the process is called ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about gadgets and technology. How many electronic devices do you own? How often do you replace your phone or computer? What do you do with old devices you no longer use?

writing.q:
Many people replace their electronic devices, such as phones and laptops, every few years, creating large amounts of waste. What are the causes of this problem, and what measures could reduce it?

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: People discard phones that still work.
Devices are abandoned when fashions change.
Phones contain poisonous substances.
Old devices end up in landfill sites.
Chemicals can leak into the soil.
Plants recover gold from old phones.
A modern facility recycles e-waste safely.

reading.title: Smart Speakers in the Home
reading.passage:
A decade ago, talking to a household object and expecting a sensible reply belonged firmly to science fiction. Now, millions of homes contain small voice-controlled devices, often called smart speakers, that can play music, set timers, answer questions and switch on the lights, all in response to a spoken command. For many families, these gadgets have quietly become part of daily life.

The convenience is real. A cook with messy hands can ask for a recipe without touching a screen, and a person with limited mobility can control heating and lighting from across the room. Parents report that children enjoy asking the device endless questions, while older relatives sometimes find speaking far easier than typing or tapping. In these ways, voice technology can make everyday tasks simpler and more inclusive.

However, the same devices raise serious questions about privacy. To respond instantly, a smart speaker must listen continuously for its wake word, the special phrase that activates it. Most of the time it is not recording, but occasional errors mean that snippets of private conversation are sometimes stored and, in a few cases, reviewed by company staff working to improve the system. Many users are unaware of how much information their helpful assistant may collect.

Manufacturers insist that recordings are protected and that users can delete them, yet trust remains fragile. Surveys suggest that a significant number of owners feel slightly uneasy about having a microphone permanently switched on in their living room or bedroom, even if they continue to enjoy the benefits.

The future of these devices will probably depend less on clever engineering than on whether companies can convince people that their conversations are genuinely safe. As voice assistants spread into cars, offices and even toys, the balance between helpful convenience and personal privacy is likely to remain one of the defining debates of the connected home.

questions.q: Smart speakers can perform several different tasks in response to voice commands.
A smart speaker only listens after a button is physically pressed.
Smart speakers are more popular than mobile phones in most homes.
According to the passage, how can voice technology be more inclusive?
options: It is cheaper than other gadgets
It helps people who find typing difficult

questions.q: What will the future of these devices most likely depend on?
options: Convincing people their conversations are safe

questions.q: A smart speaker must listen continuously for its ________ word.
Surveys suggest many owners feel slightly ________ about an always-on microphone.
Manufacturers say that users are able to delete their recordings.

listening.title: Discussion on Smart Home Privacy
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, you've both researched smart speakers. Maya, what did you find about how they work?

MAYA: Well, the key thing is the wake word. The device only starts sending audio to the company's servers after it hears that word, like "Hey Assistant." Before that, it just listens locally.

TUTOR: Good. And the privacy concern?

LIAM: The problem is mistakes. Our reading said the device sometimes wakes up by accident, maybe when the television says something similar. Then it records a few seconds it shouldn't.

TUTOR: Interesting. Did you find any numbers?

MAYA: Yes. One survey of two thousand users found that sixty per cent worried about privacy, but only fifteen per cent had ever deleted their recordings.

TUTOR: That's a big gap. Liam, any solutions?

LIAM: A few. You can turn off the microphone with a physical switch, set recordings to delete automatically after three months, or simply review them in the app each week.

TUTOR: Excellent. For next week, please compare two different brands and present your findings on Thursday.

reading.title: The Bystander Effect
reading.passage:
When an emergency occurs in a public place, many people assume that the more witnesses there are, the more likely it is that someone will step forward to help. Psychological research, however, suggests the opposite is often true. This counter-intuitive pattern is known as the bystander effect: the presence of other people tends to reduce the probability that any single individual will intervene.

The phenomenon first attracted serious scientific attention in the late 1960s, when researchers began to design controlled experiments to test it. In one classic study, participants were placed in separate rooms and led to believe they were speaking to others through an intercom. When one voice appeared to suffer a sudden seizure, those who thought they were the only listener responded quickly, often within seconds. Those who believed several others could also hear the emergency were far slower, and many did not react at all.

Psychologists have proposed two main explanations for this behaviour. The first is the diffusion of responsibility: when many people are present, each individual feels that the duty to act is shared, so personal obligation is weakened. The second is social influence, sometimes called pluralistic ignorance. People look to others for cues about how to behave, and if everyone appears calm, each observer concludes that the situation is not truly an emergency, even when it is.

Understanding these mechanisms has practical value. Safety campaigns now advise people who need help in a crowd to address a specific person directly, rather than calling out to the group as a whole. By naming an individual, the appeal removes the comfort of shared responsibility and dramatically increases the chance of receiving assistance.

questions.q: Most people expect that a larger crowd makes help more likely.
Scientific study of the bystander effect began before 1950.
In the intercom study, participants were paid for taking part.
According to the passage, diffusion of responsibility means that:
options: each person feels the duty to act is shared

options: interpret others' calmness as a sign nothing is wrong
refuse to look at other witnesses

reading.title: Why We Remember and Forget
reading.passage:
Human memory is far from a perfect recording device. Rather than storing events like a video camera, the brain constantly selects, edits and reconstructs information. This means that what we remember is shaped not only by what actually happened, but also by our expectations, emotions and later experiences.

Memories are usually divided into different types. Short-term memory holds a small amount of information for a brief period, often only a matter of seconds. Long-term memory, by contrast, can store vast quantities of information for years or even decades. For an experience to move from short-term to long-term storage, it generally needs to be repeated, rehearsed or linked to something already known.

Forgetting is not simply a failure of the system; in many cases it is useful. If we retained every detail of every day, our minds would be overwhelmed by irrelevant information. By discarding what is unimportant, the brain keeps the most meaningful memories accessible. Some psychologists argue that forgetting allows us to generalise and to focus on patterns rather than isolated facts.

Nevertheless, memory can fail in troubling ways. People are sometimes confident about events that never occurred, a phenomenon known as a false memory. Studies have shown that carefully worded questions can lead witnesses to recall details that were never present. This has serious implications for legal cases, where eyewitness testimony has traditionally been treated as strong evidence.

Researchers continue to explore how memory can be strengthened. Techniques such as spacing out study sessions, testing oneself regularly and getting adequate sleep have all been shown to improve recall. Sleep in particular appears to play a crucial role, helping the brain to consolidate newly learned material during the night.

questions.q: The brain records events exactly as they happen, like a camera.
Short-term memory can hold information for many years.
The passage states that most people have poor long-term memory.
According to the passage, forgetting is described as:
A false memory is when a person:
options: recalls something that did not happen

listening.title: A Tutorial on Study Habits

TUTOR: So, you wanted some advice on preparing for your exams?
STUDENT: Yes. I keep forgetting things even after I've revised.
TUTOR: That's common. The biggest mistake students make is cramming everything into one long session.
STUDENT: That's exactly what I do.
TUTOR: Instead, try spacing your study across several days. Short sessions of about forty minutes work best.
STUDENT: Only forty minutes?
TUTOR: Yes, then take a proper break. Your brain needs time to absorb the material.
STUDENT: What about testing myself?
TUTOR: Excellent idea. Self-testing is far more effective than simply rereading your notes. Aim to test yourself at least twice a week.
STUDENT: And does sleep really matter?
TUTOR: Enormously. Try to get around eight hours a night, especially before an exam. Sleep helps fix new information in memory.
STUDENT: I usually stay up late revising.
TUTOR: That often does more harm than good. Finish early and rest properly.
STUDENT: Thank you, that's really helpful.

reading.title: The Psychology of First Impressions
reading.passage:
Within seconds of meeting someone, we form an impression of who they are. Research suggests that these judgements can be made remarkably quickly, sometimes in less than a tenth of a second, based on little more than a glance at a person's face. Although we may believe that we judge others carefully, much of this process happens automatically and outside our conscious awareness.

First impressions are powerful because they tend to influence how we interpret everything that follows. If we decide that a stranger seems friendly, we are likely to read their later actions in a positive light. If our initial reaction is negative, the same actions may be viewed with suspicion. Psychologists call this tendency the halo effect, and it can shape decisions in areas as varied as job interviews, courtrooms and classrooms.

Several factors feed into these rapid judgements. Facial expression is one of the most important; a relaxed smile signals warmth, while a tense expression can suggest hostility. Tone of voice, posture and clothing also contribute. Interestingly, people often agree with one another about the impressions a particular face creates, even when those impressions turn out to be inaccurate.

The difficulty is that first impressions are frequently wrong. A person who appears confident may simply be hiding nervousness, and someone who seems unfriendly may merely be shy. Because the initial judgement is so resistant to change, a single misleading moment can have lasting consequences.

Understanding this bias can help us make fairer decisions. Trained interviewers, for example, are encouraged to use structured questions and to delay forming conclusions until they have gathered enough information. By slowing down the process, they reduce the influence of an impression that may have been formed in an instant.

questions.q: First impressions can form in under a second.
The halo effect only applies to job interviews.
People always disagree about the impression a face creates.
Most interviewers receive several months of formal training.

options: our first impression colours how we read later actions

listening.title: Preparing for a Job Interview

ADVISOR: Take a seat. So, you've got an interview coming up?

CANDIDATE: Ten o'clock in the morning.
ADVISOR: Good. Plan to arrive about fifteen minutes early. It shows you're reliable.
CANDIDATE: I'll aim for quarter to ten, then.
ADVISOR: Exactly. Now, remember that first impressions form within seconds, so dress smartly and offer a firm handshake.
CANDIDATE: Should I bring anything?
ADVISOR: Bring two copies of your CV and a notebook. Interviewers like candidates who ask questions.
CANDIDATE: What sort of questions?
ADVISOR: Ask about the team or training opportunities. Avoid asking about salary in the first interview.
CANDIDATE: Got it. Anything else?
ADVISOR: Yes. Speak slowly and make eye contact. And try to give examples from your experience rather than just saying you're hard-working.
CANDIDATE: That's really useful, thank you.

reading.title: Stress and the Body
reading.passage:
Stress is often described as a purely negative experience, yet it began as a survival mechanism. When our ancestors faced danger, their bodies prepared rapidly for action. The heart beat faster, breathing quickened and muscles tensed, ready to either fight or flee. This response, controlled by a surge of hormones such as adrenaline and cortisol, could mean the difference between life and death.

In moderate amounts, stress can still be helpful today. A degree of pressure before an examination or a sporting event can sharpen concentration and improve performance. Psychologists sometimes distinguish between this beneficial form of stress and the harmful form that arises when pressure becomes constant and overwhelming.

The problem in modern life is that the threats we face are rarely physical. Deadlines, financial worries and social pressures trigger the same ancient response, but there is no enemy to fight and nowhere to run. When the stress reaction is switched on for long periods, it can damage health. Prolonged high levels of cortisol have been linked to disturbed sleep, weakened immunity and a greater risk of heart disease.

Fortunately, there are effective ways to manage stress. Regular physical exercise helps the body process stress hormones and improves mood. Techniques such as slow breathing and meditation can calm the nervous system, while maintaining strong social connections provides emotional support during difficult times.

Researchers also emphasise the role of perception. Two people facing the same situation may react very differently depending on how they interpret it. Those who view a challenge as something they can cope with tend to experience less harmful stress than those who feel powerless. Learning to reframe difficulties, therefore, can be as valuable as any physical remedy.

questions.q: Stress originally developed as a survival mechanism.

The 'fight or flight' response prepares the body to:

options: the threats are usually not physical

questions.q: Prolonged high levels of ________ have been linked to disturbed sleep.

listening.title: A Lecture on Managing Stress
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today we're looking at practical ways to manage stress. Now, the first thing to understand is that some stress is actually normal and even useful. The trouble starts when it becomes chronic. So let's go through four strategies. The first is exercise. Studies suggest that just thirty minutes of moderate activity, five days a week, can significantly lower stress levels. You don't need a gym; a brisk walk is enough. The second strategy is sleep. Adults need roughly seven to nine hours a night, and skipping sleep makes everything feel harder. Third, breathing techniques. When you feel tense, try breathing in slowly for four seconds and out for six. This calms the nervous system within minutes. And finally, connection. People with strong social support recover from stressful events far more quickly. So make time to talk to friends and family. Remember, the goal isn't to eliminate stress entirely; that's impossible. The goal is to keep it at a manageable level.

reading.title: The Power of Habit
reading.passage:
Much of what we do each day is not the result of careful decision-making but of habit. From brushing our teeth to checking our phones, countless small actions are performed almost without thought. Scientists estimate that a large proportion of daily behaviour is habitual, which allows the brain to conserve energy for more demanding tasks.

Habits form through a process that psychologists often describe as a loop. First there is a cue, a trigger that tells the brain to start a particular behaviour. This is followed by the routine itself, the action we carry out. Finally there is a reward, which gives the brain a reason to remember and repeat the sequence in future. Over time, the link between cue and routine becomes so strong that the behaviour feels automatic.

This efficiency has a downside. Because habits operate with little conscious attention, harmful routines can become deeply established before we notice them. Snacking while watching television or reaching for a phone the moment we feel bored are examples of patterns that are easy to acquire and hard to break.

The good news is that habits can be changed, though rarely by willpower alone. One widely recommended approach is to keep the same cue and reward but to replace the routine in the middle. A person who smokes when stressed, for instance, might respond to that same stress by going for a short walk instead. Because the cue and the reward remain familiar, the new routine is more likely to take hold.

Researchers also stress the importance of environment. Making a good habit easier and a bad habit harder, simply by changing one's surroundings, can be more effective than relying on motivation. Placing fruit on the kitchen table while hiding sweets in a cupboard is a small change, but such adjustments can quietly reshape behaviour over months and years.

questions.q: A large share of daily behaviour is performed out of habit.
Habits are best broken using willpower alone.
The passage gives the exact percentage of actions that are habitual.
The three parts of the habit loop are cue, routine and:
The recommended way to change a habit is to:
options: keep the cue and reward but change the routine

questions.q: A ________ is the trigger that tells the brain to start a behaviour.

Harmful habits can become established before we are aware of them.

listening.title: A Talk on Building Better Habits
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Tonight I want to share a few simple ideas about building better habits. The first principle is to start small. Most people fail because they try to change too much at once. Instead of promising to exercise for an hour every day, begin with just two minutes. It sounds trivial, but the point is to make the habit easy to start. Second, attach the new habit to something you already do. For example, do ten stretches right after you brush your teeth. The existing routine acts as a reminder. Third, track your progress. Mark a calendar each day you succeed; seeing a chain of marks is surprisingly motivating. And don't worry if you miss a day, just make sure you never miss twice in a row. Finally, design your environment. If you want to read more, leave a book on your pillow. If you want to snack less, keep junk food out of sight. Small changes to your surroundings make good habits the easy choice.

questions.q: Why does the speaker say most people fail at habits?
options: they try to change too much at once

reading.title: The Library of Alexandria
reading.passage:
In the ancient Egyptian city of Alexandria, founded in the fourth century BCE, scholars built what many regard as the greatest library of the ancient world. The rulers of the city were determined to gather every important text in existence, and they pursued this goal with remarkable energy. Officials were said to inspect ships arriving in the harbour, copy any books found on board, and keep the originals for the library while returning the copies to their owners.

The library was more than a storehouse of scrolls. It functioned as a research centre where mathematicians, astronomers and physicians worked side by side. Scholars were given food, lodging and a salary so that they could devote themselves entirely to study. This support allowed important discoveries, including early estimates of the size of the Earth and detailed maps of the night sky. The library is also credited with preserving and translating texts from many different cultures.

Estimates of how many scrolls the library held vary widely, from forty thousand to several hundred thousand. Because a single work might be spread across many scrolls, counting was difficult, and ancient writers often exaggerated. What is clear is that the collection was vast by the standards of its time and attracted visitors from across the Mediterranean.

The eventual decline of the library remains a subject of debate. Popular accounts describe a single dramatic fire, but historians now believe the loss happened gradually over several centuries. Cuts in royal funding, political instability and the slow decay of fragile papyrus all played a part. By the time the city changed hands repeatedly in later centuries, much of the collection had already disappeared, leaving later generations to wonder how much knowledge had been lost.

questions.q: Officials kept the original books from ships and gave back copies.

options: It was destroyed in one fire

reading.title: The Silk Road
reading.passage:
The Silk Road was not a single paved highway but a network of routes that connected China with the Mediterranean across thousands of kilometres. For centuries, these routes carried goods, ideas and people between East and West. Despite its name, silk was only one of many products traded along the way. Spices, glassware, precious metals and horses also moved between distant markets.

Few traders travelled the entire length of the road. Instead, goods were passed from one merchant to the next, changing hands many times before reaching their final destination. With each exchange the price rose, which is one reason why silk was so expensive in Rome. This relay system meant that most merchants knew only the section of the route closest to their home.

The Silk Road carried far more than physical goods. Religions, artistic styles and technologies spread along the same routes. Paper-making, for example, gradually moved westward from China, eventually transforming the way records were kept in Europe and the Middle East. Travellers also exchanged knowledge about medicine and astronomy.

Unfortunately, the network had a darker side. The same roads that carried valuable trade also carried disease. Historians believe that outbreaks of plague were able to spread quickly because infected people and goods moved along the busy routes. By the late medieval period, changing politics and the rise of sea trade reduced the importance of the overland routes. Even so, the cultural connections created by the Silk Road shaped the development of many societies and left a legacy that is still studied today.

questions.q: Silk was the only product traded along the Silk Road.
Most merchants travelled the full length of the route.
Paper-making spread from China towards the west.
Sea trade was more profitable than overland trade.
Why was silk expensive in Rome?
What 'darker side' did the routes have?
The Silk Road connected China with the ________ region.
Goods were passed from one ________ to the next.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient Trade
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today we're looking at how ancient trade networks shaped the world. Let's start with a key point: trade was never only about money. When merchants carried goods across long distances, they also carried ideas. A trader might bring back a new tool, a new recipe, or even a new way of writing numbers.
Now, consider the markets themselves. A typical market town along a major route would host visitors speaking five or six different languages. To deal with this, merchants developed simple shared phrases for buying and selling. Some scholars argue this was the beginning of trade languages.
There's a common myth I want to correct. People often imagine one merchant travelling from China all the way to Europe. In reality, goods changed hands again and again. A single bolt of cloth might pass through twenty different sellers. For your essay this week, I'd like you to choose one traded product and trace its likely journey. Please submit it by Friday, and remember the word limit is one thousand words.

questions.q: What is the lecturer's main point about trade?

reading.title: The Printing Revolution
reading.passage:
Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, usually by trained scribes who worked slowly and carefully. A single book could take months to produce, which made it extremely expensive. As a result, reading was largely limited to the wealthy and to religious institutions. The arrival of the printing press changed this situation dramatically.

The key innovation was movable type: small metal blocks, each carrying a single letter, which could be arranged into words, locked into a frame, inked and pressed onto paper. Once the printing was finished, the blocks could be taken apart and reused for a completely different text. This made it possible to produce hundreds of identical copies in the time it had once taken to copy a single book by hand.

The effects of printing spread far beyond the book trade. As books became cheaper, more people had a reason to learn to read, and literacy slowly increased. New ideas in science, politics and religion could travel quickly and reach a wide audience. Authorities sometimes tried to control what was printed, but the sheer number of presses made censorship difficult.

Printing also helped standardise languages. When the same spelling appeared in thousands of copies, regional variations gradually weakened, and a more uniform written language emerged. Some historians argue that without the printing press, later movements in science and education would have developed far more slowly. Whether or not this claim can be proven, there is little doubt that printing reshaped how knowledge was created and shared.

questions.q: Hand-copied books were cheap and widely available.
Movable type blocks could be reused for different texts.
Authorities found it easy to censor printed material.
The first printing press was built in Germany.
What was the key innovation of the printing press?
How did printing affect languages?
options: It made them harder to read

reading.title: The Age of Exploration
reading.passage:
Between the fifteenth and seventeenth centuries, European sailors undertook long ocean voyages that connected continents in new ways. This period, often called the Age of Exploration, was driven by a mixture of motives. Some rulers hoped to find faster sea routes to the spice markets of Asia, while others sought wealth, new lands, or the chance to spread their religion. Advances in shipbuilding and navigation made these ambitious journeys possible.

Navigation at sea was extremely difficult. Sailors used instruments such as the compass and the astrolabe to estimate their position, but mistakes were common and many voyages ended in disaster. Sailors also faced storms, shortages of fresh food and diseases that could wipe out a large part of a crew. Despite these dangers, the promise of profit and fame continued to attract adventurers.

The consequences of these voyages were enormous and often troubling. The arrival of Europeans in the Americas led to the exchange of plants, animals and crops between continents, a process that changed diets around the world. Potatoes and maize travelled to Europe, while horses and wheat were introduced to the Americas. At the same time, the voyages brought conquest, forced labour and devastating diseases to many local populations.

Historians continue to debate how to judge this era. It expanded geographical knowledge and linked distant parts of the world, yet it also caused great suffering. Understanding the Age of Exploration therefore means looking honestly at both its achievements and its costs, rather than focusing on only one side of a complex story.

questions.q: European voyages in this period had only one motive.
Navigation instruments always gave accurate positions.
Potatoes were taken from the Americas to Europe.
Spices from Asia were cheaper than gold at the time.

listening.title: Radio Programme on Old Maps
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. With me today is map historian Dr Bennett. Dr Bennett, what can old maps tell us?
GUEST: A great deal. An old map is not just a picture of land; it shows what people believed about the world. Early maps often left huge blank spaces where knowledge ran out.
HOST: And those famous sea monsters in the corners?
GUEST: Yes, mapmakers sometimes drew monsters in unknown waters. Partly decoration, partly a warning that the area was dangerous and poorly understood.
HOST: Fascinating. How accurate were these maps?
GUEST: Surprisingly good near home, but wildly wrong far away. Coastlines that traders knew well were detailed, while distant lands were guesswork.
HOST: Where can our listeners see such maps?
GUEST: There's a free exhibition at the National Library running until the thirtieth of June. I'd really recommend it.
HOST: Thank you, Dr Bennett. That's all for today.

reading.title: The Industrial Revolution
reading.passage:
The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century, was one of the most significant turning points in human history. Before this period, most goods were made by hand in small workshops or homes, and most people lived in the countryside. Within a few generations, machines powered first by water and then by steam transformed how things were produced, where people lived and how they worked.

The textile industry was among the first to change. New machines could spin thread and weave cloth far faster than any human worker. Because these machines were large and required a steady power supply, production moved into factories. Workers who had once set their own pace at home now had to follow the rhythm of the machines and the demands of the factory clock.

Industrialisation also reshaped where people lived. Drawn by the promise of work, huge numbers moved from rural villages to growing industrial towns. These towns expanded quickly, often without proper planning. Housing was crowded, water supplies were poor, and air pollution from coal was severe. For many families, factory wages offered a step up, but working conditions were frequently harsh and dangerous.

Over time, the pressures of industrial life led to important reforms. Workers began to organise, demanding shorter hours and safer conditions, and governments gradually introduced laws to limit child labour and improve public health. The Industrial Revolution thus produced both remarkable economic growth and serious social problems, a combination whose effects can still be seen in modern societies.

options: Machines were large and needed steady power

reading.title: The Return of Vaccination Outreach Vans
reading.passage:
In many rural districts, the distance between a household and the nearest clinic has long been a barrier to routine immunisation. To address this, several health authorities have revived an old idea in a modern form: the mobile vaccination van. These vehicles, fitted with refrigeration units and digital record systems, travel along fixed weekly routes, stopping at markets, schools and village squares so that families do not need to make a costly journey to a fixed centre.

The concept itself is not new. In the 1970s, simple vans were used to deliver vaccines to remote communities, but the cold chain often broke down, leaving doses spoiled and trust damaged. Today's vehicles rely on solar-powered cooling and temperature sensors that send alerts to a central office whenever the interior climbs above the safe range. As a result, the proportion of usable doses reaching distant villages has improved sharply.

A recent programme in a mountainous region recorded encouraging figures. Over eighteen months, the vans reached an estimated 24,000 children who had previously missed at least one scheduled dose. Health workers reported that attendance was highest when visits were announced in advance through local radio and text messages, suggesting that communication matters as much as transport.

Not everyone is convinced that vans are the best long-term answer. Critics argue that the running costs of fuel, staff and maintenance are high, and that the money might be better spent strengthening permanent clinics. Supporters respond that vans are a bridge, not a replacement, useful for reaching populations until fixed services can be expanded. Both sides agree, however, that reliable record-keeping is essential, because a child vaccinated in one village may be recorded twice if systems do not share data.

For now, the vans continue their circuits, and the early evidence suggests that flexibility, combined with careful planning, can close gaps that traditional clinics alone have struggled to reach.

reading.title: Why Hospitals Are Rethinking Noise
reading.passage:
For most patients, a hospital ward is associated with the steady beeping of monitors, the rattle of trolleys and the murmur of conversation. These sounds may seem harmless, even reassuring, but a growing body of research suggests that excessive noise can slow recovery and disturb sleep at the very time the body most needs rest.

Studies measuring sound levels in wards have found that they frequently exceed the limits recommended by international health bodies, sometimes reaching levels comparable to a busy office. Much of this noise comes not from emergencies but from routine activity: alarms that sound too often, staff talking near beds, and equipment that has not been adjusted to a quieter setting. Patients in intensive care, who are already fragile, are thought to be especially vulnerable.

Poor sleep is more than an inconvenience. It can raise stress hormones, weaken the immune response and increase confusion in older patients. Some hospitals have therefore begun trialling simple changes. These include offering earplugs, dimming lights at night, fitting softer flooring, and training staff to lower their voices after a certain hour. One ward reported that average night-time noise fell noticeably after such measures were introduced, and that patients said they felt more rested.

Technology is also playing a part. Newer monitors can be programmed so that minor alerts are sent silently to a nurse's handheld device rather than sounding aloud at the bedside. This reduces the number of alarms that wake everyone in a room while still ensuring that staff respond promptly to genuine problems.

Not all solutions are easy to apply. Older buildings were rarely designed with acoustics in mind, and structural changes can be expensive. Moreover, some noise is unavoidable in a place where care must continue around the clock. Still, the principle is gaining ground: a quieter ward is not merely more pleasant but may actively help patients heal, and that, supporters argue, makes the effort worthwhile.

listening.title: A Talk on Sleep and Recovery
listening.script:
NURSE EDUCATOR: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to talk briefly about why sleep matters so much for our patients. We often focus on medicines and procedures, but rest is itself a kind of treatment. During deep sleep, the body repairs tissue and the immune system works at its strongest.

Now, in a typical ward, patients are woken on average six times a night, often for routine checks that could be grouped together. So the first thing I'd ask you to consider is clustering your observations where it's safe to do so.

Second, think about light. Bright corridors can disturb sleep even through a curtain, so we're trialling dimmer night lighting on Ward C. Early feedback has been positive.

Third, sound. Please lower your voices after ten in the evening, and silence non-urgent alarms. A single beeping pump can wake an entire bay.

Finally, we'll be handing out a short survey next week. I'd be grateful if you could ask willing patients to complete it, so we can measure whether these changes really make a difference. Thank you.

reading.title: The Quiet Spread of Antibiotic Resistance
reading.passage:
Antibiotics were once seen as a near-miracle, capable of curing infections that had killed people for centuries. Yet the very success of these drugs has created a serious problem. Bacteria reproduce quickly, and with each generation a few may carry mutations that allow them to survive treatment. When antibiotics are overused, these resistant strains gain an advantage, multiplying while their weaker relatives die off.

The consequences are already visible. Infections that were once simple to treat can now require longer hospital stays, stronger drugs and, in the worst cases, may not respond to treatment at all. Health experts warn that if the trend continues, routine operations could become dangerous because doctors would no longer be able to rely on antibiotics to prevent infection.

Several factors drive the problem. In some places, antibiotics are sold without a prescription, so people take them for illnesses such as colds, which are caused by viruses and cannot be treated by these drugs at all. In farming, antibiotics are sometimes added to animal feed to encourage growth, exposing huge numbers of bacteria to the drugs and increasing the chance that resistance develops.

Tackling the issue requires action on several fronts. Hospitals are introducing stricter rules on when antibiotics may be prescribed, and pharmacists are being trained to question unnecessary requests. Public campaigns aim to explain that not every infection needs an antibiotic. Meanwhile, researchers are searching for new drugs, although developing one can take more than a decade and is rarely profitable, which discourages investment.

There is, however, room for cautious optimism. Some countries that introduced controls on antibiotic use have reported a measurable fall in resistant infections within a few years. This suggests that, while resistance cannot be eliminated, it can be slowed if governments, doctors and the public act together. The challenge is to maintain that effort over time, long after the immediate alarm has faded.

questions.q: Resistance develops because bacteria can carry mutations that help them survive treatment.

listening.title: Pharmacy Advice Line
listening.script:
PHARMACIST: Hello, you've reached the pharmacy advice line. How can I help today?

CALLER: Hi. I've had a sore throat and a runny nose for three days, and I was wondering if I could get some antibiotics.

PHARMACIST: I see. Can I ask, do you have a high temperature?

CALLER: No, no fever. Just feeling a bit run down.

PHARMACIST: Right. Those symptoms usually point to a common cold, which is caused by a virus. Antibiotics only work on bacteria, so they wouldn't help here, I'm afraid.

CALLER: Oh, I didn't realise that.

PHARMACIST: It's a common misunderstanding. The best things are rest, plenty of fluids, and perhaps a simple painkiller for the throat. Most colds clear up within about seven days.

CALLER: And if it doesn't get better?

PHARMACIST: If your symptoms last longer than ten days, or you develop a high fever, do book an appointment with your doctor. We can also recommend a throat spray if you'd like something for the discomfort.

CALLER: Yes, the spray sounds good. Thank you.

PHARMACIST: You're welcome. Take care.

reading.title: Rethinking the Daily Step Count
reading.passage:
For years, the figure of ten thousand steps a day has been treated almost as a law of healthy living. It appears on fitness trackers, in workplace challenges and on smartphone screens that gently nudge their owners to keep walking. Yet the origins of this target are surprisingly weak. It is widely believed to have come not from medical research but from a marketing slogan used to sell a pedometer in the 1960s, whose name in Japanese happened to resemble the figure ten thousand.

More recent studies paint a more nuanced picture. Researchers tracking large groups of older adults found that health benefits, such as a lower risk of early death, began to appear at far fewer steps than the famous target, often around four to five thousand. Beyond a certain point, the additional benefit of each extra thousand steps grew smaller, eventually levelling off. In other words, the steepest gains came from moving from a sedentary lifestyle to a moderately active one.

This does not mean that walking more is pointless. For younger and fitter people, higher counts may still bring advantages, and exercise affects far more than longevity, influencing mood, sleep and weight. The key message, scientists argue, is that the precise number matters less than the habit of regular movement, and that an unrealistic goal may discourage those who would benefit most.

There is also a question of how the steps are taken. Walking briskly appears to offer greater benefits than the same number of slow, scattered steps. Some researchers therefore suggest paying attention to pace as well as quantity, though they caution that any movement is better than none.

The broader lesson reaches beyond walking. It is a reminder that even widely accepted health advice deserves scrutiny, and that a simple, memorable number, however appealing, is not always supported by the evidence behind it.

listening.title: A Workplace Wellbeing Briefing
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Hi everyone, thanks for joining this short briefing on our new workplace wellbeing scheme. As you know, many of us spend most of the day sitting at a desk, and we'd like to change that gently.

Starting next month, we're launching a walking challenge. The idea is simple: aim to move a little more each day. We're not setting a strict step target, because research now suggests that even moderate activity brings real benefits. Instead, we'll reward consistency.

Here's how it works. Each team will be given a shared tracker. Every time a member logs a twenty-minute walk, the team earns a point. At the end of the month, the team with the most points wins a small prize, and, more importantly, bragging rights.

We'll also be introducing walking meetings for discussions with three people or fewer. It turns out people often think more creatively on the move. And for those who prefer the indoors, the stairwell posters will remind you that the lift is not the only option. Any questions can go to the wellbeing inbox. Thanks all.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Loneliness
reading.passage:
When public health officials list the threats facing modern societies, they tend to name familiar dangers: poor diet, smoking, lack of exercise. In recent years, however, a less obvious risk has begun to appear alongside these: loneliness. A growing number of studies suggest that prolonged social isolation can damage health in ways that are difficult to see but very real.

The evidence is striking. Researchers analysing data from many countries have found that people who report feeling persistently lonely face a higher risk of conditions such as heart disease, depression and cognitive decline. Some studies have even compared the effect of chronic loneliness on the body to that of well-known physical risks, arguing that the impact, though indirect, can be comparable in scale.

Why should isolation affect the body so deeply? Part of the answer lies in stress. Humans evolved as social creatures, and being cut off can keep the body in a low-level state of alarm, raising blood pressure and disturbing sleep. Loneliness may also influence behaviour, making people less likely to exercise, eat well or seek medical help when they need it.

The problem is not confined to the elderly, as is often assumed. Surveys suggest that young adults, despite being constantly connected through their phones, can report some of the highest levels of loneliness, a reminder that online contact is not the same as genuine companionship.

Responses are beginning to take shape. Some doctors now practise what is called social prescribing, referring patients not to medication but to community activities, volunteering or group classes. Early evaluations are cautiously positive, though researchers stress that more rigorous trials are needed. What is clear is that loneliness, once dismissed as a private sadness, is increasingly being treated as a matter of public health, deserving the same serious attention as any other widespread condition.

questions.q: Loneliness has traditionally been one of the main risks named by health officials.
Persistent loneliness has been linked to a higher risk of heart disease.
According to the passage, one reason isolation harms the body is that it
options: keeps the body in a state of stress

questions.q: What does the passage say about young adults?
options: They can report high levels of loneliness despite being connected online

questions.q: Some doctors now practise social ________, referring patients to community activities.
Loneliness may raise blood pressure and disturb ________.

The author argues that loneliness should be treated as a public health issue.

listening.title: A Community Health Lecture
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good evening. Tonight I'd like to introduce a project our health centre has been running for the past year, focused on tackling loneliness in our community.

When we began, we surveyed around four hundred residents. The results surprised us. While we expected older people to feel most isolated, we found that the eighteen to twenty-five age group reported the highest levels of loneliness of all.

So we designed a programme around what we call social prescribing. Instead of, or alongside, medication, our doctors can now refer patients to activities: a gardening group, a weekly choir, a walking club, and a cookery class on Wednesdays.

The early results are encouraging. After three months, two-thirds of participants said they felt more connected, and visits to the surgery for minor complaints actually fell. That last point matters, because it suggests the scheme may save money as well as improve wellbeing.

Of course, this is a small study, and we need larger trials to be sure. But for now, the message is simple: connection is a kind of medicine. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions.

reading.title: The Rise of the Flipped Classroom
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the typical lesson followed a familiar pattern. A teacher would stand at the front of the room and deliver new information through a lecture, while students listened and took notes. Afterwards, learners were sent home to apply what they had heard by completing exercises on their own. This model placed the most demanding intellectual work, such as solving problems, at the very moment when expert help was least available.

In recent years, a growing number of schools have experimented with a method known as the flipped classroom. The principle is simple but powerful: the order of activities is reversed. Students first encounter new material at home, usually by watching a short recorded video prepared by their teacher. Classroom time is then devoted to discussion, group projects and practice, with the teacher moving around the room to support individuals who are struggling.

Supporters argue that this arrangement makes far better use of limited contact hours. Because basic explanation has already taken place, the teacher can concentrate on the points that genuinely confuse students. A study conducted in 2019 across forty secondary schools reported that learners in flipped classes scored, on average, nine per cent higher in end-of-year science examinations than their peers in conventional classes.

However, the approach is not without difficulties. Critics point out that it assumes every pupil has reliable access to the internet and a quiet space in which to study, conditions that cannot be taken for granted in poorer households. There is also evidence that some students simply fail to watch the videos, arriving at the lesson unprepared and therefore unable to participate fully. For this reason, many teachers combine flipped techniques with traditional methods rather than abandoning the lecture entirely.

questions.q: In the traditional model, students did the hardest tasks when teacher support was readily available.
In a flipped classroom, students watch recorded videos before coming to the lesson.
The 2019 study covered schools in more than one country.
According to the passage, what is the main benefit claimed by supporters of the flipped classroom?
options: It removes the need for teachers entirely
It makes better use of limited class time
It is cheaper than buying textbooks
It allows students to choose their own subjects

options: Videos are too long to watch

questions.q: In the 2019 study, flipped-class learners scored on average ________ per cent higher in science exams.

RECEPTIONIST: Good evening, Riverside Community College. How can I help?
