reading.title: Learning a Second Language as an Adult
reading.passage:
It is widely believed that children learn languages far more easily than adults, and that anyone who has passed a certain age is destined to struggle. This idea, often called the critical period hypothesis, suggests that the brain loses much of its flexibility after early childhood. Yet a closer look at the evidence reveals a more complicated and, in some ways, more encouraging picture.

It is true that young children frequently acquire a near-perfect accent, something that very few adult learners ever manage. Their brains appear to be especially sensitive to the sounds of speech during the first years of life. However, when it comes to other aspects of language, adults often hold a clear advantage. They can draw on a mature understanding of grammar, a large vocabulary in their first language, and well-developed study skills.

Researchers at a university in Canada followed two hundred adult immigrants over three years. Those who studied for at least four hours a week and practised speaking with native speakers made remarkable progress, in several cases reaching a level of fluency that allowed them to work in professional jobs. The single most important factor was not age but motivation: learners with a strong personal reason to succeed consistently outperformed those without one.

The study also highlighted the role of confidence. Many adults are reluctant to speak because they fear making mistakes in front of others. Yet the learners who improved fastest were precisely those willing to take risks and accept that errors are a normal part of the process. Far from being a barrier, adulthood may simply demand a different and more deliberate approach to learning.

questions.q: The critical period hypothesis claims the brain becomes less flexible after early childhood.
Most adult learners eventually achieve a perfect native accent.
The Canadian study compared immigrants with university students of the same age.
According to the passage, what advantage do adults often have over children?
options: A more sensitive ear for sounds

questions.q: What did the Canadian study identify as the most important factor in success?
Learners who studied at least ________ hours a week made remarkable progress.
The fastest improvers were willing to take ________ and accept their errors.

listening.title: A Tour of the University Library
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the main university library. I'll give you a quick tour so you know where everything is.

STUDENT: Thanks. Where do we find the textbooks for our courses?

GUIDE: Course textbooks are on the ground floor, in the section we call the Reserve Collection. You can borrow those for three hours at a time.

STUDENT: Only three hours? That's short.

GUIDE: Yes, because they're in high demand. General books, however, can be borrowed for two weeks.

STUDENT: Good to know. Is there somewhere quiet to study?

GUIDE: The entire second floor is a silent zone. No talking and no phone calls up there, please.

STUDENT: And group work?

GUIDE: For that, book one of the discussion rooms on the first floor through the library website. They hold up to six people.

STUDENT: When does the library close?

GUIDE: During term it's open until midnight, but in the holidays it closes at six.

STUDENT: One last thing, how many books can I borrow at once?

GUIDE: Undergraduates may borrow fifteen items at a time. Any questions, just ask the staff at the help desk.

reading.title: Why Note-Taking Still Matters
reading.passage:
In an age when lectures can be recorded and slides downloaded with a single click, some students question whether taking notes by hand is worth the effort. Surely, they argue, it is more efficient to capture every word electronically and review it later. Yet a substantial body of research suggests that the act of writing notes does far more than simply store information for future reference.

The key lies in how the brain processes language. When students type, they are often able to record a speaker's words almost verbatim, copying sentences without truly engaging with them. Writing by hand, by contrast, is slower. This forces the learner to listen carefully, decide what is important, and put ideas into their own words. In other words, the very limitation of handwriting becomes its greatest strength.

A frequently cited experiment divided a group of university students into two halves. One half typed their notes during a series of lectures, while the other wrote by hand. When tested a week later, both groups performed similarly on simple questions of fact. On questions that required deeper understanding, however, the handwriting group scored noticeably better, suggesting that they had absorbed the underlying concepts more thoroughly.

None of this means that digital tools have no place in education. Many learners find it useful to combine the two methods, writing notes by hand during a class and then organising them on a computer afterwards. The lesson, researchers insist, is not that technology is harmful, but that students should think carefully about how, rather than simply whether, they record what they learn. The method chosen shapes the depth of understanding that follows.

questions.q: Some students think recording lectures is more efficient than handwriting notes.
Typing usually allows students to copy a speaker's words almost exactly.
The experiment was repeated in several different universities.
Why does writing by hand help understanding, according to the passage?
options: Its slowness forces learners to select and rephrase ideas

questions.q: On which type of question did the handwriting group perform clearly better?
When students type, they often record a speaker's words almost ________.
Both groups were tested a ________ after the lectures.
The researchers conclude that technology is harmful to learning.

listening.title: Student Advisor Office Hours
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Hi there, come on in. How can I help you today?

STUDENT: Hi. I'm thinking about changing my major from biology to psychology, but I'm not sure how it works.

ADVISOR: That's a fairly common switch. First, you'll need to fill out a Change of Program form. You can find it on the student portal under Forms.

STUDENT: Okay. Is there a deadline?

ADVISOR: Yes, you must submit it by the end of week four of the semester. After that, you'd have to wait until next term.

STUDENT: Got it. Will I lose any credits?

ADVISOR: Good question. Two of your biology courses count toward psychology, so you'd keep those. You'd be starting roughly one semester behind, though.

STUDENT: That's fine. Do I need to talk to anyone else?

ADVISOR: You'll need a signature from the head of the Psychology Department, Dr. Reyes. Her office is in Building C, room 210.

STUDENT: Perfect. And the form is free, right?

ADVISOR: The form itself is free, but there's a small administrative charge of twenty dollars. Pay that at the finance office.

STUDENT: Thanks so much for your help.

reading.title: The Hidden Benefits of School Gardens
reading.passage:
Across many countries, an increasing number of schools are setting aside small plots of land where pupils can grow vegetables, herbs and flowers. At first glance, a school garden might appear to be a pleasant but minor addition to the curriculum, useful perhaps for keeping restless children occupied. A closer examination, however, reveals a surprisingly wide range of educational and personal benefits that extend well beyond the obvious.

The most immediate gains are academic. A garden offers a living laboratory in which abstract scientific ideas suddenly become concrete. Concepts such as photosynthesis, the water cycle and the role of insects in pollination are far easier to grasp when pupils can observe them directly. Teachers report that even children who struggle to sit still in a conventional classroom often become deeply absorbed when they are planting seeds or measuring the growth of a plant.

The advantages are not confined to science. Tending a garden requires patience, cooperation and a sense of responsibility, since plants will not survive without regular care. Pupils learn that results take time and that neglect carries consequences. Several schools have also noticed an unexpected effect on diet: children who have grown their own vegetables are markedly more willing to eat them, a finding that has interested those concerned with rising rates of childhood obesity.

There are, of course, practical obstacles. Gardens demand space, equipment and, above all, time from already busy teachers. During long holidays, someone must continue to water and weed the plots, a task that often falls to volunteers from the local community. Despite these difficulties, supporters maintain that the rewards justify the effort, arguing that a school garden teaches lessons no textbook ever could.

questions.q: School gardens are becoming more common in many countries.
The writer believes a school garden is only a minor addition to the curriculum.
School gardens have been shown to improve pupils' performance in mathematics.
Why does the passage describe a garden as a 'living laboratory'?
options: Abstract scientific ideas become concrete there
It is open only to scientists

questions.q: What unexpected effect on children did several schools notice?
options: They were more willing to eat vegetables

questions.q: Tending a garden teaches pupils patience, cooperation and a sense of ________.
During long holidays, watering and weeding often falls to ________ from the local community.

listening.title: Lecture: How Memory Is Strengthened
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today I want to talk about how we can make our memories last longer. For decades, students have relied on a method we call cramming, that is, studying intensively just before an exam. Research now shows this is one of the least effective approaches.

Instead, the most powerful technique is called spaced repetition. The idea is simple: rather than reviewing material once, you revisit it at gradually increasing intervals, perhaps after one day, then three days, then a week. Each time you recall the information, the memory becomes stronger and harder to forget.

A related principle is active recall. Instead of merely re-reading your notes, you close the book and try to retrieve the answer from memory. This struggle, although uncomfortable, dramatically improves retention. In one study, students who tested themselves remembered about fifty per cent more after two months than those who simply re-read.

Finally, never underestimate the role of sleep. The brain consolidates memories during deep sleep, so a good night's rest after studying is far more valuable than staying awake to study for one extra hour. Remember these three pillars: space your learning, test yourself, and sleep well.

questions.q: What does the lecturer say about cramming?
options: It is one of the least effective approaches

questions.q: The most powerful technique mentioned is called ________ repetition.
With active recall, you close the book and try to ________ the answer from memory.
Students who tested themselves remembered about ________ per cent more after two months.
When does the brain consolidate memories, according to the lecturer?
The lecturer's three pillars are: space your learning, test yourself, and ________ well.

reading.title: Lifelong Learning in a Changing Economy
reading.passage:
For previous generations, the path through working life was relatively predictable. A person might train for a particular trade or profession in their youth and then practise it, with only minor adjustments, until retirement. The qualifications earned at eighteen or twenty-one were often sufficient to last an entire career. In today's economy, that assumption has quietly collapsed, and the concept of lifelong learning has moved from an attractive ideal to an economic necessity.

The principal driver of this shift is the accelerating pace of technological change. Entire industries can be transformed within a decade, and roles that seemed secure may be automated or rendered obsolete with little warning. According to one widely discussed report, a substantial proportion of today's schoolchildren will eventually work in occupations that do not yet exist. In such a climate, the ability to acquire new skills repeatedly throughout one's life is no longer a luxury but a basic requirement for staying employed.

Governments and employers have responded in various ways. Some companies now offer their staff regular training and even fund university courses, recognising that a skilled workforce is a competitive advantage. Several governments have introduced individual learning accounts, into which workers and the state both contribute, allowing employees to pay for retraining at any stage of their careers. Online platforms have further lowered the barriers, making it possible to study almost any subject from home at minimal cost.

Nevertheless, significant obstacles remain. Adults with demanding jobs and family responsibilities frequently lack the time and energy to study, however willing they may be. There is also a danger that the benefits flow disproportionately to those who are already well educated and confident learners, widening rather than narrowing existing inequalities. For lifelong learning to deliver on its promise, support must reach precisely those who have traditionally been left behind, not merely those who find it easy to participate.

questions.q: In the past, qualifications gained when young often lasted a whole career.
The passage states that technological change is slowing down.
The report named the specific jobs that future workers will hold.
What is described as the main driver of the shift toward lifelong learning?
options: The accelerating pace of technological change

options: Allowing workers to pay for retraining at any career stage

options: Support reaching those traditionally left behind

listening.title: Seminar: Designing an Effective Study Schedule
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, in today's seminar we're going to design a realistic study schedule. The biggest mistake students make is planning too much and then giving up when they fall behind.

STUDENT: So how much should we aim for?

TUTOR: Be honest about your free time. If you have, say, two hours an evening, don't plan for four. A good rule is to study in blocks of about twenty-five minutes, followed by a five-minute break. That cycle is sometimes called the Pomodoro method.

STUDENT: And what about difficult subjects?

TUTOR: Tackle your hardest subject first, when your mind is fresh. Most people have the most energy in the morning, so use that time wisely.

STUDENT: Should we study every single day?

TUTOR: I'd recommend keeping at least one day a week completely free. Rest prevents burnout and actually improves long-term performance.

STUDENT: How do we measure progress?

TUTOR: Keep a simple log. At the end of each week, write down what you completed and adjust the plan for the following week. The schedule should serve you, not the other way around.

questions.q: What does the tutor say is the biggest mistake students make?
options: Planning too much and then giving up

questions.q: A good rule is to study in blocks of about ________ minutes.
Each study block is followed by a ________-minute break.
When does the tutor suggest tackling your hardest subject?
options: First, when your mind is fresh

To measure progress, students should keep a simple ________.

The table below shows the number of adults (in thousands) enrolled in different types of lifelong learning in one country in 2018 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Type of learning | 2018 | 2023

reading.title: The Rise of the Glass Facade
reading.passage:
Few features define the modern city skyline as clearly as the glass facade. A century ago, exterior walls had to bear the weight of the building above them, so they were thick and pierced by only modest windows. The development of the steel frame changed this completely. Once a skeleton of steel or reinforced concrete carried the load, the outer wall no longer needed to support anything. Architects were suddenly free to wrap their towers in a thin, transparent skin known as the curtain wall.

Early glass towers, built in the middle of the twentieth century, were admired for their clean lines and sense of lightness. Yet they were far from perfect. Single-pane glass let heat pour out in winter and flood in during summer, forcing buildings to rely heavily on artificial heating and cooling. Occupants near the windows often complained of glare and uncomfortable temperatures. For decades these drawbacks were tolerated because the look was so fashionable, and energy was relatively cheap.

Contemporary designers approach glass quite differently. Modern facades typically use two or three layers of glass with insulating gas sealed between them, and the panes are often coated with microscopic metallic films that reflect heat while still admitting light. Some buildings add external shading fins or even automated blinds that respond to the movement of the sun. The aim is to keep the visual appeal of transparency without the enormous energy bills that once accompanied it.

Critics argue that the glass tower has become a global cliche, repeated in cities from Asia to South America regardless of local climate or culture. They point out that a fully glazed building in a hot desert city wastes energy no matter how clever the coating. Defenders reply that glass, used thoughtfully and combined with good engineering, remains one of the few materials capable of giving people a genuine connection to the daylight and the city beyond. The debate is unlikely to be settled soon, but it has at least pushed architects to think harder about how, and where, glass should be used.

questions.q: Before the steel frame was developed, exterior walls usually carried the weight of the building.
Early glass towers were energy efficient compared with older buildings.
The architect who designed the first curtain wall later regretted the decision.
According to the passage, why were the drawbacks of early glass towers tolerated?
options: The buildings were cheap to construct
The style was fashionable and energy was inexpensive
Occupants did not notice any problems
Governments required the use of glass

questions.q: What is one method modern facades use to control heat?
A thin transparent outer wall that supports no load is called a ________ wall.
Modern facades often seal an insulating ________ between layers of glass.
Critics believe glass towers are often built without regard to local climate.

listening.title: Booking a Studio Tour
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Riverside Art Studios, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a place on one of your weekend studio tours.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We run tours on Saturdays at eleven and again at two o'clock. Which would suit you?

CALLER: The eleven o'clock one, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Can I take your name?

CALLER: Yes, it's Karen Mosley. That's M-O-S-L-E-Y.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And how many tickets?

CALLER: Three, please. Two adults and one child.

RECEPTIONIST: That comes to eighteen pounds in total. The tour lasts about ninety minutes and includes the printmaking room and the ceramics workshop.

CALLER: Great. Do we need to bring anything?

RECEPTIONIST: Just comfortable shoes. We provide aprons if you'd like to try the pottery wheel.

CALLER: Perfect. And where exactly are you?

RECEPTIONIST: We're at fourteen Mill Lane, just behind the old railway station. There's free parking at the rear of the building.

CALLER: Wonderful, thank you very much.

vocab.example: The glass facade reflected the morning sun.
The transparent walls let daylight flood the office.
Reinforced concrete can carry very heavy loads.

reading.title: The Quiet Revolution of the Bauhaus
reading.passage:
When the Bauhaus school opened in Germany in 1919, few people could have predicted how deeply it would reshape the objects of everyday life. Its founder believed that there should be no real divide between fine art and craft. A chair, a teapot or a lamp, he argued, deserved the same careful thought as a painting or a sculpture. This idea sounds obvious today, but at the time it was a bold challenge to the long tradition that placed the artist far above the humble craftsman.

The school's teaching was unusual. New students did not begin by copying old masters. Instead they spent their first months exploring materials directly, testing how metal, wood, glass and textiles behaved when bent, cut or joined. Only after this hands-on foundation did they move into specialised workshops. The aim was to produce designers who understood both how an object looked and how it could actually be manufactured.

Bauhaus designers came to favour simple geometric forms, smooth surfaces and an almost complete absence of decoration. They were not interested in ornament for its own sake; they wanted objects that were honest about their function and suited to mass production. A famous tubular steel chair, light enough to lift with one hand, showed how an everyday item could be both elegant and efficient. Such pieces were intended to be affordable, although in practice many were too expensive for ordinary households at first.

The school itself lasted only fourteen years before political pressure forced it to close in 1933. Yet its influence spread across the world as its teachers and students emigrated, carrying their ideas with them. The clean lines of modern kitchens, the layout of design courses in universities, and the very notion that good design should be available to everyone all owe something to that short-lived experiment. The Bauhaus produced relatively few buildings, but it changed how millions of people think about the things they use each day.

questions.q: The founder of the Bauhaus believed art and craft should be treated as separate worlds.
New students at the Bauhaus started by copying the work of old masters.
The tubular steel chair won an international design prize.
Why did Bauhaus designers avoid decoration?
options: It was illegal at the time
They wanted objects honest about their function
They could not afford materials for it

questions.q: What happened to the Bauhaus in 1933?
Students first explored ________ directly before entering specialised workshops.
Bauhaus designers favoured simple ________ forms with smooth surfaces.
The school produced a large number of buildings during its existence.

listening.title: A Talk on Choosing a Design Course
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Welcome, everyone. I'm here to help you choose between our two design pathways. The first is Product Design, which focuses on physical objects, from furniture to kitchen tools. The second is Digital Design, which deals mainly with apps and websites.

Both courses run for three years. In the first year, the content is actually shared, so you don't have to decide your specialism straight away. You only choose at the start of the second year.

Now, a few practical points. The studio is open from eight in the morning until ten at night, and you'll have your own desk space. The materials budget for each student is two hundred dollars per term, and you keep whatever you make.

Assessment is mostly by portfolio rather than written exams, which suits people who learn by doing. The one written piece is a short essay in the final year.

If you'd like a place on the open day, please email the office before Friday. The open day itself is on the twelfth of March, and it includes a tour of the workshop.

reading.title: Colour in the Ancient World
reading.passage:
Modern visitors to ancient ruins often picture white marble temples and pale stone statues standing under a blue sky. This image, however, is largely a mistake. The great buildings and sculptures of the ancient Mediterranean were once vividly painted, covered in reds, blues, golds and greens that would seem almost shocking to the modern eye. Over many centuries the pigments faded, washed away by rain or scrubbed off by later admirers who preferred the bare stone, and so the original colour was forgotten.

Recreating these lost shades is difficult, partly because the materials used were so varied. Some colours came from minerals dug out of the ground, such as the deep blue made by heating sand, copper and other substances together. Others were extracted, at enormous cost, from tiny sea creatures; a single robe dyed a rich purple might require thousands of shells, which is why that colour became a symbol of wealth and power. Cheaper pigments, made from earth or charcoal, were used for less important work.

Scientists now have clever ways of detecting traces of these vanished colours. By shining special lights on a surface, they can make faint chemical residues glow, revealing patterns invisible to the naked eye. Careful chemical analysis can then identify exactly which pigment was used. Thanks to these techniques, museums have begun to produce brightly painted copies of famous statues, allowing the public to see roughly how they might once have appeared.

Not everyone welcomes these colourful reconstructions. Some critics find the painted versions garish and argue that centuries of admiration for plain marble cannot simply be dismissed. Others reply that historical accuracy matters more than modern taste, and that understanding ancient art means accepting how strange and bright it really was. Whatever one's opinion, the research has overturned a long-standing assumption and reminded us that the past was rarely as pale and quiet as we imagine.

questions.q: Ancient Mediterranean statues were originally left as bare, uncoloured stone.
Purple dye was expensive because it was made from sea creatures.
The blue pigment was more expensive than the purple dye.
How do scientists detect traces of vanished colours?
options: By shining special lights to make residues glow
By soaking the statue in water

questions.q: Why do some critics dislike the painted reconstructions?
options: They are too expensive to make

questions.q: Cheaper pigments were made from earth or ________.
The original colours were lost partly because the ________ faded over time.
The research has changed a long-held assumption about ancient art.

listening.title: Museum Exhibition Briefing
listening.script:
CURATOR: Right, let me brief you new volunteers on the colour exhibition. The main gallery is on the first floor, and the painted statue copies are at the far end, near the window.

VISITORS usually start at the entrance video, which runs for about six minutes and explains how the colours were discovered. After that, they follow the route marked in green on the floor.

A couple of things to remember. The painted copies must not be touched, so please keep an eye on younger visitors. Photography is allowed, but without flash, because the bright light can affect the delicate dyes on display nearby.

We run a free guided tour twice a day, at half past ten and again at three. Each tour takes around forty minutes and can hold up to fifteen people.

If a visitor asks about the gift shop, it's on the ground floor, just past the cafe. And the cloakroom, where they can leave bags, is right next to the main entrance. Any questions before we open?

reading.title: Designing Cities for Walking
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, urban planners designed cities around the car. Wide roads, vast car parks and sprawling suburbs all assumed that people would drive almost everywhere. In many places this approach succeeded in moving traffic quickly, but it came at a hidden cost. Streets became noisy and polluted, shops that relied on passing pedestrians struggled, and people who could not drive, including children and the elderly, found their freedom sharply reduced.

In recent decades a different philosophy has gained ground. Known broadly as walkable design, it puts the person on foot at the centre of planning. The basic idea is simple: if daily needs such as schools, shops and parks lie within a short walk of home, fewer car journeys are necessary. Narrower streets, wider pavements, frequent crossings and shade from trees all encourage people to leave the car behind, at least for short trips.

The benefits, supporters claim, reach well beyond transport. Walking is good for physical health, and streets full of pedestrians tend to feel safer because there are more eyes watching what happens. Local businesses often do better, since people on foot stop more readily than those speeding past in a vehicle. There may even be social gains, as neighbours who walk are more likely to meet and talk than those who drive in and out of garages.

Making an existing city more walkable is far from easy. Roads that have been widened over decades cannot be narrowed overnight, and some residents resist losing convenient parking. Critics also warn that walkable neighbourhoods can become so desirable that they grow expensive, pushing out the very people they were meant to help. Even so, a growing number of cities are experimenting with these ideas, closing certain streets to cars, planting trees and redesigning junctions. The car is unlikely to disappear, but the assumption that it must dominate the city is steadily being questioned.

questions.q: Car-centred planning created some problems for shops that depended on pedestrians.
Walkable design assumes daily needs should be far from people's homes.
Most residents support narrowing the roads in their neighbourhood.
According to supporters, why might streets full of pedestrians feel safer?
What problem do critics associate with successful walkable neighbourhoods?
options: They can become expensive and push people out

questions.q: Walkable design places the person on ________ at the centre of planning.
Wider pavements and shade from ________ encourage people to walk.
The passage states that the car will soon disappear from cities entirely.

listening.title: A Lecture on Urban Green Space
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to focus on how green space shapes a city's character. We tend to think of parks as luxuries, but research increasingly treats them as essential infrastructure, just like roads or water pipes.

Let's start with health. Studies in several countries suggest that people who live within four hundred meters of a park are more physically active and report lower stress levels. The effect is strongest among older residents.

Green space also helps with temperature. A city with plenty of trees can be several degrees cooler in summer than one dominated by concrete. This matters more every year as heat waves grow more common.

There's an economic angle too. Homes near a well-kept park typically sell for a higher price, sometimes around twelve percent more. That can be a problem, of course, if it prices local people out.

Finally, parks bring people together. They host markets, concerts and sports, giving strangers a shared place to meet. For next week, please read chapter five and prepare one example of a successful urban park.

questions.q: How does the lecturer suggest we should view parks?
Health benefits appear for people living within ________ meters of a park.
Which group benefits most from living near a park?
Homes near a good park can sell for around ________ percent more.
Trees can make a city several ________ cooler in summer.
For next week, students must read chapter ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about your local area. Is the place where you live easy to walk around? What do you usually do on foot, and what changes would make your neighbourhood more pleasant to walk in?

writing.q:
Some people think that city centres should ban private cars completely in order to reduce pollution and encourage walking. Others believe this would cause too many problems for residents and businesses. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

The city redesigned several busy junctions.

reading.title: The Art of the Public Mural
reading.passage:
Walk through almost any large city today and you are likely to encounter a giant painting on the side of a building. The public mural, once associated mainly with political messages or simple decoration, has become a respected art form with its own festivals, funding and famous practitioners. Yet behind the bright images lies a surprising amount of planning, negotiation and technical skill.

Before a single drop of paint is applied, an artist must usually obtain permission from the building's owner and, often, the local council. This process can take months, and the design itself may need to be approved by a committee. Some artists complain that this oversight waters down their ideas, while others accept it as the price of working legally on such a large and visible scale. A mural, after all, cannot easily be hidden if people object to it.

The practical challenges are considerable. A wall several storeys high must be cleaned and sometimes repaired before work begins. Artists may spend days on raised platforms, battling wind, rain and the fading light of late afternoon. The paint must be durable enough to survive years of sun and pollution, so specialist materials are chosen with care. Even the surface beneath matters: a rough brick wall absorbs paint very differently from smooth concrete.

Supporters argue that murals can transform neglected districts, giving residents pride in their surroundings and drawing visitors who might otherwise stay away. There is evidence that bright, well-made artwork can discourage vandalism, since people tend to respect a wall that clearly belongs to the community. Critics, however, worry that murals are sometimes used by developers simply to make an area look fashionable before raising rents. Whether a mural truly serves the people who live around it, or merely decorates their displacement, often depends less on the artist than on who paid for the work and why.

questions.q: Public murals are now treated as a serious art form with festivals and funding.
Artists can usually begin a mural without any official permission.
Mural artists earn more money than gallery painters.
Why is specialist paint used for murals?
options: It is cheaper than ordinary paint
It must survive years of sun and pollution

questions.q: What concern do critics raise about murals?
options: They may be used by developers to raise rents
They take too long to paint

questions.q: A rough ________ wall absorbs paint differently from smooth concrete.
Some artists feel that official oversight ________ down their ideas.
Well-made murals can help discourage vandalism.

listening.title: Community Mural Project Meeting
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Thanks for coming to the mural project meeting. Let me run through the plan so everyone's clear on what happens next.

The wall we've chosen is on the community centre, facing the car park. It's about eight meters wide, so it's a big surface but very visible, which is exactly what we want.

The lead artist is Priya Nair, and she'll start the design next month. We're asking residents to suggest themes, and the most popular idea so far is local wildlife. You can vote online until the end of June.

In terms of money, the total budget is four thousand dollars, mostly from a council grant. That covers paint, equipment hire and a small fee for the artist.

Painting itself should take about two weeks, weather permitting. We'll need volunteers to help with the lower sections, no experience required, just enthusiasm.

Finally, the official unveiling is planned for the third of August, with refreshments and a short speech. Please put that date in your diary.

reading.title: The Rise of the Subscription Economy
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the typical commercial relationship was straightforward: a customer paid once for a product and then owned it outright. In recent decades, however, a different model has spread rapidly across many industries. Under the so-called subscription economy, consumers pay a recurring fee, usually monthly or annually, in exchange for continuing access to a good or service. What was once limited to newspapers and magazines now covers software, music, films, transport, and even razors and meals.

The appeal to businesses is considerable. A one-off sale generates revenue only once, whereas a subscription produces a predictable stream of income that can be forecast with reasonable accuracy. This stability makes it easier for firms to plan investment and to reassure lenders and shareholders. Subscriptions also create an ongoing relationship with the customer, giving the company repeated opportunities to gather data, refine its offering, and promote additional services.

For consumers, the benefits are less clear-cut. On one hand, spreading the cost over time lowers the initial barrier to ownership, and access models often include free upgrades and maintenance. On the other hand, the cumulative cost of many small payments can quietly exceed the price of simply buying a product once. Analysts point to a phenomenon sometimes called subscription fatigue, in which households lose track of how many services they are paying for and continue to be charged for ones they rarely use.

The long-term consequences for the wider economy remain uncertain. Some economists argue that the model encourages efficient use of resources, since goods such as cars or tools can be shared among many subscribers rather than sitting idle. Others worry that it shifts power decisively towards large platforms, which accumulate detailed knowledge of consumer behaviour and can adjust prices with little warning. What seems certain is that ownership, once the foundation of consumer life, is no longer the only path to access.

questions.q: Subscription-based payment was entirely unknown before recent decades.
Subscriptions give companies a more predictable income than one-off sales.
Most consumers prefer subscriptions to buying products outright.
The total cost of several subscriptions can be higher than buying a product once.
According to the passage, why is subscription fatigue a problem for consumers?
options: They forget about services they keep paying for

questions.q: Which concern about the subscription model is mentioned in the passage?
options: It gives large platforms more power over consumers

questions.q: A subscription produces a predictable ________ of income that firms can forecast.
Shared goods such as cars or tools avoid sitting ________, which some say is efficient.

listening.title: Opening a Business Bank Account
listening.script:
CLERK: Good morning, welcome to Northbank. How can I help you today?

CUSTOMER: Hello. I'd like to open a business account for my small bakery.

CLERK: Of course. Can I take your full name, please?

CUSTOMER: It's Helen Carter, that's C-A-R-T-E-R.

CLERK: Thank you. And the name of the business?

CUSTOMER: Sunrise Bakery.

CLERK: Lovely. For a business account you'll need two forms of identification and proof of your business address. Do you have those today?

CUSTOMER: I have my passport and a utility bill. Is that enough?

CLERK: That's perfect. There's a monthly fee of twelve pounds, but the first eighteen months are free for new businesses.

CUSTOMER: That's helpful. What about a card?

CLERK: A debit card will arrive within five working days. You can also set a daily withdrawal limit, the standard is five hundred pounds.

CUSTOMER: Could I raise that to eight hundred?

CLERK: Certainly. I'll note that on your file. Finally, would you like online banking activated today?

CUSTOMER: Yes, please.

CLERK: Done. You'll receive a confirmation email by Friday.

reading.title: The Hidden Costs of Cheap Goods
reading.passage:
Walk into almost any high-street shop and you will find clothing, electronics, and household items priced at levels that would have astonished shoppers a generation ago. The falling price of consumer goods is often presented as a triumph of modern commerce, a sign that competition and efficient production have made comfort affordable to ordinary households. Yet economists increasingly warn that the figure on the price tag does not capture the full cost of what we buy.

Many cheap products owe their low prices to externalities, costs that are not paid by the producer or the buyer but are passed on to society or the environment. A factory that pollutes a river, for example, lowers its own expenses while imposing a burden of cleaning and ill health on people living downstream. Because this cost never appears in the company's accounts, the goods seem cheaper than they truly are. Cheap fast fashion, similarly, relies on materials and labour whose real price is rarely reflected at the till.

There is also the question of durability. A garment that costs very little may last only a season before falling apart, forcing the consumer to buy a replacement. Over several years, a sequence of cheap purchases can prove more expensive than a single well-made item, a pattern sometimes described as the poverty premium because it most often affects those with the least money to spare.

Some governments have begun to respond. Taxes on pollution, rules requiring longer warranties, and labelling schemes that show a product's environmental impact are all attempts to make the true cost more visible. Whether such measures change behaviour on a large scale is still debated, but the underlying argument is gaining ground: a low price is not always a good deal, and the cheapest option is sometimes the most costly of all.

questions.q: Consumer goods are generally cheaper now than they were decades ago.
Externalities are costs paid directly by the producer.
The poverty premium tends to affect poorer consumers most.
Governments have agreed that pollution taxes are the most effective measure.
What example does the passage give of an environmental externality?
Why can a series of cheap purchases be more expensive overall?
options: Cheap goods wear out and must be replaced
Cheap goods are hard to find

questions.q: Costs passed on to society or the environment are called ________.
Governments use taxes, warranties, and ________ schemes to reveal a product's true cost.

listening.title: A Lecture on Supply and Demand
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, let's begin with the most basic idea in economics: supply and demand. Demand simply describes how much of a good people want at a given price. As a rule, when the price rises, demand falls, and when the price drops, demand tends to rise. Supply works in the opposite direction. Producers are usually willing to make more of a product when the price is high, because there is more profit to be earned.

Now, the interesting point is where these two forces meet. We call that the equilibrium price, the point at which the quantity people want to buy exactly matches the quantity producers want to sell. At that price, the market is, in theory, balanced.

Of course, the real world is messier. Imagine a sudden frost destroys half a country's coffee crop. Supply falls sharply, but demand stays the same, so the price climbs. Consumers may then switch to tea, a so-called substitute good. For your essay this week, choose one product and explain how a change in supply might affect its price. Aim for about eight hundred words, and please submit it by Thursday.

questions.q: According to the lecturer, what usually happens to demand when price rises?
The point where supply meets demand is called the ________ price.
Why are producers willing to make more when the price is high?
A frost destroying coffee plants causes supply to fall ________.
Tea is described as a ________ good for coffee.
The essay should be about ________ words.

speaking.q:
Describe a product you bought that turned out to be poor value for money. You should say:
- what the product was
- where and why you bought it
- how much it cost
and explain why you felt it was poor value for money.

writing.q:
Some people believe that governments should tax products that harm the environment so that their prices reflect their true cost. Others argue that this unfairly raises prices for ordinary consumers. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Today's low prices would astonish shoppers from the past.
Cheap goods are seen as a triumph of modern commerce.
Pollution is a common externality of cheap production.
The factory imposes a burden on people downstream.
Cleaning the river is a burden on the local community.
The durability of a product affects its real cost.
A sequence of cheap purchases can be costly.
The poverty premium burdens those with little money.
Longer warranties encourage more durable goods.
Labels make the true cost more visible.
The equilibrium price balances supply and demand.
Tea can act as a substitute for coffee.

reading.title: Why Family Firms Survive
reading.passage:
When people picture the modern economy, they often imagine vast corporations owned by thousands of distant shareholders. Yet across most of the world, the family firm remains remarkably common. From small workshops to some of the largest enterprises on the planet, businesses controlled by a single family account for a substantial share of output and employment. Their persistence has puzzled economists, who once predicted that such firms would gradually give way to more professional forms of ownership.

Several factors help explain their endurance. Because the owners are also the managers, decisions can be made quickly without lengthy consultation, and the people in charge have a direct personal stake in the outcome. Family firms also tend to think in terms of decades rather than the next quarter's results, since the founders often hope to pass the business on to their children. This long horizon can encourage patient investment that publicly traded companies, under pressure to satisfy investors, may avoid.

There are, however, well-known weaknesses. Disputes among relatives can paralyse a company, and the desire to keep control within the family may lead owners to overlook talented outsiders. Succession is a particular danger: a gifted founder is not guaranteed to produce an equally capable heir, and many promising firms have collapsed in the hands of a second or third generation.

Researchers who have studied the most durable family businesses note that the survivors tend to share certain habits. They bring in professional managers while retaining family ownership, they establish clear rules for resolving conflict, and they are willing to sell or close parts of the business that no longer make sense. In short, the firms that last are those that combine the loyalty and patience of a family with the discipline of a well-run organisation.

questions.q: Family firms are found mainly among small businesses, not large ones.
Economists once expected family firms to decline over time.

questions.q: A risky moment for family firms is ________, when control passes to an heir.

reading.title: The Economics of Tourism
reading.passage:
Few industries have grown as quickly over the past half-century as tourism. Once the preserve of the wealthy, foreign travel has become a routine part of life for hundreds of millions of people, made possible by cheaper flights, rising incomes, and the spread of online booking. For many regions, the arrival of large numbers of visitors has brought welcome prosperity, creating jobs and channelling money into local economies. Yet the relationship between tourism and the places that host it is more complicated than the impressive visitor figures suggest.

The benefits are real and easily measured. Hotels, restaurants, and transport firms employ local people, and the taxes they pay can fund roads, schools, and hospitals. Tourism can also breathe new life into traditional crafts and customs, since visitors are often eager to buy handmade goods and to watch local performances. In remote areas with few other industries, the income from travellers may be the difference between survival and decline.

However, dependence on tourism carries serious risks. An economy built around visitors is highly vulnerable to events beyond its control, from economic downturns in distant countries to natural disasters and disease. When the flow of tourists suddenly stops, the consequences for employment can be severe. There are also less obvious costs. Popular destinations may suffer from overcrowding, rising property prices that push out local residents, and damage to the very landscapes and monuments that attracted visitors in the first place.

Increasingly, governments and communities are searching for a more balanced approach. Some limit the number of visitors allowed at fragile sites, while others encourage tourists to stay longer and spread out across a wider area rather than crowding into a handful of famous spots. The aim, supporters argue, is not to discourage travel but to ensure that its rewards are lasting rather than fleeting, and shared more evenly between visitors and hosts.

questions.q: Foreign travel was once mainly available only to rich people.
Taxes from tourism can be used to fund public services.
Tourism always harms traditional crafts.
Most governments have already limited visitor numbers at popular sites.
Why is an economy built around tourism described as vulnerable?
options: Distant events can suddenly stop visitor flows

questions.q: What is one approach communities use to balance tourism?
options: Encouraging tourists to spread across wider areas

questions.q: Popular destinations may suffer from ________ that pushes out local residents.
Supporters want tourism's rewards to be lasting rather than ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Sustainable Tourism
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good evening, everyone, and thank you for coming to tonight's talk on sustainable tourism. I want to begin with a striking figure. Our small coastal town receives around two hundred thousand visitors every summer, yet the permanent population is only twelve thousand. That imbalance creates both opportunity and strain.

On the positive side, tourism supports roughly forty percent of local jobs. Without it, many shops and cafes simply could not survive the winter. But the pressure on water supplies, roads, and housing is real, and the council has decided to act.

From next year, we will introduce a small visitor charge of three pounds per night, collected by hotels. The money raised will go directly into protecting the coastline and improving public transport. We are also launching a scheme to promote the quieter spring and autumn months, so that visitors are spread more evenly across the year.

If you would like to get involved, there's a volunteer sign-up sheet at the back, and our next community meeting is on the fourteenth of March. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions.

reading.title: The Promise and Peril of Microfinance
reading.passage:
In the late twentieth century, a quiet revolution began in the world of finance. Conventional banks had long refused to lend to the very poor, reasoning that people without property or steady wages were too risky to be worth the trouble. Microfinance set out to challenge that assumption by offering tiny loans, sometimes only a few dollars, to individuals who would otherwise have no access to credit at all. The idea was simple but powerful: with a small amount of capital, a market trader could buy more stock, a tailor could purchase a sewing machine, and a household could lift itself gradually out of poverty.

Early results were widely celebrated. Repayment rates were unexpectedly high, partly because borrowers were often organised into small groups whose members guaranteed one another's loans. The social pressure to repay, combined with the prospect of future borrowing, proved remarkably effective. Supporters argued that microfinance offered a dignified alternative to charity, helping people to help themselves rather than simply handing out aid.

As the movement expanded, however, a more cautious assessment emerged. Careful studies found that while small loans could ease hardship and smooth irregular incomes, they rarely produced the dramatic escape from poverty that early enthusiasts had promised. In some places, aggressive lenders pushed borrowers into debts they could not manage, and interest rates, though lower than those of informal moneylenders, were still high enough to trap the unwary.

Most economists today take a measured view. Microfinance, they suggest, is neither the cure for poverty that its champions once claimed nor the trap that its critics describe. Used responsibly, with fair terms and proper regulation, it remains a useful tool among many. Its true lesson may be that there are no simple solutions to poverty, only a range of imperfect instruments that must be chosen and combined with care.

questions.q: Traditional banks were generally willing to lend to very poor people.
Group lending helped to keep repayment rates high.
Later studies confirmed that microfinance usually lifts people dramatically out of poverty.
Microfinance loans are now available in every country.
Why did supporters prefer microfinance to charity?
options: It helped people to help themselves

questions.q: What is the measured view of most economists today?
options: Microfinance is one imperfect tool among many

questions.q: Borrowers were often organised into small ________ that guaranteed each other's loans.
Interest rates, though lower than informal lenders', could still trap the ________.

listening.title: A Radio Feature on Small Loans
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. Today we're looking at how very small loans can change lives. I'm joined by economist Dr Alan Reeve. Alan, what exactly is a microloan?

REEVE: Thanks for having me. A microloan is simply a very small loan, often under two hundred dollars, given to people who can't get credit from a normal bank.

HOST: And who tends to use them?

REEVE: Mostly small traders and craftspeople. Around seventy percent of borrowers in our study were women, often running businesses from home.

HOST: That's a striking figure. Do people pay the money back?

REEVE: Surprisingly, yes. Repayment rates are above ninety percent, much higher than many large banks achieve.

HOST: What's the catch?

REEVE: Well, interest can be high, and a loan alone won't solve deep poverty. It works best alongside training and savings programmes.

HOST: Where can listeners learn more?

REEVE: We've published a free report on our website, and there's a public lecture next Tuesday at the city library, starting at seven o'clock.

HOST: Excellent. Thank you, Alan.

reading.title: How Comets Wake Up
reading.passage:
A comet is essentially a lump of ice, dust and frozen gas that travels around the Sun on a long, stretched orbit. For most of its journey, a comet is far from the Sun and remains cold and inactive, looking little more than a dark, dirty snowball drifting through space. It is only when it approaches the inner Solar System that the object begins to change in a dramatic way.

As a comet nears the Sun, the surface ice does not melt into liquid. Instead it turns directly from solid into gas, a process known as sublimation. The escaping gas carries dust away from the comet's solid core, or nucleus, and forms a vast glowing cloud called the coma. This coma can grow far larger than the nucleus itself, sometimes stretching across thousands of kilometres, even though the solid centre may be only a few kilometres wide.

The famous tails of comets appear next. Pressure from sunlight and the stream of particles flowing out from the Sun push the released gas and dust away from the nucleus. Because these forces always point away from the Sun, a comet's tail does not trail behind it like the wake of a boat. Instead, the tail can actually lead the comet as it swings around and heads back towards the outer Solar System.

Each time a comet passes close to the Sun, it loses a little of its material. Over many thousands of orbits, some comets gradually break apart or fade until almost nothing remains. Astronomers study these icy visitors closely because they are thought to contain frozen material left over from the birth of the Solar System, offering clues about conditions that existed billions of years ago.

questions.q: A comet stays cold and inactive while it is far from the Sun.
When a comet approaches the Sun, its surface ice melts into liquid water.
The coma is usually smaller than the solid nucleus of the comet.
Comets travel faster than any other objects in the Solar System.
According to the passage, what causes a comet's tail to point away from the Sun?
options: Sunlight and particles from the Sun

questions.q: The process by which solid ice turns directly into gas is called ________.
Astronomers value comets because they contain frozen material from the ________ of the Solar System.
What does the passage say happens to comets over many orbits?
options: They lose material and may break apart

reading.title: The Search for Water on Mars
reading.passage:
For more than a century, people have wondered whether Mars might once have been a world with rivers, lakes and perhaps even life. Today the planet appears dry and dusty, with a thin atmosphere and surface temperatures far below freezing. Yet the search for water on Mars has become one of the central goals of modern space exploration, because liquid water is closely linked to the possibility of life.

Evidence gathered by orbiting spacecraft and surface rovers strongly suggests that water once flowed across the Martian landscape. Cameras have photographed valleys that resemble dried river channels, along with deposits of minerals that usually form in the presence of water. In several locations, rovers have found smooth, rounded pebbles of the kind that are shaped by flowing streams on Earth. Taken together, these clues point to a past climate that was far warmer and wetter than the conditions seen today.

The question of where this water went remains difficult to answer. Some scientists believe that much of it escaped into space after Mars lost most of its protective atmosphere. Others argue that large amounts of water are still present, frozen beneath the surface as buried ice or trapped within minerals. Radar instruments have detected what may be layers of ice near the poles, and possibly even pockets of salty liquid deep underground.

Understanding the history of Martian water is not only a scientific puzzle. If liquid water survived long enough in the distant past, simple living things might have appeared, just as they did on the early Earth. Future missions, including plans to bring rock samples back to Earth for detailed study, may finally reveal whether Mars was ever home to life, or whether it has always been a barren and empty planet.

questions.q: Mars currently has a thick atmosphere and warm surface temperatures.
Rovers have found rounded pebbles similar to those shaped by streams on Earth.
Scientists fully agree on where the water on Mars has gone.
The first humans will land on Mars within the next ten years.
Why is the search for water on Mars considered so important?
options: Water could be used as rocket fuel
Water is linked to the possibility of life
Water proves the planet is young

questions.q: Some scientists think much of the water escaped into ________ after Mars lost its atmosphere.
What do future missions plan to bring back to Earth?

listening.title: A Student Plans a Project on Mars
listening.script:
TUTOR: Hi Maya, you wanted to discuss your science project, right? You're looking at Mars, I think.

MAYA: Yes. I want to focus on the search for water there, but I'm not sure how to organize it.

TUTOR: That's a strong topic. I'd suggest three sections. Start with the evidence, then explain the theories, and finish with future missions.

MAYA: Okay. For the evidence part, can I use photos of the dried river channels?

TUTOR: Absolutely. Just make sure you credit the source. The space agency website has free images you can use.

MAYA: Great. How long should the report be?

TUTOR: Aim for about two thousand words. And remember the deadline is the fifteenth of March, so you have three weeks.

MAYA: That should be enough time. Should I include a diagram?

TUTOR: A simple diagram of the planet's layers would really help your readers. And don't forget a short list of references at the end.

MAYA: Thanks, that's really useful. I'll send you a draft next Friday.

reading.title: Why Stars Have Different Colours
reading.passage:
When we look up at a clear night sky, the stars may seem to twinkle with a uniform white glow. In fact, stars come in a remarkable range of colours, from deep red through to brilliant blue. With careful observation, and especially through a telescope, these differences become much easier to notice. The colour of a star is not a random feature; it carries important information about the star itself.

The main factor that determines a star's colour is its surface temperature. Cooler stars, with surface temperatures of around three thousand degrees Celsius, glow red. As temperature rises, the colour shifts towards orange, then yellow, and eventually to white. The hottest stars of all, reaching tens of thousands of degrees, shine with a bluish light. This may seem surprising, since people often associate red with heat, but in the language of physics, blue light comes from the hottest sources.

A star's colour is also connected to its size and stage of life. Many of the largest stars are cool and red, swollen to enormous proportions late in their lives. By contrast, some smaller stars burn extremely hot and appear blue or white. Our own Sun sits roughly in the middle of this range. Although it is often drawn as yellow, the Sun is actually closer to white, and its slightly yellow appearance from the ground is caused by the Earth's atmosphere.

By studying the colours of stars, astronomers can estimate not only their temperatures but also their ages, masses and likely futures. Instruments far more sensitive than the human eye are used to measure these colours precisely. In this way, a feature that might seem purely decorative becomes a powerful tool, allowing scientists to read the life stories of stars from enormous distances across the galaxy.

questions.q: All stars have exactly the same colour when seen through a telescope.
The colour of a star gives information about the star.
Blue stars are hotter than red stars.
The Sun is the brightest star visible from Earth.
What mainly determines the colour of a star?
Cooler stars with temperatures of around three thousand degrees Celsius glow ________.
Why does the Sun appear slightly yellow from the ground?
By studying colours, astronomers can estimate a star's temperature, age, mass and likely ________.

listening.title: A Radio Talk on Stargazing
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back. Tonight our astronomy expert, Dr Owens, is sharing tips for beginners who want to start stargazing. Dr Owens, where should people begin?

DR OWENS: Thanks. My first tip is simple: get away from city lights. Even moving to a local park can make a huge difference to what you can see.

PRESENTER: And do beginners need expensive equipment?

DR OWENS: Not at all. I always say start with your own eyes, then move on to a pair of binoculars. A telescope can wait until you're more experienced.

PRESENTER: What about the best time of year?

DR OWENS: Winter skies are often the clearest, though they're cold. Always check the weather forecast before you go, and pick a night with no clouds.

PRESENTER: Any advice on apps?

DR OWENS: Yes, a free star map app on your phone will help you identify what you're looking at. And finally, be patient. Your eyes need about twenty minutes to fully adjust to the dark.

reading.title: The Growing Problem of Space Junk
reading.passage:
Since the first satellite was launched in the 1950s, humans have sent thousands of objects into orbit around the Earth. Many of these have provided enormous benefits, supporting global communication, weather forecasting and navigation. However, decades of launches have left behind a hidden and growing danger: a vast cloud of leftover material known as space junk, or orbital debris.

Space junk includes old satellites that no longer work, spent rocket stages, and countless smaller fragments produced when objects collide or break apart. Even tiny pieces are dangerous, because objects in orbit travel at extraordinary speeds, often several kilometres per second. At such velocities, a fragment no larger than a marble can strike a working spacecraft with the force of a small explosion, causing serious damage or even destroying it completely.

The situation is made worse by a troubling possibility known as a chain reaction. When two large objects collide, they break into many smaller pieces, each of which can then strike other objects. Some scientists fear that, if debris continues to build up, this process could spiral out of control, filling certain orbits with so much junk that they become too dangerous to use. Such a scenario would threaten not only future missions but also the satellites that modern life depends upon.

Engineers are now exploring a range of solutions. These include designing satellites that can safely remove themselves from orbit at the end of their working lives, and developing special spacecraft able to capture and dispose of dead satellites. International rules are also being discussed, aiming to limit the amount of new debris created by each launch. Whether these measures will be enough remains uncertain, but there is wide agreement that ignoring the problem is no longer an option.

questions.q: Satellites have provided benefits such as communication and weather forecasting.
Only large pieces of space junk are considered dangerous.
Space junk has already caused the failure of an entire country's satellite network.
There is wide agreement that the problem of space junk cannot be ignored.
Why is even a small fragment of space junk dangerous?
options: It travels at extremely high speed

questions.q: A worrying possibility in which collisions create more and more debris is called a ________ reaction.
Which of the following is mentioned as a possible solution?

listening.title: A Lecture on Cleaning Up Orbit
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning, everyone. Today we'll look at the challenge of cleaning up space junk, and why it matters for all of us. Let me give you a sense of the scale first.

There are now more than thirty thousand large pieces of debris being tracked around the Earth, and millions of smaller fragments too small to follow. Most of this material sits in what we call low Earth orbit, the busy region where many useful satellites operate.

Now, the key danger is speed. These objects move at roughly eight kilometres per second, which is far faster than any bullet. At that speed, even a flake of paint can damage a spacecraft window.

So what are the proposed solutions? One idea is to use a net to capture a dead satellite and pull it down so it burns up in the atmosphere. Another approach uses a laser fired from the ground to gently nudge debris into a safer path.

For your assignment, I'd like a short essay of one thousand words, due on Monday. Please focus on just one cleanup method.

Most debris sits in low Earth ________.
Objects in orbit move at roughly ________ kilometres per second.

options: Using a net to capture a dead satellite
Sending astronauts to collect junk by hand

questions.q: A ground-based ________ could gently nudge debris into a safer path.
The assignment essay should be ________ words long.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology in daily life. Which everyday technologies, such as satellite navigation or weather apps, do you use most often? How would your daily routine change without them?

reading.title: Listening to the Universe
reading.passage:
For most of human history, astronomy was a science of the eye. People studied the heavens by recording the positions and brightness of objects they could see, first with the naked eye and later through telescopes that gathered visible light. Yet visible light is only a narrow slice of the information that reaches us from space. In recent decades, astronomers have learned to study the universe in entirely new ways, including by listening to it.

One of the most powerful of these methods involves radio waves. Like visible light, radio waves are a form of electromagnetic radiation, but they are invisible to the eye. Huge dish-shaped antennas, some larger than a football field, collect these faint signals from distant galaxies, exploding stars and clouds of gas. Because radio waves can pass through dust that blocks ordinary light, radio telescopes reveal hidden regions of space that would otherwise remain a mystery.

An even more dramatic development came with the detection of gravitational waves. These are not waves of light or sound, but tiny ripples in space itself, produced when extremely massive objects, such as black holes, crash together. The waves are astonishingly weak by the time they reach Earth, stretching and squeezing space by an amount far smaller than the width of an atom. Detecting them required instruments of remarkable precision, and their discovery opened a completely new window onto violent cosmic events.

Together, these techniques mean that astronomers are no longer limited to what can be seen. By combining visible light, radio waves, gravitational waves and other signals, scientists can build a far richer picture of the cosmos. Each method captures a different aspect of the same events, and the most important discoveries increasingly come from bringing these separate streams of information together.

questions.q: For most of history, astronomy relied mainly on what could be seen.
Visible light contains all the information that reaches Earth from space.
Radio waves can pass through dust that blocks ordinary light.
Gravitational wave detectors are more expensive than radio telescopes.
What produces gravitational waves, according to the passage?
options: Clouds of gas glowing in the dark
Massive objects such as black holes crashing together
Radio antennas larger than a football field

questions.q: Radio waves are a form of electromagnetic ________, just like visible light.
What is the main point of the final paragraph?
options: Visible light is the most useful signal
Combining different signals gives a richer picture of the cosmos
Radio telescopes are no longer needed

questions.q: Gravitational waves stretch and squeeze space by an amount smaller than the width of an ________.

listening.title: A Seminar on New Astronomy
listening.script:
PROFESSOR: Welcome to today's seminar on modern astronomy. We'll explore how scientists now study the universe using more than just visible light. Let's start with radio astronomy.

Radio telescopes use large dish antennas to collect invisible radio waves. The biggest single dish in the world has a diameter of five hundred metres. These instruments can see through dust clouds that block ordinary light, which is a huge advantage.

Next, gravitational waves. These are ripples in space produced when black holes collide. The first confirmed detection happened in two thousand fifteen, and it won a major prize three years later. The signal lasted only a fraction of a second.

Finally, a quick note on teamwork. Modern discoveries often involve hundreds of scientists from many countries working together. For next week, please read chapter four on radio telescopes, and prepare two questions for our discussion. The reading is available in the online library, not the printed handout.

reading.title: The Silent Hunters of the Owl World
reading.passage:
Owls are among the most efficient nocturnal predators in the natural world, and their success depends on a remarkable set of physical adaptations. Unlike most birds, which rely heavily on sight during the day, owls have evolved to hunt in near-total darkness. Their large, forward-facing eyes gather even the faintest light, giving them depth perception that is essential for judging the distance to prey. However, an owl cannot move its eyes within their sockets, so it must rotate its entire head, sometimes by as much as 270 degrees, to scan its surroundings.

Perhaps the most extraordinary feature of an owl is its hearing. The feathered discs that surround the face act like satellite dishes, channelling sound towards the ears. In many species, the two ear openings are positioned at slightly different heights on the skull. This asymmetry allows the bird to pinpoint the exact location of a rustling mouse by comparing the tiny difference in the time it takes for a sound to reach each ear. Some owls can capture prey in complete darkness using hearing alone.

The owl's flight is equally specialised. The leading edges of its wing feathers are fringed with comb-like structures that break up the flow of air, dramatically reducing the noise of each wingbeat. A soft, velvety coating on the feather surface absorbs the remaining sound. As a result, the owl approaches its target almost silently, and the prey rarely hears the danger until it is too late.

Despite these advantages, owls face growing pressures from human activity. The loss of woodland and old barns has reduced the number of suitable nesting sites, while the increasing use of poisons against rodents can build up in an owl's body when it eats contaminated prey. Conservation groups in several countries now install nesting boxes and encourage farmers to leave rough grassland where small mammals can thrive, in the hope of supporting these silent hunters.

questions.q: Owls depend mainly on their sight to hunt during daylight hours.
An owl is able to turn its eyes within the sockets to look sideways.
In some owl species the two ears are set at different heights on the head.
Owls hunt more successfully in winter than in summer.
According to the passage, the comb-like edges on the wing feathers mainly serve to
options: reduce the noise of the wingbeats
improve the bird's balance in the air

questions.q: What threat to owls is mentioned in the final paragraph?
options: poisons that accumulate in their bodies

questions.q: Owls may rotate the head by as much as ________ degrees to scan around them.

listening.title: Joining a Local Wildlife Group
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good morning, Riverside Wildlife Group, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to join as a new member, please.
OFFICER: Lovely. Can I take your full name?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Hartley. That's H-A-R-T-L-E-Y.
OFFICER: Thank you. And your date of birth?
CALLER: The fourteenth of March, 1996.
OFFICER: Great. We have two membership types. The standard one is fifteen pounds a year, and the family option is twenty-eight pounds.
CALLER: I'll take the standard one.
OFFICER: Perfect. Members get a monthly newsletter and free entry to our nature reserve. We also run guided walks on the first Saturday of each month.

CALLER: Will do. Is there anything I should pay now?

reading.title: How Coral Reefs Build Themselves
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often described as the rainforests of the sea, and for good reason. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they provide a home for roughly a quarter of all marine species. What makes this richness possible is the slow, patient work of tiny animals called coral polyps, which together create some of the largest living structures on the planet.

A single polyp is no bigger than a fingernail. It has a soft, tube-shaped body topped with a ring of stinging tentacles, which it uses to catch passing food at night. By day, however, much of the polyp's energy comes from a surprising partnership. Inside its tissues live microscopic algae that capture sunlight and turn it into sugars. The algae share this food with the polyp, and in return they receive shelter and the chemicals they need to grow. This relationship gives healthy reefs their bright colours.

To build a reef, each polyp extracts minerals from the seawater and lays down a hard skeleton of limestone beneath its body. When the polyp dies, the skeleton remains, and new polyps grow on top of it. Over thousands of years, layer upon layer accumulates, forming the vast ridges and ledges that divers explore today. Because this process is so slow, a reef damaged by storms or human activity may take decades to recover.

The greatest danger facing reefs is rising sea temperature. When the water becomes too warm, the polyps expel the algae living inside them, and the coral turns a ghostly white. This event, known as bleaching, does not kill the coral immediately, but a bleached polyp is weak and may starve if cooler conditions do not return quickly. Scientists warn that more frequent heatwaves in the ocean could make recovery increasingly difficult, threatening the countless species that depend on these underwater cities.

questions.q: Coral reefs occupy more than one per cent of the ocean floor.
Coral polyps catch most of their food during the daytime.
The algae inside a polyp provide it with sugars made from sunlight.
Bleached coral always dies within a few days.
Coral reefs are home to more fish species than the open ocean.
What gives a healthy reef its bright colours?
Why may a damaged reef take a long time to recover?
options: polyps reproduce only once a year
the building process is very slow
few species are willing to return

questions.q: Each polyp builds a hard skeleton made of ________ beneath its body.

listening.title: A Tour of the Marine Centre
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Coastal Marine Centre. My name's Olivia, and I'll be showing you around this morning. Before we start, let me give you a few details. The tour lasts about ninety minutes, and we'll visit three main areas.
First, we'll head to the touch pool, where you can gently handle starfish and sea cucumbers. Please wet your hands first, and never lift the animals out of the water.
After that, we'll move to the reef tank. This is our biggest display, holding twelve thousand litres of seawater. You'll see living coral and dozens of tropical fish. Photography is allowed, but please switch off your flash, as it disturbs the animals.
Our final stop is the research lab, where staff study how warmer water affects young coral. There's a short film at the end that runs for fifteen minutes.
The gift shop closes at half past four, so do leave time to visit it. And if you'd like a hot drink, the café is just past the entrance. Right, let's make our way to the touch pool. Please stay together as we go.
