reading.title: The Hidden Network Beneath the Forest
reading.passage:
When we look at a forest, we naturally focus on the trees rising above the ground. Yet much of the forest's activity takes place out of sight, in a vast network of threads that connects almost every plant. These threads belong to fungi, and the partnership they form with tree roots may be one of the most important relationships in the natural world.

The fungi in question are not the mushrooms we see on the surface; those are merely the fruiting bodies. The main part of a fungus consists of fine filaments, thinner than a human hair, which spread through the soil in enormous quantities. Where these filaments meet the roots of a tree, the two organisms join in a partnership known as a mycorrhiza. The fungus reaches into tiny spaces in the soil that roots cannot penetrate, drawing up water and minerals, especially phosphorus, and passing them to the tree. In return, the tree shares some of the sugars it produces through photosynthesis.

Researchers have discovered that this network does more than feed individual trees. Because the filaments of a single fungus may connect several trees at once, nutrients can flow from one plant to another through the underground threads. A large, healthy tree growing in sunlight may pass sugars to a younger, shaded seedling that cannot yet make enough food for itself. Some scientists even believe that trees can send chemical warning signals through the network when they are attacked by insects, allowing neighbours to prepare their defences.

These findings have changed the way many people think about forests. Rather than a collection of separate trees competing for light, a forest can be understood as a cooperative community linked below the soil. This view also carries a warning: when machinery compresses the ground or when chemicals damage the fungi, the whole network may suffer, weakening trees that appear perfectly healthy above the surface.

questions.q: The mushrooms we see on the forest floor are the main part of a fungus.
Fungal filaments are thinner than a human hair.
The fungus provides the tree with sugars made by photosynthesis.
Larger trees may share sugars with shaded young seedlings.
Mycorrhizal networks are found in every forest on Earth.
What mineral does the fungus especially supply to the tree?
Some scientists believe trees use the network to
options: send warning signals about insect attacks

questions.q: The partnership between fungus and tree root is called a ________.

listening.title: A Student Talk on Forest Fungi
listening.script:
LECTURER: Right, today I want to introduce a topic for your group project: the fungi that live among tree roots. Let me give you some background and a few tasks.
First, a little history. The link between fungi and roots was first described in detail back in 1885, though farmers had noticed healthier crops near certain soils long before that.
Now, the fungi we're studying are extremely widespread. Researchers estimate that around ninety per cent of land plants form some kind of partnership with them. That's a huge proportion, and it shows how important these relationships are.
For the practical side, you'll each collect a small soil sample from the campus woodland. Take roughly two hundred grams, and store it in a sealed bag. Label it clearly with the date and the location.
We'll examine the samples under the microscope next Thursday, so please bring them to room fourteen by two o'clock. Don't be late, as we share the lab with another class.
Finally, your written report should be about one thousand five hundred words and is due at the end of the month. I'll post the full guidelines on the website tonight.

About ________ per cent of land plants form a partnership with these fungi.
Students should collect roughly ________ grams of soil.
Where will the samples be examined?

The written report should be about ________ words long.

speaking.q: Let's discuss the relationship between people and nature. Do you think modern city life makes people lose touch with the natural world? Some people say that humans should leave wild areas completely untouched, while others believe we should manage them actively. What is your view? How might attitudes towards nature change in the future?

reading.title: The Long Journey of the Monarch Butterfly
reading.passage:
Few sights in the natural world are as astonishing as the migration of the monarch butterfly. Each autumn, millions of these delicate insects leave the cooling forests and meadows of North America and travel thousands of kilometres south to a small region of mountains in central Mexico. There, they gather in such numbers that the branches of fir trees bend under their weight, and the forest seems to come alive with orange and black wings.

What makes the journey so remarkable is that no single butterfly completes the round trip. A monarch lives for only a few weeks in summer, so the insects that fly south in autumn are the great-grandchildren of those that flew north the previous spring. The generation that travels to Mexico is unusual: it lives for several months and does not breed until the following year. How these butterflies find their way to a place none of them has ever seen remains one of biology's great puzzles. Scientists believe they rely on the position of the sun, combined with an internal sense of time, and possibly the Earth's magnetic field.

The monarch's survival depends almost entirely on a single plant. Female butterflies lay their eggs only on milkweed, and the caterpillars eat nothing else. The milkweed contains bitter chemicals that the caterpillars absorb into their own bodies, making them taste unpleasant to birds. In this way the plant offers both food and protection.

Unfortunately, the monarch is now in decline. The spread of farming has destroyed large areas of milkweed, while illegal logging threatens the Mexican forests where the butterflies spend the winter. Conservationists are encouraging gardeners across North America to plant milkweed, creating a chain of safe stops along the migration route. Whether such efforts can reverse the decline is uncertain, but the disappearance of this extraordinary traveller would be a great loss to the natural world.

questions.q: A single monarch butterfly completes the entire round trip between North America and Mexico.
The generation that flies to Mexico lives longer than the summer generations.
Scientists are completely certain how monarchs find their way to Mexico.
Caterpillars feed on several different kinds of plant.
Monarch butterflies are more numerous than any other butterfly in North America.
Why do the caterpillars taste unpleasant to birds?
options: they absorb bitter chemicals from milkweed

questions.q: What are conservationists asking gardeners to do?
options: stop using their gardens in winter

questions.q: Female monarchs lay their eggs only on ________.

listening.title: Planning a Butterfly Garden Project
listening.script:
MEGAN: Hey, Tom, are you free to plan the butterfly garden project? The community center wants our proposal by Friday.
TOM: Sure. So the main idea is to plant flowers that attract butterflies, right?
MEGAN: Exactly. The center has given us a budget of three hundred dollars for plants and tools.
TOM: Okay. I read that milkweed is the most important plant for monarchs, so we should buy plenty of it.
MEGAN: Agreed. There's a nursery on Maple Street that sells it cheaply. We could order around forty plants to start.
TOM: Sounds good. We'll also need a water source. A shallow dish with stones works well, apparently.
MEGAN: Right. And we should avoid using any pesticides, since they harm the caterpillars.
TOM: Definitely. When do we actually start planting?
MEGAN: The center suggested the second weekend of April, when the frost has passed.
TOM: Perfect. I'll ask if we can get some volunteers from the local school.
MEGAN: Great idea. Let's meet again on Wednesday to finish writing it up.

The budget for plants and tools is ________ dollars.
They plan to order about ________ milkweed plants to start.

speaking.q: Let's talk about the weather and seasons. Which season of the year do you like best, and why? Does the weather affect how you feel or what you do? Do you prefer hot weather or cold weather?

reading.title: The Surprising Intelligence of the Octopus
reading.passage:
For centuries, people assumed that intelligence belonged to creatures resembling ourselves: mammals with large brains, social lives and quick reactions. The octopus challenges this assumption completely. With a body that has no bones, eight flexible arms and a nervous system unlike any other on Earth, it is among the most intelligent of all invertebrates, yet it is more closely related to a snail than to any animal we usually think of as clever.

One of the most striking features of the octopus is the way its intelligence is distributed throughout its body. Although it has a central brain, roughly two-thirds of its nerve cells lie in its arms. Each arm can taste, touch and even make simple decisions on its own, exploring a crevice or gripping an object without waiting for instructions from the brain. This arrangement allows the octopus to handle several tasks at once, something that would overwhelm a more centralised mind.

In laboratory tests, octopuses have shown a clear ability to learn. They can open screw-top jars to reach the food inside, find their way through simple mazes, and recognise individual human keepers, behaving differently towards those who have treated them well. Some have been observed using coconut shells as portable shelters, carrying the pieces across the seabed and assembling them when danger threatens. Such tool use was once thought to be limited to a handful of mammals and birds.

The octopus is also a master of disguise. Special cells in its skin allow it to change colour and even texture in less than a second, blending into rocks, sand or coral. This ability serves both to hide from predators and to ambush prey. What puzzles scientists is that octopuses appear to be colour-blind, yet they match the colours of their surroundings with great accuracy. The mechanism behind this remains unclear, and it is just one of many mysteries surrounding a creature whose intelligence evolved along an entirely separate path from our own.

questions.q: The octopus is more closely related to a snail than to a mammal.
Most of an octopus's nerve cells are located in its central brain.
Each arm of an octopus can make some simple decisions independently.
Octopuses behave the same way towards all human keepers.
Octopuses live longer in captivity than in the wild.
What have some octopuses been seen using as portable shelters?
What do scientists find puzzling about the octopus's camouflage?
options: the animal seems to be colour-blind

questions.q: About ________ of the octopus's nerve cells lie in its arms.

listening.title: A Lecture on Animal Intelligence
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we continue our series on animal cognition by looking at how scientists actually measure intelligence in non-human species. It's harder than you might think.
The first point to grasp is that there's no single test. A measure that suits a chimpanzee may be useless for an octopus, simply because their bodies and senses are so different. So researchers design tasks that fit each animal.
One common approach is the problem-solving test. The animal is given a reward, say a piece of food, hidden inside a container, and we record how long it takes to work out the solution. In one famous study, an octopus opened a sealed jar in just under five minutes.
A second approach measures memory. Here, animals are trained to associate a shape or sound with food, and then tested days, or even weeks, later to see what they remember. Some birds recall hiding places for up to nine months.
For your seminar next week, please read chapter six of the textbook and come prepared to discuss one example of tool use. The reading is fairly short, around twenty pages, so there's no excuse. We'll meet in the usual room at eleven.

questions.q: What is the lecturer's main point about measuring animal intelligence?
options: it is easy with the right equipment
there is no single test that suits every species

questions.q: In the study mentioned, an octopus opened a sealed jar in just under ________ minutes.
The second approach described by the lecturer measures the animals'
Some birds can recall hiding places for up to ________ months.
Students must read chapter ________ of the textbook.
The seminar will start at ________ in the usual room.

speaking.q: Let's talk about intelligence in animals. Why do you think humans are so interested in how clever animals are? Do you believe animals have feelings in the same way that people do? Should the way we treat animals change as we learn more about their intelligence?

Visitors by section (thousands)
Section | Spring | Summer | Autumn | Winter

vocab.example: The octopus is the most intelligent invertebrate.
Its eight flexible arms can reach into narrow gaps.

Its intelligence is distributed throughout the body.

reading.title: How Babies Learn to Talk
reading.passage:
From the moment they are born, human infants are surrounded by the sounds of language, and within a remarkably short time they begin the complex task of learning to speak. Long before a baby produces its first recognisable word, it is busy absorbing the rhythms, melodies and patterns of the speech it hears around it. Researchers now believe that this period of silent listening is just as important as the later stage when words finally emerge.

Studies show that newborns can already distinguish the language their mother spoke during pregnancy from an unfamiliar one. This suggests that learning begins even before birth, as the sounds of speech travel through the body to the developing baby. After birth, infants pay especially close attention to the exaggerated, sing-song style of speech that adults instinctively use when talking to them. This style, sometimes called 'parentese', features a slower pace, higher pitch and clearer vowels, all of which appear to help the brain identify the building blocks of words.

Between six and twelve months, babies gradually narrow their focus. At first they can detect the sound differences used in every human language, but they soon become specialists in the particular sounds of their own mother tongue, while losing sensitivity to distinctions they never hear. A Japanese baby, for example, can initially tell the English sounds 'r' and 'l' apart, yet this ability fades unless the contrast is regularly encountered.

The first true words usually appear around the first birthday, often naming familiar people, objects or actions. Progress then accelerates dramatically: by the age of two, many children command several hundred words and begin combining them into simple sentences. While the pace varies considerably from child to child, the underlying sequence is strikingly consistent across cultures, which has led many scientists to conclude that the capacity for language is, in some sense, built into the human mind.

questions.q: Babies begin learning about language only after they are born.
Newborns can tell their mother's language apart from a language they have not heard before.
Parents are formally trained to use 'parentese' with their infants.
What happens to babies' sound perception between six and twelve months?
options: They become able to hear all possible language sounds
They specialise in the sounds of their own language
They stop paying attention to speech

questions.q: According to the passage, the order in which children acquire language is:
options: faster in some languages than others

questions.q: The exaggerated style of speech adults use with babies is sometimes called ________.
By the age of two, many children know several ________ words.
The speed at which children learn to talk is identical for every child.

listening.title: Joining a Language Exchange Club
listening.script:
STAFF: Good afternoon, welcome to the City Language Exchange. Are you here to register?
VISITOR: Yes, I'd like to join. A friend recommended it.
STAFF: Lovely. Could I take your name first?
VISITOR: It's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
STAFF: Thank you. And which language would you like to practise?
VISITOR: Spanish, mainly. I'm a complete beginner, though.
STAFF: No problem at all. We have a beginners' group that meets every Tuesday evening at half past six.
VISITOR: That works for me. Where do they meet?
STAFF: In Room 4, on the first floor. The session lasts about ninety minutes.
VISITOR: And is there a fee?
STAFF: The membership costs fifteen pounds a month, which includes free coffee.
VISITOR: That's reasonable. Do I need to bring anything?
STAFF: Just a notebook, really. We provide the worksheets. Oh, and remember to download our app for the vocabulary lists.
VISITOR: Great. I'll sign up now.

reading.title: The Slow Death of Endangered Languages
reading.passage:
There are roughly seven thousand languages spoken in the world today, yet linguists warn that nearly half of them may vanish before the end of this century. A language is generally considered endangered when children stop learning it as their first tongue, choosing instead a more widely spoken language that offers greater social or economic advantage. Once that intergenerational chain is broken, the decline can be surprisingly swift.

The forces behind this loss are varied. Migration draws younger speakers towards cities, where the dominant national language is essential for work and schooling. Television, the internet and global business all reward fluency in a handful of major languages, making smaller ones seem impractical or old-fashioned. In some cases, official policies have actively discouraged minority languages, punishing children for speaking them at school.

What is lost when a language disappears is far more than a set of words. Each language carries a unique way of describing the world, encoding knowledge about local plants, weather patterns, kinship and history that may exist nowhere else. When the last fluent speaker of a language dies, an entire archive of human experience can vanish with them, often without ever having been written down.

Not everyone is resigned to this fate. Communities around the world are fighting to keep their languages alive through immersion schools, digital dictionaries and recordings of elderly speakers. Some efforts have produced encouraging results: a few languages once thought beyond rescue now have a new generation of young speakers. Success, however, usually depends on whether the community itself views the language as a living part of its identity rather than a relic of the past. Without that sense of ownership, even well-funded programmes tend to falter.

questions.q: Linguists predict that about half of today's languages could disappear this century.
A language is seen as endangered mainly when adults refuse to speak it.
Governments have always supported the teaching of minority languages.
The number of endangered languages is higher in Africa than in Asia.
According to the passage, what is lost when a language dies?
What does the passage say is essential for saving a language?
options: The community valuing it as part of its identity

questions.q: There are roughly ________ thousand languages spoken in the world today.
Communities try to preserve languages through immersion schools, digital dictionaries and ________ of elderly speakers.

listening.title: A Tutorial on a Translation Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: Hi Mia, come in. I wanted to discuss your translation project. How's it going?
MIA: Quite well, but I'm finding the deadline a bit tight.
TUTOR: I understand. Remind me, which text are you translating?
MIA: A short story originally written in Italian.
TUTOR: Good choice. The main challenge with fiction is capturing the tone, not just the meaning.
MIA: Yes, I keep struggling with the humour. Some jokes just don't work in English.
TUTOR: That's normal. Try to find an equivalent effect rather than a literal version. Footnotes can help too, but use them sparingly.
MIA: How long should the final report be?
TUTOR: Around two thousand words, explaining the choices you made.
MIA: And when is it due?
TUTOR: The fourteenth of March. Submit it through the online portal.
MIA: Should I include the original text as well?
TUTOR: Yes, please attach it as an appendix. That makes marking much easier.
MIA: Thanks, that's really helpful.

The final report should be around ________ words.

Mia should attach the original text as an ________.

reading.title: The Rise of Emoji
reading.passage:
Few inventions have spread through global communication as quickly as the emoji. First created in Japan in the late 1990s, these tiny pictures of faces, objects and symbols were designed to add warmth and clarity to short electronic messages. Within two decades they had become a familiar feature of texting around the world, with billions sent every single day. Some commentators even describe them as the fastest-growing form of communication in human history.

The appeal of emoji lies partly in their ability to convey emotion. Written messages, especially brief ones, can easily be misread; a comment intended as a joke may come across as rude or cold. A well-placed smiling face or wink can soften the tone and signal the writer's true intention, performing a role similar to facial expressions and gestures in face-to-face conversation. In this sense, emoji do not replace words so much as supplement them.

Not everyone welcomes their dominance. Critics worry that heavy reliance on pictures may weaken people's ability to express themselves precisely in writing. Others point out that the meaning of an emoji is not always fixed: the same symbol can be interpreted quite differently depending on the reader's age, culture or even the device being used, since the design varies between platforms. A gesture meant kindly in one country may seem offensive in another.

Despite these concerns, most linguists take a calm view. They note that humans have always combined images with text, from ancient cave paintings to medieval manuscripts, and that emoji are simply the latest chapter in this long story. Rather than damaging language, they argue, emoji enrich it, giving writers a flexible new tool for expressing the subtle emotional shades that plain text often struggles to capture.

questions.q: Emoji were first created in Japan in the late 1990s.
According to the passage, emoji are mainly intended to replace words entirely.
The same emoji can look different on different devices.
Teenagers use emoji more often than older adults.
Why can short written messages cause misunderstanding?
What is the general attitude of most linguists toward emoji?
Emoji can perform a role similar to facial expressions and ________ in face-to-face talk.
Linguists compare emoji to ancient cave paintings and medieval ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Sign Language
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning. Today I'd like to clear up a common misunderstanding about sign language. Many people assume there is just one universal sign language used everywhere. In fact, there are well over a hundred different sign languages worldwide, each with its own grammar and vocabulary. American Sign Language, for instance, is quite distinct from British Sign Language, even though both countries speak English. Interestingly, ASL is more closely related to French Sign Language for historical reasons. Sign languages are full, natural languages, not simply gestures or mime. They have their own word order, their own regional dialects, and even their own slang among younger users. Facial expression is essential too: raising the eyebrows can turn a statement into a question, much as the rising tone of voice does in spoken English. Children exposed to sign language from birth learn it on exactly the same timetable as hearing children learn speech, producing their first signs at around the same age that babies say their first words. This shows that the human brain is ready for language in any form.

questions.q: What common misunderstanding does the lecturer correct?
options: That there is one universal sign language
That sign language has no grammar

American Sign Language is most closely related to:

reading.title: The Power of Body Language
reading.passage:
When we think about communication, we naturally focus on words, yet a great deal of what we express to one another never passes through speech at all. Posture, gestures, eye contact and facial expression form a silent channel that runs alongside our words, sometimes reinforcing them and sometimes quietly contradicting them. Researchers in this field, known as non-verbal communication, argue that learning to read these signals is a crucial part of understanding others.

One of the most studied aspects is facial expression. A small number of expressions, such as those for happiness, fear and surprise, appear to be recognised across very different cultures, leading some scientists to believe they are partly innate rather than purely learned. Other signals, however, are far more culturally specific. The amount of eye contact considered polite, the distance people stand apart, and even the meaning of a nod can differ sharply from one society to another, and misunderstandings easily arise when these unwritten rules are broken.

Gestures are a particularly rich area of variation. A movement of the hand that seems friendly in one country may be meaningless, or even insulting, in another. Travellers who rely on familiar gestures abroad sometimes find themselves causing offence without the slightest awareness of having done so. For this reason, experts advise visitors to observe local behaviour carefully before assuming that their own habits will be understood.

Body language is also revealing because it is often harder to control than speech. While people can choose their words with care, their bodies may leak signs of nervousness, boredom or impatience that they would rather hide. Skilled communicators learn to notice this gap between what is said and what is shown. Yet experts caution against reading too much into a single gesture; crossed arms, for example, may signal defensiveness, but they may equally mean nothing more than that the room is cold.

questions.q: Non-verbal signals always reinforce the words a person is speaking.
Some facial expressions seem to be recognised across very different cultures.
The polite amount of eye contact is the same in every society.
Most travel guidebooks now include a chapter on gestures.
Why is body language often more revealing than speech?
What do experts warn about interpreting a single gesture?
The field that studies these silent signals is known as ________ communication.
Crossed arms may signal defensiveness, or simply mean that the room is ________.

listening.title: Planning a Public Speaking Workshop
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Right, let's finalise the public speaking workshop. We agreed on Saturday, didn't we?
ASSISTANT: Yes, Saturday the eighth, from ten until four.
ORGANISER: And the venue?
ASSISTANT: The main hall was booked, so I've reserved the seminar room instead. It holds thirty people.
ORGANISER: That should be plenty. How many have signed up so far?
ASSISTANT: Twenty-two, with a few more expected.
ORGANISER: Good. Now, the morning session focuses on controlling nerves, and the afternoon on body language.
ASSISTANT: Should I order the printed handouts?
ORGANISER: Yes, please. And don't forget the name badges this time.
ASSISTANT: Of course. What about refreshments?
ORGANISER: Tea and coffee in the morning, and a light lunch at half past twelve.
ASSISTANT: I'll arrange that with the caterer. Anything else?
ORGANISER: Just remind everyone to bring a short prepared speech, about two minutes long.
ASSISTANT: Got it. I'll send the email today.

reading.title: Can Machines Truly Translate?
reading.passage:
Only a generation ago, the idea of pointing a phone at a foreign menu and instantly reading it in your own language belonged firmly to science fiction. Today, automatic translation tools are used by hundreds of millions of people, turning text and even speech from one language into another in seconds. The progress has been astonishing, yet the question of whether machines can truly translate, rather than merely substitute words, remains far from settled.

Early translation systems relied on enormous sets of grammatical rules, painstakingly written by human experts. The results were often clumsy, because language rarely follows rules neatly. The breakthrough came when engineers shifted to a different approach: instead of teaching machines the rules, they fed them vast quantities of texts that had already been translated by people, allowing the system to learn patterns statistically. More recently, powerful neural networks have pushed quality higher still, producing translations that can sound remarkably natural.

Despite this, serious limitations persist. Machines handle straightforward, factual sentences well, but they stumble over the things that make human language rich: humour, irony, poetry and cultural references. A phrase whose meaning depends on context or on shared background knowledge can defeat even the most advanced system, sometimes with comic or embarrassing results. Crucially, a machine does not understand what it is translating in any human sense; it predicts likely word sequences without grasping meaning.

For this reason, most experts argue that human translators are not about to disappear. Instead, their role is changing. Rather than translating every word from scratch, many now edit and refine the output of machines, correcting subtle errors that software cannot detect. In high-stakes settings such as law, medicine and literature, where a single mistranslation can have grave consequences, the careful judgement of a skilled human remains, for now, irreplaceable.

questions.q: Early translation systems were based on large sets of grammatical rules.
Modern systems learned by being fed texts already translated by people.
Machines understand the meaning of what they translate in the same way humans do.
Neural translation tools are now more popular than human translators in courts.
What kind of language do machines find most difficult?
According to the passage, how is the role of human translators changing?
options: They now edit and refine machine output

questions.q: The major breakthrough came when systems learned patterns ________ from translated texts.
In law, medicine and ________, skilled human judgement remains irreplaceable.

listening.title: Final Lecture: The Future of Communication
listening.script: LECTURER: In this final session, let's look ahead at how communication may evolve. The first major trend is real-time translation. Within a decade, earpieces that translate conversations almost instantly could become ordinary, removing many language barriers in travel and business. Second, expect a continued rise in voice technology. We already speak to our devices, and typing may slowly give way to talking, especially among younger users. A third trend is the blending of text with images, video and sound into richer messages, which some call multimodal communication. However, I want to add a note of caution. As communication becomes faster and more automatic, we may lose patience with slower, deeper forms of conversation. Studies already suggest that attention spans are shrinking. My final point is this: the tools will change dramatically, but the human need to connect, to be understood and to belong, will not. Technology can carry our words further and faster than ever, yet the responsibility for using them wisely will always remain with us. Thank you for a wonderful term.

questions.q: What is the first major trend mentioned?

What caution does the lecturer give?
options: We may lose patience with deeper conversation

The lecturer says the human need to connect and to ________ will not change.
